### THE BOOK OF 1 & 2 CORINTHIANS{.heading_sF}

# First Corinthians {.heading_s2S}

## Introduction {#c2J.xhtml_a34B .heading_s2U}

The First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians has 16 chapters, 437 verses, and 9,489 words. The word "Corinthians" means "the ornaments."

This first epistle was probably written in A.D. 57. Scofield gives A.D. 59 as the date of authorship, and there are a variety of other opinions. So although there is no way to settle the date of the writing for certain, you can bet the general time is somewhere between A.D. 57 and A.D. 59.

Corinthians is a sort of practical commentary on Romans. Romans is telling a man how he got saved and explaining the doctrine of salvation to him. Corinthians is the outworking of that salvation in the practical Christian life.

The Corinthian church is the carnal church, and it is the church mentioned in Acts 18. It is the church with which Paul has more trouble than any other church to which he writes, and he has more corrective measures written to that church than any other epistle. There is a whole chapter in Corinthians trying to correct an abuse in tongues. There is half a chapter written to correct abuse of the Lord's Supper. There is half a chapter written about hair cuts. There is half a chapter written about arguing among yourselves. There is a whole chapter written about immorality in the church.

So the Corinthian church, as a church, is a new church that is filled with spiritual babies. That is where the Pentecostals always are. They are always in 1 Corinthians 14 where the babies are. It is strange how hung up some folks can get on 1 Corinthians 14 and not realize Corinth was the worst church in the New Testament. One of the guys in the congregation was having relations with his father's wife, and nobody was even concerned about it. They were arguing about their gifts. So with the character of this church in mind, let's begin.

[]{#c33.xhtml}

## Chapter 1 {.heading_s39}

1:1 Paul, called to be an apostle of Jesus Christ through the will of God, and Sosthenes our brother,

2 Unto the church of God which is at Corinth, to them that are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints, with all that in every place call upon the name of Jesus Christ our Lord, both theirs and ours:

3 Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ.

["Paul, called to be an apostle" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 1). The infinitive, there, ["to be,"]{.class_s36N} is in italics. You can see it makes better sense to say ["called to be an apostle,"]{.class_s36N} which was his calling.

So often the [King James Bible]{.class_s36M} will put words in italics to complete the sense of the passage, and it needs to be done. For example, look at verse 8, ["Who shall also confirm you unto the end, that ye may be blameless...."]{.class_s36N} Now, you can read, "Who shall also confirm you unto the end, blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ" but ["Confirm you unto the end, that ye may be blameless"]{.class_s36N} is a much better reading, much better English. Look again at verse 11, ["For it hath been declared unto me of you, my brethren, by them which are of the house of Chloe\...."]{.class_s36N} The italics are ["which are of the house."]{.class_s36N} If you left the italicized words out you would have, "For it hath been declared unto me of you, my brethren by them of Chloe\...." Well, "by them of Chloe" is understandable, but it is not good English.

So when you are translating from one language to another, the exact, verbatim, word-for-word translating is one thing, and producing a good translation is something else. There is no way to translate from Hebrew into English or from Greek into English without adding words in italics. There is no way. You will come upon places that if you didn't put something in, it would make the most atrocious English you ever read in your life. The [King James Bible]{.class_s36M} is honest. When it adds to the text, it indicates what words are added to the text. This makes the [King James Bible]{.class_s36M} an honest translation. You can get some [King James Bibles]{.class_s36M} that don't have these words in italics, printed straight through. I think Oxford puts out some Bibles like that. In a case like that I wouldn't worry about it. It still makes good English.             

["Unto the church of God" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 2). That is the Bible title for the Body of Christ, ["the church of God."]{.class_s36N} Not "the church of Christ." The term "church of Christ" doesn't appear anywhere in the Bible. ["Churches of Christ"]{.class_s36N} appears in Romans 16:16, but that is multiple churches. The single term is ["the church of God,"]{.class_s36N} as it stands. So the holiness people have better backing for the name of their church than the Campbellites do.

The next thing to notice about that is when Paul says ["the church of God,"]{.class_s36N} he is talking about Gentile believers at Corinth, and they are in the body of Jesus Christ.

["The church of God," ]{.class_s36N}as a designation, is a reference to the body of Christ. First Corinthians 12:13 says, ["For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}I point that out to you to show you the church of God, the Body of Christ, is here before Paul is converted. Turn to Galatians 1:13 and notice that Paul persecuted ["the church of God"]{.class_s36N} before he was saved. In Galatians 1:13, ["the church of God"]{.class_s36N} is a reference to the one body of Jesus Christ which was here before Paul was converted. Galatians 1:13, ["For ye have heard of my conversation in time past in the Jews' religion, how that beyond measure I persecuted the church of God\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}When Paul says ["church of God,"]{.class_s36N} he is not referring to a Jewish church [only]{.class_s36M}. Here he is referring to a Gentile church at Corinth, the people of the one body and one Spirit (which shows you that Cornelius Stam, although he may not be all wet, is at least damp).

A "Baptist brider" believes the only church in the Bible is the local church that you can see visibly, and there is no invisible church you can't see. They also teach that the Roman Catholic Church taught the universal church as the invisible church, and therefore, the only church there is is a local church that you can see. This is rather ridiculous when you consider that there is one independent Baptist church here and fifteen more downtown. Which one of them is the ["body"]{.class_s36N}? ["There is one body."]{.class_s36N} Right? Well, there is not one [local ]{.class_s36M}church. There are hundreds of them.

Talking about the invisible church, I don't believe all the church is invisible. I think if you are saved, and I am looking at you, and you are a member of the body of Christ, and you are part of a visible church; but you can't tell me that Martin Luther isn't in the Body of Christ. Has anybody see Martin lately? He's invisible.

["Unto the church of God which is at Corinth\..."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 2). Then ["the church of God"]{.class_s36N} is in all kinds of places. He is writing to the church of God at [Corinth.]{.class_s36M} In another place, he is writing to the church of God at [Ephesus]{.class_s36M}. At another place he is writing to the church of God someplace else. That is, the church, the body of Christ, is all over the place with local assemblies in different places.

["To them that are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints\..." ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 2). If you left out the italics you would have "called saints," which is true. A fellow said, "Let the Pope saint the dead, but let God make the living saintly." Every born-again person is a saint. There is no such thing as St. Francis and St. Perry Como and St. Bernadette and St. Clement or Danny Thomas praying to St. Jude, and all that flip flap, to get a hospital up in Memphis. Every Christian is a [saint]{.class_s36M}. If you are [saved]{.class_s36M}, you are a saint. The Catholic Church canonizes people after they are dead if they give enough money or enough publicity. If a Catholic gives enough publicity to the Catholic Church (like Mother Cabrini the American nun), then they make a saint out of her. If they can get control of a country by "canonizing" a great preacher, they will make a saint out of Pat and call him "St. Patrick" and tie up [Ireland ]{.class_s36M}for 500 years. But that isn't [Biblical.]{.class_s36M} In the Bible, saved people are the saints, and you are ["called to be saints."]{.class_s36N} You may not be [saintly,]{.class_s36M} but you are a saint.

["Called to be saints, with all that in every place call upon the name of Jesus Christ\..." ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 2). So this epistle is addressed to more than just a local church. It is addressed to ALL ["in every place"]{.class_s36N} that ["call upon the name of Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N} ["The name of Jesus Christ our Lord, both theirs"]{.class_s36N}---their saints, ["and ours"]{.class_s36N}---our saints. The saints here, the saints there, the saints anywhere.

["To them that are sanctified in Christ Jesus" ]{.class_s36N}(vs.2)

Every Christian who is saved is [sanctified ]{.class_s36M}in Christ. The word "sanctification" means "set apart." You have been set apart from the world by the Lord taking you and putting you in Jesus Christ. Turn to 1 Corinthians 6 and let me show you a verse like it. Sanctification means [to set apart for a purpose.]{.class_s36M} Sometimes it is a good purpose, and sometimes it is a bad purpose, but it is reserved for a purpose. First Corinthians 6:11, ["And such were some of you: but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If somebody comes up to you in a holiness meeting invitation and says, "Brother, have you ever been sanctified?" You say, "Yes, sir, I sure have been." Right. You have been. Of course, when he says that he means have you gone to the altar and rolled around and kicked for a while and thrashed and come up and got rid of [Life ]{.class_s36M}magazine or something like that.

["Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 3). That is the way Paul always begins to talk. Every time he opens his mouth he has got to say Jesus Christ. Verse 1, ["Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 2,[ "Christ Jesus"]{.class_s36N}; ["Jesus Christ our Lord"]{.class_s36N}; verse 3, ["Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 4, ["Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 6, ["Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 7, ["Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 8, ["Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 9, ["Jesus Christ our Lord"]{.class_s36N}; verse 10, ["Lord Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N} Do you know what that is? That is a Spirit-filled man. A man that is filled with the Holy Spirit raves about the Lord Jesus Christ. [He does not rave about the Holy Ghost. ]{.class_s36M}When the Holy Ghost came, Christ said, ["He shall not speak of himself\...He shall glorify me"]{.class_s36N} (John 16:13--14). So people that are always talking about the Holy Spirit are not "filled with the Spirit," or at least not the Holy Spirit.

["Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}There is the full name. Lord, that's His deity, God the Father. Jesus, that's the human Messiah, the Son. Christ, "Christos," anointed, that's the Holy Spirit. Jesus Christ represents the Trinity. He is not every person in the Trinity. He is one person of the Trinity, but ["in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily,"]{.class_s36N} (Col. 2:9). So in His name the Trinity is indicated, "Lord Jesus Christ."

Now, you can tell a lot by the way people refer to Christ. People who just say, "Christ, Christ, Christ, Christ. Make your decision for Christ. Receive Christ. Let Christ come into your life. Church of Christ. Christ, Christ, Christ," are people who tend to slight the Deity of Jesus Christ. Christ is a title meaning "anointed Messiah." It doesn't indicate His Deity. The devil is a "christ." The devil is anointed, Ezekiel 28, and he is a "christ," Acts chapter 4, but he is not ["the Lord's Christ,"]{.class_s36N} Luke chapter 2. People who say, "Jesus, dear Jesus" and "I love you, Jesus," and "Blessed Jesus," and "Hold my hand, Jesus," and "Jesus, Jesus, Jesus," and leave off "Christ" and "Lord," are emphasizing too much the humanity of Christ. They always have one of these love-me, kiss-me, hug-me, touch-me religions. That's the "Jesus stuff."

"Blessed Jesus hold my hand" and "dear Jesus" and "sweet Jesus" and all that stuff. A little of that stuff is all right, but you have got to remember that "dear Jesus," brethren, "sweet Jesus" is the Lord of glory, and you have got to remember that "dear Jesus," "sweet Jesus" is the Almighty, the Everlasting, the Alpha and Omega, the Beginning and the Ending. In your personal dealing with the Lord maybe that is the way you handle things. Maybe your terms of endearment and your fellowship with the Lord in your devotions brings you close enough to talk that way in prayer, and I would find no fault with you. I would have nothing to say about it. But I would be careful of it. You are not dealing with a little plaything or Santa Claus. You are dealing with God. And the title is "Lord Jesus."

I used to like to listen to Bob Jones Sr. pray. I never heard him start a prayer that he didn't say "Lord God" when he prayed. I've heard him say "Jesus" a couple of times in a prayer and "Lord Jesus" a couple of times, but when he would start to pray he would say, "Lord God." He would start off like that. Jesus is Lord, and He is God ["manifest in the flesh."]{.class_s36N} He is [the Lord Jesus Christ.]{.class_s36M}

1:4 I thank my God always on your behalf, for the grace of God which is given you by Jesus Christ;

5 That in every thing ye are enriched by him, in all utterance, and in all knowledge;

6 Even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you:

7 So that ye come behind in no gift; waiting for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ:

8 Who shall also confirm you unto the end, that ye may be blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.

9 God is faithful, by whom ye were called unto the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ our Lord.

["For the grace of God which is given you by Jesus Christ,"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). Grace comes through Christ. All the blessings you get come through Jesus Christ.

["Enriched by him, in all utterance, and in all knowledge;" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 5). Two of the blessings given to the Corinthian church by the Lord were knowledge and the ability to witness.

["So that ye come behind in no gift\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). This is the church that discusses the gifts. He has three whole chapters on the gifts of the Holy Spirit. ["So that ye come behind in no gift."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He wasn't there to tell them they couldn't have the gifts or that the gifts were wrong. He was there to correct their misuse of the gifts. ["So that ye come behind in no gift; waiting for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ:"]{.class_s36N}

Now a great promise.

["Who shall also confirm you unto the end\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 8). Did you ever hear somebody give you Matthew 24:13, ["But he that shall endure unto the end, the same shall be saved"]{.class_s36N}?[ ]{.class_s36S}When they give you that, you give them 1 Corinthians 1:8. I'm not worrying about "enduring to the end." [I am going to endure to the end.]{.class_s36S} How do I know I am? Because the Lord is going to confirm me ["unto the end."]{.class_s36N} The Lord is going to keep me all the way. ["Who shall also confirm you unto the end."]{.class_s36N}

["That ye may be blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 8).[ ]{.class_s36S}The Christian is not blameless until after the Judgment Seat of Christ is over. At the Judgment Seat he receives ["the things done in his body,"]{.class_s36N} whether they ["be good or bad"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 5:10). So it is ["blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N} in the sense of the day of the Lord, except, of course, now we know who the "Lord" is, it is the Lord Jesus. Let me show you the same thing. Second Thessalonians 2:2, ["the day of Christ,"]{.class_s36N} gives scholars so much trouble that somebody changed that thing to "day of the Lord" to try to make it make better sense. But they didn't need to because in 2 Thessalonians 2, ["the day of Christ" ]{.class_s36N}overlaps the Judgment Seat of Christ and the Advent. There is an overlap.

["God is faithful\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9).[ ]{.class_s36S}God is going to get you home. In the day of the Lord, when Christ comes back, there won't be a fault in you. This passage is referring to the end of their lives. Notice in verses 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 that there isn't any discussion of a period of time, like you have in Matthew 24, Hebrews 3, Hebrews 6, and Daniel 12. ["God is faithful\...."]{.class_s36N} Now, we are not always faithful, but God is. ["God is faithful, by whom ye were called unto the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ our Lord."]{.class_s36N}

1:10 Now I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you; but that ye be perfectly joined together in the same mind and in the same judgment.

11 For it hath been declared unto me of you, my brethren, by them which are of the house of Chloe, that there are contentions among you.

12 Now this I say, that every one of you saith, I am of Paul; and I of Apollos; and I of Cephas; and I of Christ.

13 Is Christ divided? was Paul crucified for you? or were ye baptized in the name of Paul?

14 I thank God that I baptized none of you, but Crispus and Gaius;

15 Lest any should say that I had baptized in mine own name.

16 And I baptized also the household of Stephanas: besides, I know not whether I baptized any other.

17 For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel: not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect.

["Now I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye all speak the same thing\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10). Do you reckon that prayer was ever answered? Do you know of any time in the history of the Church that Christians all spoke the same thing? ["And that there be no divisions among you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10). That would be a wild church, a church with no divisions in it! ["No divisions among you\...."]{.class_s36N}

Saints get split up. Here is how they get split up. Verse 12, one of them says, ["]{.class_s36P-0}[I am of Paul]{.class_s36N}[."]{.class_s36P-0} That's Stam, Baker, O'Hare, Ballinger and Bullinger. Somebody says, ["]{.class_s36P-0}[I]{.class_s36N} \[am\] [of]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}[Apollos]{.class_s36N}[."]{.class_s36P-0} That's an Orthodox Jew. ["]{.class_s36P-0}[I ]{.class_s36N}\[am\][ of Cephas]{.class_s36N}[."]{.class_s36P-0} That's your Roman Catholic, following Simon Peter. Then there is your Camp­bellite, ["I]{.class_s36N} \[am\] [of Christ."]{.class_s36N} When some apostate calls you a "Ruckmanite," tell them you are a Bible believer. They will say, "Well, Ruckman taught you that." You say, "Well, do you believe the Bible?" They will say, "Yes." You say, "Which one?" Then they will tell you about all the mistakes in the [King James Bible.]{.class_s36M} Then you say, "Who taught you that?" The people that listen to me come out believing it. The people that listen to them come out not believing it. Take your pick.

All right, in verse 12, one guy says he is following Paul, one guy says he is following Apollos, one guy says he is following Cephas, and one guy says he is following Christ. The guy that says he is following Christ is lying. If you are following Christ then you can "get along" with Paul, Apollos, and Cephas.

God's people split and split and split until the splinters split with the splinters and leave nothing but sawdust. You have got churches in this town that have split six times. Brent Baptist Church put out twelve churches in this town. Twelve churches came out of one church. You wait until Smyrna Baptist (Pensacola, FL) splits. You talk about putting out missions all over this town! A Catholic doesn't have a chance when the Baptists get going.

Every church splits once in a while. It has to, you know. After a big split you will have four churches in about eight years. Fifty or sixty people leaving, and taking their tithes, leaves a hole in the treasury. One man was crying to me about a big split they just had, and I said, "Cheer up. You're doing a great missionary work." I mean, two of the churches that split have split again since they left, and they've sent out two more. If the Baptists keep on splitting, there won't be room for a Catholic to move around. Pensacola has thirty-five Southern Baptist churches, fifteen independent Baptist churches, and twenty interdenominational churches that teach Baptist doctrine. That makes seventy churches teaching Baptist doctrine in this town with a population of 80,000. ["Now I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you\...."]{.class_s36N} Christians get miffed, and they get muffed, and they split up over the craziest things.

Now, not every [division ]{.class_s36M}in the Scriptures is bad. Jesus said in Luke 12:51, ["Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division."]{.class_s36N} In the context, the Lord is talking about dividing Israel up among those that believe in him and those that don't believe in Him. But there is a point in this verse that is repeated by Paul for the Church. Turn to Romans 16 and look at verse 17. Notice that Paul very definitely [recommends ]{.class_s36M}a division. In 1 Corinthians 5 and 6, Paul even recommends some people to be thrown out of the church.

There is a fine line between having your convictions and standing by your convictions and getting along with God's people, and compromising and not standing by your convictions. There is a fine line between knowing when to break off fellowship or quit fellowship with somebody over a doctrinal matter and breaking it off because you don't like them.

Most of the trouble among God's people is somebody doesn't like being told what to do. Most of the trouble among God's people is just old, carnal flesh. It's not doctrinal matters. The trouble is, some guy wants recognition and isn't getting it, or some guy wants to be thanked for doing something, and he isn't thanked for it.

I pastored one church for twelve years. I know Christians. I pastored Brent longer than any man that had ever been there, including the guy that set up the church. I know Christians. Some Christians, if they don't get the recognition they have coming to them, will just leave, and they will take two or three families with them and say the pastor split the church. We had a bunch of fine Christians in our church who took me and part of their own church to court; suing me and a couple of my deacons. The deacon board [sued ]{.class_s36M}the deacon board! They took the others to court before unbelievers over a bond program. The next Sunday seventeen families left in one shot. But those fellows divided that church because they couldn't control the money. We caught them with a bank account (about which nobody knew), with a couple thousand dollars in it. When the people found out about it, there was trouble, and so the fellows came to me and told me I would have to leave. I told them I was staying. So they left and accused me of splitting the church. The dirty rascals tried to split it, and when they couldn't split it, they left.

That bunch that left went out and started Burgess Road Baptist Church; and they had four pastors in eight years. After the third pastor, half their people left and went over to Smyrna. Then they split and went down to Jordan Street. If somebody came to me and said, "Now, Brother Ruckman, Bobby Tidwell split Burgess Road Baptist Church and went down to Jordan," I wouldn't believe him for five minutes. If somebody came to me and said, "Almond Terry split Burgess Road and left and went over to Smyrna," I wouldn't believe him for five minutes. I know that bunch that went over there. I know them. Let me tell you something, as long as they could run a church everything was just fine, but if they [couldn't]{.class_s36M} run a church, they would just raise hell and tear things to pieces. They were all saved people: premillennial, fundamental, soulwinning, Bible-believing jack rabbits. It all begins underground and works out.

Young pastors often find out when they begin pastoring a church that someone is always accusing them of splitting the church. What is meant is, "Preacher, be a good little boy and behave yourself. Just think, if you stay, there'll be division, but if you leave, we'll all get along." That is a lie!

When an issue comes up in the church there are three kinds of people in a church. A third of the congregation thinks the preacher is [right]{.class_s36M}, no matter what. They think he has been called, and he should be there. If he makes a mistake, that is between him and God, and he is God's servant. Just let it go, and God will take care of it. They will back him up. Then one group of the people think he is wrong, no matter what. They think he is too old, and the church needs a younger man. Or he is too young and doesn't have enough experience. Or his wife is meddling in the church affairs, or she never helps her husband. I mean, a third of the people think no matter [what you do ]{.class_s36M}you are wrong. And one third of the people don't care whether you are wrong or right!

So when a church splits it always splits three ways. If a preacher leaves a church, a third will go with him, and a third will stay, and a third will head for the bushes. If he stays, a third will stay with him, and a third will leave and join some other outfit, and a third will go nowhere. That is why down south, in a place like Pensacola, when you go out to do personal work, you will find Christians floating around that have not been in a church for twenty or thirty years and will never be in another one again. All they did was get saved and then get in a business meeting with a bunch of Baptists hitting each other in the face with Bibles, you know, ["in the name of the Lord,"]{.class_s36N} and blacking each others' eyes and threatening to slash the preacher's tires and burn down the pastorium. Those guys are so sick and tired of that stuff among Christians that they just swear by God they will never go in another church again, and they[ don't.]{.class_s36M} It is a shame, but that is the way it is. You go out in the country, and you see a sign out there that says, "Friendship Baptist Church." You go down about four blocks, and you see "New Friendship Baptist Church." There is nothing friendly about either of those churches.

I preached in a church where a young man felt he was called to preach in one of my meetings. He went right next door to Brent Baptist and started a church there within fifty yards of our church. He claimed he was "led of the Lord," of course.

That was "Grace" Baptist Church, right next to Brent Baptist Church. They finally moved over to Davis Highway.

Then we had another fellow come along and take our split to set up a church two miles from Brent. When he got that church set up, he had several "Christian celebrities" come in; one was Lee Roberson. (Strange how they love to come to this little, out-of-the-way place.) Roberson got up in that pulpit and began to carry on, "God bless you, brother! We thank God for this young man who has had this great burden to reach this lost community!" Who were they trying to kid, man? There was a Baptist church right around the corner, about a quarter of a mile. There was another one two miles down and still another three blocks down.

Did you know there is a lot of "baloney" in Christian work? There is a lot of promotional and motivational "jazz" that doesn't amount to anything. How are you going to get folks like that together?

["No divisions among you\...."]{.class_s36N} Why, if you put John R. Rice, Bob Jones Sr., M. R. DeHaan, Theodore Epp, Charlie Fuller, Maze Jackson, Harold Sightler, Billy Graham, and Oral Roberts in one room and shut the door, there would be blood coming out from under the door in fifteen minutes.

I told someone that one time, and he said, "Oh, no! They are all Christian men." I told him, "Boy, you don't know 'em like I know 'em." God's people, who have convictions, are hard to get along with. I have been in church splits where the church doors were locked and the police were waiting outside. One pastor had the sheriff waiting for him in the parking lot. That pastor could not even get into this own church. A lady was asked one time what position she took in the choir. She replied, "Neither, I'm neutral. I don't side with either faction."

I was invited to preach in a church in Georgia one time that was having a big "blow-up." The opposing faction sat in the back, and the bunch that sided with pastor sat up front. During that meeting, the ones that sat in the back got to "raising Cain." Some big old boy (about six feet, two) up in the front---who had just been saved about two weeks---got up, and pulled out a jackknife with a 7-inch blade on it. Turning around, he yelled to the crowd in the back, "I want you folks in the back to sit down and shut up and let me [hear this sermon]{.class_s36M}!" And they sat down, brother!

Verse 13, ["Is Christ divided?"]{.class_s36N} No. ["Was Paul crucified for you?"]{.class_s36N} No. ["Or were ye baptized in the name of Paul?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}No.

Verse 14: ["I thank God that I baptized none of you, but Crispus and Gaius."]{.class_s36N} Why? Because it's not for this dispensation? No. Why? Because there is only one baptism? No. Why? Because now that I know there is only one baptism I repent for baptizing any converts? No. ["I thank God that I baptized none of you\...Lest any should say that I had baptized in mine own name"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15). Now, you want to get that. Back to verse fourteen, ["I thank God that I baptized none of you\...."]{.class_s36N} Not because it is wrong or even because he had a different commission, but verse fifteen, ["Lest any should say that I had baptized in mine own name."]{.class_s36N}

["And I baptized\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16). Then he [did ]{.class_s36M}baptize some of them. So to say, "Well, Christ didn't send Paul to baptize," doesn't even deal with the context. He [did ]{.class_s36M}baptize, whether he was sent to baptize or not. So when you deal with the dry cleaning brethren---E. C. Moore, Baker, Bullinger, Stam, and Bill Sharpe---they will tell you, "Well, the Lord didn't send Paul to baptize," quoting verse seventeen. [I wonder why they never read to you verse 16.]{.class_s36M} Now, God didn't send [me ]{.class_s36M}to baptize. Did you know that? I can say just what Paul said. I can go in any church in this country and say, "The Lord didn't send me to baptize," but I've baptized a few folks.

["And I baptized also the household of Steph­anas: besides, I know not whether I baptized any other"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16).[ ]{.class_s36M}He lost track of the names. He didn't know how many he had baptized and how many he hadn't.

["For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 17). The dry cleaner reads it this way: "Christ didn't send me under the commission of Matthew 28, but the commission of 2 Corinthians 5." But of course, that is not what the verse said. The verse said, ["For Christ sent me not to baptize\...."]{.class_s36N} [Then why did he baptize if the Lord didn't send him to baptize?]{.class_s36M} In verse 16, he [did ]{.class_s36M}baptize them. All right, if you take verse 16 with the fact that he [did ]{.class_s36M}baptize them, but the Lord sent him not to baptize, dearly beloved, the only [proper interpretation]{.class_s36M} you can get is the Lord didn't send him [primarily ]{.class_s36M}to baptize, but to [preach]{.class_s36M}. But he [did ]{.class_s36M}baptize a few folks while he preached. You can't get any other sense out of it than that. Any other sense is nonsense. ["For Christ sent me not to baptize\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's true. The Lord sent him to preach. That's true. But that didn't prevent him from baptizing people.

["For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel\...."]{.class_s36N} What the Lord sends you to do doesn't mean that you can't do [other things.]{.class_s36M} ["Christ sent me\...to preach the gospel."]{.class_s36N} Do you think ["preach the gospel"]{.class_s36N} is all Paul did? Was ["preach the gospel"]{.class_s36N} all Paul did in Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, and Colossians? Paul didn't just ["preach the gospel."]{.class_s36N} Over half the Pauline writings are not talking about the gospel at all.[ They deal with Christian conduct.]{.class_s36M} So when it says the Lord sent him ["to preach the gospel,"]{.class_s36N} that doesn't limit his ministry to preaching the gospel and [excluding baptism. ]{.class_s36M}These people are crazy. They can't read.

["For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The gospel Paul preached had two elements in it that are found in the preaching of the other apostles and the Jews. Turn to Acts 20. Let's see what Pauline preaching looks like. It is not "dry cleaning" preaching. Paul isn't standing up there wasting all this hot air about the "body mystery" and "the mystery of the one body" and when did the body start and all that jazz. That's a scummy type of ministry, when you have got a whole New Testament to preach.

With Paul's preaching, here is what is going on. The hyper-dispensationalist thinks the contents of Paul's preaching is found in 2 Corinthians 5:18--20, where he mentions ["the ministry of reconciliation,"]{.class_s36N} and Ephesians 3:3--7, where the mystery of the Body is mentioned. So the hyper-dispensationalist thinks that Paul's preaching is limited to [two things]{.class_s36M} God showed him: the mystery of the Body of Christ and the ministry of reconciliation; therefore, Paul's ministry was simply talking about [those ]{.class_s36M}two things. Now, if you talk about those two things, do you know what you can overlook? The Tribulation, the Rapture, the Judgment Seat of Christ, the Second Coming of Christ, and prophecy. Above all, you won't ever have to preach against anybody's sins. It is a beautiful ministry, because you simply preach reconciliation and then the "one body," "one body mystery." Never mind the baptism controversy; don't baptize them. Never mind the sins; you're reconciled. You get through there just as high and clean and dry and just smell like a rose when you come out. If you think that is what Paul's ministry was, you know [nothing ]{.class_s36M}about Paul's ministry.

Let me show you Paul's ministry. This is Paul's testimony about what kind of ministry he had. Acts 20:21, ["Testifying both to the Jews, and also to the Greeks, repentance toward God\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He preached [the repentance of John the Baptist.]{.class_s36M} This dispensation may not begin with John the Baptist, but John the Baptist preached something that ought to be preached in this dispensation. Acts 26:20, ["But shewed first unto them of Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and throughout all the coasts of Judaea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, and do works meet for repentance." "Do works meet for repentance."]{.class_s36N} Did you get that? All right, that is what [John the Baptist]{.class_s36M} told them in Matthew 3 before the crucifixion. So when Paul preached, he said, "Repent, and if you are going to repent, let's see something." How is that for a grace dispensation? Old Paul had a little Methodist in him!

Paul's ministry is certainly not limited to hot air sessions in homes about the "mystery body" and the "body mystery" and all that stuff. He was a preacher. Acts 20:21,[ "Testifying both to the Jews, and also to the Greeks, repentance toward God, and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Verse 25, ["And now, behold, I know that ye all, among whom I have gone preaching the kingdom of God\...."]{.class_s36N} So Paul preached repentance, faith, and ["the kingdom of God."]{.class_s36N} That isn't all of it. Look at Acts 20:27, ["For I have not shunned to declare unto you]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}[all the counsel of God."]{.class_s36N} Paul doesn't limit his preaching to ["the ministry of reconciliation"]{.class_s36N} and the gospel. He declares ["all the counsel of God,"]{.class_s36N} everything God gave him. Everything God gave him is in Romans through Philemon.

A good preacher (that is a Bible preacher) will preach all the Bible at the places where it is [needed]{.class_s36M}, at the [situations ]{.class_s36M}where it is called for, praying for God to give him the right verse for the right [occasion ]{.class_s36M}in the [circumstances ]{.class_s36M}which he finds himself. He will never limit his ministry to ["the ministry of reconciliation"]{.class_s36N} or the mystery of the one body, body mystery, dry cleaning, one body mystery. [That is the work of a deluded heretic.]{.class_s36M} There are many people that talk about following Paul that do not follow Paul at all. They are not the least bit "Pauline" in their approach. Paul was a street preacher. I don't recall Cornelius Stam and these boys ever sticking their noses out of a radio station.

["For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel: not with wisdom of words\...."]{.class_s36N} This is what we call "wordy preaching." I have a list that wordy preachers use when they preach with words of man's wisdom. For example: "Scrutinize the three-fold apocalyptic symbology in the figurative concept of Daniel's prophetic foreview." That is just the way not to preach, ["with wisdom of words."]{.class_s36N}

["Lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect."]{.class_s36N} I want you to notice two places in your Bible on how to mess up the truth. The first one is\
1 Corinthians 1:17. You can make ["the cross of Christ\...of none effect."]{.class_s36N} And the other is to make ["the word of God of none effect."]{.class_s36N} ["Making the word of God of none effect"]{.class_s36N} is found in Mark 7:13. In Mark 7:13, a preacher can make ["the word of God of none effect"]{.class_s36N} and in 1 Corinthians 1:17, a preacher can make ["the cross of Christ\...of none effect."]{.class_s36N} That is something serious about which to think if God has called you to preach.

In Mark 7:13, you can make[ "the word of God of none effect"]{.class_s36N} by [tradition]{.class_s36M}. If you follow a scholastic tradition, you will make ["the word of God of none effect"]{.class_s36N}; it will have no effect in your congregation. If you preach with words of man's wisdom, you will make the cross of no effect, because your congregation will see that you are not living a crucified life and that you are in fellowship with the world. When you get up there and butter and apple polish and smooth and soothe and talk about the "total thrust" and the "dynamic concept" and the "areas of integration," "ethnological communications of the dialogue," and all that kind of business, they know you are not living a crucified life. A man who is crucified is out of fellowship with the world.

1:18 For the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness; but unto us which are saved it is the power of God.

19 For it is written, I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent.

20 Where is the wise? where is the scribe? where is the disputer of this world? hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world?

21 For after that in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God, it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe.

22 For the Jews require a sign, and the Greeks seek after wisdom:

23 But we preach Christ crucified, unto the Jews a stumblingblock, and unto the Greeks foolishness;

24 But unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God, and the wisdom of God.

25 Because the foolishness of God is wiser than men; and the weakness of God is stronger than men.

["For the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness; but unto us which are saved it is the power of God" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 18). ["The power of God"]{.class_s36N} is not merely the Holy Spirit. ["The power of God"]{.class_s36N} is ["the preaching of the cross."]{.class_s36N} When you stand up and preach Christ crucified and preach the crucified life, that is ["the power of God."]{.class_s36N} The Greek word is your word for "dynamite." One may say "It is the T.N.T. of God: note that acronym for an explosive stands for [T]{.class_s36V}he [N]{.class_s36V}ew [T]{.class_s36V}estament.

["For it is written," ]{.class_s36N}\[in Isa. 29:14\][ "I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 19).[ ]{.class_s36M}For Christ said, ["I thank thee, O Father\....because thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent\..."]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 11:25). This is a great verse, and every Christian should memorize it.

Verse 20, ["Where is the wise?"]{.class_s36N} Challenge them. ["Where is the scribe?"]{.class_s36N} That's the reviser, the writer. ["Where is the disputer of this world?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's the debater, the arguer, the Greek philosopher. ["Hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world?"]{.class_s36N} The crucifixion messes everything up. ["Hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world?"]{.class_s36N} Why did God do this? Why did God take ["the wisdom of this world"]{.class_s36N} and turn it into foolishness? Isn't there enough foolishness.

I read four books once on ancient civilizations and UFOs. I got hold of one book that insists UFOs are under water; very interesting book. That book was about UFOs going into the water and coming out of the water. A lot of good information was in there. But then I got another book on the wisdom of the ancients with all this stuff about the pyramids, Easter Island, etc. It was about 300 pages about how wise these people were and all they knew that we don't know and all these secret things, like raising a stick in the air to get rid of a blister, and this and that. When I got through with that book, do you know what feeling I had, as a Bible believer? I thought to myself, "Well with all they knew and all they found out, what did they do with it? They all died. They all died and were buried." I don't care what they knew. It didn't do them any good. They all died and were buried. What's the big deal?

["Hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world?"]{.class_s36N} The Bible says, ["The Lord knoweth the thoughts of the wise, that they are vain" ]{.class_s36N}(1 Cor. 3:20). Psalm 62:9 says, ["Surely men of low degree are vanity, and men of high degree are a lie: to be laid in the balance, they are altogether lighter than vanity."]{.class_s36N} What a thing, man. ["Made foolish the wisdom of this world?"]{.class_s36N}

["For after that in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God\..." ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 21).[ ]{.class_s36S}Do you know how the world got around knowing God? [Through education]{.class_s36S}. By [wisdom]{.class_s36S}. People learn to get around God and not know Him. You have to study that. ["The world by wisdom knew not God\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}Those old Greek philosophers [studied ]{.class_s36S}how to avoid the truth; they [worked at it.]{.class_s36S} The Bible calls it "willful ignorance." In Romans 1:22­, 24 says, ["Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools\...Wherefore God also gave them up\...."]{.class_s36N}

["For after that in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God, it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Not "foolish preaching: but the ["foolishness of preaching."]{.class_s36N} All the new revisions are by conceited asses who take their own preaching seriously, so all of them ([RV, RSV, NRSV, ASV, NASV, NKJV, NIV]{.class_s36M}) have altered the verse so [their]{.class_s36M} preaching cannot be called "foolishness."

Analyze that for a while. What could be [dumber ]{.class_s36M}than you coming in here and sitting here in this building and listening to me shoot my mouth off? Did you ever just analyze that thing? You never analyzed it. All you know is you're getting a blessing. You don't care. I mean, some of you are just as happy as a [dead pig]{.class_s36M} in the sun, but you don't know [why]{.class_s36M}.

I was talking about going down to the Southwide Fellowship at Panama City with Roloff and Rice, and my sons said. "Well, why do you want to go down there?" I said, "I enjoy it." They said, "Well, what if there was a big ball game or hockey game right next door?" I said, "I would go to the fellowship." They said, "What if the Super Bowl was just a mile down the road?" I said, "I would go to the fellowship." They don't understand that. There is no way to explain it. You can't explain it. [It's foolish.]{.class_s36M}

The Bible says, ["a fool's voice is known by multitude of words"]{.class_s36N} (Ecc. 5:3). ["In the multitude of words there wanteth not sin\..."]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 10:19). Paul said, ["We are fools for Christ's sake" ]{.class_s36N}(1 Cor. 4:10).

["It pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Nothing could be more dumb than a guy just standing up and going "bla, bla, bla, bla," but it pleased God to do it that way. When you fellows get up and preach the gospel and tell a man how to get saved, I don't care how foolish it sounds or how foolish it looks,[ it pleases God to see you do that.]{.class_s36M} It blesses Him. It is a blessing to [Him.]{.class_s36M}

What could be more foolish than me standing up here and telling you, "Do you want to get right and get invisible and be perfect and be superman? [OK, trust a dead Jew."]{.class_s36M} Now, how's that? Did you ever think about that? What could be any dumber than that? A guy gets up, "Keep the Golden Rule." Yeah, that's a great rule. That's a profound thing, you know. "Take the sacraments. Keep the Ten Commandments." Yeah, that's a great standard. That's a big deal, see. I say, "Trust a dead Jew." They don't like that. Don't you know when the Lord pulled that off He pulled off the biggest joke that has ever been pulled. He sent His Son down here and had His Son born of a virgin and die on a piece of wood? That did it, man. All these smart guys---it just drove them bananas.[ They can't figure it out.]{.class_s36M}

Einstein had an IQ of 170. He was so brilliant that he was half out of his mind. Right before Einstein died, a Lutheran minister came in and opened the Bible and witnessed to him. Einstein told him to put the Bible up. That Lutheran minister said, "I believe in mathematics, and I believe in God." Einstein said, "I believe in mathematics." The Lutheran minister said, "I believe mathematics and God will match each other. I don't believe there is anything false with God. God can match any mathematical formula. I think God set up the law of mathematics." Einstein said, "What if someday we prove by mathematical formula that there is no God?" The Lutheran minister said, "My dear Mr. Einstein, I have so much respect for your profession that I would not accept your conclusion of that mathematical formula until you checked it over and over again, because I would be absolutely sure you would be wrong." Then he said to Einstein, "Would you let me read to you about Jesus Christ?" Einstein said, "I cannot believe in a God who is not a mathematical formula," and then he died. He is in hell right now. He had more brains than any three people put together, and he was a [fool]{.class_s36M}. I know farm people out there in the country that never finished [the fifth grade]{.class_s36M} that will turn out with all the brains, and Einstein will turn out as a demon.

["For the Jews require a sign\..." ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 22). They should; their nation began with signs. The first time the word ["sign" ]{.class_s36N}occurs in the Bible is Exodus 4:8. Moses sticks his hand in his bosom and pulls it out leprous, and then it comes back out clean. That's a [sign.]{.class_s36S} Moses goes down and shows those signs to the Jews at the end of that chapter. When they come out of Egypt, their nation begins with [signs.]{.class_s36S} So the Jews seek for a sign, ["and the Greeks seek after wisdom."]{.class_s36N} That's the educated folks. That's why it is Alpha Beta Gamma and Phi Beta Kappa. ["The Greeks seek after wisdom."]{.class_s36N} That is why they name smart people after Greek letters. By reading your Bible, you know there are two different kinds of wisdom. For a Greek, any kind of knowledge would be wisdom, and any kind of wisdom would be knowledge, which, of course, is wrong. The Bible reader knows that there are [two different kinds of wisdom,]{.class_s36S} but a Greek wouldn't know that.[ "The Greeks seek after wisdom."]{.class_s36N}

The Greek "ideal" was wisdom. Freedom and beauty (i.e., carnal wisdom, freedom to sin, and the beauty of the naked body).

["But we preach Christ crucified, unto the Jews a stumblingblock, and unto the Greeks foolishness;" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 23).[ ]{.class_s36M}That is, some preachers may have other things to promote, but we are busy promoting Jesus Christ.

Let's take an example, Acts 17. Here is Paul preaching ["Christ crucified"]{.class_s36N} to a bunch of Greeks, and look how they respond. In Acts 17:32, he is preaching to the Athenian philosophers, who, according to the text, are idolatrous, superstitious, fickle, naive, double-minded, and skeptical. Acts 17:32, ["And when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked: and others said, We will hear thee again of this matter."]{.class_s36N} They made fun of him. To the Greeks, the preaching of the resurrection of Christ was [foolishness]{.class_s36M}.

["But unto them which are called\..." ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 24). God has called those who have received Christ to adoption, to sanctification, and to be conformed to the image of Jesus Chist. ["Unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks ]{.class_s36N}\[the saved\][, Christ the power of God, and the wisdom of God."]{.class_s36N} All right, Christ is ["the power of God"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 1:24), the Bible is ["the power of God"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 22:29), the preaching is[ "the power of God"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 1:18), the gospel is ["the power of God"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 1:16), and the Holy Spirit is ["the power of God"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 15:19).

["Because the foolishness of God is wiser than men; and the weakness of God is stronger than men" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 25).[ ]{.class_s36S}Christ was stronger as a baby in Mary's lap than Herod was on the [throne]{.class_s36S}. ["The weakness of God is stronger than men."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}Christ was stronger nailed to a cross than Judas was running up and down Jerusalem.

1:26 For ye see your calling, brethren, how that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called:

27 But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise; and God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty;

28 And base things of the world, and things which are despised, hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not, to bring to nought things that are:

29 That no flesh should glory in his presence.

30 But of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption:

31 That, according as it is written, He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord.

["For ye see your calling, brethren\..." ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 26).[ ]{.class_s36M}They're already saved. All right, if you are saved, you are called to do something[. "For ye see your calling, brethren\...."]{.class_s36N} Now that you are saved you are a [brother.]{.class_s36M} ["How that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}After you get saved and look around at the ["brethren in Christ,"]{.class_s36M} do you know what you find? You find there are ["not many wise men after the flesh,"]{.class_s36N} not many Ph.D.s; ["not many mighty,"]{.class_s36N} not many like Larry Csonka or Paul Anderson; ["not many noble,"]{.class_s36N} not many kings, not many presidents, not many governors, not many princes; a few, not many. I thank God for that "m," brother. Boy, if it said not[ ]{.class_s36M}"any," some of us would be in a hole. As it stands right there it is saying that after you are saved and look around, you will see what you have been called into and find that not many of the big shots got there.

["But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 27). Are you foolish? You are chosen[ "to confound the wise."]{.class_s36N} ["And God hath chosen the weak things of the world\...."]{.class_s36N} Are you weak? You are chosen ["to confound the things which are mighty."]{.class_s36N} ["God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty."]{.class_s36N}

["And base things\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 28). That's where I get in. That is my life verse. ["And things which are despised, hath God chosen\...."]{.class_s36N} What is my job? My job is ["to bring to nought things that are."]{.class_s36N} ["Hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not, to bring to nought things that are."]{.class_s36N} That's what it says. I don't particularly appreciate my ministry. I really don't. If I had my way, I'd like to be a big time evangelist and run up and down the country and preach to a town and get people saved. I would like to be a Billy Graham or Jack Van Impe instead of a poor man's Oral Roberts. I don't particularly appreciate my ministry. I don't particularly appreciate God calling me to spend all my time hacking away at these Christian educators and slamming these revisers and going after these fellows and calling them "asses" and "blockheads" and "dumbheads" and "meatbrains" and all that. I never particularly enjoy doing that. I would rather do something else. But you will have to do what the Lord calls you to do, and you will have to do that for which the Lord has equipped you. That is what the Lord equipped [me ]{.class_s36M}for, and that is what I'm going to do. That's what I am supposed to do. A fellow said "Billy Sunday was God's joke on the ministry and Pete Ruckman was God's joke on Christian education." I appreciate that. If that's what I can do, I'll do it.

You do what [you ]{.class_s36M}can do and what [you ]{.class_s36M}are equipped to do. Don't follow my example all the way unless the Lord wants you to. If he does, OK; if he doesn't, go some other way. Do what God wants you to do. In my background is all the equipment for what I am supposed to do. It is weird equipment. I mean, the Lord had to get somebody down low enough on the ground who would talk like common folks. So he put me in the infantry for four years. Then God had to get somebody that could handle the Hebrew and Greek. So I had to go to college for ten years. The Lord had to get somebody that would say it plain and blunt and clear. So he had to give me a background where I wasn't [raised]{.class_s36M}; I was "drug up." And all of that is equipment for what God intended me to do. In your life the same thing will be true. There will be some things in your life that won't be clear. But you keep on doing what God wants you to do the way God wants you to do it, and you will find everything in your background will begin to come in and work out for the particular job that God wants you to do. And it will be the right equipment and the right training. I have never known God to waste anything, any human life. [We ]{.class_s36M}waste things, but the Lord [doesn't.]{.class_s36M}

["That no flesh should glory in his presence" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 29).[ ]{.class_s36M}No man is going to stand up there and say, "I had the heavyweight crown in 1968 and 1969." "I won my combat commission. I was a four star general. I took charge of the field of operations." "I graduated from four universities, and I hold a doctor's degree, and I have access to the original\...." It is not going to be that way. We're going to get up there, and the Lord is going ["to confound the wise\...That no flesh should glory in his presence."]{.class_s36N} Brother, when you come to the Judgment Seat of Christ, come in on your hands and knees and don't look up until you are told to look up. There will be a lot of [surprises]{.class_s36M}.

Wouldn't it be horrible to get there and find that the only way some of you got your bills paid and got through school was that some dear saint was praying for you down there in the county home? Wouldn't that be a jolt, man? About the time you got to the Judgment Seat of Christ, you say, "Well, I prayed"; and the Lord says, "Yeah, but I couldn't answer [your ]{.class_s36M}prayers. At that particular time, the selfishness blocked it, or the unforgiving spirit blocked it, and I couldn't answer the prayer. I looked all over the country to find somebody to pray for you, and an old woman down there in the [county rest home]{.class_s36M} one night started praying for you, so I just decided I would do it for[ her]{.class_s36M}." A lot of shocking things, man!

Folks talk about "suffering for Christ" and going through all kinds of things. I tell people I haven't suffered anything. People say, "Oh, he's just low rating himself." No, no. I know the truth. I have been around where they suffer. I know what the [suffering ]{.class_s36M}is. I haven't had "the treatment." I know some who have had the treatment. When you get up there to the Judgment Seat of Christ and hear some of these guys come tearing through there, "I ran 2,000 in Sunday School; last year we had 1,200 baptisms"; I'll tell you what they had better do, man. They had better keep their big mouths shut. About that time, old Richard Wurmbrand will walk in there and take off his shirt, and it will get as quiet as a turkey farm on Thanksgiving afternoon. While they were bussing their kiddies in, he was three years in solitary confinement. Better come in quiet, man.

I know a pastor in Mobile, Alabama that had to have an operation done on his head; they had to drill those holes through his skull and put in the screws. When they started to give him anesthesia, he said he didn't want it. They wanted to know why not. He said, "This one is on me. I always did wonder if a Christian could take pain. I always wondered if the test ever came if I could stand it. I want to find out right now whether I can stand it or not." [And he went through it.]{.class_s36M} Well, he'll be at the Judgment Seat. When you come in, you'd better just come in and tiptoe over in the corner somewhere and just hope it won't be too brutal when it comes. I know a fellow (Bob Persons in Greenville), who just got on his one leg and hobbled out in the yard and rolled his wheelchair up in the back end of a truck and piled his valise and stuff in the back end of the truck and got in that truck and drove to Fort Leavenworth, Kansas, and preached to the prisoners. He's not running 1,000 in Sunday School. He's hobbling around on one leg and getting the gospel out. Some of you have got[ two legs]{.class_s36M} and aren't getting the gospel out yet. Come in quietly.

["But of him (]{.class_s36N}God[) are ye in Christ Jesus\...."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 30). Technically, there is the calling. Technically, you are not called until you receive Christ. We can talk about the gospel calling, but everybody gets that call. He says, ["many are called, but few are chosen."]{.class_s36N} Anybody is called to receive Christ. The Bible says when the Holy Spirit comes, he will convict of sin, righteousness, and judgment. Christ said, ["If I be lifted up\...]{.class_s36N}\[I\] [will draw all men unto me."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}But when you receive Christ something happens to you that didn't happen to the Old Testament saint. When you received Christ you are called into [adoption]{.class_s36M}, you are called to be [conformed to the image of His Son]{.class_s36M}, you are called "His son," you are called a member of the family, and you are called with a holy calling not according to your works. All those things take place when you receive Christ. Notice, ["But of him are ye in Christ\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}There's your position.

["But of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 30).[ ]{.class_s36M}If Christ had to be made for us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption, do you know what that means? That means we must have been [stupid]{.class_s36M}, or we wouldn't have needed the wisdom. It must mean we were [wicked]{.class_s36M}, or we wouldn't need the righteousness. It must mean we were [unclean]{.class_s36M}, or we wouldn't need the sanctification. It must mean we were[ lost and doomed,]{.class_s36M} or we wouldn't have needed the redemption. So in the Bible, the picture of us Christian people is that we are stupid, unrighteous, unclean, and lost, and we couldn't take care of any of it. So Jesus Christ becomes all of it for us. And when you take Jesus Christ you get the ["wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption."]{.class_s36N}

["That, according as it is written, He that glorieth\..."]{.class_s36N} (vs. 31). Are you going to glory about something? ["Let him glory in the Lord."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Brag about Jesus. When you get up to heaven just start yelling about Jesus Christ. Just run up and down the corridors of glory yelling, "Glory to God, hallelujah, praise the Lord," and get the attention off yourself. ["He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord."]{.class_s36N} Years ago, a preacher, who later became a famous preacher, had his boot camp at the Pacific Garden Rescue Mission. Harry Saulnier was head of the mission at that time. The first night the preacher got ready to preach, he was white in the face and nervous and all tore up and hardly able to preach. He had four or five messages ready and didn't know which one to preach. He came up to Harry and said, "What am I going to do when I get up there?" Harry said, "Brother, just rave about Jesus. Just rave about Jesus."

I have a friend in Holland, Michigan named Joe Boss, a German fellow who got his citizenship in America fighting for the American Army in Korea. When Joe Boss came into Holland, Michigan, he went to the Reformed Church and left it. He went to the Dutch Reformed Church and left it. He went to the Christian Reformed Church and left it. He went to a Methodist church and left it. Finally he came down to the Baptist church where Garland Cofield was pastor. Garland Cofield said, "What's the trouble?" Bose said, "You know something, pastor; I have been all over this town. I wish I could just get in a good, old Jesus meeting." He wanted to get in a "good, old Jesus meeting." So the thing to do is rave about the Lord. If you want to glory about something, you glory about Jesus Christ.

In 1 Corinthians 1 we find:

The call of Grac[e]{.class_s36Y}             4

The call to Learnin[g             ]{.class_s36Y}5

The call to Waiting[​]{.class_s373}6--7

The call to Holines[s             ]{.class_s36Y}8

The call to Fellowship              9

The call to Perfection              9

[]{#c7E.xhtml}

## Chapter 2 {.heading_s39}

2:1 And I, brethren, when I came to you, came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom, declaring unto you the testimony of God.

2 For I determined not to know any thing among you, save Jesus Christ, and him crucified.

3 And I was with you in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling.

4 And my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power:

5 That your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men, but in the power of God.

6 Howbeit we speak wisdom among them that are perfect: yet not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world, that come to nought:

7 But we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, even the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world unto our glory:

8 Which none of the princes of this world knew: for had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.

["And I, brethren, when I came to you, came not with excellency of speech"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1).[ ]{.class_s36S}The Bible says his speech was contemptible (2 Cor. 10:10). Paul said ["though I be rude in speech, yet not in knowledge" ]{.class_s36N}(2 Cor. 11:6).

["When I came to you, came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom, declaring unto you the testimony of God" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 1).[ ]{.class_s36M}Compare this with verse four: ["And my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The ["enticing words of man's wisdom"]{.class_s36N} and ["excellency of speech"]{.class_s36N} are how you seduce people or "politic people" or draw people to support you, or make them think you are a wonderful fellow or that your work is wonderful by attracting their attention to yourself instead of the Lord and the Book. It goes on all the time.

Somebody gave me a tract the other night by Oral Roberts called "Get Away From Me Devil; I'm God's Property." When you read through that tract, you get two impressions. First of all, you get the impression that the devil couldn't touch [anybody]{.class_s36M}. Then you get the impression that everybody is saved and everybody [belongs to God.]{.class_s36M} Neither one of those things are so. But it was worded beautifully. It was wisdom of speech and[ "excellency of speech."]{.class_s36N} We are to speak with ["sound speech, that cannot be condemned"]{.class_s36N} (Titus 2:8).

The Bible says to look out for those that ["by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple" ]{.class_s36N}(Rom. 16:18).[ ]{.class_s36M}There is a vocabulary. The youth director at Trinity Baptist Church\* was telling me that he went with his wife to hear a Bill Gothard lecture. He said they heard all kinds of things about Gothard and wanted to check for themselves firsthand. He said, "Brother Ruckman, that was the most nauseating, disgusting thing I ever heard in my life." He said he went to two sessions and quit. He said, "I got in there and found that in every one of those sessions (where there were three or four thousand people), there were hundreds of students from [Bob Jones University]{.class_s36M} taking notes and enjoying it and bragging about what a wonderful Bible study it was." There they were with their short hair and double standards and ultra separation, sitting there with the hippies and yippies and the guitars and the bikinis and the slacks and the hair down to the shoulders with all the rest of the crowd in there.

When Gothard teaches (I have a notebook full of his stuff), he runs through [seventeen lessons]{.class_s36M} before he lets anybody know that a person that is lost [needs to be saved.]{.class_s36M} Then he charges \$75.00 for the lecture. That fellow is probably making well over \$100,000.00 per year lying to unsaved people. But it is beautiful. It is all done just as smooth and slick as butter so you can never tell the difference. [There is nothing to offend an unsaved person for seventeen lessons.]{.class_s36M} By then they are adjusted to the climate, and [some ]{.class_s36M}of them get saved. So you are right back with Billy Graham and John R. Rice again: "The end justifies the means."

The way you spot these things is by vocabulary. I won't take the time to list all the words, but it's words like these: "dynamic area," "in-depth." That last one is the biggy these days: "in-depth." "An in-depth study of the structural relevance\...." All that stuff! I'll tell you, you look at those words, and every one of those words comes from philosophy or psychology or physics. "The thrust of this narrative\...." "The thrust"? [What's he talking about? He's talking about the thrust of the rocket down there at Canaveral.]{.class_s36M} And, "We need to program our ideas." He's talking about a [computer.]{.class_s36M} And, "In this area\...." He's talking about psychology. [All ]{.class_s36M}those words are borrowed from a college curriculum to make the man sound like he is smart so that you will think, "Oooh, isn't he smart?" That is all that stuff is for. There's a bunch of them like "the total" (that's a good one) and "validate." "It needs to be validated," and the "proper evaluation." That stuff is college curriculum for[ suckers.]{.class_s36M}

If you took the technical language from any other group of people, you could convince them you were educated when you weren't. If I wanted to talk to you just in the technical language I know, I could talk to you for an hour, and as God is my witness, there wouldn't be more than five people that would understand half of it. There would be about five people that would get half of it. The rest of you would get anywhere from one fourth to one tenth, and some of you would get nothing for fifty minutes. There is no point in doing that. What is the point in a medical doctor standing up here and letting you know what he learned in med school? It doesn't edify anybody.

Someday I would like to rewrite all the college textbooks, every one of them. When that guy says "Paleozoic," I'm going to say "ancient life." When he says "Mesozoic," I'm going to say "middle life." When he says "Cenozoic," I'm going to say "later life." See, you can just break the thing down to what it actually is. That's all it is.

Look at how these fellows preach about "youth tensions" and "woman fulfillment seminars." I saw it on a sign in Tulsa---Woman's Fulfillment Seminar. Ahhh, nuts. That stuff comes out of psychology books and those sex magazines on the rack. "Youth tensions" and "the dynamics of Christian ethics." All that junk. To me that is the most nauseating stuff on the face of this earth. I get rabid, man. Because all that gas is a haze and a smog thrown over you that will do one of two things. It will prevent you from finding the truth; it will lead you into a lie. Or it will make you think the person knows something when he doesn't have anything and isn't prepared and doesn't have enough brains to come in out of the rain on a dark night.

["For I determined not to know any thing among you, save Jesus Christ, and him crucified" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 2). Paul wanted to know one thing about the Corinthians: did they know Christ and did they know Christ crucified. There are two things that are implied there. First, he wanted to know if they knew Jesus Christ crucified for their sins. Secondly, he wanted to know if they knew anything about the crucified life: ["Jesus Christ, and him crucified"]{.class_s36N} in their midst, ["any thing among you."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He's talking not only about them knowing the gospel, but about the crucified life: being dead in Christ and buried with Christ and crucified with Christ. (That is brought out really strong in the book of Galatians.)

["And I was with you in weakness" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 3).[ ]{.class_s36M}At least physically. ["And in fear."]{.class_s36N} Well, Paul wasn't yellow; he wasn't "chicken." But he was afraid his message wouldn't take effect. He was afraid his preaching would be in vain; he was afraid they wouldn't get saved. He was afraid they would reject the message. [He was afraid God wouldn't use him.]{.class_s36M} That is the kind of fear here.

["In weakness, and in fear" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 3). Paul was not a coward. Paul faced death; he just walked right into it. Matter of fact, that is what he did on two occasions. One time he almost got stoned to death, and when he came to, he went right back into the city to get stoned again. On another occasion the Lord said if Paul went down to Jerusalem they were going to bind him and throw him in jail, and Paul went right down there and went into the jail. It didn't bother him a bit. But Paul's fear is something else. In Acts twenty look at his testimony in verse nineteen, ["Serving the Lord with all humility of mind, and with many tears, and temptations, which befell me by the lying in wait of the Jews."]{.class_s36N} The only thing of which he is afraid is that God won't use him or [that his ministry won't be effective]{.class_s36S} or [that his converts won't grow in grace.]{.class_s36S} ["And I was with you in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling."]{.class_s36N}

["And my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 4). Why?

["That your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). If you get converted to an anti-biblical vocabulary, what good does that do? You need to get converted to a Bible vocabulary. The words you need to get in your "craw" are words like saved, justified, heaven, hell, born again, Jesus Christ, adoption, justification, sanctification, sin, lost, damned. Those are the words. Those are the words that are going out of the American vocabulary and out of the pulpit. The pulpit is just talking around them and saying, "The total commitment\...." What is that? It isn't in the Book. Now, you know what the fellow [means]{.class_s36M}, but he keeps changing it until he doesn't have any [Bible ]{.class_s36M}left. "Let Christ come into your life." That's a goody. You might as well say, "Let the wind blow through the tunnel."

The trouble is all of these guys that are called to lead [singing ]{.class_s36M}that are trying to [preach]{.class_s36M}, like Jimmy Swaggart and Oral Roberts. They are putting out across the radio and TV a Christianity that is not a [Bible ]{.class_s36M}Christianity. So the terms aren't [Biblical]{.class_s36M}. The terms are song terms. Did you ever hear this one? "It is no secret what God can do. What he's done for others, He'll do for you. With arms wide open, He'll pardon you\...." Really? Did you ever analyze that thing? "With arms wide open\...." I know you Christians think that, but that's the trouble with Christians. They hear that stuff, and they keep thinking, "Well, that sounds all right," because they are applying it to [themselves]{.class_s36M}. What about ten million [unsaved people]{.class_s36M} who bought that record? "With arms wide open, He'll pardon you. It is no secret what God can do." [There isn't anything in that Bible that says God will pardon anybody outside of the blood atonement at Calvary---not a word, man!]{.class_s36M} "He'll come down from the skies and wipe the tears from your eyes\...." [He is not coming down from anywhere until the Second Advent.]{.class_s36M}

When I start talking like this, folks say, "Oh, well, you know what they [mean.]{.class_s36M}" Yeah, I know what they mean. [They're lying.]{.class_s36M} I'm talking about what the Bible says that is so. But that's [doctrine]{.class_s36M}. What these people want to do is get away from doctrine. They just want to talk about a bunch of nonsense. "Share your experience." Do you know what that is? That's communism. That is called communal property, [government sharing.]{.class_s36M} That is in the Communist Manifesto. "Share your experience." You can't [share ]{.class_s36M}your experience. You can tell somebody about your experience. But when you [share ]{.class_s36M}something, you split and give someone else part and you keep part, right? You see, you have got a Christianity where the words don't mean anything anymore, just like a bunch of mad men.

Several years ago, when I had more time, I used to set up my artwork at street exhibitions, downtown. I would set up some of my scenery paintings, and right in the middle I would put one of my pictures from Revelation, like two angels throwing a man into Hell. I wish you could have heard how people reacted to those pictures.

One kook came over wearing a slicker, plaid shorts, patent-leather shoes and silk stockings, a monocle, and a beret. He stared at one of those Revelation paintings, and he came back to me and asked, "Are you the artist?" I said, "Yes, I am." He said, "Thank you for [sharing]{.class_s36S} that." I didn't share it---I displayed it.

["Howbeit we speak wisdom among them that are perfect" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 6).[ ]{.class_s36M}Notice how the word ["perfect"]{.class_s36N} is used: complete, not sinless. If you want cross references, check Philippians, especially Philippians three, and study that word "perfect."

["Yet not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world, that come to nought: but we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, even the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world unto our glory:" ]{.class_s36N}(vss. 6--7).[ ]{.class_s36M}If you know the Bible and know Christ, you have access to wisdom that the ancients didn't have and the princes didn't have and the Ph.D.'s don't have. If you know the Lord and the word of the Lord, you have access to information that Socrates and Plato and the philosophers didn't have access, and you have access to wisdom that the Egyptians and the Sumerians and the Babylonians didn't have access. Psalm 119:99--100, ["I have more understanding than all my teachers."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Why? ["For thy testimonies are my meditation. I understand more than the ancients, because I keep thy precepts."]{.class_s36N}

If you know the word of God and [believe ]{.class_s36M}the word of God and love the Lord, the Lord will show you things that sometimes He won't show your teachers. That would go for me or any other teacher. These other teachers make a bad mistake in assuming the teacher is the only one who knows the Bible. The Lord can show you things, if you are searching for them in the Bible, that He hasn't shown [anybody]{.class_s36M}.

Now, you have to be careful that you don't come up with "clinkers." If you find something in there that contradicts some other verse of Scripture, then the Lord hasn't shown you anything. If you find something in there that you have to change around and twist and fit into what you want to say, He hasn't shown you anything. Pastor Robinson, who sailed on the Mayflower, said, "Luther and Calvin were precious shining lights in their days. But the Lutherans have not gotten beyond Luther, and the Calvinists have not gotten beyond Calvin. I am sure that God has much more light to throw on His precious word." Pastor Robinson was right. If a man is a Calvinist, [he is 300 years out of step with the word of God.]{.class_s36M} God has showed so much more since Calvin that you couldn't write it in fifteen books. So the thing to do is don't give up and quit. Keep on going and keep on searching and keep on looking and ask God to give you something.
