You take that word there, you may find water baptism connected with that in 1 Peter 3:21 and Mark 16:16, but you will find that a woman will be [saved ]{.class_s36M}in childbearing from being deceived, and you will also find Timothy could [save himself]{.class_s36M} and his congregation by "staying in the doctrine," and you will find the untoward generation who crucified Christ were told to [save themselves]{.class_s36M} from that generation. So the word ["saved,"]{.class_s36N} if you find water connected with it, is not too conclusive. But here are some words with which baptism is never connected:[ sanctification, salvation, redemption, eternal life. ]{.class_s36M}That's quite a load. You will find the word ["belief"]{.class_s36N} connected with [every one of those words.]{.class_s36M} Believe, believe, belief. You will also find the "blood" connected with them.

"[All things are lawful unto me, but all things are not expedient"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12). That's a great verse. You can do anything you want to do (["Unto the pure all things are pure,"]{.class_s36N} Titus 1:15), BUT (there's that horrible disjunctive conjunction) not everything[ helps out. ]{.class_s36M}That is a good rule for the Christian to learn and memorize. You're not under law. You are under grace. [You can do what you want to do. ]{.class_s36M}You have freedom. You have liberty. But there are two precautions. First, [expediency]{.class_s36M}; second, [the matter of control.]{.class_s36M}

["I will not be brought under the power of any."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Like cigarettes. Pretty soon that cigarette is telling you when to get up and when to go to bed and when to light up. [That's wrong. ]{.class_s36M}It isn't a matter of some direct verse against it that says, "Thou shalt not smoke Winston." The thing is, ["I will not be brought under the power of any."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If that thing [controls ]{.class_s36M}you, then it is wrong. If you are a man and you wife controls you, that is wrong. If you are a man, the only thing that should control you is God. Folks say, "Well, it's no worse than coffee." Then quit your coffee. If tea gets to the place in your life where tea has control over you, you have got to put the tea down. The same goes for cokes.

A lady said to me in Jacksonville, "It's horrible! Roloff took our aluminum pans and our meat, and you took our TV and our newspapers. Then Roloff took our mustard and our catsup and all that stuff, and you came along and took our new Bibles. Then Roloff took away our movies and football games, and you come along and took\.... We have nothing left. [You've taken everything we've got!"]{.class_s36M} I said, "Well, you've still got God and heaven. That beats going to hell, don't it."

Folks say, "What about this and what about that? "Well, if it gets [control ]{.class_s36M}over you, then it is wrong." That's all there is to that. If it isn't one thing it is something else. Some of these Southerners (bless their hearts), have to have fifteen cups of coffee to stay awake long enough to go to sleep. When it is something like that, there is something [wrong]{.class_s36M}, brother.

[​]{.class_s36U}Now, the expression is that ["all things are lawful"]{.class_s36N} for the Christian, but they[ "are not expedient"]{.class_s36N}; and ["all things are lawful,"]{.class_s36N} but you are not to be ["brought under the power of any." ]{.class_s36N}That's a good rule by which to go on doubtful things. TV is the same way. If you ever get to the place that you can't get along [without ]{.class_s36M}the TV, then it is time to junk it. The rule is if you ever get to the place that you can't get along [without ]{.class_s36M}a certain thing then that thing becomes somewhat of an idol between you and the Lord. You should be at the place where you can never get along without [the Lord.]{.class_s36M} [That's]{.class_s36M} the place.

13 Meats for the belly, and the belly for meats: but God shall destroy both it and them. Now the body is not for fornication, but for the Lord; and the Lord for the body.

14 And God hath both raised up the Lord, and will also raise up us by his own power.

15 Know ye not that your bodies are the members of Christ? shall I then take the members of Christ, and make them the members of an harlot? God forbid.

16 What? know ye not that he which is joined to an harlot is one body? for two, saith he, shall be one flesh.

17 But he that is joined unto the Lord is one spirit.

18 Flee fornication. Every sin that a man doeth is without the body; but he that committeth fornication sinneth against his own body.

["Meats for the belly, and the belly for meats:]{.class_s36N} [but God shall destroy both it and them"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 13). That is kind of a crude way to talk. It isn't apparent why he mentions this matter until a little bit later. A little bit later when he starts talking about what to eat and what not to eat. Notice that in 8:4 and 8:10 and 8:13 they are arguing about what kind of meat to eat and what kind of meat not to eat or whether to eat meat at all. So when he writes to them he says that God will destroy the belly and the meats[ that go into it.]{.class_s36M}

"Don't be so much taken up about these little details about what is right and what is wrong with physical things because God is going to destroy them anyway." It isn't a matter of saying, "This is lawful and that is not lawful. It's lawful to smoke. It's not lawful to smoke. It's lawful to drink beer. It's not lawful to drink beer. It's lawful to eat pork. It's not lawful to eat pork." That is not the point. God is going to destroy your nose and your lungs and your stomach anyway. If the Lord tarries the worms are going to get them. So that is not the point. The point is, do they [edify]{.class_s36S}, are they [expedient]{.class_s36S}, and do they get you [under their power]{.class_s36S}? It isn't the point of whether it is [lawful ]{.class_s36S}or not.

The purpose of the body, in salvation, is so the Lord can use it. Your body is ["for the Lord"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13); it is ["the temple of the Holy Ghost,"]{.class_s36N} on which he will comment in verse 19. When the Lord comes back, He will give you a new body (vs. 14). In the meantime, ["your bodies are the members of Christ."]{.class_s36N} A lot of Christians don't know that.

In light of that, watch what Paul says, ["shall I then take the members of Christ, and make them the members of an harlot? God forbid. What? know ye not that he which is joined to an harlot is one body? for two, saith he, shall be one flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vss. 15--16).

Look at that carefully for a while and let it sink in. Where did you read that thing about ["two\...shall be one flesh"]{.class_s36N} before? Genesis 2:24, the first marriage, ["Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The other place you read it was Ephesians, and in both these cases it is speaking about a marriage where flesh is joining flesh. Ephesians 5:31, ["For this cause shall a man leave his father and mother, and shall be joined unto his wife, and they two shall be one flesh."]{.class_s36N}

Now, go back to 1 Corinthians 6:16, ["What? know ye not that he which is joined to an harlot is one body? for two, saith he, shall be one flesh."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Paul puts the marriage statement down there on [an act of fornication.]{.class_s36M} Verse 18, ["Flee fornication. Every sin that a man doeth is without the body; but he that committeth fornication sinneth against his own body."]{.class_s36N} So that is why [fornication ]{.class_s36M}is grounds for [divorce]{.class_s36M}, because in fornication one person's body joins another person's body, and that constitutes [a marriage.]{.class_s36M}

[American Christians are Roman Catholics in this regard.]{.class_s36M} They think that a marriage is a marriage ceremony, and they think a divorcement is a [bill ]{.class_s36M}of divorcement. [But in the Bible, a marriage is where flesh joins flesh and a divorce is where flesh leaves flesh.]{.class_s36M}

The problem comes up, what about a case where the husband or wife has stepped out on you; shouldn't you take them back?

[Yes]{.class_s36M}, forgive. Forgiving ["as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you."]{.class_s36N} You are to forgive. But that prerogative is left to what we call "the injured party." That is, it is your prerogative whether you want to take them back or not. If you want to, OK. Some women take their husbands back after they are unfaithful to them ten or fifteen times. (It doesn't usually work out very well either.) I'm not going to recommend anything one way or the other. But here are two parties, and one body leaves the other body and joins another body. That is a [marriage]{.class_s36M}---another marriage---in God's sight---whether there is any ["bill of divorcement"]{.class_s36N} or not.

[Like I said, American Christians are Roman Catholic.]{.class_s36M} They think a "marriage" is where you come up and stand before somebody and go through marriage [vows ]{.class_s36M}and make it a ["sacrament." ]{.class_s36M}They think something magical happens when the preacher says, "I now pronounce you man and wife," and they slap on the ring and throw the rice. But that is a marriage [ceremony]{.class_s36M}. A [marriage ]{.class_s36M}is where [flesh joins flesh.]{.class_s36M}

Matthew 19:5--6 ["And said, For this cause shall a man leave father and mother, and shall cleave to his wife: and they twain shall be one flesh? Wherefore they are no more twain, but one flesh. What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder."]{.class_s36N}

This brings up another interesting problem: how many bodies that have been joined together did [God ]{.class_s36M}join together? God joined Adam and Eve in an innocent condition before they fell. Matthew 19:9, ["And I say unto you, Whosoever shall put away his wife, except it be for fornication, and shall marry another, committeth adultery: and whoso marrieth her which is put away doth commit adultery."]{.class_s36N} Fornication is the exception because it is legal grounds for divorce. It is legal grounds because the marriage has [already been broken up.]{.class_s36M}

All right, turn to Romans 7:1 and watch it carefully. ["Know ye not, brethren, (for I speak to them that know the law,) how that the law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth?"]{.class_s36N} Watch verse 2 carefully. ["For the woman which hath]{.class_s36N} \[present tense\][ an husband\...."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Present tense. They're not divorced. [There is no divorce in Romans 7]{.class_s36M}. You couldn't find it there with a flashlight.

["For the woman which hath an husband is bound by the law to her husband so long as he liveth; but if the husband be dead, she is loosed from the law of her husband."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Verse 3, ["So then if, while her husband liveth\...."]{.class_s36N} Not the man [she married,]{.class_s36M} brethren! Her husband. Have you got that? Some folks have a funny way of reading the Bible. ["So then if, while her husband liveth, she be married to another man, she shall be called an adulteress."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Why? [Because she stepped out on her husband.]{.class_s36M} But these dumb Christians, when reading verse 3, say, "Well, look here. She [couldn't]{.class_s36M} have married the other man unless she had a [divorce."]{.class_s36M}

Do you see how dumb Americans are? Americans think a marriage is a marriage [ceremony]{.class_s36M}.[ Why, any woman can be married to another man without a marriage ceremony: ]{.class_s36M}flesh joining flesh. It is so strong that in 1 Corinthians 6:16 Paul says that if you take your body and join it to a harlot,[ it is one flesh.]{.class_s36M} If the verse is that strong, don't you know that where a married woman leaves her husband and joins her body to another man that is a [marriage]{.class_s36M}?

There is no divorce in Romans 7.

You have got a bunch of maniacs running around saying, "Well, this woman here has got three living husbands or two living husbands." No way in the world, man! A divorce broke those two up, and there is no divorce in Romans 7. That's a woman stepping out on her husband. But once he said [married]{.class_s36M}, these fools say, "Uh huh, marriage ceremony." [There is no marriage ceremony there.]{.class_s36M}

["But if her husband be dead, she is free from that law; so that she is no adulteress, though she be married to another man"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(Rom. 7:3). Did you notice how that passage began in verse 1: ["Know ye not, brethren, (for I speak to them that know the law")]{.class_s36N}? How do you explain the fact that every scholar who ever exposited that text in their Commentary didn't go back and check and see what [the law ]{.class_s36M}said, when the text began ["I speak to them that know the law"]{.class_s36N}?

Let's go back to Deuteronomy 24 and see what [the law]{.class_s36M} says. Deuteronomy 24:1, ["When a man hath taken a wife, and married her, and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house. And when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man's wife."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Is that clear? ["I speak to them that know the law."]{.class_s36N} ["And when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man's wife."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Is that clear?

Somebody says, "Well, what if her former husband doesn't die? She's got two living husbands." Let's see about that. Deuteronomy 24:3-4, ["And if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement, and giveth it in her hand, and sendeth her out of his house; or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife; Her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife."]{.class_s36N} Is that clear? ["I speak to them that know the law."]{.class_s36N}

You have got 2,000 Fundamental preachers in America that don't know [the law ]{.class_s36M}of the New Testament or the Old Testament. Do you know what that means? These preachers are the biggest crackpots in the world. They say, "This lady out here has three living husbands." Well, what are you going to have her do? Bust up her marriage and go back to the first husband?

Here is someone who has a legal divorce on grounds of fornication. After the divorce the former party [remarries]{.class_s36M}, and this person remarries, and somebody says, "Well, that is her living husband over there." Well, what are you going to do? [Divorce the one you are living with now and go back to the first one? ]{.class_s36M}Why, that is against the law. That's unlawful. Here in Deuteronomy you can't do that. You couldn't do that [if your former husband was single.]{.class_s36M}

Boy, folks do get in a mess. In the Old Testament they divorced for [any ]{.class_s36M}reason. But that had nothing to do with the law of [remarriage]{.class_s36M}. The law of [remarriage ]{.class_s36M}says if that woman is legally divorced she can be legally remarried, and once she is[ legally remarried]{.class_s36M} she has [one ]{.class_s36M}husband. [She does not have two husbands.]{.class_s36M}

All right, turn to 1 Corinthians 7:10,[ "And unto the married I command, yet not I, but the Lord, Let not the wife depart from her husband." ]{.class_s36N}So it is the Lord's command that a couple stay together. But watch what happens if that command is broken!

[7:11 "But and if she depart, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled to her husband: and let not the husband put away his wife."]{.class_s36N} Again, the object is her husband, not some guy she [married]{.class_s36S}.

7:12 "But to the rest speak I, not the Lord: If any brother hath a wife that believeth not, and she be pleased to dwell with him, let him not put her away."

7:13 "And the woman which hath an husband that believeth not, and if he be pleased to dwell with her, let her not leave him."

See, there is a lot left up to the individual. If you are willing to forgive the spouse for being unfaithful to you and can take him/her back and live with him/her, that is fine. That's your business. Probably that is the best solution, if you can do it.

[I don't recommend divorce to any couple.]{.class_s36M} I think divorce is a mess, especially when you have got kids involved. I think the best possible thing to do is "make a go of it" any way you can. I believe that. It has complications where there has been unfaithfulness, because human nature being what it is, years later when an argument comes up, your spouse will throw it in your face. It will come up, and they will hold it like a club over your head and beat your brains out with it in future arguments. God has called us to [peace]{.class_s36M}, brother, not warfare. But if there is any way to keep the thing together, it should be kept together.

Now, along these lines, watch 1 Corinthians 7:27. ["Art thou bound unto a wife?"]{.class_s36N} How many of you fellows are bound to a wife? All right, here are your instructions: ["seek not to be loosed."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}There it is. If you are bound to a wife,[ don't try to get rid of her.]{.class_s36M} All right, let's try this one.[ "Art thou loosed from a wife?"]{.class_s36N} [You had a wife and now you are single.]{.class_s36M} How many of you are like that? All right, here's your instructions: ["seek not a wife."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Stay single.

[7:28 ]{.class_s36N}["]{.class_s36P-0}[But and if thou marry, thou hast not sinned."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Is that clear? Isn't that Bible a beautiful Book? Some people go around saying, "They're living in sin, the unpardonable sin." They condemn you to a life of a perpetual eunuch. Listen, brother, there is nothing in the world that will throw light on the commentaries like the Bible. Did you notice I don't have to run to the Greek or the Hebrew to get it? And I sure don't have to fool with the [Living Bible]{.class_s36M} and the "living language" for today's suckers.

Now, watch it. ["But and if thou marry, thou hast not sinned; and if a virgin]{.class_s36N} \[somebody who has never been married\] [marry, she hath not sinned. Nevertheless]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}\[second warning, in a remarriage or even in marriage\] [such shall have trouble in the flesh: but I spare you."]{.class_s36N}

Final qualification:

[7:39 "The wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth; but if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will: only in the Lord."]{.class_s36N}[ The qualification is that you have to marry a Christian.]{.class_s36M}

7:40 "But she is happier if she so abide, after my judgment: and I think also that I have the Spirit of God."

He is saying sarcastically, "I think I've got the Spirit," and of course, he does. All right, there are [three grounds for divorce,]{.class_s36M} and a [remarriage ]{.class_s36M}is mentioned in every case.

[The first is death.]{.class_s36M} In case of death the bodies are separate. [You're not bound.]{.class_s36M} The next is fornication. Finally, the third ground, in the passage we are reading here, 1 Corinthians 7:15, ["But if the unbelieving depart, let him depart. A brother]{.class_s36N} \[saved man\][ ]{.class_s36P-0}[or a sister]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}\[saved woman\][ is not under bondage in such cases."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}You are not bound to the party that deserts.

All right, in all those cases [a remarriage]{.class_s36M} is mentioned. ["Art thou bond unto a wife? seek not to be loosed. Art thou loosed from a wife? seek not a wife. But and if thou marry, thou hast not sinned." "The wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth; but if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Remarriage. ["Whosoever shall put away his wife, EXCEPT it be for fornication, and shall marry another\...."]{.class_s36N}

There is a remarriage in each case.

Where there is [desertion ]{.class_s36M}a person takes his body and goes off and joins with somebody else, so you're free. In a case where a person is living and hasn't deserted you but is stepping out on you and joining his body to another body, [you are temporarily single.]{.class_s36M} In the other case. when the person [dies ]{.class_s36M}the mate is then single. There is no such thing in the world as a woman that has [three living husbands,]{.class_s36M} unless she is joining her body to all three of them in polygamy [at the same time.]{.class_s36M}

In that case, she is called an [adulteress]{.class_s36M}, Romans 7:3.

Christ said to the Samaritan woman one time at the well, ["Go, call thy husband."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Do you know what she said? She said, ["I have no husband."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He said, "You told that right. You've [had ]{.class_s36M}five of them." There you go. She wasn't married to any of them by a [ceremony]{.class_s36M}.

Back around 1959--1961, Bob Jones Jr. was so worried. He wanted Horton to get our church so badly! Horton had set up his own school right next to it. Bob Jones Jr. would write to me and say, "You don't qualify to be a pastor because you aren't [married."]{.class_s36M} I would write back and say, "OK, I'll [get married]{.class_s36M} so that I qualify." He wrote back and said, "No, because if you marry then you would have[ two wives]{.class_s36M}." I asked, "What are you trying to tell me? Are you trying to tell me that [I'm single]{.class_s36M} until I get [married ]{.class_s36M}and then I have [two wives ]{.class_s36M}when I get married?" Aren't people weird? [That was the Chancellor at Bob Jones University in charge of training 4,000 young men for the ministry. Why, the kid couldn't do arithmetic!]{.class_s36M}

I said to a fellow one time, "This passage in 1 Timothy says the Bishop should be the husband of [one wife]{.class_s36M}. Does that mean only [married ]{.class_s36M}once?" He said, "Yes." I said, "Then can a man step out on his wife as much as he wants to as long as he only has one marriage ceremony?" He said, "Oh, no, no, no, not that!"

I said, "Well, when I read this passage here the fellow must be faithful to the wife [he has.]{.class_s36M} If you are saying [one ]{.class_s36M}marriage ceremony you can say a guy can have all the women he wants as long as he only goes through [one ]{.class_s36M}marriage ceremony. Are you trying to say it means [only married once]{.class_s36M}?" The fellow said, "Yes."

I said, "Do you really believe that? " The guy said, "Yes." I said, "I just want to see if you are sincere. I'm going to ask you one more time. Do you believe that no man can be a bishop unless he has only been married once?" The fellow said, ["Yes."]{.class_s36M}

I said, "What about Bob Jones Sr.? [He was married twice]{.class_s36M}." (And he was.) He said, "Yes, but his first wife [died]{.class_s36M}."

I said, "You hypocrite. Don't back out now. Don't tell me that verse meant [what you thought it meant when you had a mental reservation in your mind,]{.class_s36M} and the verse didn't really [mean ]{.class_s36M}what it said. Don't give me that stuff."

That verse means that you are to have [one wife now ]{.class_s36M}and be true to the one you've got [now]{.class_s36M}. [That ]{.class_s36M}is what that means. If you are true to the one you have now and she is true to you,[ you're married,]{.class_s36M} and you're her husband, and she is your wife. And you don't have any other wives. So don't look for them.

There is no way out except with [sound doctrine.]{.class_s36M} Anyone can say what he wants to, but I have given you [sound doctrine.]{.class_s36M} Now, if you take that for a license to sin, then you dig your own grave with your own shovel. But that is [sound doctrine, ]{.class_s36M}and that is how it is. I'm not giving you a license to sin. You pay for your sins. You will reap what you sow.

By the same token, you don't want to have the devil kick you over a cliff and say, "All is hopeless now. I've made a mess of my life, and God will never forgive me. I've committed the unpardonable sin."[ That is Catholic stuff.]{.class_s36M} That is a bunch of [rubbish]{.class_s36M}.

God said ["she is not my wife"]{.class_s36N} (Hosea 2:2).

A fellow that had the biggest church in town was married twice. He's gone now, but he had around 400 to 600 hundred in Sunday School and led about 200 souls to Christ for about ten years. A fellow from St. Petersburg that has been married twice is in the ministry and is running 1,200 in Sunday School and has an eight hundred thousand dollar plant on one of the best pieces of land I have ever seen. [He believes the Bible and preaches the Book.]{.class_s36M}

Some unfortunate Christian says, "Well, I made this mess of my life. I can't\...." [That is not true.]{.class_s36M} If that is your case, what you've got is a bunch of Pharisees who have never been through anything rough, and they are trying to take advantage of you and make you think that [they ]{.class_s36M}are first-class Christians and you are a [second-class Christian.]{.class_s36M} I'll tell you what you do. I'll give you good advice if you will listen to me. [Be a second-class Christian,]{.class_s36M} and watch God use [you ]{.class_s36M}and put some of those first-class Christians [on the shelf]{.class_s36M}. Be a second-class Christian in their sight, and you watch God use you.

I'll tell you what. If you haven't had that kind of trouble in your life and you're married to a good woman or good man and things go along fine, just praise God for it; be thankful for it and don't get too proud about it. [The Lord might give you some children, and you might go through it yet.]{.class_s36S} It happens in the best of families. Pray for some of the folks that have it a little bit worse than you do and pray that it doesn't happen to you. Galatians 6:1, ["ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness; considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted."]{.class_s36N}

Now, back to 1 Corinthians chapter 6.

["Flee fornication. Every sin that a man doeth is without the body; but he that committeth fornication sinneth against his own body"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 18).

The body is the instrument of fornication. [Fornication]{.class_s36M}, by definition in the Bible, is an [act]{.class_s36M}, and it is an act where the [body ]{.class_s36M}is illegally joined to another body.

Webster's Dictionary says that [fornication ]{.class_s36M}is illicit relationships between single people and that [adultery ]{.class_s36M}is between married people. But that won't work at all for a couple of very good reasons in the Bible. First of all, turn to Matthew 19:9 and notice that if that is what the word means according to Webster, you couldn't divorce your wife or your husband no matter how many times they stepped out on you, [provided they just stepped out with a married person.]{.class_s36M} You know that won't work.

Look at Matthew 19:9, ["And I say unto you, Whosoever shall put away his wife, except it be for fornication\...."]{.class_s36N} Well, according to Webster, if she is married, she wouldn't commit fornication. See how stupid that is? (Sometimes you have to straighten Webster out.)

All right, let's go back to the Book of Revelation and take it again. [Fornication is a physical act.]{.class_s36M} When people commit fornication, they commit [fornication.]{.class_s36M} They may or may not commit "adultery," but they certainly [commit ]{.class_s36M}fornication.

You can also commit adultery [without committing fornication.]{.class_s36M} Christ said, ["whoseover looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(Matt. 5:28). [Adultery]{.class_s36M}, not fornication. Technically, among single people, you could say the only act that is committed is fornication, whereas adultery would be an act involving a married person, somebody who is already married. Revelation 2:21--22, ["And I gave her space to repent of her fornication; and she repented not. Behold, I will cast her into a bed, and them that commit adultery with her."]{.class_s36N} Well, she's committing fornication [and ]{.class_s36M}adultery. Jezebel was a married woman. She was married to Ahab, verse 20. So fornication comes out as an [act]{.class_s36M}. Adultery can also be a [thought ]{.class_s36M}that is not actually committed.

19 What? know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which ye have of God, and ye are not your own?

20 For ye are bought with a price: therefore glorify God in your body, and in your spirit, which are God's."

["What? know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 19). Do you know to whom he wrote that? He wrote that to the people that [talked in tongues.]{.class_s36M} And isn't it strange that everybody who brags about having these [gifts ]{.class_s36M}is always doubting their salvation? And isn't it strange how they always try to convince you that [you ]{.class_s36M}don't have the Holy Ghost?

Paul had to write to those gifted people, ["What? know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}They [didn't ]{.class_s36S}all talk in tongues. In 1 Corinthians 12 he said, ["Are all apostles?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}No. ["Are all prophets?"]{.class_s36N} No. ["Are all workers of miracles?" "Do all speak with tongues?"]{.class_s36N} No. But their body was ["the temple of the Holy Ghost."]{.class_s36N}

You should memorize that verse.

["For ye are bought with a price"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 20). Then you are a slave. If you are a Christian, you are [a slave.]{.class_s36M} You ["are bought with a price."]{.class_s36N} That word Paul uses constantly throughout the New Testament; a "servant" is the Greek word for a "slave." It's a servant that is knocked down on the block and bought with money.

A little boy made a boat. It took him two years to make it; it was a little toy boat about twenty inches long. He set it in a small stream, and he lost it. A couple of years later, a man downstream showed it to the boy and bragged about what a pretty boat it was, and the boy said, "It's my boat. It's my boat." The man said, "No, it's not.[ I found it]{.class_s36M}." The boy said, "It's mine. [I made it]{.class_s36M} myself." The man said, "I'll sell it to you." So the boy worked another year and got the money and bought his little toy boat back. As he walked off, he hugged the boat to himself and said, "You're mine [twice]{.class_s36M}. I [made ]{.class_s36M}you and I [bought ]{.class_s36M}you."

That's the Christian. [The Lord made you and He bought you.]{.class_s36M}

["Therefore glorify God in your body, and in your spirit, which are God's."]{.class_s36N} The Christian's body is the Lord's, and his spirit is the Lord's.

[]{#cRH.xhtml}

## Chapter 7 {.heading_s39}

7:1 Now concerning the things whereof ye wrote unto me: It is good for a man not to touch a woman.

2 Nevertheless, to avoid fornication, let every man have his own wife, and let every woman have her own husband.

3 Let the husband render unto the wife due benevolence: and likewise also the wife unto the husband.

4 The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband: and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife.

5 Defraud ye not one the other, except it be with consent for a time, that ye may give yourselves to fasting and prayer; and come together again, that Satan tempt you not for your incontinency.

6 But I speak this by permission, and not of commandment.

7 For I would that all men were even as I myself. But every man hath his proper gift of God, one after this manner, and another after that.

8 I say therefore to the unmarried and widows, It is good for them if they abide even as I.

9 But if they cannot contain, let them marry: for it is better to marry than to burn.

The word ["touch,"]{.class_s36N} in verse 1, refers to carnal relationships. It's not talking about bumping into each other in the hallway. At Bob Jones University they carry that to the extreme: "no holding hands on the campus" and in the "dating parlor" they sit with their backs to each other across the sofa. It gets kind of wild. All this attempt to try to be like St. Francis in the monastery isn't going to work anyway, because you are dealing with a different bunch of people. You can say all you want to, but the quality of Christians has been going steadily [lower ]{.class_s36M}for nineteen centuries. You can't make a bunch of monks and nuns out of them without something else going to pieces.

So Paul is talking about sex matters here, which is obvious from what follows

Verse 2: ["avoid fornication."]{.class_s36N} Fornication is promiscuous [acts ]{.class_s36M}here and there. How do you avoid that? ["Let every man have his own wife, and let every woman have her own husband."]{.class_s36N} That is a commandment.

["Let the husband render unto the wife due benevolence"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3).[ ]{.class_s36M}That is, in the marriage relation, the husband is to give the wife what is due her. Of course, there are the ones who would say, "She didn't [earn ]{.class_s36M}much this week." Well, she can claim [some]{.class_s36M}. ["And likewise also the wife unto the husband."]{.class_s36N} But of course, in the wife's case, it is a little bit rougher, because she is told to be in subjection ["as unto the Lord"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 5:22). So the benevolence, there, is [due ]{.class_s36M}whether you rendered it or not.

["The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband: and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). In marriage, the wife's body belongs to [the husband]{.class_s36M}, and the husband's body belongs to [the wife]{.class_s36M}. So the body of a married Christian belongs to two people: it belongs to God first (1 Cor. 6:20) and then their spouse (vs. 4).

["Defraud ye not one the other, except it be with consent for a time, that ye may give yourselves to fasting and prayer; and come together again, that Satan tempt you not for your incontinency"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). The idea is that, if you are married, then you should have [normal physical relations ]{.class_s36M}with your spouse, and you are not to forego for [long periods of time]{.class_s36M}. The reason is that Satan will take advantage of your lack of control (["incontinency"]{.class_s36N}) during these periods. A couple should only cut off physical relations [when they both agree,]{.class_s36M} together, for a spiritual reason. Otherwise, the Devil will work you over. ["For we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 6:12).

Now God is going to allow Paul to say something that He does not command.

["But I speak this by permission, and not of commandment"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6). As far as Paul is concerned, a fellow would be better off like himself---single, unmarried (vs. 7). ["But every man hath his proper gift of God, one after this manner, and another after that."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Some men can stay single better than others. Go back to Matthew 19 and see where Jesus says the same thing. Matthew 19:10, ["His disciples say unto him, If the case of the man be so with his wife, it is not good to marry."]{.class_s36N}

If [what ]{.class_s36M}case is so? Verse 9: if the only reason you can get rid of a woman is because of fornication, man, then it's bad to get married! They figured there ought to be more reasons to get rid of her than that! In Deuteronomy 24, he said if you find ["some uncleanness in her."]{.class_s36N} When they ask him this question in Matthew 19, they say in verse 3, ["Is it lawful for a man to put away his wife for every cause?"]{.class_s36N} The Pharisees had twenty different ways of getting rid of them.

Back in the old days they used to have just two grounds for divorce. Then they got insanity, and then they got "[extreme ]{.class_s36M}cruelty," and then they got [physical ]{.class_s36M}cruelty, and then they got "cruelty," and then they got "[mental ]{.class_s36M}cruelty," and then they got [incompatibility]{.class_s36M}. Now the thing is so fixed that if you and your spouse have trouble, you just go down to the court and say, "That's it," and they give you a paper, and that's it.

That is a stupid way of doing things, [because it doesn't teach anybody to overcome bad habits.]{.class_s36M} It doesn't teach people how to [live with each other]{.class_s36M}. It doesn't teach you how to develop [character]{.class_s36M}. It makes you a [spoiled brat.]{.class_s36M} If the first time your spouse does something you don't like you're ready to quit your marriage that quick, you never should have gotten married in the first place.

If there is any rule this age does not need, that's the rule it does not need, because people in this day and age are immature and emotionally unstable. A young man and woman get married, and at the first two spats they have, bam! there goes their marriage down the drain. Then they are marked the rest of their life.

You can talk all you want about the broad and enlightened society, but people are not like the newspapers say they are. [They are not that way.]{.class_s36M} So if there is any rule we [don't need,]{.class_s36M} it is that one. The rule you need is: if you are not going to stick it out and try to make a go of it and do the best you can, [don't ever start.]{.class_s36M} That is the rule you need.

Matthew 19:11, ["But he said unto them, All men cannot receive this saying, save they to whom it is given."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That is, not every man can stay single. Verse 12, ["For there are some eunuchs, which were so born from their mother's womb: and there are some eunuchs, which were made eunuchs of men: and there be eunuchs, which have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom of heaven's sake. He that is able to receive it, let him receive it."]{.class_s36N}

Notice when Christ speaks about a marriage, He is talking about [physical contact,]{.class_s36M} and when he says "eunuch" he means a man who can't have children. Some men are born impotent, [sterile]{.class_s36M}.

During the Old Testament captivity, Daniel and his friends were made "eunuchs." Modern operations have various names (vasectomy, hysterectomy, etc.).

Others are like Paul: they can have children, but they refuse to marry and have children so they can serve the Lord better.

Back in 1 Corinthians 7:8, Paul addressed ["the unmarried]{.class_s36N} \[male\] [and widows ]{.class_s36N}\[female\][."]{.class_s36N} He will do the same thing in verse 28: ["But and if thou marry, thou hast not sinned; and if a virgin marry."]{.class_s36N} ["Thou"]{.class_s36N} in verse 28 is in reference to a man. See verse 27, ["Art thou loosed from a wife? seek not a wife."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's a man. ["And if a virgin marry, she"]{.class_s36N}; that's a woman. The [directive will]{.class_s36M} for unmarried men and widow women is to stay single if they can.

The "containing" of verse 9 is the controlling of the emotions and the lust, and when Paul says, ["it is better to marry than to burn,"]{.class_s36N} he is not speaking of going to Hell. It is burning with lust, incontinency, and lack of control. If that doesn't show that marriage is a [physical ]{.class_s36M}thing, what do you want for fifteen cents? That is recommending marriage on [fleshy grounds.]{.class_s36M} If you stayed single you would be able to serve the Lord better. You wouldn't have as many hindrances.

I've always thought Job was a greater man than Paul. I've always thought that. Paul did a great job, but he didn't have a wife coming around and saying, ["Curse God, and die."]{.class_s36N} Paul didn't have to bury any of his children, which Job had to do. Paul was caught up to the third heaven and saw where he was going, and Job saw nothing. Paul had 39 books he could read any time he wanted, and Job had none. I've always thought Job was the better of the two men. When you have a wife and children, you have responsibilities that can keep your mind off the Lord that otherwise you wouldn't have. Any single man has less to keep him bound to this earth than a married man.

7:10 And unto the married I command, yet not I, but the Lord, Let not the wife depart from her husband:

11 But and if she depart, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled to her husband: and let not the husband put away his wife.

12 But to the rest speak I, not the Lord: If any brother hath a wife that believeth not, and she be pleased to dwell with him, let him not put her away.

13 And the woman which hath an husband that believeth not, and if he be pleased to dwell with her, let her not leave him.

14 For the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified by the husband: else were your children unclean; but now are they holy.

15 But if the unbelieving depart, let him depart. A brother or a sister is not under bondage in such cases: but God hath called us to peace.

16 For what knowest thou, O wife, whether thou shalt save thy husband? or how knowest thou, O man, whether thou shalt save thy wife?

In verse 10, Paul is repeating a commandment already given by the Lord. The commandment is addressed to the wife: she is to stay with her husband.

However, Paul does allow for a temporary separation (vs. 11). But if the wife [does ]{.class_s36S}depart for a while, she is to remain unmarried until she be reconciled because she is plainly told not to [desert]{.class_s36S}. In such a case, the husband is not to divorce his wife but to seek reconciliation. Later in the chapter, Paul will talk about what happens if the wife deserts her husband [permanently ]{.class_s36S}with no interest in reconciliation.

["But to the rest speak I, not the Lord" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 12). This is not a secondary revelation; it isn't [lower]{.class_s36M}. He just says, "The Lord didn't say anything about this so I'm saying it." It is still inspired. It's still authoritative. When he says, ["But to the rest speak I, not the Lord,"]{.class_s36N} it doesn't mean you don't have to do this because [the Lord ]{.class_s36M}didn't say it. It means, "The Lord never said anything about this, [so I am saying it now]{.class_s36M}."And what he says is this: if you, as a saved man, are married to an unsaved woman and she doesn't want to leave you, don't divorce her (vs. 12). If you, as a saved woman, are married to an unsaved man, and he wants to go on living with you, [you are not to divorce him.]{.class_s36M}

Now, notice verse 14. In a mixed marriage (believer with unbeliever), the Lord [sanctifies ]{.class_s36M}(sets apart) the unbelieving spouse because of the believing spouse. The children of such a marriage are also set apart (holy). They are [sanctified ]{.class_s36M}and holy, [not]{.class_s36M} saved.

The Covenant churches in Holland, Michigan, and all the baby-sprinkling Covenant people, teach verse 14 to prove that if you sprinkle your child into the Covenant, then God guarantees to [save your children]{.class_s36M} because of the saved party in the house. The guarantee isn't that strong. That thing is saying that God [honors ]{.class_s36M}a marriage between a saved person and a lost person for the sake of the saved person. Because of that, the marriage bed is [undefiled ]{.class_s36M}and the Lord honours the marriage, even though an unsaved person is there, for the sake of the saved person. It is like saying the marriage is [clean ]{.class_s36M}in God's sight, and God will honor the marriage where otherwise He wouldn't honor it. (Notice Exod. 19:15 and 1 Sam. 21:4.) When two unsaved people get married in God's sight it is absolutely [nothing]{.class_s36M}. It is unclean (Lev. 15:16--19).

Verse 15 now answers the question of what happens when one party leaves permanently. If they want to leave, let them leave.[ ]{.class_s36N}You are not bound to the party But what happens when a [believing ]{.class_s36M}party departs? The Bible in 1 Corinthians 7 doesn't make any allowance for that, other than a [temporary ]{.class_s36M}departure in verse 11. It is everywhere taken for granted in the word of God that if a woman is saved she is not going to desert her husband and that if a man is saved he is not going to desert his wife. The only cases that are given are unbelievers.

In a mixed marriage, the saved person should stay in the marriage and be a good testimony so that he may lead his spouse to Christ. Of course, you don't know for sure what the Lord may do (vs. 16). But if you are the head of the household and if your wife is in subjection to you, you may claim this promise (["Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house"]{.class_s36N}). But when there are those who are [not ]{.class_s36M}under subjection to you, you don't have that promise [on them.]{.class_s36M}

The question arises, then, is a marriage between two lost people still a marriage. Well, it is a [physical ]{.class_s36M}marriage, yes. But as far as God is concerned, it isn't honored. In the first marriage in the Bible, both are saved. [The first marriage in the Bible is Adam and Eve, and they are a type of Christ and the Church.]{.class_s36M} A [lost couple]{.class_s36M} are married in the sight of the law, and as far as "flesh joining flesh" is concerned, that constitutes a marriage, but nothing in the Bible says that God [will bless it or honor it or consider it.]{.class_s36M} It just [happens]{.class_s36M}. That's all. ["What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(Matt. 19:6). [Well, God doesn't join the unsaved. ]{.class_s36M}He isn't in them. God doesn't [join ]{.class_s36M}saved and lost people. ["Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 6:14). That verse is a tough one.

So in a marriage between a saved and a lost person, the saved spouse can claim the promises and witness and try to win the other, but the unsaved spouse may not get saved. That is why, in verse 15, if the marriage doesn't work out, the saved spouse is to let the unsaved go.

7:17 But as God hath distributed to every man, as the Lord hath called every one, so let him walk. And so ordain I in all churches.

18 Is any man called being circumcised? let him not become uncircumcised. Is any called in uncircumcision? let him not be circumcised.

19 Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.

20 Let every man abide in the same calling wherein he was called.

21 Art thou called being a servant? care not for it: but if thou mayest be made free, use it rather.

22 For he that is called in the Lord, being a servant, is the Lord's freeman: likewise also he that is called, being free, is Christ's servant.

23 Ye are bought with a price; be not ye the servants of men.

24 Brethren, let every man, wherein he is called, therein abide with God.

The idea of the passage is for you to stay in the condition in which you find yourself. Paul uses circumcision to illustrate the principle in verses 18 and 19. If you are saved, you don't need to change your condition in order to get saved or stay saved. [Circumcision ]{.class_s36M}has nothing to do with salvation. [Uncircumcision ]{.class_s36M}has nothing to do with salvation. After all, the main thing about those things is whether or not they are ["the keeping of the commandments of God."]{.class_s36N} Where the commandment of God is not given then the thing doesn't amount to anything. [There is no commandment of God given in the New Testament about circumcision or uncircumcision in regards to salvation.]{.class_s36M} It isn't given. So if it is not a [commandment of God]{.class_s36M}, then don't worry about it.

The second illustration Paul uses (vss. 20--24) is slavery. Verse 21, if one were a slave, he was not to worry about it. Instead he was to use his condition to serve God. Some folks have the most terrible time with that verse, but it is explained in verse 22.

I heard a fellow say, "Well, if your 'servitude' is not to be ['the servants of men,']{.class_s36N} then you are to get free." No, that won't work. He is saying that a man that is called of the Lord, [while he is a slave]{.class_s36M}, is to treat the service given to his master (a man) as given to[ the Lord]{.class_s36M}. Salvation makes him a free man on the [inside]{.class_s36M}, even though he is a slave on the [outside]{.class_s36M}.

Paul brings this section to a conclusion in verse 24, so let's lay this thing out.

When a Christian gets saved he is ["bought with a price."]{.class_s36N} When he is saved, he is God's servant and God's slave. All right, if he is God's servant and God's slave, he is no longer a servant of any [man]{.class_s36M}. He is the servant of [God]{.class_s36M}. Therefore, if the man that God saves happens to be a slave or a servant to a man, he is not to worry about it, because he is [free]{.class_s36M}. He is ["the Lord's freeman."]{.class_s36N} So, he says if you are a slave of a man and his servant and you find yourself getting saved, don't worry about it. Use your [servitude ]{.class_s36M}as a testimony and a witness for the Lord, because you're not [really ]{.class_s36M}a slave; [you're a free man.]{.class_s36M} You are ["bought with a price." ]{.class_s36N}That is how that thing goes.

I hate to start the Civil War again, [but there is nothing in the New Testament that does away with slavery.]{.class_s36M} It is not there. The Civil War was started to get rid of State's rights. The Civil War was to prove that all the States had to be controlled [by a socialistic government in one place,]{.class_s36M} and slavery was the issue they picked. In the 1960s, they picked [school bussing.]{.class_s36M}

Look at 1 Timothy 6:1--2, ["Let as many servants as are under the yoke count their own masters worthy of all honour, that the name of God and his doctrine be not blasphemed. And they that have believing masters, let them not despise them, because they are brethren; but rather do them service, because they are faithful and beloved, partakers of the benefit."]{.class_s36N}

Now, Paul is not talking about an employer-employee relationship. He is addressing slaves who have human masters. After salvation, the slave was to stay and work for his master, not get free and run away---especially if the master was saved. A preacher is to ["teach and exhort"]{.class_s36N} these things.

If he does not (vs. 3), ["he is proud, knowing nothing"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). Then this whole business about the New Testament teaching that all men are equal and that all slaves should be set free is a [heresy]{.class_s36M}. In the New Testament, a saved servant is told to stay in his place. [He doesn't cross the Ohio River on the ice or anything else.]{.class_s36M}

["Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin\....If the Son therefore shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed"]{.class_s36N} (John 8:34, 36).

A [saved slave ]{.class_s36M}at a plantation in Alabama or Georgia back in the 1840s, 1850s, and 1860s, who was knocked down on the block and sold for a price of money and had his family split up and later raised his own family down there in a little old shack on the river, was [freer ]{.class_s36M}than Cassius Clay.

It's too late now to start another war. [There is nothing worth fighting for, anymore, except the Lord.]{.class_s36M} But if those colored folks didn't get themselves in the worst trouble by letting those white folks mislead them! Those Yankees said, "We'll set you [free]{.class_s36M}, boys; we'll set you free." [You set them free]{.class_s36M}. Now where are they? They are in Detroit, Philadelphia, and Cleveland eating dog food.[ That's]{.class_s36M} where they are. They're killing each other. That's what they're doing. They make up 80 percent of the prison population.

Now, just think about this. Get "broadminded" for a minute, brethren. When this bird was picked up, he was in the bushes, wearing a loincloth. When he was picked up, he [couldn't read]{.class_s36M} and [couldn't write]{.class_s36M}. When he was picked up, he was [worshipping the witch doctor.]{.class_s36M} When he was picked up, he was [killing his own children ]{.class_s36M}and stomping his own twins to death when they were born, because a "spell" was on them. Then when he was picked up and brought to the States, he was brought "down South" and put out on a big open place; [three meals a day were provided.]{.class_s36M} During the time he didn't work, he [hunted and fished;]{.class_s36M} he hunted possum and had possum gravy and ate watermelons and [sat around and sang.]{.class_s36M} On Sunday he was required to be at the [white master's church]{.class_s36M} and went up in the balcony and[ heard the plan of salvation and got born again.]{.class_s36M} Now, that may be terrible, but did you know worse things can happen to you in life than[ that?]{.class_s36M}

7:25 Now concerning virgins I have no commandment of the Lord: yet I give my judgment, as one that hath obtained mercy of the Lord to be faithful.

26 I suppose therefore that this is good for the present distress, I say, that it is good for a man so to be.

27 Art thou bound unto a wife? seek not to be loosed. Art thou loosed from a wife? seek not a wife.

28 But and if thou marry, thou hast not sinned; and if a virgin marry, she hath not sinned. Nevertheless such shall have trouble in the flesh: but I spare you.

Paul is now back on his original topic of discussion. The ["virgins"]{.class_s36N} of verse 25 could be any person who has never had physical relations, male or female. You read about men being [virgins ]{.class_s36M}in Revelation 14.

He speaks first to the [male ]{.class_s36M}virgins in verse 26. If a man is going to be a virgin and not get married and stay chaste, all right. Paul said, ["it is good for a man so to be"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 26). I know what the world says. Some folks say you're not grown up unless you've experimented with every kind or form of sex. Some of the most sexually promiscuous people in the world are some of the biggest [spoiled brats]{.class_s36M} you ever saw in your life. [Growing up]{.class_s36M} doesn't have anything to do with that kind of thing. [Growing up]{.class_s36M} has to do with sacrificing and putting up with things patiently and learning how to give and how to work for others and how to get along with people and to put yourself in the back seat and give somebody the first seat. That's what [growing up]{.class_s36M} is. It has nothing to do with this business here.

We have already covered verses 27 and 28 in chapter 6. Paul is applying what he said in verses 17--24 to the Christian's marital situation. If you are married, stay married. Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "I've been married to one woman for forty-five years, and I never felt like [leaving ]{.class_s36M}her, but I've often felt like [choking her to death.]{.class_s36M}"

Contrariwise, if you are divorced,[ stay single.]{.class_s36M} Notice ["loosed"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 27) is [past tense.]{.class_s36M} The person was married and is no longer married. Going to the Greek won't help you a bit. The Greek word is [lelusai]{.class_s36M}; in the Greek lexicon, [that ]{.class_s36M}is a divorce.

["But and if thou marry"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 28). Paul is going to give God's permissive will for a divorced person: ["thou hast not sinned."]{.class_s36N} [Is that clear?]{.class_s36M}! No matter what people say (and they will say [plenty]{.class_s36M}), when a saved person, who has been legitimately divorced, gets married, he has not sinned any more than a virgin who gets married has sinned. It really doesn't matter, though, if you are a virgin who gets married or a divorced person who gets [remarried]{.class_s36M}, both are going to have ["trouble in the flesh."]{.class_s36N} Paul would like to spare married Christians trouble, but [trouble ]{.class_s36M}is just a fact of married life.

7:29 But this I say, brethren, the time is short: it remaineth, that both they that have wives be as though they had none;

30 And they that weep, as though they wept not; and they that rejoice, as though they rejoiced not; and they that buy, as though they possessed not;

31 And they that use this world, as not abusing it: for the fashion of this world passeth away.

Verse 29 is quite a verse. A fellow trying to "wrest the Scriptures" could do something with that. Don't be too "married." Don't be so stuck to your wife that you cling to her skirts all day long, day and night, attending upon her every little wish and whim. You are [too ]{.class_s36M}married if you are [that ]{.class_s36M}married. You have got to have some time to give to God, brother.

The same principle applies to the wife. Look at verse 34:[ "There is a difference also between a wife and a virgin. The unmarried woman careth for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit: but she that is married careth for the things of the world, how she may please her husband."]{.class_s36N} Again, the Christian should exercise moderation. (Phil. 4:5, ["Let your moderation be known unto all men."]{.class_s36N})[ ]{.class_s36N}A woman shouldn't spend [all her time]{.class_s36M} trying to please her husband. She should spend some of her time trying [to please God]{.class_s36M}. When you are saved, you are in a peculiar position. If you are saved and married, even though you are "bone of bone and flesh of flesh" with your spouse, don't forget that your are "bone of bone and flesh of flesh" with your Saviour (Eph. 5).

["And they that weep, as though they wept not."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}When a loved one dies, go on and weep, but don't be [excessive ]{.class_s36M}about it. ["And they that rejoice, as though they rejoiced not."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If somebody sends you a couple hundred thousand dollars, start hollering and shouting, but not too long. [You could lose it.]{.class_s36M} It's only loaned to you. You have to be careful what you do with it. You'd better pray about how to spend it.

["And they that buy, as though they possessed not."]{.class_s36N} Do you have a nice house, a nice car, and nice things? Fine. But just act like it was [loaned ]{.class_s36M}to you, because it isn't really yours. It's [temporary]{.class_s36M}. Everything we have in this life is loaned to us, and then one day the Lord takes it back. One day death walks in the house and says, "OK, you've lived here long enough. Now you're going to go down and live in the graveyard a while." You'll say, "I don't want to go down there." Death will say, "Too bad, down you go." Then you realize that what you had was [a temporary loan.]{.class_s36M}

The trick is (vs. 31) to [use ]{.class_s36M}the world for [necessary ]{.class_s36M}things, and yet, not abuse it. You'll have to [use ]{.class_s36M}the world to buy groceries. You have to [use ]{.class_s36M}the world to get along with people, make contacts, get jobs, and build things. You have to [use ]{.class_s36M}it. But don't [abuse it.]{.class_s36M} Don't go "whole hog." Moderation, brother, moderation!

7:32 But I would have you without carefulness. He that is unmarried careth for the things that belong to the Lord, how he may please the Lord:

33 But he that is married careth for the things that are of the world, how he may please his wife.

34 There is difference also between a wife and a virgin. The unmarried woman careth for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit: but she that is married careth for the things of the world, how she may please her husband.

35 And this I speak for your own profit; not that I may cast a snare upon you, but for that which is comely, and that ye may attend upon the Lord without distraction.

Paul, of course, is talking about an ideal situation in verse 32. Not every single man, in the town where you live, is trying to please God. You have to take that thing in the context. That context is talking about saved men and women that are doing what the Lord tells them to do. He is talking about an [ideal situation]{.class_s36M}. In an ideal situation, if a fellow is single and saved and doing what he ought to do, then he has [one thing ]{.class_s36M}in mind and that is how to please the Lord. His loyalties aren't divided. As to whether he does it or not is beside the point. The point is that is [what he cares for.]{.class_s36M} He is concerned about[ that.]{.class_s36M}

But when you get married, you have to concerned about how to take care of your wife and family in this world. You have to, because the woman is [insecure]{.class_s36M}. She's [unstable]{.class_s36M}. The women's libbers don't know about what they are talking. You say, "Don't be so dogmatic." You can't be too dogmatic about a thing like that. You say, "Well, I know a case of a woman\...." [The exception proves the rule.]{.class_s36M}

You are living in an age where these nuts find an [exception ]{.class_s36M}and think that [changes ]{.class_s36M}the rule. You may have a woman in the WACs, and she curses and swears and makes you crawl on your stomach, and tells jokes that a thirty-year Navy man wouldn't tell, and she can spit tobacco juice thirty feet. So what?! You have got a bunch of nuts that think when you find an [exception ]{.class_s36M}to the rule that [changes ]{.class_s36M}the rule. You're up against a lawbreaker. You're up against a rule breaker. The generation in which you are living is trying to break the laws. The way they do it is by pulling out an [exception ]{.class_s36M}and saying, "OK, that proves it." [It does not prove anything of the kind]{.class_s36M}. It's the exception that [proves ]{.class_s36M}that the rule is [correct]{.class_s36M}. The fact of the matter is that a woman's physiological constitution is not like a man's.

These educators keep pushing on forward, like they are going some where.[ They're going to Hell.]{.class_s36M} That's where they're going. The fact is that [women ]{.class_s36M}are not like [men]{.class_s36M}. If you don't know that, get married and find out. A woman will sit around all the time worrying about nothing. That's right. She will sit around and listen to the devil all day long and not know that it is [the devil]{.class_s36M}. A woman will sit around, and then she will have a guilty conscience because she has listened to the devil. Then when you don't tell her you love her and make up, she feels like you don't love her because she knows she has done wrong. She feels [guilty ]{.class_s36M}herself, and she reads [her ]{.class_s36M}bad conscience into [you]{.class_s36M}. That's how that thing goes. That's how women are.

So when you get married you have to tend to those things. That matter of food and clothing and physical checkups and medicine and that business of order around the house, and neatness around the house has to be taken care of. And those are the things of the world. Those are not [spiritual things]{.class_s36M}. They're [physical]{.class_s36M}, [material things.]{.class_s36M} But they have to be taken care of.

Of course, the same thing is true of the Christian woman. The ideal Christian woman is trying to do what God tells her to do. She's putting the Lord first. But the Christian wife has to care for her husband's physical needs (vs. 34). That works the same way.

The man may not be like the woman, but the man has material things about which he's always worrying about which women don't worry. A Christian wife can't understand why her man is so worried about bills and debts. She'll say, "Well, the Lord has always provided before." And the guy is around there grumbling and messing up the whole day. You can destroy the peace of your home [worrying ]{.class_s36M}about the bills. Then your wife is always saying, "Well, I think two can live cheaper than one. Money isn't important to me." She's thinking about the [fellowship ]{.class_s36M}and the emotional [state]{.class_s36M}. And you're thinking, "Well, that's fine, but the thirteenth day of this month that [bill ]{.class_s36M}is going to be there." So those material things will affect the man.

When a man gets married and has children (I don't care what you ladies do, how kind and sweet and how much you love them, and no matter how much you think he doesn't worry about it or think about it), if he's any kind of a [man]{.class_s36M}, he worries about his children; he worries about their shoes fitting and their clothes being clean and having enough warm clothes in the winter. [He worries about them. ]{.class_s36M}He worries about them getting sick and worries about paying the doctor bills and those kind of things. When he gets to worrying about those things, brother, those are the things of this world. They're not [spiritual]{.class_s36M}. They are [physical things.]{.class_s36M}

You can't beat that Bible with a stick, brother. When that Bible says it, that is it. What I read to you right there is just as inspired as John 3:16 and Revelation 22:20.

[Worry ]{.class_s36M}is sin, and that is why he says in verse 32, ["But I would have you without carefulness."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He says to be ["careful for nothing"]{.class_s36N} in Philippians 4:6. So he is saying that because you are going to be concerned about those things; he is admonishing you to take it with a grain of salt and commit it all to the Lord. And for all practical purposes, verse 35, it is better to serve the Lord without being distracted by those things.

7:36 But if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely toward his virgin, if she pass the flower of her age, and need so require, let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry.

37 Nevertheless he that standeth stedfast in his heart, having no necessity, but hath power over his own will, and hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin, doeth well.

38 So then he that giveth her in marriage doeth well; but he that giveth her not in marriage doeth better.

In this passage I am going to give a couple of standard interpretations and let you think about them, because I have never figured it out yet. But here's the first:

["But if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely]{.class_s36N} ([unsightly]{.class_s36S}, not properly) [toward his virgin]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}(his [own ]{.class_s36S}virginity)[,]{.class_s36N} [if she]{.class_s36N} ([the girl]{.class_s36S} to whom he is engaged or wants to marry) [pass the flower of her age, and need so require, let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}(let him marry the girl). ["Nevertheless he that standeth steadfast in his heart, having no necessity, but hath power over his own will, and hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}([his ]{.class_s36S}virginity) [doeth well."]{.class_s36N}

All right, the other one is this.

["But if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely toward his virgin]{.class_s36N} ([daughter]{.class_s36M}, his daughter who is unmarried)[,]{.class_s36N} [if she]{.class_s36N} (the [virgin ]{.class_s36M}daughter) [pass the flower of her age, and need so require]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(need to marry) [let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(let him give [her ]{.class_s36M}in marriage to some fellow). ["Nevertheless he that standeth steadfast in his heart, having no necessity, but hath power over his own will, and hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin]{.class_s36N} (keep his virgin daughter single; not let her marry)[ doeth well."]{.class_s36N}

Now, I'll show you a problem with those lines of thought.

Verse thirty-six, ["But if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely toward his virgin."]{.class_s36N} If it [is]{.class_s36M} his daughter, how is a man behaving himself [improperly ]{.class_s36M}toward his virgin daughter? In what sense? By not letting her [marry]{.class_s36M}? ["If she pass the flower of her age]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(that is, [past ]{.class_s36M}where she can have children)[,]{.class_s36N} [and need so require."]{.class_s36N} Require what? To get married? [Why get married when you are so old that you can't have children?]{.class_s36M} Why would a man still have control of a virgin daughter after she is a [grown woman]{.class_s36M}? Not even in the Old Testament is a woman under bondage to her father [unless she is still in his house]{.class_s36M}. If she has [left ]{.class_s36M}his house, she's not under his control at all, after she is grown.

There is something else with it. ["And need so require, let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Who is the ["them"]{.class_s36N}? There is no ["them"]{.class_s36N} mentioned here. ["Nevertheless he that standeth stedfast in his heart, having no necessity."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}No necessity to what? [To let her get married?]{.class_s36M} ["But hath power over his own will."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Why, you would have power over [your will ]{.class_s36M}whether you let her get married or not. If you decided to let her get married, that would be[ your will.]{.class_s36M} If you decided not to let her get married, [that ]{.class_s36M}would be [your ]{.class_s36M}will. There wouldn't be any point in saying ["power over his own will."]{.class_s36N} ["And hath so decreed]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}[in his heart that he will keep his virgin]{.class_s36N} (daughter) [doeth well."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}I'll leave you to wrestle with that.

7:39 The wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth; but if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will; only in the Lord.

40 But she is happier if she so abide, after my judgment: and I think also that I have the Spirit of God.

We have already commented on these verses in chapter 6. Note, for now, Paul warns against second marriages (vs. 40), even where there is legitimate grounds for remarriage (death, desertion, or fornication). But if you do get remarried, the remarriage is to be ["in the Lord."]{.class_s36N}

[]{#cV5.xhtml}

## Chapter 8 {.heading_s39}

8:1 Now as touching things offered unto idols, we know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge puffeth up, but charity edifieth.

2 And if any man think that he knoweth any thing, he knoweth nothing yet as he ought to know.

3 But if any man love God, the same is known of him.

Now Paul addresses a specific question that arose in the local church at Corinth. The question that came up here had to do with eating and drinking in regard to idolatry. While Paul deals with eating certain types of meat in general, in other places (Rom. 14; Col. 2:16, 20--23; 1 Tim. 4:3­--4), Corinth had a particular problem: should a Christian eat any meat offered to an idol?

Verse 1, ["Now as touching things offered unto idols." ]{.class_s36N}Again in verse 10, ["For if any man see thee which hast knowledge sit at meat in the idol's temple."]{.class_s36N}

In chapter ten, look at it come up again (10:27--28), ["If any of them that believe not bid you to a feast, and ye be disposed to go; whatsoever is set before you, eat, asking no question for conscience sake. But if any man say unto you, This is offered in sacrifice unto idols, eat not for his sake that shewed it, and for conscience sake."]{.class_s36N}

What happened was that a supplicant would bring an offering of a lamb, cow, or sheep to the pagan temple. There they would offer it up to an idol and dedicate it to a pagan god. Then the priest took his portion. After that, the remainder was sold, and the proceeds given to the temple. Of course, the meat was sold in the marketplace, and everyone knew it was meat "offered to an idol." So the question came up: should a Christian buy and eat meat that has been offered up to Jupiter, Saturn, Venus, and the rest of the crew? In this passage, Paul gives his judgment on it.

The first thing about this situation is ["that we all have knowledge."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That is, when a fellow gets saved he gets knowledge. ["The entrance of thy words giveth light" ]{.class_s36N}(Psa. 119:138).[ ]{.class_s36M}When you get saved you have a different view about idols than you had before. The problem with knowledge is that ["knowledge puffeth up."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}You have knowledge. So what? Just [knowledge ]{.class_s36M}won't do it.

That's going to be one of the dangers of coming to Pensacola Bible Institute. There will be some dangers that you won't have at this school, but the danger you [will ]{.class_s36M}have is that in a month here you will learn more Bible than the faculty at Bob Jones teaches in a year. That's going to be your problem. Knowing that much may tend to puff you with pride a little bit. You will feel a kind of sense of power and authority, which is all right. But just don't forget that ["knowledge puffeth up, but charity edifieth."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}So it's ["speaking the truth"]{.class_s36N} (that's the knowledge) ["in love"]{.class_s36N} (that's the charity; Eph. 4:15). Sometimes knowledge will run away with you.

We had a student here one time, and he was a brilliant kid. He got it all and got it quick. He got in a car with a fellow. The driver was giving him a ride someplace, and he had jazz music on the radio. The student reached over and turned the guy's radio off and said, "That stuff is of the devil!" and preached him a message. The fellow got mad at him and kicked him out of the car.

The student came to me and said, "Brother Ruckman, that guy said he was a Christian, but I don't think anybody could be saved and do that." Well, what he said wasn't right. A driver's car radio is his radio. It isn't yours. If you don't like the music, plug up your ears or get out of the car. You can be so sanctimonious and holy that you aren't worth a dime.

If you come to PBI, you are going to get knowledge here; I won't lie to you about it. If you learn what you are given here, you will have no trouble correcting the men that put out the next 10 translations of the Bible. You won't have any problem. As a matter of fact, you can correct them before they put it out. You will know what verses they are going to change.

When you get in PBI, and get that knowledge, you will be tempted to use it to get attention. Sometimes the Lord will take you and knock you down and mess you up and make you sick, give you bills, or trouble your wife or husband, or break down the car to keep you humble, so you won't be tempted to think you are too big or that you are such a big shot. If you get all that knowledge and then begin to use it for the flesh, the Lord will just have to knock you down.

A guy comes down from the pulpit, and you wave a King Jame's Bible in front of his face and say, "What do you mean changing this and what do you mean\.....blah, blah, blah?" Cool it. Cool it. Keep your mouth shut. You'll get a chance to use it sooner or later. But just make sure when your time comes to perform that you can perform.

Some of you try to perform when you are not quite ready to perform yet. A guy can pin your ears back when he asks you something you're not ready to handle.

The idea of verse 2 is that if a guy is bragging about his knowledge, then he doesn't really [know ]{.class_s36M}anything. If he knew something he wouldn't brag about what he knows.

["But if any man love God, the same is known of him"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 3). So love is the second thing which you need to deal with on the issue of idols and meats. If a man loves God, you can tell it, and you can know it. It has ways of showing through, whether the man has knowledge or not.

Before John Glenn went up for his earth orbit, he asked his Presbyterian preacher if he was in the will of God; and his Presbyterian preacher told him on February 20, 1962, "Yes, it is God's will to get knowledge of the universe." So Glenn said, "Good, I'm in the will of God." Too bad Glenn didn't ask his preacher for a chapter and verse that it is God's will for man to get knowledge of the universe by going up in a rocket. That would have been quite a verse to find.

But if you love God, then it is "manifest." If you love somebody you want to [please them.]{.class_s36M} If you love somebody you [think ]{.class_s36M}about them. If you love somebody you like to [talk about them]{.class_s36M}. If you love somebody you avoid their [enemies]{.class_s36M}. If you love somebody you love their [friends]{.class_s36M}. If you love somebody it is difficult for you to believe something [evil ]{.class_s36M}of them. If you love somebody you rejoice when they [succeed]{.class_s36M}, and you like to see them [promoted]{.class_s36M}. So if you love God you seek to please God, you think of God, you talk about God, you avoid God's enemies, you love God's friends, you don't believe evil reports concerning God, and you rejoice when you see God promoted. What we need is [dedication]{.class_s36M}, not education. The last thing we need in this age is a bunch of smart aleck Christians that think they are smart enough to correct the words of God.

Sometimes it is easy to tell if a fellow loves God or a fellow doesn't love God, and sometimes it is not so easy. It's easy when you see how they respond to a spiritual situation. Some of the people who love God are some of the most backslidden people in the world. There are some Christians who are the most dedicated, consecrated, and separated but who don't love the Lord at all. They are just proud of their consecration. They're proud of their dedication.

I've got a cartoon for a sermon called "Spiritual Pretenders," and it shows a guy hiding behind a mask and the Lord looking over the mask to see him. The guy has a mask with a big smile on it, and then behind the man is pasted all these alibis: "I tithe, I go to church, we're running 2,000 in Sunday School." Those are the things that Christians put up before the Lord to try to prove they're spiritual. If you are spiritual, you love God, and if you're not spiritual, then you don't love God. And you don't have to quote a bunch of stuff to prove it. If it is there, it is [known]{.class_s36M}.

8:4 As concerning therefore the eating of those things that are offered in sacrifice unto idols, we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is none other God but one.

5 For though there be that are called gods, whether in heaven or in earth, (as there be gods many, and lords many,)

6 But to us there is but one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we in him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things, and we by him.

So if you love God and have knowledge, then the following things are apparent: First, verse 4, ["An idol is nothing in the world."]{.class_s36N} It's nothing but a hunk of wood, stone, or metal.

Second, verse 5, ["there be gods many, and lords many."]{.class_s36N} Satan is one of them (2 Cor. 4:4--["the god of this world"]{.class_s36N}). Psalm 82:1, 6 tells about some more. Ephesians 6:12 lists ["principalities"]{.class_s36N} and ["powers."]{.class_s36N}
