Finally, verse 6, ["to us there is but one God."]{.class_s36N} Those other lords and gods are nothing to us who are ["in"]{.class_s36N} the Father ["by"]{.class_s36N} Jesus Christ. So although we know about devils, angels, and spiritual rulers of various levels of authority, they mean nothing to us because we know their Master on a personal basis: we are "one" with Him.

Not every man knows there is only one, true God and that the rest of them are counterfeits and Satanic (vs. 7). And though the idol is nothing real, there are some people, even some Christians, whose conscience bothers them about the matter. They remember the old, pagan practise; they remember the "Hail Mary's" and to whom the meat was offered. When that happens, they pull back and won't eat because of a weak conscience.

Paul summarized the problem in Romans 14:23, ["And he that doubteth is damned if he eat, because he eateth not of faith: for whatsoever is not of faith is sin." ]{.class_s36N}The damnation there is not Hell, but a defiled conscience, as in 1 Corinthians 8:7.

The fact of the matter is (vs. 8) that eating meat does nothing for your relationship with God, one way or another. It doesn't "recommend" you to God at all.

8:9 But take heed lest by any means this liberty of yours become a stumblingblock to them that are weak.

10 For if any man see thee which hast knowledge sit at meat in the idol's temple, shall not the conscience of him which is weak be emboldened to eat those things which are offered to idols;

11 And through thy knowledge shall the weak brother perish, for whom Christ died?

12 But when ye sin so against the brethren, and wound their weak conscience, ye sin against Christ.

13 Wherefore, if meat make my brother to offend, I will eat no flesh while the world standeth, lest I make my brother to offend.

There are some Christians who wouldn't be bothered at all by this. Some guy would offer up a pork chop to Jupiter and then take it out there to sell it to some Christian who would eat it and enjoy it. It doesn't bother him a bit. I'm that way. It wouldn't bother me a bit. It wouldn't keep me awake for five minutes.

But a Christian is not to take advantage of a weak brother with his liberty (vs. 9).[ ]{.class_s36M}That is the whole gist of Romans 14:1--13. The Christian has liberty to do certain things according to his own conscience, but he has to be careful lest his liberty stumbles a Christian who is not strong---a Christian who is weak.

If you go out to eat on a Friday with a Catholic and you are trying to win him to the Lord, when you go through the cafeteria line, if that Catholic gets mullet and flounder, then you should get some mullet and flounder. I mean, what's the point? You could just as well eat pork or steak, but what's the point? If you are trying to win the guy, why let your liberty cause him to stumble? His conscience would bother him. Your conscience wouldn't bother [you]{.class_s36M}. But if you are trying to reach [him]{.class_s36M}, then it's 1 Corinthians 10:29, ["Conscience, I say, not thine own, but of the other."]{.class_s36N} Remember the other fellow's conscience.

If you are trying to win a Jew to the Lord, don't sit down and order [pork]{.class_s36S}. Why blow it, man, if you've got a good testimony with the guy? Why blow the whole thing and make a mess of things when you could win him to Christ?

Brethren, don't use your knowledge and liberty (vs. 10) to cause a weaker brother to sin (vs. 11) or an unsaved person to reject Christ. If some Christian there, in Corinth, had gone down to the table where they brought meat recently offered to idols, some guy with a weak conscience would see that and think that reverencing the idol was all right. Of course, the Christian eating the meat wasn't thinking about that at all. He was saying to himself, "To blazes with the idol," as he ate the stuff. Meanwhile, the "weak brother," who didn't have knowledge, was violating his conscience; sinning because he had no faith to do what he was doing and, in his mind, he was still reverencing the idol because he thought that was what the other fellow was doing.

That is what verse 11 meant when Paul used the word ["perish."]{.class_s36N} He was not saying the weak brother was going to Hell, like the word is used in John 3:16. ["Perish"]{.class_s36N} doesn't always mean that any more than ["saved"]{.class_s36N} always means going to Heaven (see comments on 1 Cor. 5:5).

So if the "weaker brother" can't do something without violating his conscience, then he needs to stop it. That is the rule for "doubtful things."

You've heard me say this before, and I'm going to say it again until the Lord shuts my mouth. The only standards we have in our catalog, for our students here at PBI, are: no smoking, no drinking, but loyalty to the government, and that is where it ends. Some schools have a big list: "Don't look cross eyed at a girl," "Don't touch anybody's hand going to the Post Office," "Put your right hand on the right side of the table and your left hand on the left side of the table," and all that business. The reason I don't do that is because I have found out that you cannot [legislate morals.]{.class_s36M} People are people. [You can't regiment human nature.]{.class_s36M} Human nature is a mess. Consequently, you have to count on the preached word of God (that's spiritual power) and prayer (that's spiritual power) to keep a congregation or a school right.

If that Book can't keep you right, nothing can keep you right. If you won't listen to God, you won't listen to me. And if you're not going to listen to God, I'm not going to do anything with you anyhow. So for that reason, I don't lay any hard, fast lines down on a student's private life. I don't interfere with them.

I've often been criticized for that. Somebody says, "Well, he's too loose. He lets them get away with too much." But I'll tell you what I believe. I believe that you, yourself, as an individual have to deal with God, eventually, and live your own life. And eventually, you and God are going to have to "have things out"; and you are going to have to die by yourself, and there isn't anybody in this building that can really step into anybody's shoes and do anything.

Now, maybe I get that because most of my life I have been alone. I don't know how many hours it has been just me in the room, or me on the beach, or me in the woods, or me in the slit trench, or me on the mountain, or me in a river, or me on the plane. There has just been [me]{.class_s36M}. That's all. And I have had to deal with God directly, and I have learned in the final analysis that, eventually, [you ]{.class_s36M}are going to have to settle everything with God.

You can't run forever. Your wife will be some help to you. Your husband will be some help to you. You children will help for a while, and your preacher may help. But eventually, it is going to be you and God, and you are going to "have it out." Because of that, when you get these doubtful things about what a Christian should and shouldn't do, I don't make up a great pile of dogmatic statements on them.

I give a student the principals by which to go, but I will be frank with you: What is right for some of you would not be right for me, and what is right for me would not be right for some of you. You can fight that thing until you are blue in the face and say, "What does the Bible say?" Yeah, I know what the Bible says. But as God is my witness, some of you folks could take the day off and go to a ballgame and enjoy yourself, and the Lord would be in it, but He wouldn't be in me doing it. That's it. Some of you fellows overwork. Some of you fellows need to just knock off and just do nothing for a day or two.

Thomas Edison's wife said, "Take a two week vacation." He said, "Where would I go?" She said, "Well, go to the place you have always wanted to go more than anyplace else." He went right back into his laboratory.

You can't legislate those kind of things.

It might be a sin for some of you to buy a ball ticket and go to New Orleans and sit there and watch the Saints play a game for three hours. For me it would be a sin. It would almost be the unpardonable sin. I could not sit in a place three hours and just watch a bunch of monkeys run up and down there. I watch those bowl games with my kids, and I can sit through about a quarter. By half time I'm walking around the room and back to get something to eat and back to check the tape and back to check the book.

To me the time is precious, and I am told to redeem the time, for ["the days are evil."]{.class_s36N} You say, "We are told the same thing, too." Yeah, but a lot of you have more time than I've got. You say, "We don't know how much time we've got." I know you don't know that, but still, you've got a head start. Many of you reading this page can find Luke in the New Testament. I guarantee you that I couldn't find it when I was twenty-six years old. Some of you aren't twenty-six yet.

When you get into these doubtful things, some of you fellows are going to find that, when you get up to preach, you can say things and God will honor them and use them that He will not honor when another preacher uses them. I've heard Bob Jones Sr. say some things that I have tried to copy, and some of those things, when I said them, just fell flatter than a flounder. By the same token, I have gotten up and said some things that Sam Jones said, and it sounded like it came out of my mouth. You cannot say everything that Pete Ruckman says and get away with it; and the Lord won't bless it.

You will get mad sometime, and might get up in the pulpit and say, "You bunch of dumb thumps. You haven't got the sense that God gave a brass monkey!" Instead of sitting there and laughing at you, which people will do when I say it, they'll sit there and frown and then blow up.

I mean, people are different. Eventually, you and the Lord have to deal with the thing. You and the Lord have to "have it out."

When it comes to doubtful things, you may have a right to do something, but it might be a sin (vs. 12). That is that stuff like smoking. What's wrong with smoking? "Well, to ['the pure all things are pure.']{.class_s36N} So I can smoke if I have a clear conscience." Well, I doubt that. But if you can, what am I going to say? Am I going to say you can't?

A fellow in the Carolinas says, "Well, my grandma smoked a corncob pipe for years, and she knew the Bible from cover to cover. She was as fine a Christian as you ever saw." What are you going to say? Maybe she didn't have any conscience about it. I don't know. You can't say who had the conscience and who didn't have the conscience. All you have got to know is your own conscience.

But when you do something like that, you [sin ]{.class_s36M}by "wounding the weak conscience" of a brother in Christ. They've got a bad conscience. You don't, but still you've sinned against Christ.

Don't sit down on the curb and drink water out of a Four Roses bottle. Is there anything wrong with drinking [water?]{.class_s36M} Is there anything wrong with drinking water [from a bottle?]{.class_s36M} Is a Four Roses bottle made out of a different kind of glass than any other bottles? No, but the problem is [the other guy's conscience.]{.class_s36M} A guy goes by and sees you and your bottle and he says, "Uh, huh, drinking whiskey!" You're [not ]{.class_s36M}drinking whiskey, but it does just as much damage.

So, how does Paul conclude this discussion (vs. 13)? Well, again, the question is not whether it is all right to do. The question is, "Will it hurt my brother in Christ?" If the answer is "Yes," then forego the questionable thing (vs. 13).

[]{#cX7.xhtml}

## Chapter 9 {.heading_s39}

9:1 Am I not an apostle? am I not free? have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord? are not ye my work in the Lord?

2 If I be not an apostle unto others, yet doubtless I am to you: for the seal of mine apostleship are ye in the Lord.

3 Mine answer to them that do examine me is this,

4 Have we not power to eat and to drink?

5 Have we not power to lead about a sister, a wife, as well as other apostles, and as the brethren of the Lord, and Cephas?

6 Or I only and Barnabas, have not we power to forbear working?

In chapter 9, Paul is trying to show that, although he has a right to do certain things, he is [not]{.class_s36M} doing them because of added blessing in not doing them and because of his testimony to the Corinthians. This is the "law of liberty" in Christ. In his own ministry as an apostle, he shows the Corinthians that he forgoes the things he has a right to do, for their sakes. He does that so that those Corinthians who feel they have the right to eat meat offered to idols (see chap. 8) will forego it for the sake of the weaker brother. Now Paul is using himself as an illustration of that principle.

The whole chapter is on the ministry. Paul is talking about how he conducts himself in the ministry. He gives up things and denies himself things in order to reach others. Although he has a right to do anything that anybody else does, he doesn't take advantage of those rights. That is the gist of the whole chapter.

Now, in verse 1, Paul lays the foundation for this argument. ["Am I not an apostle?"]{.class_s36N} Yes, he was. He was ["free,"]{.class_s36N} in the sense of verse 19; that is, he was not a slave to any man or government (Acts 22:28). But not only was he ["free"]{.class_s36N} physically, he was free spiritually. He tells the Romans, ["The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free from the law of sin and death"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:2). In other words, the reason he abstained from questionable things was [not]{.class_s36M} because someone was [making]{.class_s36M} him do it. It was a free choice on his part.

He goes on and gives his credentials as an apostle: he had seen the risen Christ and had been called by him to the ministry (Acts 26:16). The Corinthians believers were the proof of that ministry (vs. 2); they had first received the gospel from him (Acts 18:1--11) and had witnessed the signs and wonders that had accompanied his work as an apostle.

Nevertheless, there were those in the Corinthian church who questioned Paul (vs. 3). They questioned his apostleship, they questioned his actions, and they questioned his motives. (If you are called to the ministry, you'll experience that.)

Paul answered those who questioned him by listing the privileges he had as an apostle. He had the liberty to eat and drink what he wanted and with whom he wanted (vs. 4, cf. Gal. 2:12). He could travel in his missionary journeys with a wife (vs. 5).

There are two important things to note in verse 5. First, a man's wife is called his "sister." That is because in the [first marriage,]{.class_s36M} the man's wife [was]{.class_s36M} his sister. Adam and Eve both had the [same father]{.class_s36M}---God. The first husband and wife were [brother and sister.]{.class_s36M} This is true of a Christian marriage. If a Christian man is married to a Christian woman as he should be (see chap. 7), then they both have the same Father, spiritually. The two are brother and sister [in Christ]{.class_s36M} (1 Pet. 3:7).

Next, note that other apostles were married. "Cephas" is another name for Simon Peter. Peter was married and had a mother-in-law (Matt. 8:14; Mark 1:30). That is quite something for a "pope," don't you think?

The final privilege that Paul lists is that he did not need to work (vs. 6). Now, he did; he had worked with Aquila and Priscilla, in Corinth, as a tent maker (Acts 18:1--2). But he could have quit.

9:7 Who goeth a warfare any time at his own charges? who planteth a vineyard, and eateth not of the fruit thereof? or who feedeth a flock, and eateth not of the milk of the flock?

8 Say I these things as a man? or saith not the law the same also?

9 For it is written in the law of Moses, Thou shalt not muzzle the mouth of the ox that treadeth out the corn. Doth God take care for oxen?

10 Or saith he it altogether for our sakes? For our sakes, no doubt, this is written: that he that ploweth should plow in hope; and that he that thresheth in hope should be partaker of his hope.

11 If we have sown unto you spiritual things, is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things?

12 If others be partakers of this power over you, are not we rather? Nevertheless we have not used this power; but suffer all things, lest we should hinder the gospel of Christ.

13 Do ye not know that they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple? and they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar?

14 Even so hath the Lord ordained that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel.

15 But I have used none of these things: neither have I written these things, that it should be so done unto me: for it were better for me to die, than that any man should make my glorying void.

16 For though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of: for necessity is laid upon me; yea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel!

17 For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward: but if against my will, a dispensation of the gospel is committed unto me.

18 What is my reward then? Verily that, when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge, that I abuse not my power in the gospel.

Here, Paul expounds on the question he asked in verse 6, ["have not we power to forbear working?"]{.class_s36N}. The answer, of course, is "yes." Why? Well, if you join the Army during time of war, do you have to "foot the bill" (vs. 7)? Of course not. Can't you eat grapes from your own vineyard (vs. 7)? Sure you can. You are entitled to it if you planted it, pruned it, and took care of it. And can't you drink the milk from your own flock that you fed and watered (vs. 7)? And anyone can see that the answer is "yes."

These are three pictures of the ministry. It is like a soldier going to battle (2 Tim. 2:3--4), it is like a husbandman planting a vineyard (2 Tim. 2:6), and it is like a shepherd tending a flock (1 Pet. 5:1--4). In the ministry, you have a right to live off the ministry, just as a soldier is paid by the government to fight. A farmer makes a living off his crop, and a shepherd lives off his flock. There is nothing wrong with a preacher getting paid for his ministry.

That is only common sense that any man can understand (vs. 8), but if that were not enough, Paul invokes the law: ["saith not the law the same also."]{.class_s36N} The verse he quotes is Deuteronomy 25:4, ["Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out the corn."]{.class_s36N} Paul adds in verse 9, ["Doth God take for oxen?"]{.class_s36N} Yes, He does. The Bible says, ["A righteous man regardeth the life of his beast: but the tender mercies of the wicked are cruel"]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 12:10). God made provision for that ox so that when he was going around threshing the grain, he could bend his head down from time to time and get himself a bite to eat. Therefore, the farmer was not to muzzle him.

Now, if God treats an animal that way, won't He treat His own servants better? Verse 10:[ "Or saith he it altogether for our sakes? For our sakes, no doubt, this is written."]{.class_s36N} The New Testament application of Deuteronomy 25:4 is to ["the elders that rule well\...who labour in the word and doctrine"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 5:17).

The conclusion Paul draws from the Old Testament rule is: ["The labourer is worthy of his reward"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 5:18). Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "A minister is entitled to anything God gives him along the line of God's purpose for his life." So just make sure that whatever you do is done for God and His glory, with the right motive, and be thankful for whatever the Lord brings your way.

Back to verse 10:[ "he that ploweth\...he that thresheth."]{.class_s36N} That has to do with the ministry. Much of your ministry is going to be plowing and threshing. I hope you harvest a lot; but a lot of it is going to be planting, a lot of it is going to be plowing, and a lot of it is going to be threshing.

Every summer God gives me a harvest in the prisons with Bro. Peacock over in Jacksonville. This last year (2001), we saw 160 saved, most of them men.

Now, there's a bit of [reaping]{.class_s36M}. But I've been in many meetings where all I did was plow and plow and plow until my hands ached and my feet were sore and I couldn't even see the strip.

You won't understand what I am saying until you get out in the ministry, but when you get out in the ministry, you will see exactly what I am tell you. You pray for the Lord to give you some souls, and there are no souls. You pray for the Lord to "get them right," and they don't get right. You pray for the Lord to give you some results, and you don't get results. You pray, and you pound, and you holler, and you yell, and you get through and go back to that motel room, and you feel like you have been digging ditches five hours. I have mowed my yard (an acre) and then plowed my garden (a half of an acre) with a rotary tiller twice and not been as tired as I have been sometimes on Saturday night after one of those meetings. That's the dying truth. It just drains you.

But he says if you plow you are to ["plow in hope."]{.class_s36N} So while you're plowing, every once in a while the Lord will let some ["handfuls of purpose"]{.class_s36N} drop your way. You'll get one here and one there, and it will keep you going on.

Threshing (vs. 10) is another big part of the ministry. It is even worse than the plowing. The plowing is before you plant, so the plowing has to do with the unsaved; but the threshing is the people that have already been harvested. The threshing is knocking the chaff and the dust off the real grain so the grain can come through. That threshing machine is a thrasher. That's for [Christians]{.class_s36M}. So the plowing, tilling, harrowing and cultivating is before you plant. Then you plant. Then the praying is watering the crop and fertilizing the crop. Then when it comes up, you pick it, and that is the harvest. (You also have to spray it for bugs while it is growing.) Then after you pick that stuff you have to thrash it and beat it so the good grain comes through.[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, boys, that's it. Many a ministry in this country is just sanctification: trying to knock the chaff and dirt of this world off the saints.

So the ministry is sowing ["spiritual things"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). In light of that, Paul asks, "[Is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things?"]{.class_s36N}

And the implied answer is "No." In plainer words, when a man gets out in the ministry and gets to ministering the word of God to people and ministering to them [spiritual things]{.class_s36M}, it is their duty to take care of his food and clothing. That Bible says, ["They which preach the gospel should live of the gospel"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14).[ ]{.class_s36M}If they don't pay the preacher, their sin will find [them ]{.class_s36M}out.

If a man sows ["spiritual things,"]{.class_s36N} he is entitled to ["carnal things."]{.class_s36N} When a fellow teaches and preaches the word of God and prays for his congregation, he is entitled to support. If you get out there and begin to minister to people and visiting them, and going around and taking care of them, and counseling them, knocking on doors and helping out with their problems, and preaching and teaching the word of God to them, it is [their job]{.class_s36M} to see that [your bills]{.class_s36M} are paid. That is their job. If they're not going to do it, don't fool with them.

["If others be partakers of this power over you, are not we rather? Nevertheless we have not used this power; but suffer all things, lest we should hinder the gospel of Christ" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 12).[ ]{.class_s36M}He is saying, "You have paid others wages to minister to you, and in my ministry I haven't taken anything from you, even though I had power to do it."

["Do ye not know that they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple?"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13).[ ]{.class_s36M}That's true. In the Old Testament if a Jew brought his sacrifice into the temple, the fellows who waited on the Jews, the Levites, ate what was offered. So much would belong to the priest and so much to somebody else, and when they brought their offering the priests ate it. ["And they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}So they ate the sheep, lamb, and ox that were sacrificed at the altar.

So, based upon a Scriptural principle found in the Old Testament, God ordains the New Testament rule: ["They which preach the gospel should live of the gospel"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14). That is how the Lord wants it.

The exception to the rule is found in verse 15. Paul did not use this rule on the Corinthians. He put no charge on them and did not ask for any support. Why? ["For it were better for me to die, than that any man should make my glorying void."]{.class_s36N} Paul gloried in the fact that he ministered freely to the Corinthians.

Paul's "glory" was not in the preaching of the gospel. He described that as a ["necessity"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16). He had no choice about it.

Rather, the glory for Paul came from preaching the gospel ["willingly"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 17). This brought ["a reward."]{.class_s36N}

Now, the ["dispensation"]{.class_s36N} of verse 17 is not a [period of time. ]{.class_s36M}Get that straight! The hyper-dispensationalists never could. There is no [time period]{.class_s36M} called ["the gospel"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 17). There is no[ time period]{.class_s36M} of grace (Eph. 3:2). Grace, [as a period of time]{.class_s36M}, stretches from Genesis 6:8 to Revelation 22:21. There is no[ time period]{.class_s36M} called ["God"]{.class_s36N} (Col. 1:25). The Lord messes up the hyper-dispensationalists with a two-letter word (["of"]{.class_s36N}) just like He messed up the Campbellites with a three-leter one (["for"]{.class_s36N}---Acts 2:38).

In the Bible, a dispensation is where God gives something to someone to take care of.

The Lord gave Paul the gospel and entrusted him with it. It was committed to him, and he had to do something with it. The question was: would he preach the gospel ["willingly"]{.class_s36N} or "against his will"?

Simon Peter says, ["Not by constraint, but willingly"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 5:2). You have a right to expect a reward for doing what God told you to do, although the Lord may fool you sometime and send it some way that you don't expect it, or may delay it to test you. You can't expect to fork over five and then say, "OK, next week I get back ten." But I have seen it work that way. I've seen some strange things. I have turned loose of \$1,000 and had it come back in the mail in a week.

["What is my reward then?" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 18). If Paul didn't get any money from them for doing it, then what did he get out of it? ["Verily that, when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge, that I abuse not my power in the gospel."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He is saying that the wages he gets is [the joy of not receiving a wage.]{.class_s36M} That's what he is saying. You can get a [blessing ]{.class_s36M}by knowing that you did it[ free.]{.class_s36M}

9:19 For though I be free from all men, yet have I made myself servant unto all, that I might gain the more.

20 And unto the Jews I became as a Jew, that I might gain the Jews; to them that are under the law, as under the law, that I might gain them that are under the law;

21 To them that are without law, as without law, (being not without law to God, but under the law to Christ,) that I might gain them that are without law.

22 To the weak became I as weak, that I might gain the weak: I am made all things to all men, that I might by all means save some.

23 And this I do for the gospel's sake, that I might be partaker thereof with you.

24 Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize? So run, that ye may obtain.

25 And every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things. Now they do it to obtain a corruptible crown; but we an incorruptible.

26 I therefore so run, not as uncertainly; so fight I, not as one that beateth the air:

27 But I keep under my body, and bring it into subjection: lest that by any means, when I have preached to others, I myself should be a castaway.

As we have already commented in verse 1, Paul was ["free"]{.class_s36N} both physically [and ]{.class_s36M}spiritually. Nevertheless, concerning the gospel, he made himself a slave to both Jews and Gentiles (vs. 19). He said in Romans 1:14, ["I am debtor both to the Greeks, and to the Barbarians."]{.class_s36N} The ["gain"]{.class_s36N} that he "got" from this arrangement was more ["fruit"]{.class_s36N} to his account (Rom. 1:13; John 15:1--8). Even as Christ came ["not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 20:28), even so Paul made himself a slave (spiritually) to reach all men for Jesus Christ.

Paul's common practise, when he entered a city, was to go ["to the Jew first"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 1:16). Paul, as a Jew, would go into the synagogue and preach to his kinsmen (Acts 13:5; 14--44; 14:1; 17:1--2, 10, 17; 18:4). He wanted to see the Jews saved so badly (Rom. 10:1) that he once consented to go to the temple and purify himself and shave his head to show the Jews that he kept the Law (Acts 21:20--24). ["Unto the Jews I became as a Jew, that I might gain the Jews; to them that are under the law, as under the law, that I might gain them that are under the law"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 20).

But when Paul went to the Gentiles, he did not act as a Jew (vs. 21). This is what caused Paul to rebuke Peter in Galatians 2. Peter was willing to "practise" what Paul "preached" in verse 21 up until the point where other Jews saw him. Fearing their criticism, he backed off and became a Jew once more in front of his Gentile brothers in Christ. Paul's rebuke to Peter for not walking ["uprightly according to the truth of the gospel"]{.class_s36N} was ["If thou, being a Jew, livest after the manner of Gentiles, and not as do the Jews, why compellest thou the Gentiles to live as do the Jews"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 2:14).

Paul had no qualms whatsoever to go to the Gentiles, in front of the Jews, when the Jews had rejected the gospel (Acts 13:42--48, 18:4--6, 28:17--31). He laid no claims of the Law upon his Gentile converts (Acts 15:1--2), except those stipulations laid down by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem (Acts 15:19--22, 21:25).

That is what he is saying in verse 21: ["To them that are without law, as without law, (being not without law to God, but under the law to Christ,) that I might gain them that are without law."]{.class_s36N}

Then there is a great passage in verse 22 that you should memorize: ["I am made all things to all men, that I might by all means save some."]{.class_s36N} Calvin didn't care for that verse a bit. Paul is taking credit for [saving people:]{.class_s36M} ["that I might by all means save some."]{.class_s36N} The abuse and misuse of that verse are done by people like Billy Graham and Dave Wilkerson and Kathryn Kuhlman and Bill Gothard who carry it to extremes and use that verse as an alibi for [compromising ]{.class_s36M}in order to "reach" men.

The truth that you want to see in that is the difference between Billy Sunday and Billy Graham. Billy Sunday had modernists cooperate in the campaigns, just like Billy Graham; Billy Sunday had the liberals on the platform during the meetings, just like Billy Graham; the modernists churches were represented in his meetings, just like in Billy Graham's. But the difference is that Billy Sunday would never ask them to come down and lead in prayer; he would not even recognize them, and what is worse, in the middle of the message, Billy Sunday would turn around and preach at them.

Billy Graham keeps saying, "I want to reach these fellows." Billy Sunday would "reach them." Sunday would turn around in the middle of the message and point his finger at them and say, "I want to tell you bunch of preachers, if you're not born again, you are going to Hell, and you are going to burn. You're going to burn." Boy, there would be some coughing and spitting around up there! That's how he "reached" them.

The "hippy version" is that you can't reach the hippies unless you [join them.]{.class_s36M} So the hippy version is, this girl comes down the street in a Budweiser beer shirt and a cigarette in her hand and her hair down the middle of her back; she is swaggering down the sidewalk passing out tracts and giving people Bible verses. Well, it doesn't work. You may think it's working, but it is not. They're sizing you up and saying, "Well, she's just like one of us." That does not [win ]{.class_s36M}them.

The world's philosophy is [compromise]{.class_s36M}. If you can't whip them,[ join them.]{.class_s36M} That's the world's philosophy. They will be very angry at some of you, and they will say you're not winning anybody to Christ and you're driving them from Christ by the way you witness. The world can't understand that. The answer to that is there is hardly anybody who is ever won to Christ that isn't [repulsed ]{.class_s36M}first. When a man is asleep you have to wake him up, and you can't wake him up unless you [kick him. ]{.class_s36M}Nobody likes to be awakened out of a sound sleep by somebody [kicking them.]{.class_s36M} So you have to give them the initial [kick.]{.class_s36M}

In applying the verse, you can make any adjustment that is legitimate. In plainer words, if you are out with a Catholic on Friday, order fish. It won't kill you. If you're dealing with a Jew, don't order a pork sandwich or barbecue pork. If you're dealing with somebody of some class and culture, act like a gentleman.

You say, "Well, you're a fine one to be talking." Well, I've had to give lectures to some of my students on manners. I really have. I've had to lecture them on how to deal with people and how to talk with people.

["And this I do for the gospel's sake, that I might be partaker thereof with you"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 23). Paul wanted to be a partaker of the blessings from the gospel. So for this cause, Paul gave up his right, as a minister, to live off his converts. He looked for a greater reward, and that comes up in the next few verses.

Now, here is a great, great passage for Christian service.

["Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize?]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}[So run, that ye may obtain"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 24).

This passage is one of three where Paul likens the Christian life to a [race ]{.class_s36M}(cf. Phil. 3:13--14, Heb. 12:1--2). As in the Olympic races, only one man gets the prize. In the Christian life, a believer is to run to win, to break the tape. You are not to run merely to place or show, [but to win.]{.class_s36M} That is your goal.

The trouble with Christians today (among other things), in regards to the crucified life and self-denial, is that some of them are not running to win. Now, I've never been a trackman. I'm too short and too slow. I was too short for basketball and too little for football back in high school. I swam in college: the Big Ten. I don't know about track, but I know about the swim meets. But I'm sure that in track there are rules about staying in your own lane. I'm certain of it. In swimming, if you get out of your lane, you're disqualified. Your hand may bump into that cork and that will slow you down, but if you get out of your lane, you are out of the race. Christians are always getting in someone else's lane. You have [your lane,]{.class_s36M} and it stretches out in front of [you]{.class_s36M}, and you are ["looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith."]{.class_s36N}

I notice those guys that jump the hurdles never look down at the hurdles. Those guys run and look out in front of them. They look at the hurdle before they get to it, but as they approach it their eyes are up over the hurdle. They do not run up there and look down at the hurdle to see if they are going to get their legs over.

The Bible says, ["looking unto Jesus."]{.class_s36N}

The moral is when you are running to win the race you are not to look at the obstacles. That's the problem we have. If you would just keep your eyes on Jesus, you wouldn't have any problem. Peter got out there in the water and started looking at the wind and the waves, and down he went. He was looking at the obstacles. The Bible says, ["He that observeth the wind shall not sow; and he that regardeth the clouds shall not reap,"]{.class_s36N} Ecclesiastes 11:4.

When you get taken up with the obstacles, then you will trip on one of those things and fall down in the track.

There are more rules. You don't start before the gun. [You have a false start. ]{.class_s36M}It happens in swimming. You get up there, and the guy says, "Swimmers, take your marks." Then he says, "Get set," and when he fires the gun you take off. Many times the swimmers dive before the gun is fired, and they have to come back and start over. They allow two false starts, and then they are out of the race---disqualified.

This whole passage is talking about being disqualified from the race. You have your track, and I've got my track. I've got to keep my eyes on the Lord, and you've got to keep your eyes on the Lord. Don't ever get to be an expert in [other people's affairs. ]{.class_s36M}Don't ever do that. When dealing with the brethren, I don't even bother to find out what they are doing, except when they mess with the word of God. If they mess with the word of God, I'll mess with them. If they step on that word, I'll say something about it. But what John R. Rice does in his private life is no concern of mine. I could care less. What Bob Harrington does in his private life, I could care less. The Bible says, ["to his own master he standeth or falleth."]{.class_s36N} He has got his track, and I have got my track.

In swimming, there is a line of blue tile on the bottom of the pool on which to keep your eyes so you will stay in your lane. That is a picture of keeping your eyes on the Lord---blue. That Jew had to have a [blue hem ]{.class_s36M}right around the bottom of his garment.

There isn't any time to "grandstand" at all. I don't know how it is in track, but I've watched those Olympic runners. They didn't look to the right or to the left. They didn't look behind them. If anybody cheers them, they didn't look at them. The runner didn't do anything until he got through. When he got through then he began to wave. A lot of Christians start running the track, and they start looking at the grandstand. "Hey, look at me!" You'll break your fool neck. Some guy will go by you.

In the Christian race, you run to win. You don't run to "place" or to "show." You run to win. A half-hearted effort is not what the Bible calls for. Some of you athletes know about that, and some of you don't. I'm a believer in athletics. I think it is good for a young man to participate in them.

When I would swim, I would swim to win. I've lost some of them, and I've won some of them. I've finished races where I didn't win, and I was so tired I couldn't get out of the pool. I haven't seen that in these Olympic games for a good while.

I have been in races when I was sick, so sick I would vomit in the water. You say, "What is the moral there?" Well, you go to [win]{.class_s36M}. You go to win, sick or well or whatever. You get in there to [win it.]{.class_s36M}

["And every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 25). In swimming and track and boxing, there are two things that you do not do. You do not drink alcoholic beverages, and you do not smoke. There is no way you can do it. There is no way you can box and smoke: no way in the world. You will hop around that ring, shifting and dancing, and by the time you have danced out there for ten rounds, your knees feel like rubber stilts.

In swimming, after you have gone the length of that pool and back as fast as you can, your heart is pounding, and you don't have any lungs left. If you are fooling with tobacco, you might as well stay out of the water.

I'm sure that runners do not smoke. They used to have famous runners recommend Chesterfield and Camel on the back of these magazines, [after ]{.class_s36M}they quit sports. I see ads now of a bunch of fellows sitting around a room drinking beer. You better hadn't drink much beer in pro football. You're not going to run down there and take a ball out of the air, man, if you are smoking. I'll bet pro basketball players do not smoke. You are not going to run down that court and back and run down that court and back and smoke cigarettes. So one of the best reasons for quitting smoking for the Christian is 1 Corinthians 9:25, ["every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate."]{.class_s36N} If you are going to master the thing, you have to be [temperate ]{.class_s36M}in your habits.

Now, what is the reward for being[ "temperate in all things"]{.class_s36N}?[ ]{.class_s36M}Well, an unsaved athlete of Paul's day ran for a laurel wreath, ["a corruptible crown."]{.class_s36N} But in the Christian race, you receive an incorruptible crown. Peter calls it ["an inheritance incorruptible, and undefiled, and that fadeth not away, reserved in heaven for you" ]{.class_s36N}(1 Pet. 1:4).

So there is an "incorruptible crown" for the Christian who keeps his body in subjection and[ keeps it under]{.class_s36M} and stays in training condition as an athlete.

Did you ever see "grass drills" in the summer for football players? Sometimes they puke or faint. If an athlete trains like that, how much more should a Christian?

Let's go to 2 Timothy for some more on this. The Christian is in training. He should be like a [trained athlete]{.class_s36M}. He shouldn't oversleep; he shouldn't under­sleep. He shouldn't overeat; he shouldn't undereat. You should be in training. Second Timothy 2:3, ["Thou therefore endure hardness, as a good soldier of Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, here's our verse. ["And if a man also strive for masteries, yet is he not crowned, except he strive lawfully"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 2:5). It matches 1 Corinthians 9:25.

In the locker room of one of the NHL teams a sign says, "If you don't win fair, you don't win at all." That's the old philosophy. The [new ]{.class_s36M}philosophy is, "Win at any cost." The new philosophy put out by all these pros is winning the thing, win, win, win. Well, the Bible says run to [win]{.class_s36M}, but it also says if you don't win [lawfully ]{.class_s36M}you don't get [crowned]{.class_s36M}. So when he says, ["So run, that ye may obtain,"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}that doesn't mean anything goes to win. It means you keep the rules to win or you [don't win.]{.class_s36M}

In God's league if you don't win fair, you don't win at all. That means if you are going to run the Christian life and do what God wants you to do, you can't say, "OK, ['I am made all things to all men, that I might by all means save some.']{.class_s36N} So I'll win all the souls that I can to Christ any way I can." You won't get crowned. If a man does what is [wrong ]{.class_s36M}to win souls, he may get the soul, but he won't get [the crown.]{.class_s36M} You want to keep that in mind, because that is applicable to today's evangelism.

Somebody said, "Well, it's the number you reach. Wouldn't you rather preach to 10,000 than 200?" Well, sure you would. Whose ego doesn't like to be flattered? But what has that got to do with being [crowned?]{.class_s36M} One of the reasons we have so many puny conversions is because the winning was done with a rotten method. The real soulwinning does not begin with John 3:16.

For an example on how [not ]{.class_s36M}to win people to Christ, I suggest you follow Bill Gothard. That is how [not ]{.class_s36M}to do it. The way Bill does it is rent an auditorium and charge everyone \$17.00 to come in and sit down. Then he will start out by saying. "Here are the qualities that God wants to develop in you: A pleasing personality, kindness, goodness, generosity, love, forgiveness, and an integrated personality." Ah, yes. "Lesson number two: How shall we go about to develop these basic personality qualities?"

Now, what he has got in the audience may be 2,000 Christians who will never amount to a hill of beans plus five hundred unsaved people who have come in there to get their personality psychoanalyzed?

Then after seventeen lessons, he makes an accidental remark that the man needs to be saved. After seventeen lessons---seventeen hours! By that time some of them have been coming for seventeen hours, and some of them receive Christ. But they receive Christ on a strictly goody-goody basis. And when they go out, they are goody-goody Christians. [They ain't worth a dime.]{.class_s36M} Somebody said, "Well, they got saved." Maybe, but they could have got saved better with a street preacher. If all you are doing is just reaching the people the wrong way, [you are preventing the next generation from finding Christ. ]{.class_s36M}So it is not right.

[I therefore so run, not as uncertainly; so fight I, not as one that beateth the air"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 26). "Beating the air" is like throwing haymakers; you keep swinging and maybe the draft will give your opponent a cold.

Once a black fighter got knocked down about the third round. As he was on the mat at about the count of six, his manager said, "Come on, boy, you're going to fight some more, ain't you?" That boxer looked up off the mat and said, "Yeah, I'm going to fight some more, but not tonight." That's the way a lot of Christians are. You're going to fight, but not [tonight.]{.class_s36M}

In running you have to be certain of the course. You don't weave all over the track. He says in Philippians 3:14, ["I press toward the mark."]{.class_s36N}

In boxing, you aim your blows so they will connect with your opponent.

["But I keep under my body, and bring it into subjection" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 27). Generally speaking, if the man will go hard and do his best for the Lord, he won't wind up in the hospital. You should put your flesh down and keep the body under control.That means denying the flesh to make it behave. That's athletic training which is good. Folks say, "I would rather burn out than rust out." Well, the answer to that is don't burn out and don't rust out. Keep burning, but don't rust. Make the body behave; make it do what you tell it to do.

["Lest that by any means, when I have preached to others, I myself should be a castaway."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That is a picture of the fellow who didn't keep the body under subjection. After having ministered to other people, he is put on the shelf and can no longer minister to others.

The idea is that the body gets out of hand. Whether that is a case of lust and fornication or lust and VD or whether it is a case of rheumatics and flu and pneumonia from overexposure, or whether it is a case of hardening of the arteries and heart attack and cholesterol from overeating, the point is still the same. The point is the body needs to be held in subjection and trained as an athlete if the man is going to minister. That's the point.

Ministers are bad about this. I don't know of any ministers who drive slow. If there is a minister in America who drives slow, I've never been with him, and I've been with over 500 of them. The worst one was Howard Sears of Middleton, Ohio. He would put one hand over the back seat and put that thing at seventy-five miles per hour while talking to you in the back seat. I prayed for a highway patrolman all the time I was with him.

Most ministers don't eat slow. They eat fast. They get after me about my fast eating. I eat pretty fast. I usually finish before anybody else has started. But there is a preacher who could outeat me, and that was Wendell Zimmerman in Jacksonville. When I was halfway through, he had had dessert. Most ministers are overweight.

Right before Beauchamp Vick died, he went to Korea. He was on that plane about fifteen hours, and after he got back they said he wasn't quite the same again.

I preached "Old Soldiers Never Die" while I was there in August, and he died before Christmas. He didn't look the same when I was with him in August. He was under the care of a physician. I've been on those planes, and by the time you have been on a plane five hours, there is something about traveling at 400 miles per hour for which the human body is not made. When you get off that plane you are as tired as if you had driven a car twelve hours. They pull the blinds and show a movie. So what you are in up there is really a movie theater with smoke all over the place at 35,000 feet.

What I am saying is the modern demands of the ministry are going to tax your health to the limit. When a pastor gets out in the work, he is are going to have to reserve some time for hoeing and tilling, or hunting or fishing or walking or running or something, or it will drive you bananas.

Harold Henniger of Canton, Ohio, built a great work. He had three light heart attacks and then open heart surgery. Dallas Billington of Akron, Ohio, had three heart attacks, and then the fourth one got him. John Rawlings in Cincinnati, Ohio, had ulcers---had bad trouble almost to the point of a nervous breakdown, with building programs and all that stuff.

A lot of my friends in the ministry are overweight, twenty pounds overweight, thirty pounds overweight, forty, fifty pounds overweight. Then they will say, "I'm having trouble with my blood," or "My ear hurts," or "My kidneys bother me," or "I have liver trouble," or "I don't know what's wrong. I'm going to the doctor for tests." Brother, when you are fifty pounds overweight, anything [can ]{.class_s36M}go wrong. You have got to keep that body under and ["bring it into subjection," ]{.class_s36N}lest that by any means when you preach to others you become a castaway. It will have to be trained and brought into subjection.

[]{#c111.xhtml}

## Chapter 10 {.heading_s39}

10:1 Moreover, brethren, I would not that ye should be ignorant, how that all our fathers were under the cloud, and all passed through the sea;

2 And were all baptized unto Moses in the cloud and in the sea;

3 And did all eat the same spiritual meat;

4 And did all drink the same spiritual drink: for they drank of that spiritual Rock that followed them: and that Rock was Christ.

Now, the core of the Corinthian church was made up of Jewish believers (Acts 18:8). When he addresses the ["brethren,"]{.class_s36N} in verse 1, and uses the expression ["our fathers,"]{.class_s36N} he is speaking to Jewish believers in Corinth who know their Old Testament. He is making spiritual application by saying that the inheritance of Christians goes back to the Jews.

The baptism of verses 1 and 2, of course, is one of seven baptisms discussed in the sermon booklet [The Seven Baptisms ]{.class_s36M}(Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1998), so we will not go into an exposition of them here. But though this baptism was one of aspersion (sprinkling in a mist---"[in the cloud"]{.class_s36N} \[vs. 2\]), the picture is nevertheless one of immersion (["under the cloud"]{.class_s36N} \[vs. 1\]).

When the Jews went out of Egypt and went through the Red Sea, there was water over their heads. They were immersed. But they weren't immersed in fluid water. They had water on both sides and water over their heads, but the water over their heads was a cloud. Nevertheless, they were immersed.

For a full discussion of this baptism, see the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Exodus]{.class_s36M} (pp. 328--331, Bible Baptist Bookstore, 1993).

Note in verses 3--4 that although the ["meat"]{.class_s36N} which the Israelites ate in the wilderness was physical (manna---John 6:31­--32, 49), it pointed to a ["spiritual meat"]{.class_s36N}---Jesus Christ (John 6:33--35, 50--51). The same is true of the ["spiritual drink"]{.class_s36N} of verse 4. Christ said, ["But whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst; but the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life"]{.class_s36N} (John 4:14).

Then the "rock," back there in Exodus, is a type of Christ. And though they drank literal water out of a literal rock, that literal rock and literal water was a type of the ["spiritual rock"]{.class_s36N} and spiritual Water, the ["spiritual rock that followed them: and that Rock was Christ."]{.class_s36N} Christ came on later. ["That Rock,"]{.class_s36N} capital R, was not Simon Peter. Look at verse 4. [The ]{.class_s36M}Rock was not Peter. [The ]{.class_s36M}Rock is Christ.

This explains why Moses never got into the Promised Land. One time when they were hollering for water, the Lord told Moses to go up there and strike the rock; Moses got his rod and hit that rock. The rock clave open and water came out of the rock. That is what Paul is talking about in 10:4. He is talking about Exodus 17:6. Then, a year later, when they were hollering and bellyaching about water in the wilderness during the forty years, the Lord told Moses to go out and [speak ]{.class_s36M}to the rock. Moses got mad and took his staff and went out there and [hit ]{.class_s36M}that rock and water came out, but the Lord had told Moses ["speak"]{.class_s36N} to it. That explains why Moses wouldn't get in the Promised Land.

That shows you how careful God is about [types]{.class_s36M}. God would prevent a fellow from getting into the Promised Land for breaking the [typology ]{.class_s36M}of Scripture.

The way that works is this: If the rock is Christ, the Rock has to be smitten [once]{.class_s36M}. Right? Christ died [once]{.class_s36M}. It doesn't have to be smitten again. Once Christ died, it was done. [Once ]{.class_s36M}you receive Jesus Christ, you do not go to mass every Sunday morning and get Him [again]{.class_s36M}.

Those rascals down here, offering a "sacrifice" every Sunday are offering Christ up as a sacrifice fifty-two times a year. That is what got Moses kicked out of the Promised Land. The second time you don't [smite ]{.class_s36M}the Rock. [You speak to the Rock.]{.class_s36M}

Another good example of this is Christ saying that if a man is washed [all over]{.class_s36M} he is clean (John 13). He doesn't have to wash all over again, but he has to wash his [feet ]{.class_s36M}again. When you receive Jesus Christ you are washed in the blood all over. [You never get washed all over again.]{.class_s36M} You cannot get saved [again]{.class_s36M}. You are saved [once]{.class_s36M}. When you are saved you are washed all over. But you may get your feet dirty, so you will have to wash your feet again and again and again and again. But he that is washed is clean all over (John 13). When the ["Rock"]{.class_s36N} was smitten, Christ died for your sins and shed his blood; you are just as good as in heaven with the door shut. You say, "What if I sin again? What if I get dirty again?" [Speak ]{.class_s36M}to the rock (1 John 1).

10:5 But with many of them God was not well pleased: for they were overthrown in the wilderness.

6 Now these things were our examples, to the intent we should not lust after evil things, as they also lusted.

7 Neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them; as it is written, The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play.

8 Neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed, and fell in one day three and twenty thousand.

9 Neither let us tempt Christ, as some of them also tempted, and were destroyed of serpents.

10 Neither murmur ye, as some of them also murmured, and were destroyed of the destroyer.

11 Now all these things happened unto them for ensamples: and they are written for our admonition, upon whom the ends of the world are come.

12 Wherefore let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall.

Verse 5 refers to the events that took place in the book of Numbers. Israel would get to complaining, and God would wipe out a few thousand of them. When the Lord said He was not well-pleased ["with many of them"]{.class_s36N} that was "the under-statement of the year."

That ["many"]{.class_s36N} was a whole generation! A whole generation died. In forty years a generation perished. The Lord said one time to Moses, "I'll kill them all and just take you." Jack Hyles said one time that if God and Moses had ever gotten into a killing mood at the same time, there wouldn't have been anyone left but God and Moses.

["Now these things were our examples, to the intent we should not lust after evil things"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6).[ ]{.class_s36M}Compare this with verse 11, ["Now all these things happened unto them for]{.class_s36N} [ensamples and they are written for our admonition."]{.class_s36N}

If you are going to be a hyper-dispensationalist you are going to have to get rid of 1 Corinthians, because 1 Corinthians says that Exodus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy were written for the Christian's admonition. It is "present truth for the Body mystery." If you are going to mess around with Bullinger and Baker and O'Hare and that bunch, eventually you are going to have to throw out 1 Corinthians, because 1 Corinthians says the Old Testament is written for the Christian.

["Neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 7).[ ]{.class_s36S}Verse 7 implies a Christian can be an idolater.

The Corinthians lived in a nation filled with idols (Acts 17:16). John warns the Christians to whom he wrote, ["Little children, keep yourselves from idols" ]{.class_s36N}(1 John 5:21). So going back to those idols was an ever present danger for the Greeks.

Now, it is no different today. Human nature has not changed since A.D. 54. Paul said, ["Covetousness...is idolatry"]{.class_s36N} (Col. 3:5), and without a doubt, America is a nation full of idolaters, even among Christians. The gods of America are money, sex, and education; just about in that order. Just because they don't all bow down to statues, Americans think they aren't "heathen." Most of the shrines of the idols, in this country, are on the inside (Ezek. 14:1--11).

Verse 8 is a reference to Israel intermarrying with the Moabites in Numbers 25. When Balaam couldn't curse the children of Israel, he got Balak to get his unsaved girls to "date" the saved boys in the "youth group." Of course, then each girl put the pressure on her boyfriend "to go to mass" with her (Rev. 2:14). Solomon had the same problem (Neh. 13:26). And though Balaam could not curse the Israelites, he managed to get them to bring a curse on themselves.

Sexual fornication is the second great idol of Americans. It brings down the judgment of God on us just like it did on Israel. America has sown pornography, "sex education," and sex perversion; and it is reaping over a million abortions a year, a skyrocketing VD rate, AIDS (news media doublespeak for Gay Related Immunity Deficiency), and an exploding prison population of rapists, child molesters, and sex murderers.[ "Neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed, and fell in one day three and twenty thousand."]{.class_s36N}

Verse 8 is one of those famous "contradictions" in the Bible. Numbers 25:9 says 24,000 died in the plague; 1 Corinthians 10:8 says 23,000 died in the plague. Of course, 1 Corinthians 10:8 is the number that died in [one day,]{.class_s36M} while Numbers 25:9 records the number dead in the [entire plague.]{.class_s36M}

While Scofield correctly reconciles the two passages, he can't help but bow to the last great American idol---[Education]{.class_s36M}. "Some [discrepant statements ]{.class_s36M}concerning numbers are, however, found in the existing manuscripts of the Hebrew Scriptures. These are most naturally ascribed to the fact that the Hebrews used letters in the place of numerals\....[Error in transcription]{.class_s36M} of Hebrew numbers thus becomes easy, preservation of numerical accuracy difficult" (pg. 1220 of 1909 ed.).

Now, doesn't that sound like "the voice of reason"; no "rabid ramblings like Ruckman," right? But that little tip of the hat toward [scholarly infidelity ]{.class_s36M}allowed the [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s36M} to question 2 Samuel 8:4 and 1 Chronicles 18:4, 1 Samuel 6:19, 2 Samuel 15:7, and 1 Kings 2:11, and 1 Samuel 13:1. And of course, it didn't stop with questioning God's ability to [count]{.class_s36M}. It went on to change [what God said]{.class_s36M} in 1,000 places, while still calling itself the [Authorized King James Version.]{.class_s36M} Brethren, the rule is simple: when the Bible says one thing and Scholarship say another, don't even give Scholarship "the time of day." If you give in even a little, [it will take all it can possibly get away with.]{.class_s36M}

The story of the fiery serpents (vs. 9) is found in Numbers 21:5--9. If you want to find out how the children of Israel tempted God, then read verse 5 of Numbers 21. They questioned God's [purpose ]{.class_s36M}in delivering them (Exod. 16:3; Num. 14:3), and they questioned God's [provision ]{.class_s36M}for them in the wilderness. In fact, they actually despised the [goodness of God]{.class_s36M} so that they actually ended up counting the good things that God gave them as [evil]{.class_s36M}. Satan took Eve's attention away from all the fruit God [had]{.class_s36M} given her and centered it on the one fruit God [withheld ]{.class_s36M}from her. He then ascribed [selfishness ]{.class_s36M}to God's purpose in withholding the fruit (Gen. 3:5). What Paul describes as[ tempting God ]{.class_s36M}is what led the human race into 6,000-plus years of misery, suffering, hospital beds, and graves.

The final ["example"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6) Paul pulls from Numbers is Israel's murmuring in the wilderness. The people began complaining three days after their deliverance at the Red Sea (Exod. 15:24), and the last recorded use of the word in their wilderness wanderings was in Numbers 17:5 when God stopped it by causing Aaron's rod to blossom (a type of the resurrection of Christ).

When the people complained in Numbers 11:1, God sent His fire and destroyed the gripers. But the reference [here ]{.class_s36M}is a reference to Numbers 16:41--50, where 14,700 people died because they complained about the Lord destroying Korah, Dathan, Abiram, and those priests that stood against Moses and Aaron.

Now, [griping ]{.class_s36S}is caused by a lack of thankfulness to God for what He gives to us and what He does for us. We feel we are not getting what we want or what we deserve. The big rule when I went to Bob Jones University was "NO GRIPING TOLERATED." Your ingratitude spreads to others like a plague. Bob Jones Sr. said, "When gratitude dies in the heart of a man, that man is well nigh hopeless." The [cure ]{.class_s36S}for a griping, complaining spirit is to "count your many blessings, name them one by one, and it will surprise you what the Lord hath done."

["Now all these things]{.class_s36N} (the tempting, the murmuring, the fornication, the idolatry) [happened unto them for ensamples: and they are written for our admonition"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). [Don't you ever limit your reading to the New Testament.]{.class_s36M} Don't ever get to the place where you are just finding Christian doctrine in the New Testament. You're out of place. The Bible believer has the whole Bible. It is true that some individual parts may not refer [doctrinally ]{.class_s36M}to you, and it may be true that some individual parts are not as important as other individual parts, but you cannot cut up that Bible and say, "Well, just this part is for me and the rest of it is not." Turn to Romans 15:4, ["For whatsoever things were written aforetime were written for our learning, that we through patience and comfort of the scriptures might have hope."]{.class_s36N} The reference there was not to [salvation]{.class_s36M}. The reference in Romans is a Christian going through trouble and trial and tribulation and going back to the [Old Testament ]{.class_s36M}to get his morale up and his courage up. So the Old Testament is for the admonition and comfort of the [New Testament ]{.class_s36M}Christian.

The "falling" of verse 12 has nothing to do with a Christian going to Hell. No Christian loses his salvation in verse 12. The cross reference is to verse 8 where 23,000 people were killed.[ ]{.class_s36N}It's talking about a Christian living like the devil and the Lord killing him. It's [not ]{.class_s36M}talking about losing your salvation.

10:13 There hath no temptation taken you but such as is common to man: but God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able; but will with the temptation also make a way to escape, that ye may be able to bear it.

14 Wherefore, my dearly beloved, flee from idolatry.

15 I speak as to wise men; judge ye what I say.

The country way of saying verse 13 is, "The Lord don't put on us more than we can carry," or "The Lord won't put on us more than we can bear." That's what they say. But of course, the verse doesn't say that. The verse says, ["There hath no temptation taken you but such as is common to man."]{.class_s36N}

Any temptation with which [you ]{.class_s36M}are tempted is one with which[ I]{.class_s36M} am tempted. Any temptation with which I'm tempted is one with which you're tempted. In light of that, the promise is this: God will allow no temptation to come your way that you can't stand. The "catch" is that along with the temptation, the Lord provides the way to [escape it.]{.class_s36M}

Now, there are several things about that passage. First, when a fiery trial comes up, sometimes we need a [fire escape]{.class_s36M} to escape it. If you don't look for the escape, then you burn. Our trouble is this: When the thing comes up, instead of saying, "Lord get me out. Lord find me a way out," sometimes we kind of delay it and think about it a while before we get out. There's that [presentation ]{.class_s36M}of the temptation. There's the [illumination ]{.class_s36M}of whether it is right or wrong. Then you begin to [debate ]{.class_s36M}about it and think about it and toss it back and forth. [That's where you mess up. ]{.class_s36M}Once you know it's wrong then you have to say, "Lord, help. I'm in trouble. Get me out. Lord, get me out quick." If you start that, the Lord will get a way for you. But you can't mess around. [You can't lay down terms.]{.class_s36M}

For example, you may get in a thing sometime where the temptation is really bad, and you'll say, "Now, Lord, I can't stand this anymore, and if You don't send some help, I'm going to mess up and do something wrong and make a big mess of things. I've got to have a way out, and I've got to get it fixed. You said you would supply an escape. Now, where is it?" The Lord will say, "It is [here,]{.class_s36M}" and show you a certain thing. And you'll say, "Well, not [that,]{.class_s36M} Lord," and the Lord will say, "You asked and there it is." You'll say, "Yeah, but that won't work either." The Lord will say, "Look here, who are you telling what will work and what won't work? [That ]{.class_s36M}is the escape." You'll say, "Yeah, but that won't work." The Lord will say, ["OK, forget it." ]{.class_s36M}And you're stuck in the mud. So when you get to that place where you are tempted and ask the Lord for a way out and [the Lord shows you the way out, ]{.class_s36M}you have got to take the way out, unless you just want to yield to temptation and get your brains knocked out.

[There are three basic temptations,]{.class_s36M} and they are found in 1 John 2:16 and Genesis 3:6 and Matthew 4:1--11. The three basic temptations are, first of all, [do it yourself.]{.class_s36M} Don't bring God in on it. The second one is [let God do it all ]{.class_s36M}and you sit down and do nothing. The third one is do something wrong and [let the devil take care of you. ]{.class_s36M}Those are the three basic ones. The more I study electronics and the more I study air waves, the more it seems like as soon as you make any attempt to reach out and contact the Lord, verbally or silently in your mind, some kind of transaction takes place between here and the third heaven, and the devil will sure be aware of it.

The ladies will be tempted most [to open their mouths, ]{.class_s36M}because the first sin ever committed on this earth was a woman [opening her mouth;]{.class_s36M} then she put something in her mouth, then she put her foot in her mouth. So the biggest temptation you are going to have is to say something when there is no need to say it or say it wrong or misquote it, or say it [secondhand]{.class_s36M}. That's the biggest temptation you will ever have. That was Eve's temptation.

The biggest temptation a man will have is to [blame somebody else.]{.class_s36M} That is the first thing he does, and the next temptation will be to haul off and smack somebody and kill them. [The first man born on this earth]{.class_s36M} [was a murderer.]{.class_s36M} Man has five fingers on each hand. That's death. The first man born was a killer. The biggest temptation you will ever have when you get mad is just haul off and shoot somebody. That will be the temptation you are going to have. Those temptations are common. [They are common to man.]{.class_s36M} There is nothing new. You don't have any pressure somebody else hasn't got.

["But God is faithful\..."]{.class_s36N} Notice, we're not, but God is. ["God is faithful"]{.class_s36N} to keep you secure in Jesus Christ (1 Cor. 1:8; 1 Thess. 5:24). He is [faithful ]{.class_s36M}to ["stablish you, and keep you from evil" ]{.class_s36N}(2 Thess. 3:3). When we confess our sins, ["he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness"]{.class_s36N} (1 John 1:9). And here in 1 Corinthians 10:13 He is [faithful ]{.class_s36M}to provide escape from temptation. That is the verse to learn and to claim if you want victory over sin.

For a moment, let's take a look at this matter of victory over sin. A Christian's main concern should be to live as closely in fellowship with his Saviour as possible; if you are going to do that, you have to get victory over sin in your life. The first step should be to make up your own mind that you will try your [best ]{.class_s36M}to stay away from sin. The Scriptures tell us that ["Daniel purposed in his heart that he would not defile himself with the portion of the king's meat, nor with the wine which he drank"]{.class_s36N} (Dan. 1:8). The question arises, is it possible to get victory in your own self? And the answer is, with some temptations it is and with others it is not.

For example, if some of you were temped to smoke a cigarette, how many of you could get victory over it without praying, "Lord, please get me a way out of it"? For most of you it wouldn't have that big a hold on you. Some of the rest of you might be a little bit different.

Different sins have holds on men in different ways. You take sex, money, dope, and liquor. If any one of those four things get a good hold on you, you will have to cry out for help. I'll kid you not. There are some other things that get a hold on you that aren't so bad. Some of you have an advantage. Some of you were saved when you were young, and you've had time to cultivate good habits and discipline, and those things never had any hold on you at all.

When I got saved I got rid of liquor in twenty-four hours and got rid of cigars in about seven days and that was the end of it. They never popped up again anywhere. On cursing and saying "damn" and "hell" and that kind of stuff, it took almost [two months. ]{.class_s36M}The first time I gave a testimony it had "damns" in it. The first testimony I gave was at Camp Calvary. Lee Roberson was preaching that night and asked me to give a testimony. I had only been saved about a month and a half. You talk about [rough,]{.class_s36M} man. I'm a gentleman now, brother, believe me. I'm [smooth,]{.class_s36M} boy.

One day after that I was walking down the street with a hillbilly who used to play in a dance band with me. He was playing the guitar in a Church of God here, but he was saved. We were walking down the street, and he said, "You know, that [language ]{.class_s36M}has got to go, Ruckman." I said, "What? What language?" He said, "What you're saying." I said, "What am I saying?" (I didn't know.) He said, "Well, you said ------" and then he listed the stuff, and honest to goodness, it shocked me when I heard it. I didn't realize I was still talking that way.

Now, all of that stuff got fixed real quick, but to this day that [rhythm band beat ]{.class_s36M}just does not disappear. It stays right there. I can be studying the Bible at my desk, and it will come up. [That's that old dance band.]{.class_s36M} That stuff will stick with you and stick with you and stick with you.

Some of you don't have trouble with [cigarettes]{.class_s36M}. You take a guy that has been smoking two packs a day, when he tries to quit it is just like hell on earth. It's just like stabbing him with pins like a pin cushion.

So the best thing is just to make up your mind [never to get into sin in the first place.]{.class_s36M}

Next, you have got to saturate yourself with the word of God. David said, ["Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way? by taking heed thereto according to thy word\....Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 119:9, 11). The Lord defeated the temptations of Satan by [quoting Scripture. ]{.class_s36M}Read your Bible daily. Reading ten full pages, front and back, will put you through it [once every three months. ]{.class_s36M}When I was first saved, I did that. I can't do it anymore; the eyesight is going at age 80, but I still listen to it played over tapes or CDs. Some how, some way, you have got to get that Bible [inside you.]{.class_s36M}

I have recommended several verses so far for memorization in this Commentary. Have you taken the time to sit down and go over them until you know them by heart? If you [don't]{.class_s36M} memorize those verses, then get somebody's program of Scripture memory and get busy. One of these days, brethren, that Book is going to be taken away from you. You had better have enough of it committed to memory that you can still get the victory anyway.

Prayer and "promise claiming" is the next step to victory over sin, and that is what we have covered in 1 Corinthians 10:13. Claim the promise that God gave you in that verse and ask Him for ["the way to escape."]{.class_s36N} And when He gives it to you---TAKE IT!

Another step is what I call "dead reckoning," and it is found in Romans 6:11 where Paul says, ["Likewise reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord."]{.class_s36N} The idea is that if the old nature is dead, then it can't sin. The problem is that old "Adam" keeps climbing up from the grave or down from the cross. That is why each day you have to consciously die to self (1 Cor. 15:31). I don't like it any more than you do; after all, who wants to be nailed to a cross? But brethren, let's just face it. When [you ]{.class_s36M}are on the cross, then Christ is on the [throne;]{.class_s36M} and when [you ]{.class_s36M}are on the throne, then [Christ ]{.class_s36M}is on the cross. You just might as well make up your mind to ["mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth"]{.class_s36N} (Col. 3:5).

The final step to victory over any sin is either to fight or to flee. James 4:7 says, ["Resist the devil, and he will flee from you."]{.class_s36N} This requires ["the whole armour of God"]{.class_s36N} found in Ephesians 6 (see comments in [Bible Believer's Commentary on Galatians--Colossians]{.class_s36M}). At other times, though, you are not to stay and fight; you are to[ flee. ]{.class_s36M}In verse 14, you are to ["flee from idolatry."]{.class_s36N} Back in chapter 6, verse 18, your were told to ["flee fornication."]{.class_s36N} In 1 Timothy 6:11, a ["man of God"]{.class_s36N} is to flee covetousness and ["the love of money."]{.class_s36N} And the young preacher, Timothy, was told to ["flee also youthful lusts"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 2:22). The Bible example is Joseph who, when he was being seduced by Potiphar's wife, fled from her. He didn't hang around to see how long he could resist temptation. He ran! And as Lester Roloff said, "He may have lost his [coat]{.class_s36M}, but he kept his [character."]{.class_s36M}

10:16 The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?

17 For we being many are one bread, and one body: for we are all partakers of that one bread.

18 Behold Israel after the flesh: are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar?

19 What say I then? that the idol is any thing, or that which is offered in sacrifice to idols is any thing?

20 But I say, that the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils, and not to God: and I would not that ye should have fellowship with devils.

21 Ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord, and the cup of devils: ye cannot be partakers of the Lord's table, and of the table of devils.

22 Do we provoke the Lord to jealousy? are we stronger than he?

["The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16).

Now, that is the Roman Catholic mass proof text. This is used with 11:27, ["Wherefore whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, unworthily, shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}And again, 11:29[, "For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body."]{.class_s36N}

So with these two verses they will tell you this: When that fellow picks up the cup and blesses it, it is the [blood ]{.class_s36M}of Christ. When he takes the wafer and blesses it, the wafer is the [body ]{.class_s36M}of Christ. Therefore, if you eat that bread and drink that cup and don't realize that is Christ's corpse, you eat and drink [damnation ]{.class_s36M}to yourself. That is the teaching.

Now, back to 10:16. Notice in 10:16 how these folks get all messed up on their Bible. This time we will read carefully. Now, watch it carefully. ["The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ?"]{.class_s36N} He didn't say it was the [blood of Christ.]{.class_s36M} He said it was[ "the communion of the blood of Christ."]{.class_s36N} All of Christ's literal blood spilled out on the ground. When Christ died on the cross, the soldier came by and put the spear in Jesus' side, and there came out water and blood, and that was the end of [that ]{.class_s36M}blood. So you can't say he is talking about literal blood. The [literal blood]{.class_s36M} was on the ground. (For a detailed discussion of the blood of Christ in the atonement, see comments on Heb. 9:11--14 in that Commentary.)

In like manner, the bread is [not ]{.class_s36M}the body, but ["the communion of the body of Christ."]{.class_s36N}

Now, watch verse 17 carefully. ["For we being many are one bread, and one body\...."]{.class_s36N} The body of Christ is [not ]{.class_s36M}the corpse that died on the cross.
