When a man takes communion and thinks he is eating and drinking the [body ]{.class_s36M}and [blood ]{.class_s36M}of Christ, he eats and drinks damnation to himself every Sunday morning at eleven o'clock.

First Corinthians 10:17 tells you the body of Christ is[ not Christ's corpse.]{.class_s36M} The [Body of Christ]{.class_s36M} is the body of born-again believers. Look at it. ["For we being many are one bread, and one body."]{.class_s36N}

The Eucharist or "mass" is the communion of the Body of Christ, [not the dead corpse of Jesus.]{.class_s36M} (There's no indication about how often you should have communion. We don't have it often enough here. I would say we should have it at least every two months.)

The damnable thing about the Catholic Church is they instill that stuff in to you to the point that it almost ruins your Christianity [after ]{.class_s36S}you get saved. If you had to be a Communist or a Catholic, you had better choose to be a Communist.[ They can't control your conscience. ]{.class_s36S}They can control your money and your family, and that's damnable. But they can't control your [conscience]{.class_s36S}. The Catholic Church can control your conscience. I used to go to mass and kneel there and say, "That's the [body. ]{.class_s36S}That's the [blood.]{.class_s36S} No, it's not.[ Yes, it is. ]{.class_s36S}I've got to believe it is. I've got to believe it. [It can't be.]{.class_s36S} Yes, but it[ is. ]{.class_s36S}Well, he said to abstain from all food twenty minutes after I eat it. Why did he say that? Because it was [bread.]{.class_s36S} Digestive process takes twenty minutes. Well, if it is [bread ]{.class_s36S}it isn't Christ. Why did he say twenty minutes if it isn't [bread?]{.class_s36S} [It isn't Christ.]{.class_s36S} Yes it is Christ. I've got to believe it's Christ." That's the mess. [It will just keep you in a sweat all your life over nothing.]{.class_s36S} But I'm here to tell you, brother, it ain't Christ! And if you want a demonstration, I'll take a cup of new wine and unleavened bread, and [I'll dump the wine on the floor]{.class_s36S} and [step on the bread,]{.class_s36S} if that will help you. Amen!

Now, Paul ties this discussion of the Lord's supper back into his discussion on eating food offered to idols. In the Old Testament, the Levitical priests get part of the sacrifice offered at the altar. The picture was that if the altar was sanctified by God and the sacrifice was sanctified by God, then the priest was sanctified to God, or else he couldn't touch it (vs. 18). In the same way, if you take the Lord's supper, you show that you are already in Christ's Body; [you are already saved]{.class_s36M} (vs. 17).

What, then, does this have to do with meat offered to idols? Well, although an idol, of itself, ["is nothing in the world,"]{.class_s36N} and is not a real "god" (1 Cor. 8:4), it represents the spirit behind it, and that spirit is a devil (vs. 20).

When that meat was at the heathen altar, down at the heathen temple, it was "off limits." They had no business fooling around with the heathen pagan sacrifices there at Corinth. Paul was warning them[ ]{.class_s36N}not to fool with the heathen sacrifices going on there or any place else. I guess, perhaps, the major one in America was the Old American Indian who offered up sacrifices; although most of them believed in the Great White Father and the Great Spirit, the ones that did were idolaters and had idols (totem poles), and they would sit around and pass the "peace pipe." That's where your [tobacco ]{.class_s36M}and [dope ]{.class_s36M}came from.

Verse 22: ["Do we provoke the Lord to jealousy?"]{.class_s36N} Are we doing harm to Him by acting this way? ["Are we stronger than he?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}No. In plainer words, when we are doing what we are doing that's wrong here, verses 20--21, we're just cutting our own throats. The believer who takes the Catholic mass brings damnation on himself; the Lord does not do it.

10:23 All things are lawful for me, but all things are not expedient: all things are lawful for me, but all things edify not.

24 Let no man seek his own, but every man another's wealth.

25 Whatsoever is sold in the shambles, that eat, asking no question for conscience sake:

26 For the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof.

27 If any of them that believe not bid you to a feast, and ye be disposed to go; whatsoever is set before you, eat, asking no question for conscience sake.

28 But if any man say unto you, This is offered in sacrifice unto idols, eat not for his sake that shewed it, and for conscience sake: for the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof:

29 Conscience, I say, not thine own, but of the other: for why is my liberty judged of another man's conscience?

30 For if I by grace be a partaker, why am I evil spoken of for that for which I give thanks?

31 Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God.

32 Give none offence, neither to the Jews, nor to the Gentiles, nor to the church of God:

33 Even as I please all men in all things, not seeking mine own profit, but the profit of many, that they may be saved.

In verses 23--24, the apostle Paul gives the key to Christian liberty. [A Christian can do anything he wants:]{.class_s36M}[ "All things are lawful for me."]{.class_s36N} Drinking, smoking, and going to the movies won't affect your salvation one way or another. ["But all things are not expedient." ]{.class_s36N}Some things will not help you to move forward in your Christian life. They won't "expedite" you along the way (Phil. 3:14).

["All things edify not."]{.class_s36N} There are things that you can do that won't help you [grow ]{.class_s36M}one inch in your Christian life. So just because you [can]{.class_s36M} do something doesn't mean that you [should]{.class_s36M} do something. Sins against your body and imagination, as a Christian, will be paid for there. Just remember when you get ready to do a thing, that some things bring the [scourge]{.class_s36M} of a loving Father to His son (Heb. 12:6--7). So if you don't want "the fire beaten out of you" for something, DON'T DO IT!

["Let no man seek his own, but every man another's wealth"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 24).[ ]{.class_s36M}There's a good verse if you want to pervert it. I mean, don't seek to make your own living; [steal from someone else.]{.class_s36M} But that isn't what he is talking about. He's talking about your [spiritual welfare.]{.class_s36M} He is saying this: instead of just keeping your eye on your own growth and what's good for you, also try to conduct your life [so that it benefits other people, besides just yourself.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 25: ["Whatsoever is sold in the shambles."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}That's a pagan, heathen meat market. That's where our word comes from. It originally meant a [cheap ]{.class_s36S}meat market. When they say a thing is a "shambles" now a days, they mean it is a wreck. If you really want to see this, go to the Philippines. I'm sure there are places in Laos and Cambodia like it, but in the Philippines you will see meat hanging around there in those open markets for two weeks. It just turns purple with flies all over it. On a windy day you can smell that stuff for fifteen miles[.]{.class_s36N}

"Whatsoever is sold in the shambles, that eat, asking no question for conscience sake."

In other words, you are [not ]{.class_s36M}in the heathen temple; you are [not ]{.class_s36M}before the altar; you are [not ]{.class_s36M}associating with the sacrifice to devils. The meat has passed on. So if you don't want your conscience wounded (1 Cor. 8:7), don't ask any questions. [Just buy the meat and enjoy it.]{.class_s36M}

Why? Verse 26: ["For the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof."]{.class_s36N} God put the grass out for the cattle to eat. It's all His anyway.[ "For every beast of the forest is mine, and the cattle upon a thousand hills"]{.class_s36N} (Ps. 50:10). Paul tells Timothy ["For every creature of God is good, and nothing to be refused, if it be received with thanksgiving: For it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 4:4--5).

In verses 27--28, Paul gives a case of what he is talking about. If an unsaved person invites you to come out to dinner, and you feel like going, go. There is nothing wrong with that. If you were to be out there with a bunch of heathen people and they passed you something, don't ask any questions; [cut it and eat it.]{.class_s36S}

But if a man is getting ready to eat the meat, and the guy says, "Oh, by the way, we offered that this morning down at the temple to Jupiter," you should say, "No, thank you. I don't care for any." Now, really, you could go ahead and eat it, couldn't you? ["Meats for the belly, and the belly for meats: but God shall destroy both it and them"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 8:13).[ ]{.class_s36S}["An idol is nothing in the world"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 6:4). But you do that for his sake. If he sees you eat that, he says, "Well, it's all right for a Christian to fool around with the [temple ]{.class_s36S}and [idolatry." ]{.class_s36S}You don't want to give that impression. So you say, "No, thank you." ["For the earth is the Lord's, and the]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}[fulness thereof" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 28); [there are other things that God made that you can eat.]{.class_s36S}

Again, in verse 29, you could do it with a clear conscience. But you have to watch out for the other fellow. [He ]{.class_s36M}might not be able to do it, and it would mess him up. No matter how much [you ]{.class_s36M}don't like it, your liberty will be ["judged of another man's conscience."]{.class_s36N}

Verse 30: ["For if I by grace be a partaker]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(you can do it with a clear conscience), [why am I evil spoken of for that for which I give thanks?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}A fellow bows his head and says, "Lord, bless this stuff," and eats it. [He's got a good conscience about it.]{.class_s36M} Paul said, ["I am made all things to all men, that I might by all means save some"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 9:22). In one place he says, "I'm going to go along and do anything that is [right ]{.class_s36M}in order to win them," but on the other hand he says, "If I'm going to do something and give them a [wrong impression]{.class_s36M} about what a Christian is, then I'm not going to do that thing." You've got to get that balance. To reach the hippies you do [not ]{.class_s36M}let your hair grow down your back and run around smoking pot. That is [not ]{.class_s36M}the way to win them. That isn't being ["all things to all men, that I might by all means save some."]{.class_s36N} That's defiling their conscience,[ so that they have a bad impression of what a Christian is.]{.class_s36M}

Here's the rule in verse 31: ["Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That is a good verse. I say it is a "good verse." The Catholics have done it to death. They've killed Protestants "for the glory of God" and played bingo for the "glory of God" and got on the dance floor and played the drums for the "glory of God." But that is presumption. That is stretching things. You know what will glorify God and what won't.

Verse 32 gives three groups into which humanity is divided in this age: ["the Jews," "the Gentiles," ]{.class_s36N}and ["the church of God."]{.class_s36N}

That is a major division in Scripture. Every verse in the Bible is written primarily to a Jew or to a Gentile or to ["the church of God."]{.class_s36N} God divides all men into those three brackets.

If you are [saved]{.class_s36M}, you are a member of the Body of Christ, the Church, and you are [not ]{.class_s36M}a Jew and you are [not ]{.class_s36M}a Gentile. If you are [unsaved]{.class_s36M}, you are a Jew or a Gentile. So all men are lost or saved. [That's two branches.]{.class_s36M} If you are saved, you are in the Body of Christ, and if you are not in the Body of Christ, you are a Jew or you are a Gentile.

["Rightly dividing the word of truth,"]{.class_s36N} when you pick up a passage, you want to ask yourself, "Is this thing here aimed at a Christian?" If it is aimed at a Christian, then in Christ there is neither Jew nor Gentile. Or "Is it aimed at a Jew, or is it aimed at a Gentile?" Many times you can take a verse written to the Jew and get [spiritual application ]{.class_s36M}out of it. I'm talking about [doctrine ]{.class_s36M}primarily. Sometimes you will find a [doctrine ]{.class_s36M}given to a Jew that is [repeated ]{.class_s36M}to a Christian.

["Even as I please all men in all things, not seeking mine own profit, but the profit of many, that they may be saved"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 33).[ ]{.class_s36M}That is high ground. That is the highest ground on which the Christian walks. ["Even as I please all men in all things."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's [impossible]{.class_s36M}. You [cannot ]{.class_s36M}please all men in all things 100 percent. But the idea there is as much as [you can]{.class_s36M} and then don't go out of your way to [offend]{.class_s36M}. ["Not seeking mine own profit, but the profit of many, that they may be saved."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That is saying as far as the unsaved people go, he is not going out of his way to antagonize them about their convictions, the unsaved people's convictions. As far as pleasing [all ]{.class_s36M}men in [all things,]{.class_s36M} it is impossible. You will cross somebody every time you do something God told you to do. But the thing here at which you are looking, 10:27--32, is the things you can afford to give in on. If you're dealing with something you[ can't]{.class_s36M} afford to give in on, OK. But if there is a thing on which you [can ]{.class_s36M}afford to give in on for a brother's sake, then do it---[if you can.]{.class_s36M} If you [can't]{.class_s36M} that's something else.

You take this preaching-on-the-street business. If I understand the New Testament, [that is a New Testament practice and a thing that needs to be done and a thing that the Lord wants done.]{.class_s36M} That may offend people. I'm not going to back down on it, but I'll back down [somewhat]{.class_s36M}. I mean, I won't go down there every Saturday, fifty-two Saturdays a year, and harass them. So you can back down a little. Of course, you aren't going to please them. Any preaching is going to upset some of them. But in those things where you don't [have ]{.class_s36M}to offend, then don't do it. If you [have to,]{.class_s36M} OK.

The Lord might call some of you to be a missionary to South America or Africa, and maybe your mother and father won't want you to go. Maybe your wife won't want you to go. Maybe you will have trouble in the family over it. Maybe you will have to do something the Lord wants you to do that will offend [a lot of people. ]{.class_s36M}When those times come and you know the Lord wants you to do it, then you are to go ahead and do it. [The verse is not talking about that.]{.class_s36M} He is talking about a thing where you can afford to be lenient to get around offending a brother. Then you be lenient and don't offend him. Now, if you can do that ["in all things"]{.class_s36N} you will get up there around where John Wesley and Paul were. But even John Wesley and Paul got the brethren mad at them a number of times. Where you [have ]{.class_s36M}to be offensive, then just go ahead.

The classic story is the old man and the boy in Germany going to town on the mule. They go into town, and both of them are walking into town beside the mule. Somebody goes by and says, "Isn't that a couple of fools? They've got a mule to ride and they're walking." [So both of them got on the mule.]{.class_s36M}

They rode about five miles, and a bunch of folks saw them going down the street and said, "Isn't that something? A man and a boy on that donkey, about to break that donkey's back. [Poor little donkey.]{.class_s36M} You'd think a big strong man like that could walk. Both those fellows riding that donkey are about to kill it."

So the man got off and the [boy ]{.class_s36M}rode the donkey.

They went about a mile, and somebody went by and said, "Isn't that something? This younger generation. Look at that lazy, good for nothing boy riding that donkey and letting his poor old father [walk]{.class_s36M}."

So they changed places again, and the old man got on the donkey and the [boy ]{.class_s36M}walked. They went about a mile down the road, and somebody went by and said, "Isn't that something? There's a full grown man riding that donkey, and a little old boy there that can hardly stand up having to walk along by himself."

So they finally arrived in town, and when they arrived in town [both of them were carrying the donkey. ]{.class_s36M}That is the mess you get into when you try to please everybody. [You can't do it.]{.class_s36M}

[]{#c151.xhtml}

## Chapter 11 {.heading_s39}

11:1 "Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ."

Notice, there you are recommended to [follow a man.]{.class_s36M} Folks say, "Well, they're just following a man." Well, if you are following a man that is following Christ, then that is a good example to follow. Folks always worrying about, "Following a man, following a man." Make sure that you don't follow a man that [doesn't win souls.]{.class_s36M} Make sure you don't follow a man that [doesn't believe that Bible.]{.class_s36M} If you want to follow a man, then follow a man that [follows Christ.]{.class_s36M}

["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}You have to be careful whom you pick for your "heroes." You have to remember that all "role models" have holes in their armor. Paul didn't say, "Be ye followers of me because I am sinless." You have to look out for that. You have to look out for making the people you follow into [sinless heroes. ]{.class_s36M}Then like Cassius, they show up with feet of "Clay."

There won't be a hero you have ever picked in this world that doesn't have [a hole in his armor]{.class_s36M} somewhere. So when you are going along these lines, it's all right to follow a good man that loves the Lord and follows the Lord. I wouldn't hesitate a minute. I have imitated Bob Jones Sr. in a lot of things he [did ]{.class_s36M}and a lot of things he [said ]{.class_s36M}and a lot of things he [believed]{.class_s36M}. Doesn't bother me a bit. I've read a lot of things Sam Jones said and took it to heart and [copied ]{.class_s36M}it and [put it into practice.]{.class_s36M} Doesn't bother me a bit. Folks say, "Just following a man, just following a man." Well, it depends on whether the man loves God and where he's going, and what the Lord is doing with him. It also depends upon not following a man when [God leads the other way. ]{.class_s36M}You can't put any man ahead of God. You can't put any man [even with God.]{.class_s36M} It has to be God [first ]{.class_s36M}and others [next ]{.class_s36M}and you[ last.]{.class_s36M}

There is nothing wrong with following a good example. If John R. Rice wins souls, it would be a good idea to follow his example. Some of those folks do more [talking about it]{.class_s36M} and less [doing of it]{.class_s36M} than any bunch of people I ever met in my life. I have been to about five Sword conferences, and I've never seen [anybody ]{.class_s36M}saved in a Sword conference one time in five shots. I don't know what the conferences are for, unless it is to sell [Swords]{.class_s36M}.

You have to learn how to get the good out of a thing and leave the bad there. Anytime you think, "Well, this thing has got some bad in it," then you've got to go look in the mirror and see that you've got some in you.

First Timothy 1:15--16, ["This is a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners; of whom I am chief. Howbeit for this cause I obtained mercy,]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(watch it) [that in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering, for a pattern to them which should hereafter believe on him to life everlasting."]{.class_s36N} He put himself up pretty high.

["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}You take that thing right there, Paul being an example. Paul wasn't [sinless]{.class_s36M}. He resisted the will of God. [The Lord told him four times what to do, and he didn't do it.]{.class_s36M} All these Christian leaders into whom you will run, up and down the country, have holes in their armor. The only ones that I know of that [don't ]{.class_s36M}have any holes in their armor are the ones who want you to follow [them ]{.class_s36M}instead of following the Lord.

I know one great and good fellow who has a large work of about four or five thousand and that fellow doesn't drink or smoke or dance, doesn't do this and doesn't do that, but when he gets mad, he will put out a damn here and a hell there, when he gets to really blowing his top. I've heard him do it.

I know a fellow that has a work of about four, five, six, seven thousand, doing a great work; but every time you check on the guy, he is trying to [buy off]{.class_s36M} the top workers and top business men of the places where he is invited to speak. [He offers them a salary to leave their church and come work with him.]{.class_s36M} I don't think that is too ethical. Then you get a case where a guy has a bus ministry and is getting people saved, and everything is just fine except [he is putting dollar bills on the front seats of the buses for the kiddies.]{.class_s36M} You are going to find "kinks" in the armor. What you have just got to remember is ["all flesh is as grass, and all the glory of man as the flower of grass,"]{.class_s36N} and they've [all ]{.class_s36M}got holes in them. You want to be very careful to remember that [because you are one of them.]{.class_s36M} Some of the brethren seem to think the only holes are in the big shots, the leaders. You are quite wrong. Some of the followers have got holes in them, too.

["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, they can say what they want to about it, but that's it. I wouldn't hesitate at all to recommend that you follow the example of these fellows in a lot of things that they do. If you even get a chance to get on television and witness to big shots, then follow Harrington's example. [He witnesses to them. ]{.class_s36M}That much of his example you can follow. If you ever get a chance to witness or preach in a beer joint, if the Lord ever opens the opportunity and you get the chance, do a good job of it. I'm not telling you to preach as he preaches. Frankly, I don't think much of his [preaching]{.class_s36M}, but as far as the example goes, that is a good example. There are plenty of wicked towns like New Orleans that need a witness right downtown out there in the street. That's a good idea. I'd like to see a Sunday School in Pensacola get up there around 4,000 like they do at Jack Hyles. If any of you fellows can build a Sunday School of 4,000 in Pensacola, build it. In plainer words, you have got to go through there and see what is [right ]{.class_s36M}and what is [good ]{.class_s36M}and use that and learn to take the rest with a grain of salt. You have to live with yourself, too.

11:2 Now I praise you, brethren, that ye remember me in all things, and keep the ordinances, as I delivered them to you.

3 But I would have you know, that the head of every man is Christ; and the head of the woman is the man; and the head of Christ is God.

4 Every man praying or prophesying, having his head covered, dishonoureth his head.

5 But every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head: for that is even all one as if she were shaven.

6 For if the woman be not covered, let her also be shorn: but if it be a shame for a woman to be shorn or shaven, let her be covered.

7 For a man indeed ought not to cover his head, forasmuch as he is the image and glory of God: but the woman is the glory of the man.

8 For the man is not of the woman; but the woman of the man.

9 Neither was the man created for the woman; but the woman for the man.

10 For this cause ought the woman to have power on her head because of the angels.

11 Nevertheless neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord.

12 For as the woman is of the man, even so is the man also by the woman; but all things of God.

13 Judge in yourselves: is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered?

14 Doth not even nature itself teach you, that, if a man have long hair, it is a shame unto him?

15 But if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her: for her hair is given her for a covering.

16 But if any man seem to be contentious, we have no such custom, neither the churches of God.

In regards to verse 2, [the Baptists ]{.class_s36M}have the best of it. The Baptists teach that there are [two ordinances]{.class_s36M} observed by the local church---Believer's Baptism and the Lord's Supper. The hyper-dispensationalists insist that [water baptism]{.class_s36M} is not an ordinance, and the more radical (Bullinger) also eliminate the [Lord's Supper]{.class_s36M} as an ordinance, although that is the context of 1 Corinthians 11:17--27. The haircuts (vss. 3--16) are NOT an ordinance, so the Scriptural problem is why there is more than ONE ordinance (he said ["ordinances"]{.class_s36N}). The Catholics convert this to "sacraments." ["Ordinances"]{.class_s36N} refers to "ceremonies" in modern language. The only CEREMONY in the chapter is the Lord's Supper, not the haircuts. The only two ceremonies mentioned in Scriptures are the Lord's Supper and Baptism (Luke 22:19­--20; Matt. 28:19). That is what Baptists have always taught: [two offices]{.class_s36M} (Bishops, also called pastors and elders, and Deacons) and [two ordinances]{.class_s36M} (Baptism and the Lord's Supper).

Ordinances are also listed in Colossians 2:20, and these refer to neither Baptism nor the Lord's Supper. [They are rules and regulations of an Old Testament nature.]{.class_s36M} Ordinances are mentioned more than [fifty times]{.class_s36M} in the Old Testament. The ordinances in 1 Peter 2:13 are [commandments ]{.class_s36M}(rules and regulations to be obeyed; see Rom. 13:2).

The best conclusion is that Acts 15:28--31 is part of the[ "ordinances"]{.class_s36N} that Paul delivered, along with two "ceremonies": the Lord's Supper and Baptism. Since the gospel dealt with BURIAL and RESURRECTION as well as [death]{.class_s36M}, only TWO ordinances would show that, and it would take TWO, not ONE. The Supper would set forth His death, and Baptism would set forth His burial and resurrection. THAT IS PAUL'S ["GOSPEL OF THE GRACE OF GOD"]{.class_s36N} ACCORDING TO 1 CORINTHIANS 15:1--4.

Starting with verse 3, Paul discusses the position of the woman in the church and family.[ ]{.class_s36N}The order is God, Christ, man, woman.

From God's point of view, it is men's lib,[ not women's lib.]{.class_s36M} Man is supposed to be over the woman. When Israel gets messed up in the Old Testament, the Lord said, ["women rule over them"]{.class_s36N} (Isa. 3:12). The reason that a man is to rule over the women is given in verse 8, ["For the man is not of the woman; but the woman of the man."]{.class_s36N} [Adam is not made from Eve.]{.class_s36M} The Lord did not say to Eve, "Eve, I think [you ]{.class_s36M}need a help­meet."

When the devil wanted to mess up mankind, he came to the woman. In 1 Timothy 2:14, Paul says, ["And Adam was not deceived, but the woman being deceived was in the transgression."]{.class_s36N} Adam, as a type of Christ, consciously made the decision to die for his wife, but Eve was tricked by the serpent (2 Cor. 11:3). So in this passage, Paul will be dealing with problems that occur when the positions of the man and woman in the Body of Christ and the local church are "out of place."

["Every man praying or prophesying, having his head covered, dishonoureth his head"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4).[ ]{.class_s36M}Indicating that when a man prays he should take his hat off. The idea is that Christ is man's "head." The idea is that a man standing there with a hat on top of his head has put something between him and Christ, in type, in figure, because Jesus Christ is his "head."

These verses, 3--10, are talking about how Christians ought to behave when they assemble to eat and drink together. In context, it would be when they met together in the assembly.

["But every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head: for that is even all one as if she were shaven" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 5).[ ]{.class_s36M}So when a woman prays or prophesies, she is to have some covering on her head. If she doesn't have a covering on her head, it is just the same as if her hair was all shaved off. They should wear a [hat ]{.class_s36M}unless they have [long hair.]{.class_s36M} Look at verse 15, ["But if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her: for her hair is given her for a covering."]{.class_s36N}

Now (vs. 6), if a woman will not keep her head covered either by a hat or by long hair, then Paul says cut off all of her hair (["let her also be shorn"]{.class_s36N}). [But if that is a shame for a woman,]{.class_s36M} then she had better cover up her head. Some of these Holiness women brag about having hair coming all the way down the middle of their back.

The Bible never says just how long it has to be. In a few verses, though, we will see the difference between the short hair, demanded of a man, and the long hair of a woman.

Verse 7 contains two very important points. The first is [doctrinal]{.class_s36M}, and the other is [practical]{.class_s36M}. Doctrinally, note what the Lord says about the saved man: ["Forsasmuch as he is the image\...of God."]{.class_s36N} The saved man has regained what Adam lost. Adam was made ["in the image of God"]{.class_s36N} (Gen. 1:27). When he sinned, the part of him that died was his [spirit]{.class_s36M}, the part that communed with God (Eph. 2:1; Rom. 8:16). The ["image"]{.class_s36N} that God made (Gen. 2:7) was marred. Notice that Seth was not born ["in the image of God,"]{.class_s36N} but ["after his]{.class_s36N} (Adam's)[ image"]{.class_s36N} (Gen. 5:2). We sin because we are ["in Adam,"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 5:19); we die because we are ["in Adam"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 15:22; Rom. 5:17). But when a man receives Christ as his Saviour, he is no longer ["in Adam"]{.class_s36N}; he is then ["in Christ"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 12:13, 27; Eph. 5:30).

Moreover, Christ is in [you ]{.class_s36M}(Col. 1:27) in the person of the Holy Ghost (Rom. 8:9--16). When God looks at a saved man, He does not see a sinner in Adam's image, but His own Son who is ["the image of God"]{.class_s36N} Himself (2 Cor. 4:4; Heb. 1:3).

The question naturally arises, "What about the fact that we are still in a sinful body?" That is the purpose behind spiritual circumcision:[ to divide the old Adamic nature in the flesh from the new man in Christ Jesus]{.class_s36M} (Col. 2:11--13, 3:10). The added bonus is that someday God will change that sinful body and adopt it into His family too (Phil. 3:21; 1 Cor. 15:50--54; Rom. 8:23--29).

Practically speaking, verse 7 shows that any time a country begins to worship women or magnify and glorify women, they go to pieces pretty quick. We read in Romans 1:23, ["And changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}You are told that man (from the standpoint of the original creation, cf. vss. 8--9) ["is the image and glory of God: but the woman is the glory of the man."]{.class_s36N}

So when a nation begins to magnify [women ]{.class_s36M}they are magnifying [man's glory,]{.class_s36M} not God's glory. That's the beginning of the end. The surest sign in the world that this country is going to pot is that kind of business. You don't have to be told about it. You can see the magazines and newspapers and billboards and signs and know where it is going. ["The woman is the glory of the man."]{.class_s36N} So when the woman is magnified, [that is man bragging on his own glory, not God's glory.]{.class_s36M}

Although I have already commented on verses 8--9 under verse 3, note that when Paul wants to affirm the Scripture authority of the man over the woman, he does not go back to the curse placed on Eve in Genesis 3:16. Instead, he goes back to Genesis 2;[ to the original order and purpose of creation: ]{.class_s36M}Adam first, then Eve (vs. 8); Eve for Adam, not Adam for Eve (vs. 9, cf. Gen. 2:18). Man's authority over the woman does not begin with the result the curse. So any modern, "Christian" feminist, who thinks that salvation is going to make her an "equal" with her husband, has rocks for brains. [If a man does not rule his house, he will ruin it.]{.class_s36M}

["For this cause"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}is associated with what he just said about Adam and Eve in the previous verses. The ["power on her head"]{.class_s36N} matches the covering in verse 5, ["every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head."]{.class_s36N} ["Her head"]{.class_s36N} is defined in verse 3, ["the head of the woman is the man."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The connection is that the man is over the woman, and the covering on her head is a symbol of the fact that [she is under him]{.class_s36M} and he is [over her.]{.class_s36M}

["For this cause ought the woman to have power on her head."]{.class_s36N} Now, it either can come as a [covering,]{.class_s36M} verse 5, or else it can come as [long hair, ]{.class_s36M}verse 15, ["if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her: for her hair is given her for a covering."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The question comes up, "Cover what?" I mean short hair will cover your head. The Bible definition of long hair is hair that covers [the neck.]{.class_s36M} Folks say, "How long is long?" If it covers the neck, it is [long]{.class_s36M}. It's that simple. Look at verse 6, ["if it be a shame for a woman to be shorn or shaven."]{.class_s36N} In other words, there wasn't a hard line laid down on it. There was no hard and fast rule. For example, look at verse 16, ["if any man seem to be contentious, we have no such custom,]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}[neither the churches of God."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}So as far as the woman is concerned, there is no hard and fast rule on it, but the saying that if it is a shame for a woman to have her hair cut or to be shaven, then let her put a veil or a hat on her head to cover her head. [If not, then OK. ]{.class_s36M}There's no argument on it.

["For this cause ought the woman to have power on her head because of the angels."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Well, that's rough.

The two interpretations are these. [The first interpretation]{.class_s36S} is that when a woman doesn't cover her head either with a covering like a veil or long hair, then it is a sign that she is in rebellion against authority, and therefore, that reminds [the angels ]{.class_s36S}of their rebellion against God's authority. Now, that is the standard interpretation you pick up if you picked up the first thirty-five commentaries. [That interpretation assumes the angels are around watching. ]{.class_s36S}That assumes that angels are observing women now. That interpretation implies that not only are the angels [present ]{.class_s36S}and they are not only reminded of their rebellion, but they will [rebel again, which they do in Revelation 12.]{.class_s36S} The next interpretation is that it is possible for the angels to have [relationships with the women, ]{.class_s36S}as they did in Genesis 6. The Bible says, ["But I fear, lest by any means, as the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtilty." "I have espoused you to one husband\...as a chaste virgin to Christ"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 11:3, 2).

In verse 11, Paul applies verses 8--9 to the Christian marriage. Although both the saved man and the saved woman are positionally (doctrinally) equal in Christ (Gal. 3:28), that does not change the [practical application]{.class_s36S} that a Christian wife is to be in subjection to a Christian husband (Eph. 5:21--24). In the Christian marriage, the wife owes the husband [submission ]{.class_s36S}and [help]{.class_s36S}. The husband owes the wife [love ]{.class_s36S}and [protection ]{.class_s36S}(Eph. 5:25--31). At the same time, both are to be serving the Lord (1 Pet. 3:7).

According to verses 8--9, the wife is to be in subjection to the husband because Eve was made from Adam. But why does a man owe a woman anything? [Because there wouldn't be a man around if it wasn't for a woman. ]{.class_s36S}You had a mother. As a child, you owed that mother honor (Exod. 20:12) and obedience (Prov. 1:8; Eph. 6:1). When you get married, the love you gave to that mother now goes to your wife (Eph. 5:31; 1 Pet. 3:7). So the order is the woman from the man, and the man from the woman; but when all is said and done, [both are from God. ]{.class_s36S}So all obedience is traced back to your obligation to your Creator (Ecc. 12:1).

["Judge in yourselves: is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered?"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13). Here, Paul appeals to their common sense. "Does it seem right for a woman to pray uncovered?" That's why I keep saying the ["power"]{.class_s36N} in verse 10 is talking about a [covering ]{.class_s36M}as a [veil ]{.class_s36M}and not just a [man's authority,]{.class_s36M} because the context is still that the woman's hair is for covering. ["Judge in yourselves: is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Does it look right to see a woman praying to God with no veil or no hat, or short hair and not covered with long hair? The Catholics say, "No." So when you go to the Catholic Church, you see all the ladies walking in with little old [handkerchiefs ]{.class_s36M}covering their heads.

["Doth not even nature itself teach you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14).[ ]{.class_s36M}Then there are some things you know by nature that you don't have to be taught from the Bible. Just good [common horse sense]{.class_s36M} teaches you. ["Doth not even nature itself teach you, that, if a man have long hair, it is a shame unto him?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The question comes up then, how long is long? Well, whatever you've got, if it covers the [ears ]{.class_s36M}or the neck or the forehead then it is like a woman's hair, because a woman's hair is supposed to cover her ears and cover her neck, as well as her head.

Long hair is given for a [covering]{.class_s36M}, not short hair. Your natural body and your natural constitution tells you that it is a shame for [a man]{.class_s36M} to have long hair. For example, look at Romans 1:26, ["For this cause God gave them up unto vile affections: for even their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}See that. ["Against nature."]{.class_s36N} Verse 27, ["And likewise also the men, leaving the natural use of the woman, burned in their lust one toward another."]{.class_s36N}

That is talking about something that is natural and [unnatural]{.class_s36M}. Nature (that is [human nature,]{.class_s36M} your physical setup itself in you) teaches you that it is [natural ]{.class_s36M}for a man to have short hair and a woman to have long hair. [You don't have to be taught that anywhere.]{.class_s36M}

They have all kinds of ways of handling it. They say, "What about those folks back in 1400, 1500, 1600. Here's King James with his wig on, and here's George Washington with his pretty goldilocks, and all these people with hair down the middle of their back." Which overlooks two things. It overlooks the fact that [there wasn't a barber shop within ten miles of anybody at that time.]{.class_s36M} The barber back in those times was a leach; he wasn't a [haircutter]{.class_s36M}. If he was a haircutter, it would cost you a fortune. Next, those fellows patterned their hair after Revelation 1:14, which said that Christ, the Ancient of days, [came with white hair.]{.class_s36M} That powered wig and that long white hair was given a man as a [judge ]{.class_s36M}in authority, as a [politician]{.class_s36M}, or as a [lawyer ]{.class_s36M}or a [king]{.class_s36M}, not a bum.

If you want to wear long hair, [why ]{.class_s36M}do you want to wear it? Like that old colored preacher said, "De Lord don't care what you do. What de Lord wants to know is [why ]{.class_s36M}do you do what you do." The whole thing is motive.

I don't dictate a hard and fast line. The question is why Christians do what they do. That's the question. Anybody who has followed that "hair thing" knows when the hair thing came over here and from [where ]{.class_s36M}it came. They also knew with [whom ]{.class_s36M}it was associated and who [sold it]{.class_s36M} and put it across to American youth.

Now, we know that. Maybe some people don't know that. But if you are observant, you know. We also know [who ]{.class_s36M}took it up and what he said about it and what he said it meant. (We know what it means. I don't know what it means to you. I'm not going to speak for you, but I know what it means to the fellows who brought it over and sold our people on doing it. I know what it meant to them.) So the question is why does a male want to do it. A fellow says, "Well, it's the style today." I know that. I see the pictures once in a while when I go to these motels, and I see what's going on there. [Everybody looks like a musk-ox or a sheepdog.]{.class_s36M}

My boys had a terrible time with that thing. I gave Mike orders to get it cut or to get out, to take his choice. His point was when he went to the barber to try to get it trimmed, the barber told him it wasn't long enough to trim or style.[ So you've got the barber against you.]{.class_s36M} Then you've got this. You get a boy in who gets to playing football or baseball, and what are you going to do? He's got the shortest hair on the team, and the [coach ]{.class_s36M}looks like Tiny Tim. Get their picture, and it looks like about fifteen Goldilocks sitting there. If you blanked out each face you would swear there were fifteen [women ]{.class_s36M}sitting there.

The question comes up, why do you do what you do? That's the question. It isn't whether the thing is going to be legally right or legally wrong, [but what have you got in the back of your mind?]{.class_s36M} Folks say, "It's the style." Well, if it's the style I'll get mine cut. Maybe some day it will be the style to have burr haircuts. When it is, [I'll let my hair grow long.]{.class_s36M} Somebody said, "Why?" Just for sheer meanness. My [motive ]{.class_s36M}in doing it is this: [I'm not going to let the world tell me what to wear.]{.class_s36M} I wear blue denims [because ]{.class_s36M}they are comfortable. I wear boots [because ]{.class_s36M}they are comfortable. I don't care whether they are "in style" or not; I'll wear them anyway. My approach toward the thing is that they are not going to tell me what to do, [because they sent me to Hell for twenty-seven years.]{.class_s36M} They don't love my God, they don't love my Saviour, they don't love my Bible, and they don't love me.

You know how the girls are, "Oh, George, your hair is so beautiful." Well, you know, any guy that believes that is a fool. In the first place, any girl that could talk a guy into letting his hair get long would want to [run him]{.class_s36M}. Don't you understand that? [She ]{.class_s36M}is the one that is supposed to have the covering, [not him.]{.class_s36M} And when she does that then she brings him down to her level. Then he needs a covering to protect him. Don't you see that thing? Yes sir, brother!

The question arises in these matters about hair: Why did [Jesus ]{.class_s36M}have long hair? For the answer, turn to Leviticus 19. These aren't regulations given to a Nazarite. [There are regulations given to the whole nation.]{.class_s36M} I've always drawn pictures of Christ with long hair, but the reason for doing that is because He was an [Orthodox Jew]{.class_s36M} under the Law. Leviticus 19:26--27, ["Ye shall not eat any thing with the blood: neither shall ye use enchantment, nor observe times. Ye shall not round the corners of your heads, neither shalt thou mar the corners of thy beard."]{.class_s36N}[ That is the regulation for every Jew alive.]{.class_s36M} Every Jew alive had to let his hair grow so that you couldn't see the [shape of his head ]{.class_s36M}and had to let his beard grow where you didn't see the [shape of the face,]{.class_s36M} and the beard came out beyond the chin. The reason the Lord did that is because it's a "shame for a man to have long hair," and the Jews were a peculiar people to bear His [shame ]{.class_s36M}and His [reproach]{.class_s36M}. If you ever doubt that for a minute, you've got to remember the other regulation. The other regulation was every male child is to be [circumcised ]{.class_s36M}when he's eight days old. Is circumcision contrary to nature or by nature? Contrary to nature. [You're not born circumcised.]{.class_s36M}

Christ had to have long hair.[ He was a Jew.]{.class_s36M} If He cut His hair, [He violated the law.]{.class_s36M} Now, I love brother Hugh Pyle. He led me to Christ. Hugh Pyle and Jack Hyles and some others have been so upset about the hair problem, they've written these little pamphlets to prove that Christ had a [short ]{.class_s36M}haircut. That is going on all over this country. I've been in five churches in the last year where when I finished giving the stuff the pastor said, "Boy, you sure blew my message." I said, "Well, what was your message?" The pastor would say, "I've been teaching that Jesus had [short hair]{.class_s36M}." Well, where did you get that from? Oh, from some of these pamphlets by Frank Garlock of Bob Jones University and Jack Hyles and Hugh Pyle, showing that Christ had short hair. Well, [He did not.]{.class_s36M} I love the brethren and appreciate them, but if Christ had short hair [He broke the law and was a sinner.]{.class_s36M} Leviticus 19 says He was not to do it.

You see, a fellow gets the truth (it's a shame for a man to have long hair), and then [he tries to make the whole Bible teach that one truth that he knows. ]{.class_s36M}It won't work. I'm sure that when Paul wrote that to Corinth and Asia Minor and Greece, everybody understood that. In America, you couldn't understand it no matter what you did with it. Here's the thing, ["Doth not even nature itself]{.class_s36N} (horse sense) [teach you, that, if a man have long hair, it is a shame unto him?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Nature teaches you that. The question comes up how long is long? The answer is that if you are [wondering ]{.class_s36M}"how long is long," then it is too long.

Verse 15 is self-explanatory in light of the comments made on verses 4--10.

The context of verse 16 is the woman's haircut, not the man's. There is no need to get a fight, or make a doctrinal issue like the Holiness people do, on how long is long for a woman's hair.

11:17 Now in this that I declare unto you I praise you not, that ye come together not for the better, but for the worse.

18 For first of all, when ye come together in the church, I hear that there be divisions among you; and I partly believe it.

19 For there must be also heresies among you, that they which are approved may be made manifest among you.

20 When ye come together therefore into one place, this is not to eat the Lord's supper.

21 For in eating every one taketh before other his own supper: and one is hungry, and another is drunken.

22 What? have ye not houses to eat and to drink in? or despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not? What shall I say to you? shall I praise you in this? I praise you not.

In verse 17, Paul "switches gears." He has said all he is going to say about haircuts. The rest of the chapter deals with the behavior of a local congregation when they take the Lord's Supper.

From the information Paul had about the Corinthians (vs. 18), he could not give them any praise about what they were doing at the Lord's table. The Lord's Supper was supposed to bring the believers into closer fellowship with each other as they remembered the Lord's sacrifice for them and looked forward to His return. Instead, there was infighting amongst them so that the Supper was actually doing them more harm than good.

Verse 19 is a great verse. It says that there [has to be heresies in the Body of Christ]{.class_s36M} so that those who tell the truth will show up. In that way you can contrast [truth from error]{.class_s36M} and tell the [difference]{.class_s36M}. John told those to whom he wrote, ["Little children, it is the last time: and as ye have heard that antichrist shall come, even now are there many antichrists; whereby we know that it is the last time. They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would no doubt have continued with us: but they went out, that they might be made manifest that they were not all of us" ]{.class_s36N}(1 John 2:18--19).

Now, note that ["they which are approved"]{.class_s36N} are [never scholars in your major Christian schools who are involved in self-promotion.]{.class_s36M} The cross-reference is 2 Corinthians 10:18, ["For not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord com­mendeth." ]{.class_s36N}A man is ["approved unto God" ]{.class_s36N}when he studies and rightly divides the word (2 Tim. 2:15); not when he [corrects it]{.class_s36M} like modern translators do when they mutilate 2 Timothy 2:15 in the new versions.

In the Bible, the local churches copied the pattern of the Lord and disciples at the Last Supper. If you recall, what we call the Lord's Supper was not instituted until [after ]{.class_s36M}the Passover Supper. In these early local churches, a fellowship supper was substituted for the Passover Supper, with the Lord's Supper immediately following. Paul deals first with this fellowship supper. That is why he says in verse 20, ["When ye come together therefore into one place, this is not to eat the Lord's supper."]{.class_s36N}

In this fellowship supper, there were abuses taking place (vs. 21). Some were eating at home first and not bringing anything to the supper. Some were coming expecting to eat, and there would be nothing left. So the fellowship around the dinner table was lost by one group snubbing the other group.

The ["drunken"]{.class_s36N} of verse 21 can either mean having drunk all one was going to drink, or it could mean a person was actually drunk. Either way, a second abuse was taking place.

Paul's admonition, then, to this abuse of the supper (vs. 22) was if you are going to act that way and hurt those who did not have anything to bring to the supper, then everyone take that meal at home and meet solely for the Lord's Supper.

["For I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you, That the Lord Jesus the same night in which he was betrayed took bread"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 23). Although Paul was not [physically ]{.class_s36S}there the night that Christ instituted the Lord's Supper, he says he ["received]{.class_s36N} (it)[ of the Lord."]{.class_s36N} This probably took place in Arabia when he was personally trained by the Holy Spirit (Gal. 1:15--17). What follows is the account of the Lord's Supper as recorded in Matthew, Mark, and Luke.

["This is my body, which is broken for you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 24).[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, [the bones]{.class_s36M} weren't broken (John 19:36). But the [body ]{.class_s36M}was certainly broken when the soldier put the spear in it. When the soldier put the spear in His side, [the flesh]{.class_s36M} was broken[. ]{.class_s36N}So a lot of the new Bibles try to help the Lord out here, and since a bone in Him wasn't broken,[ they take that verse out.]{.class_s36M}

Now, there are three things in verse 25. First, [he took the cup, when he had supped."]{.class_s36N} The Lord's Supper took place after the Passover. You could do away with the fellowship supper that was being abused without eliminating the Lord's Supper. Next, ["as oft as ye drink it."]{.class_s36N} There is no set rule as to when a local church is to take the Lord's Supper or [how often]{.class_s36M}. In most Baptist churches, unless it is set up in the church constitution, that decision is usually left up to the pastor, who looks after the spiritual condition of his congregation. Finally, ["This do ye, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me."]{.class_s36N} It is [not]{.class_s36M} a sacrifice. It is a [memorial]{.class_s36M}. There is [nothing ]{.class_s36M}in the elements that transmits grace to the recipient, so it is not a "sacrament." What "gives grace" is the One whom you are remembering, [not the unleavened bread and juice.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 26: ["For as often as ye eat this bread]{.class_s36N} (without saying how often)[, and drink this cup]{.class_s36N} (without saying how often)[,]{.class_s36N} [ye do shew the Lord's death."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The Catholic stops right there, but the Bible goes on and says, ["ye do shew the Lord's death TILL HE COME."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Nobody should take the communion without commemorating the [second coming of Jesus Christ.]{.class_s36M} Isn't that something? These Catholics who make so much about the mass and eucharist and sacraments! [If you went to a Catholic church for 100 years]{.class_s36M} (A.D. 400--A.D. 1900), [you would never hear a sermon on the second coming of Christ.]{.class_s36M} Isn't that strange? The Lord's Supper is to show His death [till He comes.]{.class_s36M}

In 1987, I had a debate out in California with a lawyer named Karl Keating, who later on became the chief apologist for the Roman Catholic Church. (This debate is available in both audio and video formats from the Bible Baptist Bookstore.)

During that debate, I mentioned that when the Pope gives the mass, he never opens his mouth about the Second Coming of Christ. When the Pope makes his visits to the United States, he never says [anything ]{.class_s36M}about Jesus Christ coming back. When Keating got up he said, "Well, Mr. Ruckman has misrepresented us here. [Every Catholic is taught about the Second Coming of Christ."]{.class_s36M} And when he said that, there were about [forty Mexicans ]{.class_s36M}on the first three rows, and they all shook their head "No" [in unison]{.class_s36M}. You talk about Saul's sheep bleating on him (1 Sam. 15:13--14)!

All Catholics claim to take verse 27 literally; i.e., the bread is Christ's [literal body ]{.class_s36M}and the wine is Christ's [literal blood. ]{.class_s36M}But look at the verse: ["and drink this cup."]{.class_s36N} Nobody takes [that ]{.class_s36M}literally. Nobody drinks the [cup; ]{.class_s36M}they drink the [contents]{.class_s36M} of the cup. The very language itself is [symbolic]{.class_s36M}. But the Catholic is taught that when he takes that mass, if he doesn't really believe that is Christ's corpse, His dead body and His blood. He is guilty of the death of Christ, because he is chewing up a corpse. And if he does [that ]{.class_s36M}unworthily, without confession on Saturday night, then he is guilty of eating and drinking the literal blood; he has murdered Christ. So the Catholic is kept in a sweat every Sunday morning of whether he is worthy to take the mass or not. [Most of them just quit going for forty years and then call for a priest right before they die.]{.class_s36M} That's how most of them do it.

Now, let's look at this thing carefully. The ["body"]{.class_s36N} and the ["blood"]{.class_s36N} of the Lord in verse 27 is obviously [not ]{.class_s36M}his [physical ]{.class_s36M}body and his [physical ]{.class_s36M}blood because of what it says in verse 24. Was Christ's body broken at the table? [Why, of course not.]{.class_s36M} Obviously, He is talking about His spiritual body. He's not talking about His [corpse]{.class_s36M}. Look at verse 24. It has to be the spiritual body. Then in verse 27 it is spiritual blood. Where is it defined? Look at the previous chapter. First Corinthians 10:16, ["The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?"]{.class_s36N} What body? Verse 17, ["For we being many are one bread, and one body."]{.class_s36N} [It had nothing to do with the physical corpse of Jesus Christ.]{.class_s36M} Absolutely nothing. It was on a spiritual basis all the way through. Christ said,[ "the words that I speak unto you, they are spirit, and they are life"]{.class_s36N} (John 6:63). When a Catholic jumps on you and says, "You people don't believe the Bible [literally]{.class_s36M}, because we believe this liquor in this cup is literal blood and this bread is literal body. You Protestants don't believe that. You believe it's spiritual. We're literalists. We believe it and you Protestants don't." Then you hold it up to the priest's face and say, "Look here. I've got a passage that says to drink [this cup.]{.class_s36M} Do you people drink [the cup]{.class_s36M}?" That's it. That will hang him.

The next question that comes up is this: What denotes whether the man is "worthy" or not? Is anybody really "worthy"? No. So you have some people that won't take the Lord's Supper because they say, "Well, Brother Ruckman, I'm just [unworthy]{.class_s36M}." I've heard them say that. "I just don't feel like I'm worthy." [Nobody is worthy.]{.class_s36M} We're not worthy to be saved. But worthiness is [defined ]{.class_s36M}in verse 28. The man that is [worthy ]{.class_s36M}is in verse 28 and verse 31. [The man that is worthy is,]{.class_s36M} verse 28, [a man who has examined himself.]{.class_s36M} [That ]{.class_s36M}would make him worthy. ["And so let him eat of that bread, and drink of that cup."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Verse 29, ["For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(see how it is tied in with verse 27) [eateth and drinketh damnation to himself]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(look at this)[,]{.class_s36N} [not discerning the Lord's body."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Every Catholic at eleven o'clock mass [damns himself,]{.class_s36M} because when he takes that bread and that cup he thinks it is[ Jesus Christ's corpse.]{.class_s36M} And that is what it is [not]{.class_s36M}.

["The Lord's body"]{.class_s36N} is the [body of people ]{.class_s36M}who believe on Him in this age. And if you don't discern [that]{.class_s36M}, you [drink damnation every time you take it. ]{.class_s36M}What you should do is in verse 31. Judge yourself. Don't take the mass if you don't understand what ["the Lord's body"]{.class_s36N} is. Look at 1 Corinthians 12:13--20 and tell me what ["the Lord's body"]{.class_s36N} is. It is the Church, the Body of Christ. [It has nothing to do with Christ's dead corpse.]{.class_s36M} (Note: For a more detailed discussion of the mass, see [The Corrupt Catholic Cult]{.class_s36M} and [The Mass,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore; Pensacola, FL.)

Before you come to the Lord's table, you are to [examine yourself ]{.class_s36S}(vs. 28) to see if there is any unconfessed sin in your life. If there is and you partake of the Lord's Supper, you bring God's judgment on you (vs. 29). That judgment is defined in verse 30 as ["weak," "sickly,"]{.class_s36N} and ["sleep"]{.class_s36N} (death). So the Lord will judge you by making you [weak]{.class_s36S}, making you [sick]{.class_s36S}, or[ killing you.]{.class_s36S}

["For if we would judge ourselves, we should not be judged"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 31). Now, if you want to keep from getting weak and sick and dying, [judge yourself.]{.class_s36M} Don't judge the other fellow. [Judge yourself.]{.class_s36M} Some people come to a service, and they say, "Hum, there they are. Huh, so and so is not here today. That's not a very good paint job on the wall. They don't have nice ushers here. When are they going to get a parking lot? That preacher preaches too loud. I didn't like that song by the choir. That woman was off key when she sang."

They sit there, and those people will say, "Judge not, lest ye be judged"; and from the time they come in that building until the time they leave, they are doing nothing [but ]{.class_s36M}judging. [Those are professional critics.]{.class_s36M} Those people are sitting there judging all that stuff without judging [themselves]{.class_s36M}. If you don't want God to "beat the fire out of you," get down in the corner and say, "That's wrong and that's wrong, and I shouldn't have done that and I shouldn't have said this." The way to do it is take that brilliant intellectual critic that you are and put [him ]{.class_s36M}in front of a mirror and go after [him]{.class_s36M}. Then you would stay in good health. Don't make the Lord judge you.[ You do it.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 32: ["But when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord, that we should not be condemned with the world."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}God's judgment is not like the judgment of the [unsaved]{.class_s36M}. You do not go to Hell, you do not "fall from grace," you don't lose the Holy Spirit. [God's judgment on his sons is called "chastisement."]{.class_s36M} He takes out His "belt" and gives you a whipping (or as the Southerners say it, "a whuppin'"). Chastisement shows that the Lord loves and that you are His son (Heb. 12:5--8). God sends it our way so that we will correct the problem and live (Heb. 12:9). Some Christians, though, are just too tough for their own good (vs. 30).

Paul concludes his discussion on the Lord's Supper this way:

1\. All the church is to take it together (vs. 33, ["tarry one for another"]{.class_s36N}).

2\. Satisfy your hunger at home (vs. 34).

["That ye come not together unto condemnation: And the rest will I set in order when I come"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 34). The passage is aimed at a [local church]{.class_s36M}, and it is aimed at the local church as a [group.]{.class_s36M}

[]{#c188.xhtml}

## Chapter 12 {.heading_s39}

This chapter is on the gifts of the Spirit, so the charismatics have more trouble with this part of the Bible than any other part. Chapters 12--14, as a unit, deal with the gifts of the Body of Christ. Chapter 15 goes into something entirely different. Twelve, thirteen, and fourteen are one unit. It starts with 12:1, ["Now concerning spiritual gifts,"]{.class_s36N} and ends with 14:40, ["Let all things be done decently and in order."]{.class_s36N} Claude Bonham's wife, in Texas, gave us a good verse on how to deal with folks about tongues. She pointed out, ["Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels"]{.class_s36N};[ ]{.class_s36N}you know---an unknown tongue, a heavenly tongue. She pointed out to somebody that in 2 Corinthians 12 Paul was caught up to [the third heaven ]{.class_s36M}and heard ["words which it is not lawful for a man to utter."]{.class_s36N} [That's a good one.]{.class_s36M} So if they get to talking in a [heavenly language,]{.class_s36M} they are violating the Scriptures.

12:1 Now concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I would not have you ignorant.

2 Ye know that ye were Gentiles, carried away unto these dumb idols, even as ye were led.

3 Wherefore I give you to understand, that no man speaking by the Spirit of God calleth Jesus accursed: and that no man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Ghost.

["Now concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I would not have you ignorant" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 1).[ ]{.class_s36S}So they are [ignorant]{.class_s36S}. Spiritual gifts are for power and not for [character]{.class_s36S}. By that, I mean you can always tell the people that are "Gung Ho" on getting power because they don't pay much attention to the part of the Holy Spirit that deals with [character]{.class_s36S}. For example, you remember ["the fruit of the Spirit"]{.class_s36N} in Galatians 5 is ["love, joy, peace,"]{.class_s36N} and so forth. That has to do with [character]{.class_s36S}. The gifts of the Spirit have nothing to do with character. [The most carnal church in the New Testament was the church at Corinth, and they had these spiritual gifts.]{.class_s36S} So one way to spot the carnal Christian is that he is always hungering for the gifts that are connected with [power]{.class_s36S}, but not the fruit that is connected with [righteousness]{.class_s36S}.

Verse 2: ["Ye know that ye were Gentiles."]{.class_s36N} A Christian is no longer a Gentile. He's a Christian. The word ["dumb"]{.class_s36N} in the verse does not mean "stupid," but that the idols can't speak, as in the expression "dumb animals."

["Wherefore I give you to understand, that no man speaking by the Spirit of God calleth Jesus accursed: and that no man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Ghost"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3). You often hear that phrase misquoted by the charismatics as "Jesus is Lord."[ That is an expression used by the Devil]{.class_s36M} (Phil. 2:10--11). It is the Holy Ghost that leads a man to say that Jesus Christ is ["the Lord"]{.class_s36N}; that is, the one, true God. When a man says that, if he is an unsaved man, he is still led by the Holy Spirit. He's not led by the devil to say that.

["Wherefore I give you to understand, that no man speaking by the Spirit of God calleth Jesus accursed."]{.class_s36N} The account isn't given as to why he said that. There is nothing in the context in the chapter or anywhere near it that would say why he warned them about that. But it is a warning. Verse 3 indicates that somebody had been calling Him "accursed" and then claiming it was the Spirit of God.

That brings up another problem. If you are talking a language you don't understand, how do you know you're [not ]{.class_s36M}calling Jesus "accursed"? And if you are calling Him "accursed," it[ isn't ]{.class_s36M}the Spirit of God talking through you.

Now, there is only [one group of people ]{.class_s36M}that have officially [cursed ]{.class_s36M}Jesus Christ, and that is the Roman Catholic Church. The Roman Catholic Church says if any man says that [Tobit, Judith,]{.class_s36M} and[ Bel and the Dragon]{.class_s36M} are not part of the Old Testament inspired Scripture, [let him be anathema.]{.class_s36M} Let him be cursed. And Jesus never quotes from one of those books. When Jesus cites the Old Testament canon, He cites from the law and the prophets and the writings; [never the Apocrypha.]{.class_s36M} Christ did not recognize the Apocrypha as part of the Old Testament. Therefore,[ He is anathema,]{.class_s36M} according to the Roman Catholic Church. [That is the only body of organized religion that officially cursed Jesus Christ. ]{.class_s36M}Not even the Communists have done that.

12:4 Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit.

5 And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord.

6 And there are diversities of operations, but it is the same God which worketh all in all.

7 But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal.

8 For to one is given by the Spirit the word of wisdom; to another the word of knowledge by the same Spirit;

9 To another faith by the same Spirit; to another the gifts of healing by the same Spirit;

10 To another the working of miracles; to another prophecy; to another discerning of spirits; to another divers kinds of tongues; to another the interpretation of tongues:

11 But all these worketh that one and the selfsame Spirit, dividing to every man severally as he will.

Notice that the ["Spirit"]{.class_s36N} of verse 4 is the ["Holy Ghost"]{.class_s36N} of verse 3---["the same Spirit." ]{.class_s36N}Do you know why that is so important? Because when you get to verse 13, the Charismatic will tell you that the baptism of the Holy Ghost is [different ]{.class_s36M}from that of the Holy Spirit, and the Charismatic is [wrong]{.class_s36M}. Verse 13, ["For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body."]{.class_s36N}[ That Spirit is the Spirit of verse 7, 4, and 3, and that Spirit in verse 3 is called]{.class_s36M}[ "the Holy Ghost."]{.class_s36N}

Notice also how the word ["Lord"]{.class_s36N} keeps popping up. Verse 3, ["Jesus is the Lord"]{.class_s36N}; verse 5, ["but the same Lord."]{.class_s36N} You have to look out for people who keep referring to the Lord as simply "Jesus." I know that's His name. ("Blessed Jesus hold my hand," "Jesus keep me near the cross," and "Jesus is sweetest name I know" are all right; but you have to be careful of that [constant use,]{.class_s36M} because that is the use of a [man's name]{.class_s36M}.) I know it's the name ["above every name"]{.class_s36N} and ["at the name of Jesus"]{.class_s36N} every knee shall bend. [I know that.]{.class_s36M} But you still have to be careful of people who use that name [just by itself, ]{.class_s36M}because it shows the concept of the Godhead that is a little bit [humanistic]{.class_s36M}. It's a little bit emotional. [It isn't very spiritual.]{.class_s36M} In the Bible, it is the Lord Jesus. It's the Lord and the Lord Jesus Christ.

["And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5).[ ]{.class_s36M}All right, there are different kinds of gifts (vs. 4), and they are given out for different kinds of purposes (["administrations,]{.class_s36N} vs. 5), in order to accomplish different things (["operations,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 6). ["But it is the same God which worketh all in all"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6).

The expression ["manifestation of the Spirit"]{.class_s36N} has been grossly misused by modern Charismatics to include jerking, laughing hysterically, dancing around like a chicken, howling like a wolf, and fainting dead away. (See [The Clownsville Carnival,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1998.) But in the passage, the ["manifestation"]{.class_s36N} is the evident working of the Holy Spirit in the life of the believer; in the context it is displayed through the types of gifts, the administrations of the gifts, and the operations of the gifts as dispensed by the Spirit of God.[ There is not a hint of any charismaniac shenanigans anywhere in the passage.]{.class_s36M} The word ["withal"]{.class_s36N} is an archaic English word meaning "together" or "at the same time." So the picture given is a body with all of its members [working in harmony]{.class_s36M} for the good of the whole. The gifts are for the benefit of all in the local assembly, not for just a few deprived children seeking attention.

So starting with verse 8, here come the gifts.

1\. ["The word of wisdom."]{.class_s36N} That gift is certainly still in the church. [It is not a sign to Israel.]{.class_s36M}

2\. ["The word of knowledge."]{.class_s36N} That is certainly still in the church.[ That is not a sign. ]{.class_s36M}As you read down through here, every one of these gifts that is not [a sign to Israel]{.class_s36M} is still in the church and active.

Before we look at what the Bible says about wisdom and knowledge, let's get something straight. ["The word of wisdom"]{.class_s36N} and ["the word of knowledge"]{.class_s36N} have nothing to do with some charismatic fakir getting on the Boob Tube and saying, "Oh, yes! The Lord has shown me that there is someone out there in our viewing audience who has inoperable cancer. Right at this moment, God is removing that cancer. Bless you, Jesus!" (One charismatic woman preacher named Mary Twinkles used to read the contents of [unopened letters over the radio.]{.class_s36M} Don't you know that really showed the power of God!)

Have you ever noticed that when these TV evangelists have "a word of knowledge" it is just general enough to apply to one out of a hundred or so of a total viewing audience of, let's say, [twenty-five million.]{.class_s36M} If you were watching some fakir like Oral Roberts or Benny Hinn and the doctor had just told you the day before that you had inoperable cancer, you would say, "Why! That's me." You don't know that; he didn't call you by name. The chances are that out of all the people watching that program there would be [two dozen people]{.class_s36M} in the same situation. Do you mean to tell me that God would heal all of them on that one ["word of knowledge"]{.class_s36N}?

American Christians are so gullible. If that guy was really giving out [supernatural knowledge]{.class_s36M}, don't you know he could not only call you by name (Isa. 44:28, 45:1), [but he could tell you where you lived, how you were dressed, and what you had for breakfast.]{.class_s36M} Christ had no problem telling someone He had never met exactly what he was doing [a half an hour before He met him]{.class_s36M} (John 1:47­--50).

Now, what exactly are these gifts of wisdom and knowledge? [Knowledge ]{.class_s36M}is being acquainted with or knowing the facts that deal with any kind of situation. [That's knowledge.]{.class_s36M} Not all knowledge is [good ]{.class_s36M}(Job 15:2). In the Scriptures, it is usually knowledge for which you have no natural aptitude. For instance, Solomon asked for knowledge and wisdom on how to conduct himself as king; he knew he did not naturally know how to judge the people properly (2 Chron. 1:10--12). The Christian is given the knowledge of God and His glory, a knowledge which the natural man does not have (Eph. 1:17, 2 Cor. 4:6; cf. 1 Cor. 2:14--16). God used John the Baptist to give the ["knowledge of salvation"]{.class_s36N} to the nation of Israel when they had no idea who Jesus Christ was (Luke 1:77). So ["the word of knowledge"]{.class_s36N} is a gift from God available to the [New]{.class_s36M} [Testament Christian.]{.class_s36M}

Knowledge is knowing the facts, but [wisdom,]{.class_s36S} on the other hand, is [how to apply the facts you learn.]{.class_s36S} The Bible also adds a third quality not found here, and that is [understanding]{.class_s36S}. Understanding is knowing the [relationship ]{.class_s36S}between wisdom and God. Just as not all [knowledge ]{.class_s36S}is good, not all [wisdom ]{.class_s36S}is good. Satan has both wisdom and knowledge (Ezek. 28:3, 7), but he lacks [understanding ]{.class_s36S}in relation to God. His understanding profits only himself in temporal things (Ezek. 28:4). [True wisdom and understanding are defined in Job 28:28,]{.class_s36S} ["And unto man he said, Behold, the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom; and to depart from evil is understanding."]{.class_s36N}

Again, wisdom and understanding are gifts from God. A child is given wisdom by ["the rod and reproof"]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 29:15). A disciple of Christ is given wisdom under persecution to answer his tormentors (Luke 21:15). Paul prayed that God would give the Ephesian Christians ["the spirit of wisdom"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 1:17).

[Understanding ]{.class_s36M}is nearly always mentioned in relation to wisdom. Proverbs 4:7 says, ["Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding."]{.class_s36N} Solomon not only asked for ["wisdom and knowledge"]{.class_s36N} but also [understanding ]{.class_s36M}(1 Kings 3:9). He undoubtedly asked for that because that is what his father David wanted God to give Solomon (1 Chron. 22:12). David asked for [understanding ]{.class_s36M}concerning the Bible (Psa. 119:34, 73, 125). Gabriel gave Daniel [understanding ]{.class_s36M}concerning prophecy (Dan. 9:22). Paul prayed God would give Timothy ["understanding in all things"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 2:7).

So knowledge is [what ]{.class_s36M}you know. Wisdom is [how to apply]{.class_s36M} what you know. Understanding is knowing the [relationship ]{.class_s36M}between both of those and the Lord. Knowledge is not enough. Wisdom is not enough. When these new bibles say "Beware of [knowledge ]{.class_s36M}falsely so-called," they are trying to say that knowledge falsely so-called is wrong, [but knowledge that is real knowledge is right.]{.class_s36M} "Real" knowledge is not necessarily [right]{.class_s36M}. The devil has all the knowledge there is. But, he isn't [right]{.class_s36M}. So changing the word "science" to "knowledge" doesn't accomplish anything.

A lot of people have wisdom and no knowledge, and a lot of people have [knowledge ]{.class_s36M}and no [wisdom]{.class_s36M}. The devil has knowledge and wisdom. [He has no understanding.]{.class_s36M} Most uneducated people, especially in the country, have wisdom but very little knowledge. Most educated people have knowledge, but no wisdom. That's right. There are people in the country that have much more wisdom than educated people, but they don't have access to as many [facts]{.class_s36M}. Knowledge is a number of facts that you can get your hands on and grasp and understand, but that isn't necessarily wisdom. So the ["word of knowledge"]{.class_s36N} is knowing [what]{.class_s36M} to say, and the ["word of wisdom"]{.class_s36N} is knowing [how]{.class_s36M} and [when ]{.class_s36M}to say a thing.

["Faith"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9) is the third gift. Some Christians have more faith than others. It's obviously still in the church. Paul told the Romans ["to think soberly, according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 12:3). It's not a sign[.]{.class_s36N}

["To another the gifts of healing"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). This is the fourth gift listed.[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, you've got to make a distinction.

The first man that was ever healed in the Scriptures was Moses (Exod. 4:6--7). When he got [healed ]{.class_s36M}he put his hand in his bosom, and it came out leprous. Then he put it back, and it came out [whole]{.class_s36M}. God called that a [sign ]{.class_s36M}(Exod. 4:8), and ["the Jews require a sign"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 1:22). So obviously the gift of [healing ]{.class_s36M}is a Spirit gift that was only active in the church when God was dealing [with Israel at the time of the apostles, ]{.class_s36M}as He is at the time Paul writes 1 Corinthians. Apostles have all kinds of ways to get out of it. The Baptists say, "Well, now the doctors have the gift of healing." There are all kinds of ways you can mess around with it. But let's just face it, ["these signs shall follow them that believe\...they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(Mark 16:17--18). It didn't say they would put them in the cancer ward or put them under an oxygen tent or through therapy. They'll put hands on them, and ["they SHALL recover."]{.class_s36N}

Now, if a man has the "gift of healing," he puts his hands on a fellow, and the fellow recovers. He doesn't bring a tent into town and take up a collection and get on the radio. No. He just lays his hands on them, and they get healed. If I had the gift of healing and knew I had the gift of healing of the Holy Spirit, I wouldn't get a tent, I wouldn't get a radio program, I wouldn't even get on TV. It would just be a waste of time and a waste of money. I would get in an automobile and get enough gas to get me from here to Miami. [I'd go by Tallahassee and go into the cancer ward, and to Jacksonville and go into the cancer ward, and go to Orlando and go into the cancer ward, and Tampa and St. Petersburg and Miami.]{.class_s36S} When I left Miami and came up through Fort Myers and Fort Lauderdale and up to Atlanta, [I would have more money that I could carry on a train. ]{.class_s36S}That's right. I would have so much publicity I couldn't stand to read it.
