Why waste my time spending five hundred million dollars on TV programs, when, if I did that, I would get so much publicity I couldn't even buy all the newspapers in which the articles would appear? What would happen if you just suddenly cured [twenty millionaires of terminal cancer,]{.class_s36S} and they all got up out of their hospital beds and began to tell people about it?[ Do you think you would have to]{.class_s36S} [buy a television program?]{.class_s36S}

["The working of miracles"]{.class_s36N} is gift five (vs. 10).[ ]{.class_s36M}It is obviously a [sign]{.class_s36M}. The miracles begin with the calling of the Jews out of the land of Egypt. That's what they are called.

["Prophecy"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10) is the sixth of the gifts. Prophecy is not given as a [sign]{.class_s36S}. You probably still have that in the church. It's not given as a sign anywhere.

["To another discerning of spirits"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10---gift seven). Nowhere is that ever given as a [sign]{.class_s36M}. You still have that in the church. ["Discerning of spirits"]{.class_s36N} is to be able to tell whether a thing is of the Lord and whether it is not. Spiritual discernment. After the New Testament is completed, the Lord said, ["the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy,"]{.class_s36N} implying that every Christian has some type of gift of prophecy because the Holy Spirit will lead and ["guide you into all truth."]{.class_s36N}

Some people are going to be more gifted than others, and those that are gifted will be able to take the Bible and show you what is going to take place from the word of God. I would say very definitely that Clarence Larkin had the gift of prophecy. I would say that everything that Salem Kirban and Lindsey and Dr. DeHann and LaHaye and Bob Jones and Billy Graham and Charlie Fuller knew about prophecy, they got from one man.

8\) ["Divers kinds of tongues"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10).[ ]{.class_s36M}What are tongues? First Corinthians 14:22, ["Wherefore tongues are for a sign."]{.class_s36N} Then they are no longer in the Body. (For further study, get the sermon booklet [Tongues, Signs, and Healing,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1995.)

The last, and ninth, gift listed here is ["interpretation of tongues."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Now we can [spiritualize]{.class_s36M}. We can say, "Well some people are more gifted at learning [foreign languages]{.class_s36M} than others." We can say, "Some people are more gifted at interpreting [foreign languages]{.class_s36M} than others." Well, that may be true, but that is a spiritual application of the text. [That is not a doctrinal application.]{.class_s36M} In the doctrine, where it is, they are speaking of foreign tongues as a [sign ]{.class_s36M}to the [Jews.]{.class_s36M}

The word ["severally"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11) is a way of saying that the Holy Spirit takes all of these and gives them out separately; that is, one here and one there. Paul used to say to Timothy, ["Stir up the gift of God, which is in thee by the putting on of my hands"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 1:6).

When you got saved, or when you got ordained, the Lord equipped you to do a certain kind of work. In John 15:16 Christ said, ["I have\...ordained you."]{.class_s36N} The Lord equipped you and put the potentiality and latent equipment in you to do a certain kind of thing. Not every Christian finds out what it is. Paul said, ["Neglect not the gift that is in thee"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 4:14). It is up to you to find out what the Lord has given you that He hasn't given to anyone else. He has given you something that he hasn't given somebody else.

Americans are brainwashed with TV. They keep thinking "gifted people" or "This person has great charisma," the gifted. And "How is the lovely very talented singer?" It's always beautiful so-and-so or glamorous so-and-so. They ought to vary it sometime and say, "Now here is homely Loretta;[ sing it homely."]{.class_s36M} Americans keep thinking that to be "gifted" or to be "talented" is to be able to write poetry or sing or play a musical instrument or shake your stomach around. That's supposed to be [gifted]{.class_s36M}. But some people have a talent to fix automobiles, and the Lord put that stuff in them. You say, "Oh, it's just natural." No, it is not. Believe me, [it is not.]{.class_s36M}

People watch me draw, and they say, "Where did you learn to draw like that?" I don't know what to tell them. I wasn't trained for [anything ]{.class_s36M}but to be an infantry officer and a radio announcer. That's my [training]{.class_s36M}. And I'm not either one of them. All the rest of it is just brute trial and error, hit or miss. But I couldn't deny that God hasn't had a hand in it. I'll have to say that God did have a hand in it. But in giving me some talent, there's plenty that He [didn't]{.class_s36M} give me. I get sick and tired of people sitting around and saying, "Well, you're gifted and you're talented, and the Lord didn't give me anything." Half the body of Christ are much more [gifted ]{.class_s36M}than I am in many ways, but [they just don't take what they have and give it to the Lord.]{.class_s36M}

12:12 For as the body is one, and hath many members, and all the members of that one body, being many, are one body: so also is Christ.

13 For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body, whether we be Jews or Gentiles, whether we be bond or free; and have been all made to drink into one Spirit.

14 For the body is not one member, but many.

15 If the foot shall say, Because I am not the hand, I am not of the body; is it therefore not of the body?

16 And if the ear shall say, Because I am not the eye, I am not of the body; is it therefore not of the body?

17 If the whole body were an eye, where were the hearing? If the whole were hearing, where were the smelling?

18 But now hath God set the members every one of them in the body, as it hath pleased him.

19 And if they were all one member, where were the body?

20 But now are they many members, yet but one body.

21 And the eye cannot say unto the hand, I have no need of thee: nor again the head to the feet, I have no need of you.

22 Nay, much more those members of the body, which seem to be more feeble, are necessary:

23 And those members of the body, which we think to be less honourable, upon these we bestow more abundant honour; and our uncomely parts have more abundant comeliness.

24 For our comely parts have no need: but God hath tempered the body together, having given more abundant honour to that part which lacked:

25 That there should be no schism in the body; but that the members should have the same care one for another.

26 And whether one member suffer, all the members suffer with it; or one member be honoured, all the members rejoice with it.

27 Now ye are the body of Christ, and members in particular.

Verses 12--27 begin a practical discussion of the Body of Christ. We have discussed the doctrine on the Body of Christ in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Galatians---Colossians.]{.class_s36M} Here in 1 Corinthians, the Baptist Briders try to take the Body of Christ and make it a local Baptist church. There is no such thing as [the]{.class_s36M} local church anymore than there is [the]{.class_s36M} Church of Christ in the New Testament (Rom. 16:16). There is no [one]{.class_s36M} local church, much less [one]{.class_s36M} local Baptist church. There are local [churches;]{.class_s36M} Pensacola has over [eighty of them]{.class_s36M} in one town. But verse 12 says, ["the body is one"]{.class_s36N} and ["one body."]{.class_s36N} Then the Body is not the local church.

["For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13). Now, you can't make that a local church no matter what in the world you do, because ["the body is one,"]{.class_s36N} verse 12, and there are [many ]{.class_s36M}local churches. Noel Smith had an article in the [Baptist Tribune]{.class_s36M} called "The Bodies of Christ" where he tried to prove there was more than one Body and that every local church was "a Body of Christ." [About two years later he was dead.]{.class_s36M}

If you sent a letter to Pensacola and said, "Paul, an apostle, to the church at Pensacola," who would get the letter? When you start teaching that your little group is the only [one ]{.class_s36M}and the rest of them [aren't,]{.class_s36M} you are teaching heresy.

Somebody said, "The local Baptist church is the Body of Christ." OK, is it the First Baptist Church? "Oh, no." Why not the First Baptist Church? It's a Baptist church, and the first one there. "Oh, they've apostatized."

OK, Freewill Baptist. "Oh, no, they've got false doctrine."

Well, then the hardshell Baptist. "Oh, no, they've got false doctrine."

Well, come on, man, cut it out! You can't make that thing [the ]{.class_s36M}Body and Bride of Christ as the independent Bible Baptist [churches ]{.class_s36M}from Springfield. You can't do that. You can't say the Body of Christ is the independent Baptist churches from Arlington. You can't do that unless you are a [heretic]{.class_s36M}. You can't say that we're way ahead of them (all the other Baptists). "We're not connected with anything." Don't they say a local Baptist church is to be independent? We're [independent]{.class_s36M}. We're not connected with any kind of fellowship. [We ]{.class_s36M}get the letter. Do you see how crazy that is?

Now, look here. In Corinth, all the saved people met in [one group.]{.class_s36M} When you wrote to [the ]{.class_s36M}church at Corinth, you were writing to every saved person in Corinth, and they were all meeting in[ one assembly,]{.class_s36M} and they [all ]{.class_s36M}get the letter. [You can't do that any more.]{.class_s36M} They are scattered all over the country.

Ask your brethren this, when you are dealing with them, when did the local church [start?]{.class_s36M} What will they say? "The local church began with John the Baptist." "The first local church had twelve apostles in it when Christ called them out." Right? All right, if the local church is [the ]{.class_s36M}Body of Christ, [then a devil was in the Body of Christ.]{.class_s36M} Right? Wasn't Judas a member of the local church? Then ["by one spirit was the devil baptized into Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36M} You can't do that for love nor money. The local church has [unsaved ]{.class_s36M}people in it. The Body of Christ has [no unsaved people]{.class_s36M} in it. The local church is an [institution]{.class_s36M}. The Body of Christ is an [organism]{.class_s36M}.

["For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Then it's [not ]{.class_s36M}the local church. It is the one body composed of members. ["For by one Spirit are we all baptized."]{.class_s36N} Then every Christian has the baptism of the Holy Spirit;[ every one of them. ]{.class_s36M}Look at verses 29 and 30, and you answer me yes or no. ["Are all apostles? are all prophets? are all teachers? are all workers of miracles? Have all the gifts of healing? do all speak with tongues?"]{.class_s36N} Then speaking in tongues [cannot be]{.class_s36M} "the initial evidence of the baptism of the Holy Ghost," because [all do not speak with tongues, ]{.class_s36M}but all [are ]{.class_s36M}baptized by one Spirit into one body. Every Christian has been baptized by the Holy Spirit into Christ's Body, whether he talks in tongues or not. The ["drink"]{.class_s36N} of verse 13 is obviously a spiritual drinking (cf. 1 Cor. 10:4).

The next thing you need to know about the passage is 1 Corinthians is written long before Ephesians is written. So Bullinger, Stam, and O'Hare are cuckoo in trying to make you think that the ["one baptism"]{.class_s36N} of Ephesians was written when Paul suddenly realized that water baptism was [wrong]{.class_s36M}. Paul knew about the ["one baptism"]{.class_s36N} of Ephesians when he wrote 1 Corinthians 12:13. In 1 Corinthains 12:13, [Paul knew all about one Spirit and one baptism, and he wrote that to converts who had been baptized in water.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 14, ["For the body is not one member, but many."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}This verse is illustrated in verses 15--17, where Paul has different parts of a human body arguing with each other as to their importance. The point of verses 15 and 16 is that once you are in the Body of Chrsit, you are a member forever, no matter what you say or do.

An old song says, "Safe in the arm of Jesus." I've got something better than that. I'm safe [as]{.class_s36M} an arm of Jesus. I'm not [in]{.class_s36M} His arms; I'm [part of]{.class_s36M} His arms. John 10:29 says, ["No man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand."]{.class_s36N} The Christians [position]{.class_s36M} is even better than that. [I am part of His hand]{.class_s36M}. The Holiness people say, "Nobody may be able to pluck you out, but you can jump out." Well, you might if you are merely [in]{.class_s36M} His hand, but I am not just [in]{.class_s36M} His hand: I [am]{.class_s36M} His hand.

In verse 17, Paul is saying that God has given a particular job to each member of the Body of Christ. Each Christian is to do the job the Lord has set for him to do (vs. 18). If everyone did the same job, it would be utter chaos (vs. 19). For example, what if every member of a church were the pastor? Who would teach the children? Who would watch the nursery? Who would drive the buses? Who would clean the building? Why, you would never get anything done! Everyone would be trying to run the church at the same time.

In a body you have different [parts]{.class_s36S}, and every part does a different [job]{.class_s36S}. When each part does its own job in the right way, the body works in harmony, and you can get something accomplished (vs. 20).

Verse 21, ["And the eye"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(here's a Christian who is looking out for the Lord) ["cannot say unto the hand"]{.class_s36N} (a fellow who works for the Lord) ["to the feet"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}(going where God told him to go) ["I have no need of you."]{.class_s36N}

Now, the head is Jesus Himself, and He needs our feet, just as your body needs every part. There is nothing superfluous. [There is a function for every part of your body.]{.class_s36M} So there is a function for every child of God. If you are saved, there is something you are to do, and the Lord knows what you are to do and puts you in the body to do what He wants you to do.

The old time Christians had it down really good. They spoke about the church [militant ]{.class_s36M}and the church [triumphant]{.class_s36M}, and that is the best way to put it. The body of saved people [on this earth]{.class_s36M} is the church [militant]{.class_s36M}. We're engaged in warfare, and our fight is not over. The body of saved people who have [died ]{.class_s36M}and gone on to heaven are a part of the church [triumphant]{.class_s36M}. That's the best way to put it.

Verses 22--23 are about the Body of Christ and about the cohesion of the Body, how it sticks together and operates together. It shouldn't have any schism or splits in it. He likens it to a human body.

In the human body, those parts which don't seem to be very important usually get more attention. It has to do with body development. If you think some part of your body is weak, then you develop it (or padding in modern clothes and styles take care of it. It takes something that is not what you think it ought to be and makes it[ look right. ]{.class_s36M}From the standpoint of men, shoulder pads are the big thing).

The word ["comely"]{.class_s36N} in verse 24 (["comeliness,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 23) means [handsome ]{.class_s36M}or [beautiful]{.class_s36M}. The ["comely parts,"]{.class_s36N} the handsome and good looking in the Body of Christ, usually are all right. [They get the attention.]{.class_s36M} They have the friends, etc. But it is those who are uncomely, ["that part which lacked,"]{.class_s36N} to whom God gives His attention (vs. 24). Then some of the most unlovely Christians, some of the ugliest Christians have some of the greatest graces. And some of the Christians whom you think have "nothing on the ball" have more "on the ball" than you've got, because God gave them more [to make up for what they don't have.]{.class_s36M} That's how that thing goes.

I met a fellow up in Baltimore, Maryland, one time, who must have been the ugliest fellow I have ever seen in my life. He was a hunchback and had warts all over his face. He also had a club foot and a cleft palate. He could hardly talk. But he was saved, and he loved the Lord. I used to hold street meetings up there for Peter and John Bisset. While I preached, I would watch this guy do personal work. He would try to pass out tracts to people and witness to them. Most wouldn't have anything to do with him, and some would cuss him and tell him to get out of the way. But at least twice, I saw him take someone aside and lead him to Christ. Now, you take that fellow: he was so ugly that nobody would have anything to do with him. He had no wife, no mother, no daddy, no children---nobody! [But God made it up to that man by giving him more souls than He has given many Christianss who look right and are in good health.]{.class_s36M}

So if you are a Christian you are a part of Christ's body. And the Lord has the body altogether. You have to look out for that because you tend to slight things and overlook things.

For example, one of the brothers came in talking about how he was down about things and was worried about the impression he had made here at the school and the reputation he may have gotten from shooting off his mouth too much at school. Well, if I get on a student, he has shot his mouth off too much. I have already put up with a lot, because I was a new Christian once. (I don't believe the Body of Christ has really accepted me yet, and I've been saved for fifty-three years!)

When a child of God first gets saved, he makes a lot of mistakes. They're like little children. I don't expect you to be mature and understanding, and all this stuff, "right off the bat." I don't expect that of you. I expect you to make mistakes. Almost every church has a "crackpot" in the church to keep the church humble. That's right; he is the "church dummy."

A church should be a place of sanctuary from the world for the Christian. A Christian should be able to come there and have fellowship around the Lord and His word and not worry about his clothes, his car, his social status, or his income. There should be no division between the "haves" and the "have nots" in the church.

There is no ["respect of persons"]{.class_s36N} with the Lord, and there should be no ["respect of persons"]{.class_s36N} among Christian people. Every [rich Christian ]{.class_s36M}should have some [poor ]{.class_s36M}friends. It is a disgusting thing when churches divide off according to income. [It's a disgrace.]{.class_s36M} All the rich people in town are down at the First Baptist Church. That's sinfulness. That's wickedness. You're not to have respect of the person who wears ["the gay clothing"]{.class_s36N} (James 2:3). Your friendship and fellowship in Christ should be based upon your [convictions]{.class_s36M}, what you believe from the word of God. The people who believe the same should have fellowship together.[ Income should have nothing to do with it.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 26, ["And whether one member suffer, all the members suffer with it; or one member be honoured, all the members rejoice with it."]{.class_s36N} That is [ideally]{.class_s36M}. Maybe you don't think they do, but [ideally ]{.class_s36M}they do. [That is, they should.]{.class_s36M} If Arlin Horton gets a D.D., you should be glad that he is given an honorary doctorate. Rejoice with him. If your friend comes up to you and tells you she got engaged, you should look at her engagement ring and rejoice with her. What we are talking about here is an ideal situation which you have never yet known to exist. [But it should.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 27, ["Now ye are."]{.class_s36N} Definition: ["the body of Christ."]{.class_s36N} Plural, ["ye are the body]{.class_s36N} [of Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}There's the one Body. There are many churches, but there is one Body that is THE Church. ["And members in particular."]{.class_s36N}

12:28 And God hath set some in the church, first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers, after that miracles, then gifts of healings, helps, governments, diversities of tongues.

29 Are all apostles? are all prophets? are all teachers? are all workers of miracles?

30 Have all the gifts of healing? do all speak with tongues? do all interpret?

31 But covet earnestly the best gifts: and yet shew I unto you a more excellent way.

["The church"]{.class_s36N} of verse 28 is ["the body"]{.class_s36N} of verse 27. The hierarchy in the Body of Christ, then, is what follows: ["first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers, after that."]{.class_s36N} It is an order of [importance]{.class_s36M}. The [first thing]{.class_s36M} that is given importance is the apostles, and the next thing of importance is the prophets, and the next thing of importance is the teachers. ["After that miracles, then gifts of healings, helps, governments, diversities of tongues."]{.class_s36N}

The reason you should notice that verse is when your charismatic friends start giving you a hard time, point out to them that [tongues ]{.class_s36M}are the [last thing on the list. ]{.class_s36M}Ahead of tongues are Bible teachers. Teachers are ahead of the people having the gift of tongues. ["After that"]{.class_s36N} is not a time element. It is an order of importance. When he said ["after that,"]{.class_s36N} he is not talking about time, because teachers do not come before miracles. You've got Bible teachers right on down to the Rapture. So it has nothing to do with when these gifts show up in time. [The main thing to get here is that tongues are last, always.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 29, ["Are all apostles?"]{.class_s36N} No. ["Are all prophets?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}No. ["Are all teachers?" ]{.class_s36N}No. ["Are all workers of miracles?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}No.

["Have all the gifts of healing?" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 30). No. ["Do all speak with tongues?"]{.class_s36N} [No]{.class_s36M}. Any man that tells you that if you don't talk in tongues you don't have the Holy Ghost, or if you don't talk in tongues you're not saved, or any church that doesn't talk in tongues is not a saved church, [is a liar.]{.class_s36M} ["Do all speak with tongues?"]{.class_s36N} [No, they do not.]{.class_s36M} ["Do all interpret?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}No.

["But covet earnestly the best gifts"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 31).[ ]{.class_s36M}Look at that carefully. Covet what? ["The best gifts."]{.class_s36N} According to what you read, was [tongues ]{.class_s36M}one of the best gifts? No. [Then why covet it? ]{.class_s36M}Do you see how people are? They say, "Well, I just want all God has for me. I just want the gift that the Lord told us to desire." [He told you nothing of the kind.]{.class_s36M} What the Lord told you was not to desire tongues. The commandment was to covet ["the best gifts."]{.class_s36N} And tongues are not one of ["the best gifts."]{.class_s36N} The ["more excellent way"]{.class_s36N} is ["a more excellent way"]{.class_s36N} than coveting ["the best gifts,"]{.class_s36N} so if it is one of the gifts, it still isn't the[ best way.]{.class_s36M}

[]{#c1BN.xhtml}

## Chapter 13 {.heading_s39}

13:1 Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal.

2 And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge; and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing.

3 And though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing.

Now we are in for trouble. We are in a book written to the most [carnal church ]{.class_s36M}in the New Testament. We have reached the thirteenth chapter, containing thirteen verses. Watch out for the [thirteen!]{.class_s36M}

The chapter is called the "Love" chapter because, here, Paul describes the kind of love God has for the believer and which the believer is to have for others. You would think that would be a positive thing, but with the overemphasis that this generation places on [love,]{.class_s36M} the liberals and "Christian-grade-school" fundamentalists have blown this chapter completely out of proportion to its importance.

A god who is all [love ]{.class_s36M}is a [perverted god.]{.class_s36M} Love [without hate]{.class_s36M} is not real love. If you love cleanliness, you will hate dirt. If you love your wife and kids, you will hate anything that tries to harm them.

The important thing to remember about God is His [holiness]{.class_s36M}. The love of God is rooted in God's [holiness]{.class_s36M}. All of God's love, in this age, is vested in the righteous life and sacrifice of Jesus Christ. Apart from Christ, God loves no man (John 3:16; Rom. 5:8); apart from Christ, the sinner abides in God's wrath (John 3:36).

So, keeping these things in mind, let's start.

Verse 1, ["Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels."]{.class_s36N} This phrase goes back to what Paul said about the gifts in the last chapter. He is not saying that tongues is one of the[ best gifts]{.class_s36M} that the Corinthians were commanded to covet. Instead, Paul picks the least gift, the gift with which the Corinthians were having a problem (chap. 14), and says, "Suppose that I did ['speak with the tongues of men and angels.'"]{.class_s36N} He didn't, but suppose [he did.]{.class_s36M}

In the Bible, the tongue of angels is [Hebrew]{.class_s36M}. When the Lord Jesus spoke from heaven to Paul in Acts 9, He spoke in Hebrew. The Holy Spirit teaches us to call God ["Abba"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:15; Gal. 4:6); [that's Aramaic.]{.class_s36M} When the citizens of heaven rejoice over the destruction of Babylon, they cry, ["Alleluia"]{.class_s36N}; that's Hebrew (Rev. 19:1--6). The first thing ever written on earth was written in Hebrew; the Old Testament was written in Hebrew. Why [wouldn't]{.class_s36M} Hebrew be the language of heaven?

["The tongues of men"]{.class_s36N} are found in Acts 2:8, ["How hear we every MAN in our own TONGUE, wherein we were born?"]{.class_s36N} Luke lists about[ eighteen different languages ]{.class_s36M}spoken by men at Pentecost in Acts 2. Today, Wycliffe Bible Translators recognizes about 6,000 different languages spoken on earth. About 2,500 of these have at least one book of the Bible translated into them. When the Charismatics began their missionary movement, they believed that God would supernaturally give them the gift of tongues and that they would be able to [miraculously ]{.class_s36M}evangelize the whole world in ten to twenty years. They found out, though, that they had to go to language school just like anyone else. That is why you find "tongues" confined to the [inside ]{.class_s36M}of a charismatic local church.

The supposition, then, is, "Suppose I am able to speak all these languages without having the kind of love God has for the hearers" (["and have not charity"]{.class_s36N}). The modern bibles don't like that word, ["charity,"]{.class_s36N} so they change it to "love." Then they turn right around and complain that "love" doesn't express the [exact meanings]{.class_s36M} of the Greek words for "love" in the New Testament. They will go into some deep, philological discussion of "agape" love and "phileo" love to prove that you poor, stupid Bible believers can't understand your Bible [apart from the Greek scholars.]{.class_s36M}

The truth is 1) the Greek words for love are used [interchangeably ]{.class_s36M}in the New Testament and are, therefore, not as exacting as the Greek scholars would have you believe (see Sam Gipp's [The Answer Book,]{.class_s36M} Day Star Publishing, Northfield, OH, 1989, pp. 128--130), and 2) the English of the [King James Bible]{.class_s36M} does have precise words for love, and ["charity"]{.class_s36N} is one of them. [The scholars change the word to destroy your confidence in the Book.]{.class_s36M}

Now, without changing what God said, let's look at that word ["charity."]{.class_s36N} The chapter is talking about how a Christian ought to love; it is the human love a Christian shows to others. When you talk about human love, then charity is the best word to use. Charity is giving; it is a handout. In charity, [you give.]{.class_s36M} Love is giving (e.g., ["God so loved\...that he gave,"]{.class_s36N} (John 3:16).

Love that doesn't [give ]{.class_s36M}is not real [love]{.class_s36M}. Any of you folks that have been married more than two or three years know that in order to keep the love affair going you have to [give]{.class_s36M}. You have to [give in ]{.class_s36M}and [give out]{.class_s36M} and [give up.]{.class_s36M} Where there isn't that element, if you're not willing to give, and it's all just going to be take, take, take; that is not the Bible picture. So I would leave ["charity"]{.class_s36N} where it stands.

["I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal."]{.class_s36N} The idea is that even if Paul could speak every language in earth and heaven, if he did it without God's love for others, ["charity,"]{.class_s36N} all it would produce would be a cacophony of sound, just a bunch of banging and clanging around. The same idea is expressed in verse 2. If you did have enough faith to move mountains, it would be nothing without [charity ]{.class_s36M}toward others. The statement of verse 2 is pretty strong:[ "I am nothing."]{.class_s36N}

In the old days at the church at Brent, we used to have these preachers' fellowships. There was always one pastor who would stand up, and before he had gone very far in his message, he would be crying. All the other preachers would say, "Bless him, Lord." I would sit there and pray, "Kill him, Lord. Shut him up." His "love" was a stage play.

I used to live with some of the students who went to PBI. When we got home from one of those fellowships, I would go over to the cabinet below the sink and say, "And now a testimony from Bro. So-and-so!" and then I would kick all the pots and pans out of the cabinet and send them clanging and banging to the floor. That is just the way his [crying ]{.class_s36M}struck me. Now, don't get me wrong; there's nothing wrong with weeping over lost souls and having compassion on the suffering. But those characters would get up in the pulpit and say, "And (choke) the other night (sniffle) our quartet won first place in the singing competition!" and then he would break down bawling. That fellow used crying as [a tool ]{.class_s36M}just to get a reaction out of his audience. ["Charity"]{.class_s36N} had nothing to do with it.

["Though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3). That's giving! But without charity ["it profiteth me nothing."]{.class_s36N} You say, "Well, good night, if a guy gave his body to be burned and gave all he had to the poor, that [would ]{.class_s36M}be charity." But that is giving. You can [give ]{.class_s36M}without [loving]{.class_s36M}. You can't love without [giving]{.class_s36M}. When he is talking about giving his body to be burned, he is talking about [motive]{.class_s36M}. When he is talking about giving all that he has to the poor, he is talking about motive. In plainer words, if your [motive for giving ]{.class_s36M}is not charity and if your motive for offering your body to be burned is not charity, then you are going to get nothing out of it from the Lord. The Lord is going to put the work on the fire and try the work ["of what sort it is"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 3:13).

Did you ever write down on a sheet of paper what you did and [why ]{.class_s36M}you did what you did? Suppose you gave a gift recently. Write down, "I gave this gift because\..." and then tell the truth. Write down, "I gave this away because\..." and then tell the truth about it. Present your body ["a living sacrifice"]{.class_s36N} wholly acceptable unto God. "I turned myself over to the Lord." [From what motive?]{.class_s36M}

The love which God gives to us is [different ]{.class_s36M}from a gift like faith. Faith is a gift that varies in amount and grace is a gift that varies in amount. But "[the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us,]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 5:5), and you love according as you yield yourself unto God to love through you.

Charity is you loving God enough to be willing to give in at all the places where you are crossed, for ["charity suffereth long"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). If you love the Lord like you ought to love the Lord, then you will suffer for His sake and suffer through a long period of time [without complaining about it.]{.class_s36M} That is [giving]{.class_s36M}, brother.

13:4 Charity suffereth long, and is kind; charity envieth not; charity vaunteth not itself, is not puffed up,

5 Doth not behave itself unseemly, seeketh not her own, is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil;

6 Rejoiceth not in iniquity, but rejoiceth in the truth;

7 Beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things.

This passage lists [sixteen characteristics ]{.class_s36M}of charity. Seven are positive; [nine are negative.]{.class_s36M} ["Suffereth long" ]{.class_s36N}not only speaks of suffering a long time for the Lord, but putting up with something a long time. We sometimes use the word "patience" to define it. [Longsuffering ]{.class_s36M}is one of the fruits of the Spirit (Gal. 5:22). It is part of the process of Christian growth which leads to charity (2 Pet. 1:5--7). Ephesians 4:2 says, ["With longsuffering, forbearing one another in love."]{.class_s36N} The point, brethren, is that if God loved you enough to put up with your foolishness as long as He did (and does!), can't you love your brothers and sisters in Christ enough to put up with some of [their ]{.class_s36M}idiosyncracies and problems?

["Charity\...is kind"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). To whom? To the brethren" Of course, Ephesians 4:32, ["Be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you."]{.class_s36N} But it goes beyond just showing kindness to the brethren in Christ. Jesus Himself said, ["But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil. Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 6:35--36).

["Charity envieth not"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). Charity is a people relationship. If a Christian loves God as he should, then that love will overcome things like envy. Envy was one of the problems in the church at Corinth (1 Cor. 3:3) and is listed as one of the works of the flesh in opposition to the fruit of the Spirit (Gal. 5:21). It is the result of [self-glorification]{.class_s36M} (Gal. 5:26). So charity will seek to benefit others (1 Cor. 10:24). It will not vaunt (lift up) itself (vs. 4); it won't brag about itself (["is not puffed up"]{.class_s36N}). Charity doesn't act like the hypocrites in Matthew 6:2; it doesn't broadcast where, when, and how much it is giving so that men will glorify it.

["Doth not behave itself unseemly"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). Where a man loves God like he should, his [behavior ]{.class_s36M}is not improper. You take this "Southern Christian Leadership Conference" and all that bunch of unsaved, demon-possessed, hell-raising fanatics. When they put on their demonstration in Dayton, Ohio, to stop the trade at the stores, the colored men laid down in the doorways[ on their backs so the women would have to step over them going in the stores.]{.class_s36M}

That's the "Christian" conference, is it? Charity doesn't [behave itself]{.class_s36M} in an unbecoming way. Charity doesn't lead a colored man to come into a white church and walk down and test the membership to see if they will open or close the door, like White Plains, Georgia. [That isn't charity.]{.class_s36M} The fellow might be saved. So what? A lot of people are saved. [Demas ]{.class_s36M}was saved. Charity does not do that. [Charity doesn't behave itself in a way that is unbefitting Jesus Christ.]{.class_s36M}

I talked with an evangelist once. He was having a meeting in town. He said, "I've been all over town the last two or three days doing personal work with the pastor. Brent Baptist Church has produced half the Baptist churches in this town, hasn't it?" I said, "About nine of them."

He said, "Everywhere I go, I talk to these people, and everybody in this town is saved. I don't know what in the world to do about it."

Then he said, "What about those boys of yours that went into that Catholic church? Did you put them up to that?" (He was referring to one of our students interrupting the Mass.) I said, "No." (Of course, he had been told I did put them up to it.) Well, now, [charity ]{.class_s36M}doesn't behave itself in an unseemly fashion. I had to go down and apologize to that priest for what a student did. Can you imagine me apologizing to a priest? I would have rather been shot at sundown than to apologize to that bird. But I had to do it.

["Seeketh not her own."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If you love the Lord like you should, you're not worried about [your rights.]{.class_s36M} What is all this stuff about civil rights, women's rights, children's rights; what is all that stuff? It's put out by a bunch of folks talking about [love]{.class_s36M}. Those modernists are the biggest bunch of loving people in the world. Love, love, love, love. That is all you ever hear from the liberal pulpit. Be positive, be positive, be positive. Love, love, love.

How come all the people connected with that stuff are sitting under liberals? A man, who loves God like he should, doesn't worry about [his ]{.class_s36M}rights. Did you ever hear Paul say, "Whatever did I do to deserve this?" You never caught Peter, James or John looting stores, stealing TV sets, or setting towns on fire. [That isn't love.]{.class_s36M} That isn't Christian charity. Those people aren't Christians, and if they are, [they are living like the devil.]{.class_s36M}

["Is not easily provoked."]{.class_s36N} You may get provoked, but not easily. ["Thinketh no evil."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If you love God like you should you think no evil in his sight, so the Holy Spirit won't be grieved or quenched. By the same token, if you love the brethren like you should, you don't impute evil motives to them or evil things about them and make up and think up stuff about them that is bad. If you ever find your mind going that way, get it off. Turn off another way. Love covers ["a multitude of sins."]{.class_s36N}

Verse 6, ["Rejoiceth not in iniquity."]{.class_s36N} If you love God like you should, you're not happy when you hear a report of a brother falling or a Christian making a wreck of his life. ["But rejoiceth in the truth."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Look out for any man that doesn't rejoice in the preaching and teaching of the word of God. [Charity rejoices in the truth.]{.class_s36M} If you find a man spending all his life in a school correcting the word of God, he has not got ["the fruit of the Spirit."]{.class_s36N}

Verse 7, ["Beareth all things."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If you love, you can [put up with things.]{.class_s36M} You parents should understand these verses. Haven't you ever put up with a lot from your children? ["Believeth all things."]{.class_s36N} All things that are right. ["Believeth all things" ]{.class_s36N}in the sense of what's good and best. ["Hopeth all things."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}In the Bible, hope is an expectancy of the fulfilled promises of God. A Christian's hope is not in something that may or may not happen, but in something that [will]{.class_s36M} happen.

The ["all things"]{.class_s36N} are the things God has promised, like the redemption of the Christian's body (Rom. 8) or the Rapture of the church (Titus 2:13). ["Endureth all things."]{.class_s36N} A Christian who loves the Lord will endure everything the world, the flesh, and the devil throws at him. He is called on to endure ["persecutions and tribulation"]{.class_s36N} (2 Thess. 1:4), hardness (2 Tim. 2:3), ["all things for the elect's sakes"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 2:10---things like imprisonment \[2 Tim. 2:9\] and the things mentioned in 2 Cor. 11:23--28), afflictions (2 Tim. 4:5), the chastening of God (Heb. 12:7), suffering (James 5:10--11), and grief (1 Pet. 2:19).

13:8 Charity never faileth: but whether there be prophecies, they shall fail; whether there be tongues, they shall cease; whether there be knowledge, it shall vanish away.

9 For we know in part, and we prophesy in part.

10 But when that which is perfect is come, then that which is in part shall be done away.

Verse 8, ["Charity never faileth: but whether there be prophecies, they shall fail."]{.class_s36N} Why? Because they'll come to pass. ["Whether there be tongues, they shall cease."]{.class_s36N} Why? Because they were [a sign to Israel ]{.class_s36M}(1 Cor. 14:22 and 1 Cor. 1:22). ["Whether there be knowledge, it shall vanish away."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Knowledge will vanish away because there is going to be a time when you won't even need it anymore. The day will come when you will be perfect in heaven and living like Christ for ever and ever. What in the world would you need to know about how much a bushel of pears cost or how to fix a carburetor after you've been out there in heaven 20,000,000 years? It won't make any difference. You won't need knowledge for anything.

Ideally, if you are able to put God first every time and the other person second and yourself last, you will overcome every problem you run into. I guess Paul and Wesley came as close to doing it as any two men that ever lived.

["For we know in part, and we prophesy in part"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). In this world, you will never know everything. There is always something God is going to hide from you. The same is true of future knowledge, prophecy. God never reveals every minute detail about a future event.

["That which is perfect"]{.class_s36N} in verse 10 is the Second Advent. If you went to any school except this school, they would say, "['When that which is perfect is come,"]{.class_s36N} [the completion of the Bible, ]{.class_s36M}["then that which is in part shall be done away.'"]{.class_s36N} That is done to try to get rid of the tongues. Some people get so anxious to overthrow a heresy, [they ]{.class_s36M}become heretical. You can't say that knowledge is done away with at the completion of the New Testament. Somebody said, "Well, why didn't Paul say, "When he who is perfect is come." Well, the Lord is sometimes referred to in the neuter. The Lord Jesus Christ is referred to as ["it"]{.class_s36N} in Genesis 3:15. He is called ["that holy thing"]{.class_s36N} in Luke 1:35. In 1 John 1:1--2, you will find the identical expression that Paul used, ["That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled, of the Word of life; (For the life was manifested, and we have seen it)." "That which."]{.class_s36N} It's a reference to Jesus Christ.

Now, back to 1 Corinthians 13:10, and I'm going to read it two ways so you can see it. First, I'm going to read it through like it is generally [taught]{.class_s36M}. Then I'm going to read it through like it is going to have to stand.

Starting with verse 9, "['For we know in part,"]{.class_s36N} before the New Testament is complete, ["and we prophesy in part,"]{.class_s36N} before the New Testament is complete. But when the New Testament which is complete is come, ["then that which is in part"]{.class_s36N} (prophecy, knowledge, tongues)[ "shall be done away. When I was a child, I spake as a child, I understood as a child, I thought as a child: but when I became a man, I put away childish things. For now,"]{.class_s36N} before the New Testament is complete, ["we see through a glass, darkly]{.class_s36N}; [but then,"]{.class_s36N} [when the New Testament is complete, ]{.class_s36M}["face to face: now,"]{.class_s36N} [before the New Testament is complete,]{.class_s36M} ["I know in part; but then,"]{.class_s36N} [when the New Testament is complete,]{.class_s36M} ["shall I know even as also I am known."]{.class_s36N} Don't be ridiculous. You don't know yourself right now as well as God knows you. Whatever he is talking about there, when that thing takes place, you will know yourself as you are known, passive, [as God knows you]{.class_s36M} [right now.]{.class_s36M}

Do you see where he said ["face to face"]{.class_s36N}? The clincher is that the term ["face to face"]{.class_s36N} never means anything [written]{.class_s36M}. If he said ["face to face,"]{.class_s36N} he might have meant a lot of things, but I'll tell you one thing he could [not ]{.class_s36M}have meant. He could not have meant any book ever [written ]{.class_s36M}by anybody.

Second John 12, ["Having many things to write unto you, I would not write with paper and ink: but I trust to come unto you, and speak face to face."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Third John 13--14, ["I had many things to write, but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee: But I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face."]{.class_s36N}

Now, if there is one thing ["face to face"]{.class_s36N} does not mean, it does [not ]{.class_s36M}mean when the New Testament is [written]{.class_s36M}. That is what it does [not ]{.class_s36M}mean.

Now, let's go back and read it through another way. First Corinthians 13:9, ["For we know in part," ]{.class_s36N}in this life, ["and we prophesy in part,"]{.class_s36N} [in this life.]{.class_s36M} ["But when that which is perfect is come,"]{.class_s36N} the Second Advent, ["then that which is in part,"]{.class_s36N} [our life now, ]{.class_s36M}["shall be done away."]{.class_s36N} Illustration: ["When I was a child,"]{.class_s36N} hadn't grown up yet, ["I spake as a child, I understood as a child;"]{.class_s36N} I don't understand everything [right now.]{.class_s36M} ["I thought as a child: but when I became a man,"]{.class_s36N} like I'm going to be in the Second Advent,[ "I put away childish things,"]{.class_s36N} what I know now. For now in this life, before Christ comes, I'm looking ["through a glass darkly,"]{.class_s36N} the word.

James says when a fellow comes to that thing he is like a man that beholds his face in a mirror, like a fellow looking through smoked glassed. He can't see clearly. ["But then,"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}when Christ comes[,]{.class_s36M}[ "face to face: now,"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}before I meet him[,]{.class_s36M} ["I know in part; but then]{.class_s36N}[,"]{.class_s36M} when I see Him[,]{.class_s36M}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}["shall I know]{.class_s36N}[,"]{.class_s36P-0} active voice[,]{.class_s36M}[ "even as,"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}comparison,[ "also I am known."]{.class_s36N}

When I get to heaven, I will know everything about myself [the Lord knows about me right now,]{.class_s36M} and I will know everything about everybody the Lord knows about [them]{.class_s36M}. I will have [perfect knowledge,]{.class_s36M} complete knowledge, and there won't be anything [partial ]{.class_s36M}anymore. When I go down the street in heaven and see the 2,000 martyrs that got killed in Holland when Wallenstein came in and burned them, I will greet them [by name,]{.class_s36M} and I will love them [as much as I love my own family right now.]{.class_s36M} When I get to heaven and see Ignatius, I will know him at sight [without ever having seen him, ]{.class_s36M}and I know immediately that it is him and [all about him ]{.class_s36M}and greet him as good or better [than I greeted my own mother and daddy.]{.class_s36M} If it weren't that way, Heaven would be Hell in twenty-four hours.

So when faith has turned to [sight ]{.class_s36M}(Second Advent) and hope has been [fulfilled ]{.class_s36M}(Second Advent), [charity ]{.class_s36M}remains (vs. 13). The love of God does not have an [ending]{.class_s36M}. So charity is [greater ]{.class_s36M}than faith, and charity is greater than hope. It goes without saying, then, that charity is greater than any of the gifts mentioned in 1 Corinthians 12:28--30. It is the ["more excellent way"]{.class_s36N} of 1 Corinthians 12:31. Naturally, it is greater than tongues, so [all]{.class_s36M} spiritual gifts must be exercised with charity (1 Cor. 14:1).

[]{#c1DK.xhtml}

## Chapter 14 {.heading_s39}

14:1 Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts, but rather that ye may prophesy.

2 For he that speaketh in an unknown tongue speaketh not unto men, but unto God: for no man understandeth him; howbeit in the spirit he speaketh mysteries.

3 But he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort.

4 He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself; but he that prophesieth edifieth the church.

5 I would that ye all spake with tongues, but rather that ye prophesied: for greater is he that prophesieth than he that speaketh with tongues, except he interpret, that the church may receive edifying.

Notice the two commands of verse 1:[ "follow after charity"]{.class_s36N} and ["desire spiritual gifts."]{.class_s36N} Charity is a ["way"]{.class_s36N} (12:31) in which you walk. Along that ["way,"]{.class_s36N} there are gifts that God can give you to accomplish the purpose to which He has called you. If you want to see the distinction, turn to Psalm 103:7, ["He ]{.class_s36N}(God)[ made known his WAYS unto Moses, his ACTS unto the children of Israel."]{.class_s36N} The spiritual gifts correspond with the ["acts"]{.class_s36N} of God in that passage.

In other words, the spiritual man will see the [way]{.class_s36M} God wants him to live; along that way God will perform certain [acts ]{.class_s36M}through the [gifts]{.class_s36M} the Lord gives to that man. The carnal man is only concerned with the gifts so he can be a showoff. I'll tell you right now, if you are given a choice between [knowing God]{.class_s36M} and [seeing God's works,]{.class_s36M} you are better off choosing to [know Him.]{.class_s36M}

Now, among the gifts, Paul recommends one over all the rest, and that is the ability to prophesy. Prophecy, then, is among ["the best gifts"]{.class_s36N} (12:31) that a Christian can get. In the book of Acts, prophecy is still the ability to foretell the future by the supernatural revelation of Jesus Christ. Agabus was the best known of the prophets in the early church (Acts 11:28, 21:10). All of the New Testament writers give prophecies somewhere in their works, but when the Apostle John closes out the one book of the New Testament that is entirely dedicated to prophecy, he writes, ["the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 19:10).

So there are some things that every true Christian should know about the future: he should know that he has eternal life as a present possession, he should know that he is destined for the New Jerusalem, he should know the eternal destination of the lost, he should know the certainty of the return of Christ. Moreover, he should tell others about those things. A prophet doesn't just [know]{.class_s36M} the future; he [broadcasts]{.class_s36M} it to others.

In verse 2, Paul begins to deal with the one gift that was giving the Corinthians such a fit---tongues. In the New Testament, there are ["new tongues"]{.class_s36N} (Mark 16:17); i.e., languages that are new to the speaker which he has never learned before. There are ["other tongues"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 2:4; 1 Cor. 14:21); those are languages not native to the speaker, which he may (1 Cor. 14:18) or may not (Acts 2:4) have learned, but which are understood by the hearers (Acts 2:8). Here in 1 Corinthians 14, Paul speaks of ["unknown tongues."]{.class_s36N} These are languages that are [unknown to everyone in the congregation]{.class_s36M} and, therefore, need an interpreter, whether it be the speaker (vs. 13) or [someone else]{.class_s36M} (vs. 27).

The Corinthians were talking in languages which nobody in the congregation knew. If someone is doing that then they ["speaketh not unto men, but unto God."]{.class_s36N} The only one who understands the language is God. And have no doubt about it, what Paul is talking about here is a [language ]{.class_s36M}(13:1), not charismatic gibberish.

Now, if you are speaking in an [unknown ]{.class_s36M}language, then you are speaking ["mysteries"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2). These are not mysteries as in 1 Corinthians 4:1; these are mysteries insomuch as no one understands what you are saying. Look at verse 16, ["Else when thou shalt bless with the spirit, how shall he that occupieth the room of the unlearned say Amen at thy giving of thanks, seeing he UNDERSTANDETH not what thou sayest?"]{.class_s36N}

Notice the second thing about this. ["Howbeit in the spirit he speaketh mysteries."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}In the what?[ Well, that isn't the Holy Spirit.]{.class_s36M} There isn't a charismatic in this town who could ever read that second verse in this chapter. There was not [one word ]{.class_s36M}in that passage about the Holy Spirit. Not a word. Turn back to 1 Corinthians 2:11---same book by the same author---and look at what he is talking about. There isn't any charismatic who ever lived who could read [English]{.class_s36M}. They are like the Church of Christ people. They have the most terrible time reading their own language. First Corinthians 2:11, ["For what man knoweth the things of a man."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}There's a man understanding a man. ["Save the spirit of man which is in him? even so the things of God knoweth no man, but the Spirit of God."]{.class_s36N}

Back to chapter 14. ["Howbeit in the spirit he speaketh mysteries."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If you pick up a short-wave set and run the dial across there and pick up a fellow coming through there talking a tongue that nobody in the room knows, he is talking in an [unknown ]{.class_s36M}tongue. Amen? That is all there is to that.

Another way we know this interpretation is true is to look at verse 14. Watch this same thing following through. ["For if I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}See that small "s" right in the context? ["My spirit prayeth, but my understanding is unfruitful."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, here is how a charismatic [reads ]{.class_s36M}that. "If I pray in an unknown tongue my spirit prays, [but I don't understand what I am saying." ]{.class_s36M}That is not what the verse says. It said, ["If I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit prayeth, but my understanding."]{.class_s36N} I understand what I'm saying. ["My understanding is unfruitful."]{.class_s36N} My understanding doesn't bear [any fruit.]{.class_s36M} Why? Because the guy out there doesn't know what I'm saying. How do you know that is the right interpretation? Verse 16. Look at it. Look at verse 17.

In those two verses, the [speaker ]{.class_s36M}knows he is giving thanks to God, but the [hearer ]{.class_s36M}does not.

One of my church ladies was in her back yard hanging out clothes one time, and Brother Summerall's wife was out in her backyard. That's the pastor's wife out there at Liberty (a charismatic church in Pensacola). Mrs. Summerall was going up and down that yard [reading a piece of paper]{.class_s36M} out loud, "Hobbly gobbely blubbely hostalashondi untie a bowtie sheena amaly sheemy." My church member said, "What are you doing?" Mrs. Summerall said, ["I'm practicing my tongues." ]{.class_s36M}My church member said, "Practicing your tongues?" Mrs. Summerall said, "Yes, if you don't [exercise ]{.class_s36M}your gift, you will [lose ]{.class_s36M}it." I'll tell you, brethren, when you find a thousand people going out to hear that kind of Bible study, you have got a sign from God that this town has gone off its rocker. You can't think like that unless you are [nuts]{.class_s36M}. "Exercise your gift" reading a piece of paper?! You call "exercising a gift" memorizing your hobbely gobbley fubble bubble?

When I speak right now out of my mouth (I'm a man. That's a [man's]{.class_s36M} mouth), those words coming out of my mouth are a [man's words. ]{.class_s36M}All right, I'm speaking with the spirit of man. Now, if I open my mouth and I'm prayed up and I'm yielded to the Holy Spirit, when those words come out, the Holy Spirit comes out with them, and the Spirit of the Lord comes out of my mouth. If I'm not "prayed up" and I'm led by the devil in what I'm going to say and am deliberately or purposely lying and misrepresenting the truth, when I open my mouth, the spirit of the devil comes out of my mouth. If I am just giving [my own opinion]{.class_s36M} and my own ideas, that is the [spirit of man]{.class_s36M} coming out. Every time you open your mouth, [one of three spirits comes out of your mouth]{.class_s36M}. One is the human spirit, the spirit of man. One is the Lord's spirit, the Holy Spirit. One is the devil's spirit, an unclean spirit. That is the way that thing works. What he is talking about here is [men ]{.class_s36M}speaking a [language]{.class_s36M}.

Where in the Bible did God tell anybody to try to get "tongues"? You haven't found it yet, and you have been through thirteen chapters of 1 Corinthians. Nobody is told to covet tongues yet or find out how to get it, or [try ]{.class_s36M}to get it or [want ]{.class_s36M}it. First Corinthians 14 is a whole chapter written correcting the [misuse of tongues.]{.class_s36M} There is not [one positive thing]{.class_s36M} said about tongues anywhere in the New Testament.

On the other hand, though, Paul [has ]{.class_s36M}told you to covet the gift of [prophecy]{.class_s36M}. Tongues may speak ["mysteries"]{.class_s36N} that no one can understand, but [prophecy ]{.class_s36M}["speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, shouldn't that be far enough if the person is dedicated? Stop here and think for a minute. Suppose all you had of this chapter was verses 1, 2, and 3. Suppose that is all you had. Would you go around trying to get tongues? Isn't it amazing the weight these people put on verse 2 without looking at verse 1 and verse 3? If you knew by doing one that you edified people and exhorted people and [comforted ]{.class_s36M}people, would you have to guess which one the Lord wanted you to have? Just suppose they are right. Just suppose I sat here and hobbley bobblye gobbly fubble dubble to edify myself. Suppose I do. What would [that ]{.class_s36M}mean? That would mean I was [selfish]{.class_s36M}. If a man prophesies, he edifies others and comforts them and exhorts them. If anything the tongues speakers said was so, what they had wouldn't be worth five minutes of your time trying to get.

Verse 4, ["He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Whom? ["Edifieth himself."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}This brings up an interesting problem. If a person doesn't know [what he is saying,]{.class_s36M} how does he edify [himself]{.class_s36M}? Let's see if we have the right interpretation here. Verse 7, ["Even things without life giving sound, whether pipe or harp, except they give a distinction in the sounds, how shall it be known what is piped or harped?"]{.class_s36N} Verse 9, ["So likewise ye, except ye utter by the tongue words easy to be understood, how shall it be known what is spoken?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, how do you edify yourself by just banging on the piano? Just banging on the keys has no distinction of sound. You can't say that edifies [anybody]{.class_s36M}. That wouldn't edify a cat walking across there. Even so, when that man talks in tongues, if he doesn't understand what he is saying himself, he isn't kidding anybody but himself. He's [not ]{.class_s36M}edifying himself. He is just scrambling his mind.

Back to verse 3, ["But he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}That's to give others light. ["And exhortation."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}That's to stir them up. ["And comfort."]{.class_s36N}

[14:4 "He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}He doesn't exhort himself and he doesn't comfort himself. He only profits one point out of the three. He is only one third as good as the other. [He's just confined it to himself. ]{.class_s36M}Prophecy, though, edifies the whole church.

["I would that ye all spake with tongues"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5).[ ]{.class_s36S}I told you a moment ago that there is not one positive verse in the New Testament. The first part of verse 5 seems to say exactly the opposite. Of course, that is not the entire verse. The ["but rather"]{.class_s36N} indicates that the thought is continued. ["But rather that ye prophesied: for greater is he that prophesieth than he that speaketh with tongues."]{.class_s36N} The clause ["I would that ye all spake with tongues"]{.class_s36N} is negated by Paul's statement that he would [rather ]{.class_s36S}they prophesy. The one exception (and the exception [proves]{.class_s36S} the rule) is if one is able to [interpret ]{.class_s36S}tongues (his own or someone else's) so that the whole church may profit from the message given in tongues.

14:6 Now, brethren, if I come unto you speaking with tongues, what shall I profit you, except I shall speak to you either by revelation, or by knowledge, or by prophesying, or by doctrine?

7 And even things without life giving sound, whether pipe or harp, except they give a distinction in the sounds, how shall it be known what is piped or harped?

8 For if the trumpet give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?

9 So likewise ye, except ye utter by the tongue words easy to be understood, how shall it be known what is spoken? for ye shall speak into the air.

10 There are, it may be, so many kinds of voices in the world, and none of them is without signification.

11 Therefore if I know not the meaning of the voice, I shall be unto him that speaketh a barbarian, and he that speaketh shall be a barbarian unto me.

12 Even so ye, forasmuch as ye are zealous of spiritual gifts, seek that ye may excel to the edifying of the church.

Verse 6, ["If I come unto you speaking with tongues."]{.class_s36N} He's admitting he [can ]{.class_s36M}speak in tongues. He knew Hebrew, Greek, and Latin for certain and maybe five or six more languages (see vs. 18). ["What shall I profit you, except I shall speak to you either by revelation."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Something God showed me. ["Or by knowledge."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Something I know. ["Or by prophesying."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Telling the future. ["Or by doctrine."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Something that is true from the word of God. When you help the saints out, you help them out by giving them something God showed you or something you know to be so or something about the future or Bible doctrine.

In verse 7, Paul gives the example of musical instruments. If I go over to the piano (["harp"]{.class_s36N}) or organ (["pipe"]{.class_s36N}) and start banging on the keys, how is anyone to know what is being played? You can't know.

Now, if you only had 1 Corinthians 14 down to verse 7, what would you think he is trying to say? It is amazing how they hop, skip, and jump. What if you just had those seven verses? Would you have any doubt in your mind what he thought about tongues. Is he trying to get something across to you? What I've got so far tells me to [look out]{.class_s36M} for tongues, and [especially ]{.class_s36M}tongues that nobody understands. That's what I've gotten so far if I can read.

Verse 8, ["For if the trumpet give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Nobody. In the military, you have a trumpet call to get up (revelry), a trumpet call to go to sleep (taps), a trumpet call to charge, a trumpet call of retreat, a trumpet call for just about everything. In the Navy it's pipes or bells. I read of a bunch of cavalry horses one time that were trained to dance at some show. When they got out to the next battle, and the trumpet started, the horses thought they were supposed to [dance ]{.class_s36M}and didn't charge. That's an "uncertain sound." He says you are to give a [distinct ]{.class_s36M}sound.

The lesson they taught us in the Army (OCS) was to give a [clear ]{.class_s36M}command. They would stress that thing to the point that they said, "I don't care if you give the [wrong ]{.class_s36M}command, make it [plain.]{.class_s36M}" That thing was driven into us and driven into us. They would say give it [clear ]{.class_s36M}and project it; breathe out when you command. You guys that preach on the street have probably learned that. If you haven't learned it, you will learn it.

In regards to that ["uncertain sound"]{.class_s36N} in verse 8, do you know what you have in this town? You have 2,000 Christians just as batty as a bat in the belfry, sitting around trying to escape persecution by dealing with Christians, and trying to get out of witnessing by talking only to Christians and trying to get out of persecution [by talking other Christians out of their salvation. ]{.class_s36M}They are getting around on a "lovey dovey, love me, I love you, we love everybody" testimony and becoming liberal in their theology and overthrowing the word of God and getting rid of it. They are talking where nobody knows what in the world they are saying. Nobody is going to prepare themselves for battle listening to something like that. That's what you have in Laodicea.

Verse 9 is self-explanatory.

Verse 10 should end any idea of tongues being a bunch of mumbo-jumbo jibberish. Paul calls tongues ["voices in the world."]{.class_s36N} Moreover, these ["voices"]{.class_s36N} have ["signification."]{.class_s36N} They [mean]{.class_s36M} something to someone somewhere on earth. If you don't know is being said, then the speaker is ["a barbarian"]{.class_s36N} to you (vs. 11). Today, we think of a[ "barbarian"]{.class_s36N} as an uncultured oaf. But in the Bible, a barbarian was someone who didn't know Greek or Latin. A ["barbarian"]{.class_s36N} was a foreigner to the languages of civilization.

Now, verse 12, there is nothing wrong with being ["zealous of spiritual gifts."]{.class_s36N} In fact, Paul tells you to ["seek"]{.class_s36N} them if you want them---[with one stipulation.]{.class_s36S} You are to seek after those gifts that ["EXCEL to the edifying of the CHURCH."]{.class_s36N} If that's the case, then [DON'T ]{.class_s36S}SEEK TONGUES! Tongues edify only the speaker (vs. 4), [not the church.]{.class_s36S}

14:13 Wherefore let him that speaketh in an unknown tongue pray that he may interpret.

14 For if I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit prayeth, but my understanding is unfruitful.

15 What is it then? I will pray with the spirit, and I will pray with the understanding also: I will sing with the spirit, and I will sing with the understanding also.

16 Else when thou shalt bless with the spirit, how shall he that occupieth the room of the unlearned say Amen at thy giving of thanks, seeing he understandeth not what thou sayest?

17 For thou verily givest thanks well, but the other is not edified.

18 I thank my God, I speak with tongues more than ye all:

19 Yet in the church I had rather speak five words with my understanding, that by my voice I might teach others also, than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue.

If you do speak in a tongue that no one else knows (vs. 13), then you had better pray that God will let you be [bilingual ]{.class_s36M}and give you the ability to take what you can get in one language and [put it in another.]{.class_s36M}

We have already discussed verse 14 in the comments on verse 2, but just notice, once more, that the person praying [has understanding of what he is saying,]{.class_s36M} verses 16 and 17. He knows he is blessing (vs. 16); he knows he is giving thanks (vs. 17). The ["understanding is unfruitful"]{.class_s36N} insomuch that it is not bearing any fruit [among those who hear. They]{.class_s36M} don't understand it and get anything from it. The charismatic interpretation is that the one who prays ["in an unknown tongue"]{.class_s36N} doesn't understand what [he ]{.class_s36M}is praying.

The [Living Bible]{.class_s36M} reads, "For if I pray in a language I don't understand, my spirit is praying, [but I don't know what I am saying.]{.class_s36M}" All of the rest of the new Bibles fall into "rank and file" with this private teaching of the Pentecostals, who are so deluded that they don't understand what they are saying when they "speak in tongues." When you change [one word]{.class_s36M} of a [King James Bible,]{.class_s36M} you are in danger of losing sound doctrine. So the first rule of Bible interpretation is "DON'T CHANGE WHAT THE BIBLE ([AV,]{.class_s36M} 1611) SAYS"---[not even one word.]{.class_s36M}

Now, the gifts are being exercised in the local assembly. There is no such thing as a "private prayer language" being taught here. The prayer of verses 14 and 15 is being offered in the church. In light of that (vs. 15---["What is it then?"]{.class_s36N}), the goal is to be understood by [those in the congregation]{.class_s36M} whenever you open your mouth. If you pray, make sure [the others]{.class_s36M} can understand you. If you sing, make sure that [others ]{.class_s36M}can understand you.

["Else when thou shalt bless with the spirit, how shall he that occupieth the room of the unlearned say Amen at thy giving of thanks, seeing he understandeth not what thou sayest?"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16).[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, here are the new Bibles. [Phillips]{.class_s36M}, "If I pray in a 'tongue' my spirit is praying but my mind is inactive." Then he is in a passive state. If he is in a passive state, it is an opening for [demons]{.class_s36M}. The[ New English Bible]{.class_s36M}, "If I use such language in my prayer, the Spirit in me prays, but my intellect lies fallow." That's a fine, "updated," modern version to get rid of this archaic Elizabethan English. "My intellect lies fallow. What then? I will pray as I am inspired to pray, but I will also pray intelligently. I will sing hymns as I am inspired to sing, but I will sing intelligently, too." [Phillips]{.class_s36M}, "I am therefore determined to pray with my spirit and my mind, and if I sing I will sing with both spirit and mind." [Footnote]{.class_s36M}: "This is the sole instance of the translators departing from the accepted text. I felt bound to conclude from the sense of the next three verses that here we have either a [slip of the pen on the part of Paul or probably a copyist's error.]{.class_s36M}" Or probably, he is a charismatic devil. There are all kinds of ways to look at it.

Let me explain some things here for a minute. I've talked to you about this before, but I am going to say it again. This great charismatic flapdoodle has been aimed at getting young people. [It is a young people's movement.]{.class_s36M} Largely connected with it are Kathryn Kuhlman and Dave Wilkerson and The Logos Publishing Company out in California and Pat Boone. Its background is the drug culture, "the children of God," and the "Jesus people." The reason why it is so popular among those people is that it is mostly [emotionalism]{.class_s36M}.

Now, don't misunderstand me. There is nothing wrong with God having your [emotions]{.class_s36M}. There is nothing wrong with being happy about God, and there is nothing wrong with praising God. There is nothing wrong with [feeling good]{.class_s36M} about God. All that is good stuff. But you have to be careful [when you lose control of your nervous system.]{.class_s36M} Nothing in the world will mess up your nervous system any quicker than [drugs]{.class_s36M}. A lot of those people that were on drugs, when they got saved, had a great [emotional experience, ]{.class_s36M}and it affected their [nervous system]{.class_s36M}. Their nervous system wasn't functioning properly and their nervous system came all apart, and the "tongues" began to come out. As soon as they did, somebody got hold of them and [coached them.]{.class_s36M} That's how they got in the thing. That is why it is so hard to straighten them out on it, because if you ever connect that with your [salvation ]{.class_s36M}or the [power of God]{.class_s36M} you keep thinking, "Well, if I deny that experience, I've denied the power of the [Holy Ghost.]{.class_s36M}" And then they've got you. And you think, ["If I ever criticize that or go back on it, I've committed the unpardonable sin."]{.class_s36M} Then they have really got you.

To encourage your heart, let me say this: When I got saved, my nervous system wasn't shot, but it was only a miracle of God that it wasn't. I've been living on "borrowed time" since I was about thirty-five, and if any man ever tried to ruin himself, I tried in those days. About a week after I got saved I had an "experience with the Lord," with the Holy Spirit (I suppose), in a certain prayer situation. Something happened to me which I would not describe to you. I've thought about that many times, and if I wanted to start a new cult I could just claim [that thing there ]{.class_s36M}was "the baptism of the Holy Ghost." But that thing had much more of an [immersion element ]{.class_s36M}in it than just talking in tongues. It had much more of a [blood element]{.class_s36M} in it than just goose pimples. But because I know the Bible like I do and know the Lord like I do, I would never put that experience to anybody as something [they ]{.class_s36M}needed or should have or [should seek,]{.class_s36M} or that it matches anything in Scripture that is for a Christian. I would never bring it up because it would [mislead you]{.class_s36M} and [misguide you.]{.class_s36M} That is something the Lord had for me in the condition I was in, and if I had had that experience apart from the finished work of Jesus Christ I would go to Hell with it.

Now, you get saved by faith, and you [begin ]{.class_s36M}by faith, and you [walk ]{.class_s36M}by faith, and you [live ]{.class_s36M}by faith. You don't want to put too much weight on those other things. Have you ever noticed how sometimes we can get to a "high point" in these services on Sunday? You get the [song service]{.class_s36M} going and things get going, man. They really get moving. But you can't count on it. Have you noticed that?

Then you go to work Monday morning, and you're [laid off.]{.class_s36M} Your wife has a baby and you have [no insurance.]{.class_s36M} Down on the bottom again. There is no way that it is safe to depend upon your [emotions]{.class_s36M}. So if you had a great emotional experience, praise the Lord. I hope you have many more of them, but don't bet ten cents on it. [Depend on the Bible.]{.class_s36M} The reason why a charismatic never knows whether he is saved or not is once you place the tongues as evidence of the baptism, what happens when you [lose ]{.class_s36M}the tongues? You've lost the [baptism]{.class_s36M}.

We have already gone over verse 17 twice, now, and we have already seen in verse 6 that Paul spoke more than one language (vs. 18). In light of that fact, look at verse 19.

[Yet in the church I had rather speak five words with my understanding, that by my voice I might teach others also, than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 19).[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, how many words have I written in this Commentary so far? All right, ["I had rather speak five words with my understanding, that by my voice I might teach others also, than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue."]{.class_s36N}

How do you fellows account for all this argument about tongues being right or wrong with[ that verse]{.class_s36M} looking you right in the face? What are you going to do with [that ]{.class_s36M}thing? I mean, if you granted that all these people who have tongues are baptized by the Holy Ghost and speaking by the Holy Spirit, that wouldn't amount to enough attention to be worth your time to go and look at it. If every tongue talker in this town was speaking in genuine tongues, [it wouldn't be worth your time to waste five minutes.]{.class_s36M} If you could stand up in a building and say [five words]{.class_s36M} a man can [understand]{.class_s36M}, you would catch up even with them for a whole week. That's right. In this Commentary, I have done more "good" to the Body of Christ than every charismatic in Pensacola has done for the past [ten years]{.class_s36M}---according to verse 19. When you say a thing like that, somebody just blows their top. "Well, who does he think he is\..." blah blah blah blah. [Verse 20 is for that somebody.]{.class_s36M} Boy, he sure knows how to place them, doesn't he?

14:20 Brethren, be not children in understanding: howbeit in malice be ye children, but in understanding be men.

21 In the law it is written, With men of other tongues and other lips will I speak unto this people; and yet for all that will they not hear me, saith the Lord.

22 Wherefore tongues are for a sign, not to them that believe, but to them that believe not: but prophesying serveth not for them that believe not, but for them which believe.

Verse 20---don't be a baby all your life: GROW UP! If you're going to get mad at me and throw a fit and kick the slats out of your crib, OK; but for heaven's sake, [get what I say]{.class_s36M} and understand it like [a man.]{.class_s36M}

The question arises, then, is there ever a legitimate use for tongues in the life of a New Testament Christian. The answer is given in verses 21--22. Verse 21 is a quotation of Isaiah 28:11.[ "This people"]{.class_s36N} in both passages[ is not a reference to any Christian. ]{.class_s36M}There is nothing there about two Christians speaking to each other in tongues. I saw a video of Kenneth Copeland and Rodney Howard Brown, two charismatic TV fakirs, telling jokes to each other in tongues. They must have been because each would [laugh ]{.class_s36M}at what the other was saying.[ That is not Christian, that is not New Testament, and that is not Bible.]{.class_s36M} That is two charismatics making fools out of each other and causing [unbelievers ]{.class_s36M}to blaspheme God and salvation because of [their foolishness.]{.class_s36M}

You charismatics who make such a big "to do" in verses 4--11, 13, 19, 21, 24, 26, 28, 37, and 39; why don't you ever spend any time in verses 21 and 22. The quote is from ["the law." "This people"]{.class_s36N} are [Jews]{.class_s36M}, not Christians. ["The Jews require a sign"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 1:22), and, lo and behold, in the context of ["this people,"]{.class_s36N} up shows verse 22, ["wherefore tongues are for a sign."]{.class_s36N}

In the context of verse 22, the ["sign"]{.class_s36N} is to an [unbeliever]{.class_s36M}, but when you see how tongues were used in the book of Acts, it becomes clear that the [unbeliever ]{.class_s36M}is an unbelieving JEW.
