In Acts 2, the apostles speak in tongues to convince a bunch of unbelieving Jews [that they have crucified their Messiah. ]{.class_s36M}In Acts 10, a group of saved Jews hears a family of newly saved Gentiles speak in tongues [so that they will believe that God has given His Holy Spirit to the Gentiles---without baptism.]{.class_s36M} In Acts 19, there are some disciples of John who had not yet believed on Christ and had never heard about the Holy Ghost. Upon receiving Christ and being baptized, [they spoke in tongues to show them that Christ had indeed given them the Holy Ghost.]{.class_s36M}

So tongues are a sign to [Israel ]{.class_s36M}no matter where you find them. They are given to make believers out of them. Moreover this sign was given to [the apostles]{.class_s36M} and their [direct converts]{.class_s36M} (Mark 16:17), and the sign [disappeared ]{.class_s36M}with people like Timothy, Mark, and Luke. Now, God can heal a fellow even though the [sign of healing]{.class_s36M} is no longer there, and God could enable a man to talk in a different language in certain situations. But the gift of tongues is no longer here. [The signs are past tense.]{.class_s36M}

As far as tongues in the Corinthian church goes, the Lord granted the gift to that church to witness to the [unsaved Jews ]{.class_s36S}of that city. You will remember that the original meeting place for the church was in a ["house joined hard to the synagogue"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 18:7). The problem was that the Corinthians tried to bring tongues into the assembly as proof of their superiority over each other. Paul's purpose in chapter 14 is to bring tongues back to its [original function ]{.class_s36S}and importance: a witnessing tool[ to the Jews]{.class_s36S} and the least of all gifts.

14:23 If therefore the whole church be come together into one place, and all speak with tongues, and there come in those that are unlearned, or unbelievers, will they not say that ye are mad?

24 But if all prophesy, and there come in one that believeth not, or one unlearned, he is convinced of all, he is judged of all:

25 And thus are the secrets of his heart made manifest; and so falling down on his face he will worship God, and report that God is in you of a truth.

26 How is it then, brethren? when ye come together, every one of you hath a psalm, hath a doctrine, hath a tongue, hath a revelation, hath an interpretation. Let all things be done unto edifying.

The problem of tongues in the congregation at Corinth was [worsened ]{.class_s36M}by the fact that everyone was trying to speak in tongues during the meeting (vs. 23). If you were an unsaved fellow and happened into the service and heard that mess going on, what would you think. Why, you would think exactly what Paul says you would think in verse 23:[ ]{.class_s36N}"These people are a bunch of kooks."

But there has been many an unsaved person who has heard a sermon on the return of Christ, or has read a book by Hal Lindsay on prophecy, or has seen some Christian movie on the Revelation and has come to Christ (vs. 24). The Commentary which I consider to be the [least ]{.class_s36M}of all my commentaries is the one on Revelation, yet, that is usually the one most people get their hands on first. I have had several testimonies of people getting [saved ]{.class_s36M}by reading it. Since my [Apocalypse]{.class_s36M} book came out, I have had parents write me telling how their children got saved because they didn't want "those monsters" or "that big lizard" (referring to the picture of the Devil) to get them. Well, that's what prophecy does: it [convicts and it judges]{.class_s36M} (vs. 24). For the Christian, it [edifies ]{.class_s36M}(vss. 22, 5).

["And thus are the secrets of his heart made manifest; and so falling down on his face he will worship God, and report that God is in you of a truth" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 25). This is a good verse to apply to Bible preaching. When the Holy Spirit is in a sermon, an unsaved person will think the preacher has been spying on him. That's because the Holy Spirit knows everything about you and is using that preacher to [tell it.]{.class_s36S} The indication in the verse is that the exercise of prophecy in the early church did exactly that. Don't you know that would shake you up: to come into a service and hear the preacher talking about what you did the week before. But by doing that instead of talking in tongues, the church caused God to be worshipped and had the testimony that God was there.

Verse 26, ["How is it then, brethren? when ye come together, every one of you hath a psalm."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}A song. ["Hath a doctrine."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Some truth from Scripture. ["Hath a tongue."]{.class_s36N} The Lord had given a fellow a language to speak. ["Hath a revelation."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}God showed a fellow something out of the Bible. ["Hath an interpretation."]{.class_s36N} In the context, God gives someone the [meaning ]{.class_s36M}of an unknown language [being spoken.]{.class_s36M} Those are things where God is showing individual Christians individual things from the Bible that aren't clear at that time before the New Testament is complete. And there is no doubt in my mind, today, even with the New Testament complete, that God still shows a man a revelation and God still shows the fellow the [proper interpretation of a passage]{.class_s36M}. I'm absolutely certain of it. The pastor of the Pilgrims (1620) said, "I am very confident that the Lord hath more truth and light yet to break forth out of His Holy Word."

Now, that's the [doctrinal side]{.class_s36M} of that verse. The practical side for the local church is this: When the Corinthians assembled, everyone wanted to show off something God had showed them. Now, if I allowed every member of my congregation to take five minutes every service to tell something God had showed them, we would be there all day Sunday: morning, noon, and night. Not everyone can do something in every service. It is ultimately the pastor's responsibility to decide who preaches, who sings, who gives testimonies, etc. He may delegate responsibilities to others in these matters, but the [pastor ]{.class_s36M}is to lead in the order of service. And the rules laid down by Paul in this matter are ["let all things be done unto edifying"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 26) and ["let all things be done decently and in order"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 40). The service is to be [orderly]{.class_s36M}, and the service is to build up the members of the congregation in the faith.

14:27 If any man speak in an unknown tongue, let it be by two, or at the most by three, and that by course; and let one interpret.

28 But if there be no interpreter, let him keep silence in the church; and let him speak to himself, and to God.

29 Let the prophets speak two or three, and let the other judge.

30 If any thing be revealed to another that sitteth by, let the first hold his peace.

31 For ye may all prophesy one by one, that all may learn, and all may be comforted.

32 And the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets.

33 For God is not the author of confusion, but of peace, as in all churches of the saints.

Starting with verse 27, Paul lays down the [rules ]{.class_s36M}for exercising the gifts of prophecy and tongues in the church. First, in regards to tongues, ["if any man"]{.class_s36N}; there are to be no women (vs. 34). Next, ["let it be by two, or at the most three."]{.class_s36N} In any assembly, you are never to hear more than[ three men]{.class_s36M} speak in tongues. ["And that by course."]{.class_s36N} The instructions are that if there is going to be tongues in the assembly, then the two or three allowed are to take turns: [one at a time.]{.class_s36M} If two speak [at once]{.class_s36M}, they have violated the rules laid down by the Holy Spirit for that gift.

The final rule in verse 27 is ["let one interpret."]{.class_s36N} [If no interpreter is present, then the tongues speaker is to keep his mouth shut in the assembly]{.class_s36M} (vs. 28). If the guy wants to pray in tongues, that's between him and the Lord, but he is to ["keep silence in the church."]{.class_s36N}

The rules for prophecy are much the same: ["two or three"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 29) and each in turn (vs. 30--31). Instead of an ["interpreter,"]{.class_s36N} the prophets have a judge to say whether the prophecy is so or not. The standard for judging whether something was so was, of course, the Scriptures (Acts 17:11). And that is what the charismatics could never tolerate: [judging their experience by the Scripture.]{.class_s36M}

As we observed back in verse 25, prophecy can be equated with [Bible preaching.]{.class_s36M} In verse 31, the purpose of prophecy in the local congregation is ["that all may learn, and all may be comforted."]{.class_s36N} Sometimes, then, prophecy goes [beyond ]{.class_s36M}just telling the future. Sometimes it entails the preaching and teaching of the word of God (Rom. 15:4).

Verse 32 is a great verse that you should memorize. ["And the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}There is no such thing as a man getting so full of the Holy Ghost that he falls down in the aisle and rolls around and kicks over benches. There is no such thing as a man getting so full of the Holy Ghost that he suddenly goes into some jibber jabber and passes out and is in a coma four or five days.

[If you are saved, you can control your spirit.]{.class_s36M} And with God to help you, you have no excuse for [not ]{.class_s36M}controlling it. Bob Jones Sr. had three young men come in to him one time. They were putting the charismatic stuff out around the campus, and the old man called them in and sat them down. He said, "Now, you boys have the gifts of the Spirit. Is that right?" They said, "Yes, sir." He said, "Well, what is your gift?" One said, "I've got the gift of healing." He asked another one, "What is your gift?" The guy said, "I have the gift of tongues." He asked the last one, "What is your gift?" The last one said, "I have the gift of interpreting tongues." The old man said, "Well, I've got a gift, too. My gift is [discerning of spirits.]{.class_s36M} I discern you are of the [devil!"]{.class_s36M}

One of our members at Brent, years ago, came back from St. Petersburg. "Oh, Brother Ruckman, I just know that Kathryn Kuhlman is of the Lord. I just know there never was a woman so filled with the Holy Ghost. I went down there to see her, and you just wouldn't believe it. I saw people falling over down there in front of her, and I just didn't believe it until I got down there myself. The power of God in that woman was so great that it just knocked me flat of my back."

Well, think about that a minute. [Where did you read in the Bible about the Holy Spirit knocking a Christian flat on his back?]{.class_s36M} Would anybody care to show it to me? They get it from the crowd that fell back in the Garden of Gethsemane (John 18:6), but that was [unsaved people ]{.class_s36M}trying to [kill ]{.class_s36M}Jesus. Why would a person think the Holy Ghost would knock a [Christian ]{.class_s36M}down? Because he knocked a bunch of scribes and Pharisees and [murderers ]{.class_s36M}down? This lady said Kuhlman mentioned Saul in the Old Testament lying on the ground prophesying, and I said, "Why, Saul was a [demon-possessed suicide.]{.class_s36M}" That's no example for a Christian.

You have gotten to the place in America where people have been watching that TV for so long that all they can do is just feel and feel and feel and feel, and it has to get wilder and wilder to make them feel. People didn't believe me when I told them that there would be X-rated movies on TV for family entertainment before long. If you know what is on some cable channels, you don't doubt it any more. And that thing is to make you respond. They get to the place where they think if they get [any ]{.class_s36M}response it's the [Holy Spirit.]{.class_s36M} And if they get no response they think the Lord couldn't be in it. Christians are at the place where if a quartet gets up and sings "Sweet Hour of Prayer" the congregation will just blink at you. But if another group comes along with a dance band to back them up and sings "I'm Headed up to Gloryland," then that must be of the [Holy Spirit.]{.class_s36M} They've got everything twisted around.

Brethren, this modern charismatic movement is nothing but confusion from start to finish, and verse 33 says, ["God is not the author of confusion."]{.class_s36N} So when this charismatic mess started, [it was not from God.]{.class_s36M} There hasn't been [one ]{.class_s36M}major Bible teacher, pastor, evangelist, or missionary, in the history of the Christian church, who ever talked in tongues or [mentioned ]{.class_s36M}talking in tongues. The charismatics have to rewrite church history and put in people like Oral Roberts, Benny Hinn, and Kathryn Kuhlman to make a different history. But the truth of the matter is that there is [nobody ]{.class_s36M}in apostolic times, before or after Nicaea, during the Dark Ages, Renaissance, or Reformation, or during the Great Awakening, the Second Great Awakening, the Cumberland Valley Revival, or the revivals of Moody, Torrey, or Sunday that ever [mentioned ]{.class_s36M}speaking in tongues.

The modern tongues movement began in 1900 at the Azuza Street Mission in downtown Los Angeles. It started in an [integrated church]{.class_s36M} with a [woman preacher.]{.class_s36M} Then it went to Topeka, Kansas, in the 1920s and 1930s. In the 1930s, Aimee Semple McPherson started the Foursquare Full Gospel Tabernacle at Long Beach, and from there it infiltrated the Christian Business Fellowship, making it the Full Gospel Christian Business Fellowship Committee after World War II. Before that the Christian businessmen used to preach on the streets and pass out tracts. [After ]{.class_s36M}the Full Gospelers got ahold of it, all it was good for was working out money deals. [They could then all stay in their liberal churches and have fellowship.]{.class_s36M}

The Full Gospel Business Fellowship became an organization where, when a business man (a lawyer, a banker, a doctor, or other professional man) got saved, he could stay in[ a liberal church]{.class_s36M} and support it with a clear conscience. In that way, he wouldn't have to leave it and get in a fundamental church. So the Full Gospel Business Fellowship reached out in the Episcopal, Presbyterian, and Methodist churches, claiming it was bringing a great, end-time, "latter rain," charismatic revival to all these churches. The truth of the matter was that those fellows in the Full Gospel Fellowship didn't have enough guts to [leave those churches]{.class_s36M} and join [Bible-believing churches.]{.class_s36M} The charismatic movement is interdenominational, so that a saved man can stay in the church that was damning him to hell and [support liberals and modernists. ]{.class_s36M}That is [not]{.class_s36M} the ["peace"]{.class_s36N} God gave to ["all churches of the saints."]{.class_s36N}

14:34 Let your women keep silence in the churches: for it is not permitted unto them to speak; but they are commanded to be under obedience, as also saith the law.

35 And if they will learn any thing, let them ask their husbands at home: for it is a shame for women to speak in the church.

The context of verse 34 (["let your women keep silence in the churches"]{.class_s36N}) is tongues. [It has nothing to do with a business meeting. ]{.class_s36M}A woman is allowed to testify in church, ["Let the redeemed of the ]{.class_s36N}[Lord ]{.class_s36Z-0}[say so"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 107:2). [A woman is allowed to sing in church,]{.class_s36M} ["Let every thing that hath breath praise the ]{.class_s36N}[Lord]{.class_s36Z-0}["]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 150:6). There are women in the New Testament that prophesy, though not in the assembly (Acts 21:9). However, 1 Corinthians 11:5 implies that a woman [might]{.class_s36M} prophesy in church (["every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoreth her head"]{.class_s36N}).

When it comes to learning anything from (or about) the tongues or prophesying that goes on in church, the woman is to be under the [teaching authority of her husband.]{.class_s36M}

Now, I understand that there are exceptions: What about if she's married to an unsaved man? What if she is single? What if her husband doesn't know his Bible or is less spiritual? That is why a pastor, a man, is over the congregation. But all things being what they should be, the rule is the woman in subjection to the man, the man in subjection to Christ, and Christ in subjection to the Father (1 Cor. 11:3). The woman is never to usurp the [teaching and pastoral position]{.class_s36S} over the man in church (1 Tim. 2:12). She is to ["learn in silence with all subjection"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 2:11).

14:36 What? came the word of God out from you? or came it unto you only?

37 If any man think himself to be a prophet, or spiritual, let him acknowledge that the things that I write unto you are the commandments of the Lord.

38 But if any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant.

39 Wherefore, brethren, covet to prophesy, and forbid not to speak with tongues.

40 Let all things be done decently and in order.

The idea of verse 36 is that God did give His word by revelation to some in the church at Corinth, but that they weren't the only ones. Paul is trying to get them to see and to acknowledge that he also is speaking God's word to them.

So if that be the case, verse 37, any man who claims he is a [prophet ]{.class_s36M}or a [spiritual ]{.class_s36M}man will acknowledge the things Paul has said in this chapter to be [absolute truth]{.class_s36M}: ["the commandments of the Lord."]{.class_s36N} That ought to settle it. It has been settled too many times. I don't care what these people are doing to try to explain away the verses, there is one thing for certain. [It is certain when a man speaks in tongues, an unknown tongue with no interpreter, nobody is edified. ]{.class_s36M}Therefore, he ought to keep his mouth shut. That's clear. [It is clear that women are not to speak in tongues in the local assembly.]{.class_s36M} That is clear. It is clear that if anybody does speak in tongues that is never to be more than three. [That's clear.]{.class_s36M} If a man says five words you can understand, he has done more than someone who says ten thousand words that you can't understand. [That is clear.]{.class_s36M} And those are the [commandments of God.]{.class_s36M}

If a fellow won't take that, it is because he has no use for the word of God, like most Christians don't. Unsaved people don't believe the word of God anymore, but the reason they don't is because they are living with [Christians ]{.class_s36M}who don't believe it either. If the saved people don't believe it, do you think the [unsaved ]{.class_s36M}are going to believe it? An unsaved man goes to Sunday School, and the teacher gets up there with the Sunday School quarterly, and every other word he says, "This shouldn't be in the Bible," and "This verse shouldn't be there," and "A better version is\...." The unsaved guy sits there, and he can't figure that thing out.

Infidelity doesn't spread from the unsaved people. [It spreads from the Christians.]{.class_s36M} Make sure you believe something. Or if you don't believe it, don't [profess ]{.class_s36M}to believe it. If you hear me profess to believe it, I believe it. If I [quit ]{.class_s36M}believing it, I will tell you.

Verse 38, ["But if any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's the summation. I mean, if a man is going to argue about it and [not ]{.class_s36M}believe it, just leave the stupid ignoramus alone. Let him go on in his stupidity. It's not saying to let him mess up your service. Try your best to give the idiot the truth, and if he rejects it,[ just leave him alone.]{.class_s36M} Look at verse thirty-seven. If a man rejects ["the commandments of the Lord"]{.class_s36N} after you have given it to him, that is all you can do with him. [Let him go.]{.class_s36M}

Let me show you one just like it. Turn to Titus 3:10--11, ["A man that is an heretick after the first and second admonition reject; knowing that he that is such is subverted, and sinneth, being condemned of himself."]{.class_s36N}

In the Bible there are different [types ]{.class_s36M}of unbelief. An [infidel ]{.class_s36M}is someone who doesn't believe the Bible at all. [All infidels are unsaved. ]{.class_s36M}You can show compassion on them and try to witness to them as long as they will listen and consider what you say. The moment they interfere with someone else getting saved, though, you come down hard on them (Acts 13:8--12). An [apostate ]{.class_s36M}is someone who professes to believe the Bible, but does not. That person can be saved or lost---I have no respect for an apostate at all because they are the most dangerous of the bunch. A [heretic ]{.class_s36M}is someone who misuses the Scripture to teach something that isn't so. If you try to straighten out a heretic on his doctrine you are only obligated to [try twice.]{.class_s36M} After you try twice don't waste any more time with them. [Let them go.]{.class_s36M} If he won't listen to sound doctrine I'd break fellowship with him, and I'll show you why. Romans 16:17--18, now, let's see what you do with a man who doesn't obey[ "the commandments of the Lord." "Now I beseech you, brethren, mark them which cause divisions and offenses contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned; and avoid them. For they that are such serve not our Lord Jesus Christ, but their own belly; and by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple."]{.class_s36N}[ That's]{.class_s36M} the charismatic. If a man rejects the [commandments of God,]{.class_s36M} you are to get away from him. I avoid them. If our paths cross and have to cross I deal with them, but I don't go looking for them. But realize, if you avoid everybody, after a while it is going to get kind of lonely.

I hate to say it, brethren, but that is what God has called you fellows to do if He has called you to preach. You are going to find, after a while, that the loneliest man in the world is a [Bible-believing preacher. ]{.class_s36M}You will have your own congregation, but as far as fellowship with other preachers goes, you are not going to have much. I'll tell you why. Because while you believe the book these other schools are turning out 8,000 preachers a year that do [not ]{.class_s36M}believe the Book. And they are going out entrenched in fellowship. You'll have a Bob Jones clique over on one side and a Baptist Bible Fellowship clique over here and a Tennessee Temple clique over here, and [all of them will be attacking the Bible and changing the Bible, denying the Bible, and using other versions.]{.class_s36M} They will do the best they can to mess up [your church]{.class_s36M} and mess up [your work.]{.class_s36M} They really will. I would advise you to avoid them.

Did you ever stop to think how many preacher friends I've got in Pensacola? Did you ever think about that? Right now in Pensacola, I am in fellowship with no preachers outside of my own church. Most of them that believe what I believe know they should be out here learning something, and they're under conviction. (Speaking to PBI students) They are going to hate your guts because you are sitting here [getting what they could have gotten if they had enough guts to get it.]{.class_s36M}

They will hate your guts because when you get out you will know something [they ]{.class_s36M}don't know but could have gotten. They are going to be laying for you like a bear trap.

The rest of them are very upset with me because I will not put PBI into the Springfield Fellowship. Every couple weeks I get a phone call about it. I know what they are up to.[ They want to put this school into that thing so they can come in here and teach. ]{.class_s36M}You get great nuggets from that bunch. If they come here to teach, then they can hire their graduates to come here and teach, and pretty soon we will all have a [NASV]{.class_s36M} and pretty buildings, and[ it will be just like the rest of them. I see the "handwriting on the wall."]{.class_s36M}

Verse 39, ["Wherefore, brethren, covet to."]{.class_s36N} Speak in tongues? No. One more time he tells you not to desire tongues. ["Covet to prophesy."]{.class_s36N} Learn how to [tell the future.]{.class_s36M} Learn about [the future]{.class_s36M} from the word of God. Be able to tell folks [what is going to happen.]{.class_s36M} Tell them what is [going to happen]{.class_s36M} if they die without Christ. Tell them what is [going to happen]{.class_s36M} if they die with Christ. Tell them what is [going to take place ]{.class_s36M}on earth in the next twenty to forty years. Tell them about eternity. That's what you should learn; not this other junk. ["And forbid not to speak with tongues."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}The Bible tells the whole truth, and the truth is that in [that ]{.class_s36M}church where they [had ]{.class_s36M}the signs, if they exercised them [properly ]{.class_s36M}they were allowed to do it.

["Let all thing be done decently and in order"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 40).[ ]{.class_s36M}In regards to that last verse, let's tie up a loose end that really has nothing to do with the context of chapter 14 (tongues and prophecy) but which could conceivably cause a problem if misunderstood. The question arises: [What about everybody praying out loud at once in church?]{.class_s36M} I know some pastors have a problem with that, and each local church will have to decide what it allows. But [prayer ]{.class_s36M}is a different thing than tongues and prophecy. It is not a [gift ]{.class_s36M}to be [exercised]{.class_s36M}. It has nothing to do with edifying the church. In prayer, you are talking to God; [it is between you and the Lord, not someone else.]{.class_s36M} The Lord hears each prayer and knows each prayer, and if He can't hear thirty of us praying at once, then He can't hear any of us at all. After all, the Lord is taking about 150 million prayer requests a day simultaneously.

Here is what I have learned in my ministry. If you instruct certain people to lead in prayer, each in turn, there are [big gaps]{.class_s36M} between the prayers. Then some Christians get their feelings hurt because [they ]{.class_s36M}were not asked to lead in prayer. When you leave it up to anyone to pray, not only are there the big gaps between prayers, but quite often two start to pray at the same time, then each quits suddenly so each one won't interfere with the other's prayer. Then they will wait a moment or so, and the whole thing happens again. To me, [that is confusion,]{.class_s36M} much more so than having everyone praying at once. If that weren't enough, when you have someone lead in prayer, then quite often everyone else is listening to the prayer. When that happens, then they are not praying. So I just have everyone pray at once. That may have a Holiness tint to it, but I have always been more Methodist than Baptist when it comes to a thing like that.

[]{#c1HJ.xhtml}

## Chapter 15 {.heading_s39}

15:1 Moreover, brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you, which also ye have received, and wherein ye stand;

2 By which also ye are saved, if ye keep in memory what I preached unto you, unless ye have believed in vain.

3 For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures;

4 And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures:

We have come to the longest chapter in 1 Corinthians, and chapter 15 deals with the resurrection: in respect to both Christ and the Christian. What sets Christianity apart from [every other religion in the world]{.class_s36M} is that the [founder ]{.class_s36M}of our religion didn't stay [dead]{.class_s36M}. And what sets the New Testament believer apart from the Old Testament believer is that we don't have to wait until ["the last day"]{.class_s36N} to come up [from the dead]{.class_s36M} (John 11:23--26).

In verses 1--4 Paul gives the definition of ["the gospel."]{.class_s36N} We had a five point Tulip Calvinist in once, a fellow named Bob Ross out of Pasadena, Texas. He had been bragging all over the internet about how much smarter he was than Ruckman and how he was going to drop an "atom bomb" on Ruckman's students. So we had him in to do an "Open Bible Forum" like I do all up and down the country. The students could ask him anything they wanted, and he had to come up with a Bible answer in five seconds or less. Someone asked him, "What is [the Gospel]{.class_s36M}?" And do you know what that crazy nut said? He replied, "Everything from Matthew to Revelation is [the Gospel]{.class_s36M}." You have hardly heard anything nuttier in your life.

[The word "gospel" means "good news."]{.class_s36M} Do you think the antichrist showing up and sitting in the temple at Jerusalem (2 Thess. 2) is [good news]{.class_s36M}? Do you think turning a fellow over to ["Satan for the destruction of the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 5) is [good news]{.class_s36M}? How about demoniac monsters coming out of the pit (Rev. 9)? Here's a good "gospel" verse for old Booby Ross: ["Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the devil and his angels"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 25:41). You call that "good news," do you?

Brethren, the [good news]{.class_s36M} for us is that when we were headed for Hell, someone paid the full debt to God for our sins and lives to secure our salvation before God.

Another heresy taught by some Fundamentalists is that the "Gospel" is the same wherever it appears in the New Testament. It is related to the [heresy ]{.class_s36M}that people in the Old Testament were saved by looking forward to the cross while people in the New Testament are saved by looking back to the cross. The truth is that in the New Testament [ten ]{.class_s36M}gospels are mentioned, and each is different from the others. You have Christ coming and preaching ["the gospel of the kingdom"]{.class_s36N} in Matthew 4:23. It is a Gospel preached exclusively to the Jews (Matt. 10:5--7), and it is confirmed by signs (Matt. 4:23--24, 10:8). It is the good news that the King of the Jews was present and offering the kingdom to the nation of Israel, conditioned upon their repentance and acceptance of the King.

When Christ died, He went and preached ["the gospel\...to them that are dead"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 4:6). During Christ's time in the tomb, He went down to Paradise, Abraham's Bosom, and proclaimed the "good news" to the Old Testament saints that He had taken away their sins, [which the Old Testament sacrifices could not, ]{.class_s36M}and had made a way for them to live in Heaven with God.

In Acts 20:24, Paul says he testified to ["the gospel of the grace of God."]{.class_s36N} This is the Gospel of 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. It is the good news that a sinner, by the free grace of God (Eph. 2:8--9), can be saved from sin and Hell by receiving the person of the Gospel, Jesus Christ (vs. 1, cf. John 1:12), and putting his complete trust for salvation in His atoning work on the cross and His resurrection from the dead (vss. 3--4, cf. Rom. 10:9--10).

It is also called ["the gospel of God"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 1:1), ["the gospel of his Son"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 1:9), ["the gospel of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 1:16), ["Christ's gospel"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 2:12), ["the gospel of your salvation"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 1:13), ["the gospel of peace"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 6:15), ["the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N} (2 Thess. 1:8), and ["our gospel"]{.class_s36N} (2 Thess. 2:14). This is the gospel of which Paul says, ["If any man preach any other gospel unto you than that we have received, let him be accursed"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 1:9).

In Romans 2:16, Paul uses the term ["gospel"]{.class_s36N} in a broader sense, though, when he calls it ["my gospel."]{.class_s36N} Not only is it the Gospel of 1 Corinthians 15:1--4 (2 Tim. 2:8) which is to unsaved people, it is the good news given [specifically ]{.class_s36M}to Paul of the Body of Christ (Gal. 1:11--17). It refers to the entire body of sound doctrine committed to the Church. It is in this sense that Paul uses it when he calls it ["the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which was committed to my trust"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 1:10--11).

In writing to the Hebrews, the author says, ["For unto us]{.class_s36N} (Hebrews)[ was the gospel preached, as well as unto them"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 4:2). In the context, the ["them"]{.class_s36N} refers to the children of Israel as they were on the verge of entering Canaan in Numbers 13--14. The good news, here, was that if the Israelites would just trust God, the Lord would give them the land of Canaan by [military conquest ]{.class_s36M}(Num. 13:30, 14:6--9). The writer of Hebrews uses that story to tell the Jews to whom he was writing (and the doctrinal application is to Jews in the Tribulation) that if they will just trust Christ, God will give them a Millennial rest in [the land of Canaan. It has nothing to do with the personal salvation of a sinner.]{.class_s36M}

Finally, there is an angel in the Tribulation who preaches ["the everlasting gospel\...to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 14:6--7). So this gospel is different from ["the gospel of the kingdom"]{.class_s36N} preached by the 144,000 Jewish witnesses (Matt. 24:14 cf. Rev. 7, 14:1--5). And if you examine the message, it is certainly NOT ["the gospel of the grace of God."]{.class_s36N} The message is ["Fear God\...give glory to him\...worship him"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 14:7). That is NOT 1 Corinthians 15:1--4 or John 1:12 or Romans 10:9--10 or Ephesians 2:8--9.

There is ["an angel from heaven]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 1:8) preaching ["another gospel"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 11:4), and according to Paul, he should ["be accursed"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 1:8). BUT HE'S NOT! So salvation is [not]{.class_s36M} the same in every dispensation, and these "Gospels" are [not]{.class_s36M} all the same message.

Add to those six listed here the four written accounts of Christ's life, and you have ten different "Gospels."

Now, back to 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. In verse 2, some of our Arminian brethren like to use the conditional clause that starts with ["if"]{.class_s36N} to try and prove that you can lose your salvation.

But what Paul is doing there is writing to the Corinthian church. In writing them he addresses not only the saved people in the church but also the [unsaved people ]{.class_s36M}in the church. When he writes to a church as a group, he doesn't take for granted that everybody in the church is saved. He takes for granted that maybe some of them aren't saved.

When he writes this epistle at the start, he addresses himself to ["the church of God which is at Cornith, to them that are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints."]{.class_s36N} But over and over again in this book you will find things aimed at [unsaved people.]{.class_s36M} For example, look at 2:6--8. [That ]{.class_s36M}is not aimed at a saved person. Look at 3:18--20. [That ]{.class_s36M}isn't aimed at a saved person. Look at 5:10, [that ]{.class_s36M}isn't aimed at a saved person. Look at 10:20. [That ]{.class_s36M}isn't aimed at a saved person. You will find that all the way through there.

What he is saying in verse 2 is that if they remember what he preached to them, then they will remember that it was indeed the Gospel mentioned in verse 1 and described in verses 3--4. That is the Gospel that saved them unless they did [not ]{.class_s36M}believe it or unless it was not true. It is like when you are out witnessing and a person tells you he is saved. You ask him to tell you about his experience. He will either recount the time he heard the Gospel preached and received Christ, or he will tell you something else.

That often gets people to wondering about [how ]{.class_s36M}they believed, and [when ]{.class_s36M}they believed or [if ]{.class_s36M}they believed right or wrong. But you know as well as I do that, if you are saved, you know what you did to get saved and what you [believed ]{.class_s36M}to get saved. There is not anybody in this building that doesn't know that when you got saved you [had to do something]{.class_s36M}. I'm not going to tell you what you did. I'm going to let [your ]{.class_s36M}memory conjure it up. You remember what it was. [You ]{.class_s36M}remember what you trusted. Now, as to whether you did it the right way and the way the Bible prescribes is immaterial. The point is [you ]{.class_s36M}remember. And the point is you know [what ]{.class_s36M}you believed, and the point is you did something about it. Now, [what ]{.class_s36M}did you believe? Verses 3 and verse 4. There isn't anybody reading this page, and is saved, that didn't believe [verses 3 and 4]{.class_s36M}. Verses 3 and 4 are what he preached to them. And if he had people there that didn't remember [that ]{.class_s36M}and didn't take the thing to heart, then they were not saved.

Now, here was the preaching.

Verse 3, ["For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received."]{.class_s36N}[ This is the Gospel.]{.class_s36M} The gospel has five parts to it, and baptism is never connected with it. [There is no baptism connected with the Gospel.]{.class_s36M} The Gospel is this: ["how that Christ died for our sins."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's one. ["According to the scriptures;"]{.class_s36N} That's two.

Verse 4, ["And that he was buried."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's three. ["And that he rose again the third day."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That's four[. ]{.class_s36M}["According to the scriptures."]{.class_s36N} That's five. That's the Gospel. The Gospel has [five parts ]{.class_s36M}to it: Death, sins, burial, resurrection, and according to the Scriptures.

The last element of the Gospel, ["according to the scriptures,"]{.class_s36N} is so important that Paul says it [twice,]{.class_s36M} making the second mention the fifth part of the Gospel. The reason it is important is that there are many professing Christians who believe in the death, burial, and resurrection; they just do not believe it ["according to the scriptures."]{.class_s36N}

You take a Roman Catholic. Does he believe Christ died? Sure, he does; that's why he wears a crucifix everywhere. He'll even say he [believes ]{.class_s36M}that Christ died for sins. But when it comes to believing on the death of Christ for sins [the way the Bible defines the doctrine, that's another story.]{.class_s36M} The Catholic will deny that the death of Christ is [solely efficacious]{.class_s36M} for our sins (Rom. 5), so he will [add good works to it.]{.class_s36M} Then he will replace the ["one sacrifice"]{.class_s36N} of Christ on the cross ["for sins for ever,"]{.class_s36N} ["the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 10:8--12) with the continual sacrifice of the Mass. So a Catholic does [not ]{.class_s36M}believe ["according to the scriptures."]{.class_s36N}

Moreover, the Gospel has to be taken [personally]{.class_s36M}. Paul says, ["Christ died for OUR sins."]{.class_s36N} The ["our"]{.class_s36N} is not used in the sense of a corporate body like the Jews praying ["Our Father"]{.class_s36N} in Matthew 6:9. It is a group of [individuals]{.class_s36M}, with whom Paul includes himself, who have already believed the Gospel. When Paul gives a synopsis of the Gospel in Romans 10:9--10, he is much more pointed with the unsaved man, ["That if THOU shalt confess with THY mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in THINE heart that God hath raised him from the dead, THOU shalt be saved."]{.class_s36N} Paul was not a "we" preacher: "If WE will receive Christ, WE may have eternal life." Paul knew he was saved and, therefore, excluded himself when he spoke to the lost.

Also, the belief in the Gospel cannot be mere[ mental assent]{.class_s36M} to a creed. There are thousands (and perhaps millions) of professing Christians who stand up every Sunday morning and recite the Apostles' Creed who are no more saved than Judas Iscariot or Genghis Khan. There is a big difference between saying "I believe that this pulpit will hold me up" and [actually resting my weight on it]{.class_s36M} and letting it do it. I'm depending on the blood of Jesus Christ [alone ]{.class_s36M}to take away my sins. If I get to the judgment and God says, "Ruckman, that's not good enough," then I'll have to go to Hell. [I have no other recourse]{.class_s36M}. That is the difference between the two thieves who died with Christ. The [lost ]{.class_s36M}one said, ["If thou be the Son of God."]{.class_s36N} He had reservations. The other put all of his "chips" on Jesus Christ and said, ["Lord, remember me."]{.class_s36N} He was the one who died and met Christ in Paradise.

Now, Paul says in Galatians 1:9 if any man preaches another Gospel than the one defined here, 1 Corinthians 15, that man is ["accursed."]{.class_s36N} In light of that, you can see that most Americans are not "Gospel hardened," because most Americans have never heard the Gospel. Did you ever stop to think how many times in a sermon on the radio you have heard a description of Christ's death, burial, and resurrection, [according to the Scriptures? ]{.class_s36M}I'll bet you haven't heard it often. I've heard Oliver Greene a good many times giving it. He gets to preaching the blood and gets real bloody. He'll put it right out. But you take the rest of them, they don't talk about it much. I've heard M. R. DeHann and Richard off and on for ten to fifteen years. Once in a while they will cross over there, but not a great deal. You take Kathryn Kuhlman, [I never heard her mention it. ]{.class_s36M}I've heard Oral Roberts for twenty-eight years, and I've heard Jimmy Swaggart for five years, [and I have never heard either of those fellows give the plan of salvation one time.]{.class_s36M} ["If they speak not according to this word, it is because there is no light in them"]{.class_s36N} (Isa. 8:20).

There was a rock opera out ("Jesus Christ, Superstar"), and they said it was a "modern interpretation of the Gospel." That wasn't the Gospel, because it wasn't according to the Scriptures. At the [end ]{.class_s36M}of the rock opera, Judas was [alive]{.class_s36M}, and Jesus Christ was [dead]{.class_s36M}. [That is not according to the Scriptures.]{.class_s36M} In the Scriptures, Judas is dead, and Jesus Christ is alive. To be saved, a man has got to believe that Jesus Christ [died]{.class_s36M}, that he was [buried]{.class_s36M}, that he [arose]{.class_s36M}, that he died for [your sins,]{.class_s36M} the way the [Scriptures ]{.class_s36M}declare it. He has got to believe that. [That's the Gospel.]{.class_s36M}

15:5 And that he was seen of Cephas, then of the twelve:

6 After that, he was seen of above five hundred brethren at once; of whom the greater part remain unto this present, but some are fallen asleep.

7 After that, he was seen of James; then of all the apostles.

8 And last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time.

The difference between the Bible and other "holy" books is that the Bible is a collection of [eyewitness accounts ]{.class_s36M}of God's dealings with man. The Bible is a [history book,]{.class_s36M} verified by those who saw the events unfold (see [1 in 23,000,000,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 2001). [The Koran is not that way.]{.class_s36M} It is an illiterate, epileptic egomaniac in a cave listening to an unclean spirit describe supposedly historic events from over 4,000 years before Mohammed was born. On top of that, there is not one shred of evidence that anything the [Koran]{.class_s36M} describes in history is true, [unless it was stolen from the Bible.]{.class_s36M} Every time an unsaved historian casts doubt on the Bible, an unsaved archaeologist digs up the evidence that shows the Bible was telling the truth all of the time.

In this passage, Paul lists the [eyewitness accounts]{.class_s36M} of the resurrection of Jesus Christ. In the Gospels, of course, Mary Magdalene and the women who come to anoint His body were the first to see Him. Paul skips these accounts and goes to those of the leadership of the early church.

The first was Peter. ["Cephas"]{.class_s36N} was the Aramaic name given to him by Christ in John 1:42, and contrary to Roman Catholic interpretation, the name means ["a stone,"]{.class_s36N} not a rock. Paul again refers to him as ["Cephas" ]{.class_s36N}in Galatians 2:9.

The Lord made a special appearance to Peter before the other apostles, because Peter lost his [discipleship ]{.class_s36M}when he denied Christ. In Mark 16:7, the angel says, ["But go your way, tell his disciples and Peter."]{.class_s36N} When the two men who talked with Christ on the road to Emmaus came to report their story, they found the eleven ["Saying, the Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared to Simon"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 24:34). So the Lord appeared to Simon Peter, before He appeared to the other ten disciples, and reinstates him.

["Then of the twelve"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). The next eyewitnesses that Paul lists are the [twelve Jewish apostles.]{.class_s36M} That's important, because it doesn't say ["the eleven"]{.class_s36N} as the Gospels do. So Paul did not count himself as one of the twelve apostles. Moreover, he does count [Matthias ]{.class_s36M}(Acts 1:26) as the apostle to replace Judas, and Matthias is present with the eleven when Christ appears in the upper room. So the qualification laid down by Peter in Acts 1:21--22 was a legitimate one. Matthias had to be with the other apostles ["to be a witness WITH US of his resurrection"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 1:22).

Notice in Luke 24:33 that when Christ appeared to the apostles in the upper room, there were [other disciples ]{.class_s36M}with them: ["And they rose up the same hour, and returned to Jerusalem, and found the eleven gathered together, AND THEM THAT WERE WITH THEM."]{.class_s36N} At least two of those were Matthias and Justus (Acts 1:23). This is even more important when you consider Christ's words in John 20:23. The power to "remit and to retain sins" [was not just given to the apostles to be passed along to a priestly class]{.class_s36M} (as in Catholicism)[; it was given to all of the disciples in the upper room.]{.class_s36M}

The next group of eyewitnesses was ["above five hundred brethren at once"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6), and many of them were still alive at the time Paul wrote 1 Corinthians.

This is an account that will stand up in any honest [law court]{.class_s36S} in the world. No evidence has ever been produced to discount this[ recorded account ]{.class_s36S}of the resurrection. The law of jurisprudence in the United States teaches that if [eyewitness testimony]{.class_s36S} that is [unrefuted ]{.class_s36S}is reduced to writing, and that writing is kept, that, unless [conclusive evidence]{.class_s36S} is produced to disprove the eyewitness, the written testimony [cannot be set aside or nullified. ]{.class_s36S}That is the law of the courts. The law of the courts states that unless the body of Jesus Christ is [found]{.class_s36S}, this written testimony will stand up in any court in the United States: [five hundred eye witnesses. ]{.class_s36S}To disprove it they would have to find a skeleton and prove it was Jesus Christ.

["After that, he was seen of James"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). That's a special appearance, and that is obviously [not ]{.class_s36M}James Zebedee. There would be no reason for saying James Zebedee. The only thing we can determine from that is that ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_s36N} had a special appearance by the Lord Jesus Christ, because James got [converted]{.class_s36M}, and he was the only [brother ]{.class_s36M}of the Lord Jesus Christ that [did ]{.class_s36M}get converted. Go back to John and notice that his brothers, his human brothers born of Mary, did not believe on Him. I teach that ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_s36N} is ["James the Lord's brother."]{.class_s36N}

The Scofield notes will make him "James the Lord's cousin." I teach he is [not ]{.class_s36M}a cousin because when the Bible means "cousin" it says "cousin." In Luke 1:36, when it speaks about Elizabeth being Mary's[ "cousin," ]{.class_s36N}it says ["cousin."]{.class_s36N} So if it had wanted to say "James was a cousin," it would have said it.

John 7:5, ["For neither did his brethren believe in him."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36S}So every indication is that ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_s36N} [does ]{.class_s36S}get converted, so he has a [special post-resurrection appearance.]{.class_s36S} This also would qualify him to take over the council at Jerusalem in Acts 15. You remember the fellow who was moderator at the council was James, and it couldn't have been James Zebedee. Why not?[ James Zebedee had his head cut off in Acts]{.class_s36S} [12.]{.class_s36S}

["Then of all the apostles"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). So there were other apostles, [even before Paul, ]{.class_s36M}who were not of the twelve and yet still were "apostles": witnesses of Christ's resurrection, especially sent out by Jesus Christ with the apostolic signs. We do not know whether this is a reference to ["the seventy"]{.class_s36N} appointed and sent out by Christ in Luke 10:1--17 or to someone else. What we do know is that Paul addresses ["Andronicus and Junia"]{.class_s36N} in Rome ["who are of note among the apostles, who also were in Christ before me"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 16:7).

Barnabas was saved [before ]{.class_s36S}Paul, and he is also called an apostle (Acts 14:14). ["James the Lord's brother"]{.class_s36N} is also called [an apostle]{.class_s36S} (Gal. 1:19). Whoever they were, they were [not ]{.class_s36S}the Twelve Apostles, and they were apostles and saw the resurrected Christ before Paul was even saved.

["And last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 8). You'll want to mark verse 8. Verse 8 is one of the greatest verses in the Bible for dealing with a Campbellite, because that verse says that Paul was [born ]{.class_s36M}as a Christian when? [When he saw Christ.]{.class_s36M} See that. When was he baptized? [Three days later.]{.class_s36M} Therefore baptism has nothing to do with the new birth. [Paul got his new birth when he saw Christ, not when he got baptized.]{.class_s36M} Paul was blind three days and three nights, and at the [end ]{.class_s36M}of three days and three nights, Acts 9, Ananias came to him and put his hands upon him and said, ["Brother Saul\...receive thy sight."]{.class_s36N} [So Paul wasn't baptized until three days after he was born again.]{.class_s36M}

15:9 For I am the least of the apostles, that am not meet to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God.

10 But by the grace of God I am what I am: and his grace which was bestowed upon me was not in vain; but I laboured more abundantly than they all: yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me.

11 Therefore whether it were I or they, so we preach, and so ye believed.

Without a doubt Paul is the greatest Christian in the New Testament. Certainly, he is the [pattern ]{.class_s36S}God chose for living the Christian life (see comments under 1 Cor. 11:1). But that is certainly not the way Paul viewed himself. He considered himself ["the least of the apostles"]{.class_s36N} and ["not meet to be called an apostle"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). He called himself the ["chief"]{.class_s36N} of sinners (1 Tim. 1:15). Why? ["Because I persecuted the church of God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). The reason why that is so very important to notice is because of 1 Corinthians 10:32. There are only three classes of people: Jew, Gentile, and[ "the church of God."]{.class_s36N}

Everything in the Bible is written to a [Jew ]{.class_s36M}or to a [Gentile ]{.class_s36M}or to ["the church of God."]{.class_s36N} The people who call themselves the Church of God have more Scriptural authority for the name of [their church]{.class_s36M} than the Campbellites do, because there is no "Church of Christ." There is a ["church of God."]{.class_s36N}

But the reason that is so important is Cornelius Stam and the Dry Cleaners will tell you that the church that Paul [persecuted ]{.class_s36M}was [not ]{.class_s36M}the Body of Christ, and they will tell you there was no Body of Christ [until the time of Paul.]{.class_s36M} But in 1 Corinthians 10:32, Paul says that there are only three people in the world: [Jew, Gentile, and Church.]{.class_s36M} There's no "Body" sticking out different from the "Church." The Body [is ]{.class_s36M}the Church. The Church [is ]{.class_s36M}the Body. In Christ, there is neither Jew nor Gentile. Right? All right. Then the Church is composed of Christians. [They are neither Jew nor Gentile.]{.class_s36M} [Outside ]{.class_s36M}of the Church, there is Jew and Gentile. So when he says the ["church of God,"]{.class_s36N} he is referring to the [Body of the]{.class_s36M} [Lord Jesus Christ.]{.class_s36M} So in 1 Corinthians when he said, ["I persecuted the church of God,"]{.class_s36N} that told you the Body was there [before ]{.class_s36M}Acts 9. [Cornelius Stam does not know what he is talking about. ]{.class_s36M}Neither did Bullinger.

Verse 10 is a good verse. Paul is saying that, although he did not [deserve ]{.class_s36S}it, God's grace is what made him the apostle he was. God's grace put him into the ministry, and when he got in there he worked harder at it than all of the other apostles. Then he catches himself and says, ["Yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me."]{.class_s36N} When you are tempted in the ministry to brag about how hard [you ]{.class_s36S}are working for the Lord, just remember, it is [God ]{.class_s36S}working through you. You had better give the credit to Him and not take any glory for [yourself]{.class_s36S}.

Verse 11 reminds us of what Paul said back in chapter 3, verse 5:[ "Who then is Paul, and who is Apollos, but ministers by whom he believed."]{.class_s36N} In other words, it doesn't matter which of the apostles did the preaching, it was the message, the Gospel, that saved the Corinthians when they believed.

15:12 Now if Christ be preached that he rose from the dead, how say some among you that there is no resurrection of the dead?

13 But if there be no resurrection of the dead, then is Christ not risen:

14 And if Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain.

15 Yea, and we are found false witnesses of God; because we have testified of God that he raised up Christ: whom he raised not up, if so be that the dead rise not.

16 For if the dead rise not, then is not Christ raised:

17 And if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins.

18 Then they also which are fallen asleep in Christ are perished.

19 If in this life only we have hope in Christ, we are of all men most miserable.

If there was one thing that marked the message of an apostle it was the preaching that [Christ rose from the dead.]{.class_s36S} The Greek philosophers had a hard time with that (Acts 17:18, 32). Apparently, there were some people in the church at Corinth questioning the doctrine of the resurrection (vs. 12).

["But if there be no resurrection of the dead, then is Christ not risen:"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13).[ ]{.class_s36M}Now, there are a number of things here you want to mark on ["if Christ be not risen."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}This makes a great sermon. There's a natural four or five points. If Christ isn't risen from the dead then there is a certain [number of things]{.class_s36M} that are true.

The first thing that is true is this. If Christ is not risen, verse 14, ["then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain."]{.class_s36N} There's the first two things. If Christ didn't come up from the dead, I am [preaching ]{.class_s36M}nonsense, and [your faith]{.class_s36M} is nonsense. Your faith is only as good as the historical facts behind it. If you believed on a lie, what good would that do you?

I mean, don't you know it's [true]{.class_s36M}? Even if you were an atheist or an agnostic, wouldn't you begin to wonder? [Nineteen centuries of this stuff.]{.class_s36M} How does it keep going on if it's a lie, man?

The third thing, verse 15, if Christ didn't come up from the dead, I am [a liar]{.class_s36M}; ["we are found false witnesses."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Sincerity of belief is irrelevant. [Either Christ rose or He did not.]{.class_s36M}

Next, verse 17, ["ye are yet in your sins."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}If Christ [didn't]{.class_s36M} come up from the dead, [you are part of your sins,]{.class_s36M} and [your sins are part of you.]{.class_s36M} If Christ didn't come up from the dead, [you're not circumcised spiritually.]{.class_s36M} If Christ didn't come up from the dead, you're not loosed from this body of flesh, [your soul is stuck to your flesh,]{.class_s36M} and the sins your flesh commits your soul commits, and [you will die in your sins.]{.class_s36M}

Next, verse 18, if Christ didn't come up from the dead, [then the dead Christians are perished; ]{.class_s36M}you are never going to hear from them again. That's the end of them. They are either asleep in Abraham's Bosom, or they are in Hell. If you buried a Christian mother or Christian wife or Christian child or grandmother, you are never going to see them again, and that is the end of them. [All that's left of them is a handful of dirt.]{.class_s36M}

Then, finally, verse 19, if Christ didn't come up from the dead, [Christians are the most miserable people on the face of this earth.]{.class_s36M}

We would still be ["without Christ\...aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope, and without God in the world"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 2:12)---IF Christ is still dead. Now, you talk about [proofs ]{.class_s36M}of the resurrection. How about [those ]{.class_s36M}proofs right there? I know some of you have been pretty miserable since you've been saved. You've had some pretty rough times, but honestly, all things considered, wouldn't you have to admit that even when you're in misery as a Christian and everything is all wrong, that down deep inside you felt a kind of stabilizing force that you didn't feel before you were saved? Have you noticed that?

15:20 But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept.

21 For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead.

22 For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive.

23 But every man in his own order: Christ the firstfruits; afterward they that are Christ's at his coming.

24 Then cometh the end, when he shall have delivered up the kingdom to God, even the Father; when he shall have put down all rule and all authority and power.

25 For he must reign, till he hath put all enemies under his feet.

26 The last enemy that shall be destroyed is death.

27 For he hath put all things under his feet. But when he saith, all things are put under him, it is manifest that he is excepted, which did put all things under him.

28 And when all things shall be subdued unto him, then shall the Son also himself be subject unto him that put all things under him, that God may be all in all.

29 Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?

["But."]{.class_s36N} Thank God for the disjunctive conjunction. ["Now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}So of all the people back in the Old Testament that all died and all passed away, He was the first one of them to get out of the grave [permanently]{.class_s36M}. He is the firstfruit, ["the first begotten of the dead"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 1:5), in that He is the first man that ever came up from the dead [never to die again.]{.class_s36M}

We know that the widow of Nain's son came up and [died]{.class_s36M}; Lazarus came up and [died]{.class_s36M}; Jonah came up and [died]{.class_s36M}; Eutychus came up and [died]{.class_s36M}; Dorcas came up and [died]{.class_s36M}; the widow of Shunem's son came up and [died]{.class_s36M}; but Christ is the first man that came up [never to die again.]{.class_s36M} Moses died and will come back and die again. Elijah went up alive one time, but he will die later. Enoch went up without dying. The only man that died and came up and will never die again is Jesus Christ. He is the firstfruits. [He is the first in a whole series.]{.class_s36M}

Verses 21--22 are reminiscent of what Paul wrote in Romans 5:12--21. Adam sinned and passed that sin and its results (death) down to all of his children (us). You die because you are ["in Adam."]{.class_s36N} Adam's death was on the [inside ]{.class_s36M}first, then on the [outside ]{.class_s36M}over 900 years later. So when you are born, you are born dead on the [inside ]{.class_s36M}(Eph. 2:1), and someday you will be put to bed with a shovel.

When you receive Christ, though, you are placed by the Holy Spirit [into the Body of Christ]{.class_s36S}. You are ["in Adam"]{.class_s36N} by birth (no choice); you are ["in Christ"]{.class_s36N} by the New Birth (your choice). So you are no longer ["in Adam"]{.class_s36N}; you are ["in Christ."]{.class_s36N} When you receive Christ, you are ["made alive"]{.class_s36N} inside, and someday, if the Lord tarries, you will be ["made alive"]{.class_s36N} on the [outside]{.class_s36S}.

In verse 23, there is an order to the resurrection. This order runs according to a garden with a harvest. If you keep on reading, it says in verses 35--38, ["With what body do they come? Thou fool, that which thou sowest is not quickened, except it die: And that which thou sowest, thou sowest not that body that shall be, but bare grain\....God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him."]{.class_s36N}

He likened this resurrection, therefore, to a [harvest]{.class_s36M}. The order then is going to have to be the order of a harvest. ["Every man in his own order: Christ the firstfruits."]{.class_s36N} So when the crops get ripe, you go out and pick the first ones, [firstfruits]{.class_s36M}. Then a little bit later, when they all get ripe, you harvest them, [but you never get all of them]{.class_s36M}. So a little bit later you go out and get [the gleanings.]{.class_s36M} A harvest has those three parts to it. If you have a vegetable garden, you know what that is. If you have tomatoes, you go out and get the first ones, and then after a little while a lot of them get ripe.[ But they don't all get ripe. ]{.class_s36M}After you have harvested all the main stuff, then one pops out and then another one a little bit later, [gleanings]{.class_s36M}.

Christ is the firstfruits. ["They that are Christ's at his coming"]{.class_s36N} are the main harvest, the second part. First Thessalonians 4:14 says, ["For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him."]{.class_s36N} Verse 16 of that same book and chapter says, ["And the dead in Christ shall rise first."]{.class_s36N}

Verse 24, ["Then cometh the end."]{.class_s36N} Three. Now, a lot later there, Paul talks about the Lord delivering up the kingdom to the Father out in eternity, but still at the end of verse 25 it says,[ "For he must reign, till,"]{.class_s36N} which shows the [end is at the beginning of his reign.]{.class_s36M} So when Christ comes to sit on the throne of David and reign over the earth at Jerusalem, [the first resurrection has to be over,]{.class_s36M} and the first resurrection has three parts. Here's how we know that.

Turn to Revelation 20, and notice that the Bible doesn't [mention ]{.class_s36M}the first resurrection until Christ comes back to [reign]{.class_s36M}. The Bible never calls the Rapture of the Church the first resurrection. The first resurrection isn't mentioned until the [beginning of the Millennial reign.]{.class_s36M} Revelation 20:4--5, ["And I saw thrones"]{.class_s36N} (Millennial reign), ["and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them: and I saw the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus, and for the word of God, and which had not worshipped the beast, neither his image, neither had received his mark upon their foreheads, or in their hands; and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years. But the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection."]{.class_s36N}

All right, Christ reigns on earth 1,000 years, and when he says, ["This is the first resurrection,"]{.class_s36N} it has to include the Rapture. [So it has three parts.]{.class_s36M} In Deuteronomy 16:16 these three parts are typified by [three feasts.]{.class_s36M} The first feast here is called the feast of the Passover or unleavened bread. At this feast, Christ is the firstfruits. The second feast takes place after the resurrection. [Then this harvest is the feast of Pentecost which comes fifty days after the resurrection]{.class_s36M}. The post-Tribulation rapture of the Tribulation saints is the feast of harvest or in-gathering, the [feast of tabernacles,]{.class_s36M} between September and October, [which dates the advent of Jesus Christ.]{.class_s36M}

This shows you there are three raptures. There is a rapture of [Old Testament saints]{.class_s36M} at the resurrection of Christ (Matt. 27:52--53). There is a rapture of [New Testament saints.]{.class_s36M} There is a rapture of [tribulation saints]{.class_s36M} around the Advent of Christ. All these fools who teach that the Christian goes [through the tribulation]{.class_s36M} are teaching that because they will not obey the word of God and [believe ]{.class_s36M}what they [read]{.class_s36M}. They're in Matthew 24. And if you want to get screwed up, Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews will screw you up. That rapture you read about in Matthew 24 is a [post-Tribulation rapture]{.class_s36M} of Tribulation saints, and the one in 1 Thessalonians is the Rapture of the Church. And the one you read about in Matthew 27 is a resurrection of [some ]{.class_s36M}of the Old Tesament saints. Matthew 27:52--53 says, ["And the graves were opened; and many bodies of the saints which slept arose, And came out of the graves after his resurrection, and went into the holy city and appeared unto many."]{.class_s36N}

First Thessalonians 4:16--17, ["The Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first: Then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds."]{.class_s36N} Then Matthew 24:31, ["And he shall send his angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they shall gather together his elect from the four winds."]{.class_s36N} Compare that last verse with Revelation 11:12, ["They heard a great voice from heaven saying unto them, Come up hither."]{.class_s36N}

Three parts. [Your first resurrection has three parts.]{.class_s36M} I don't know how close all these are. If I was going to guess, I would [guess ]{.class_s36M}that the Rapture of the Church takes place on Pentecost, fifty days after the resurrection. I believe Song of Solomon 2:10--13 is a reference to the Rapture of the Church, because in the Song of Solomon the reference is the [Bride of Christ]{.class_s36M}. So the first resurrection has three parts.

Now, I'll tell you a strange thing. In the Bible there is an expression that says, ["Come up hither."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}That expression only occurs [three times ]{.class_s36M}in the word of God---Revelation 4:1, Revelation 11:12, and Proverbs 25:7. Those references mark [the three raptures]{.class_s36M}. Proverbs 25:7 would refer to the resurrection of the Old Testament saints. John in Revelation 4, a type of the Bride of Christ, would refer to the Rapture of the Church, and Revelation 11, Moses and Elijah, would refer to a post-Tribulation rapture of the Tribulations saints. (For a thorough study of this subject, get [The Two Raptures,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1996.)

Before we go any further, isn't that a beautiful format? Did you ever see anything laid out like that, man? That word of God is laid out like an organism, and what I gave you runs from Deuteronomy to Proverbs, to Matthew to Revelation, to Thessalonians and Corinthians. [And it all comes out as one unit.]{.class_s36M}

Any fellow who tries to tell you the Christian has to go through the Tribulation is not much of a Bible student. Anybody who is a Bible student can see that. If a Christian goes through the Tribulation, why does Paul keep saying we're looking [for the Lord from heaven,]{.class_s36M} looking for the [appearing ]{.class_s36M}of our great God and Saviour? I'm not looking for the Tribulation. I'm looking for [Christ]{.class_s36M}. If I'm going through the Tribulation, do you know what I should be looking for? I should be looking for the Tribulation. [I'm not looking for the Tribulation.]{.class_s36M} I'm looking for the Lord. Like a fellow said, "I'm not looking for a [sign]{.class_s36M}. I'm listening for a [sound]{.class_s36M}."

While the material in verses 20--23 is well laid out in the Scriptures, I must admit, verses 24--29 are not quite as clear. When I get in an Open Bible Forum, the questions drift toward the future: "What's the date of the Rapture?" "When will the Temple be rebuilt?" "What about Daniel's 70th Week?" "What will we be doing during the Millennium"? "What happens to the lost when they're cast into the Lake of Fire?" etc. Eventually the questions get around to Eternity and [this passage.]{.class_s36M} While I don't understand everything about these verses, here is the simple explanation. Verse 25 would be the Millennial reign of Christ culminating in the fiery destruction of Gog and Magog at the last battle on Jerusalem (Rev. 20:1--9). Verse 26 takes place at the Great White Throne Judgment where Satan, who has the power of death (Heb. 2:14), is cast into the Lake of Fire (Rev. 20:10). Revelation 20:14 says, further, ["And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death."]{.class_s36N}

At the Great White Throne Judgment, God judges man by Jesus Christ (Rom. 2:16; Acts 17:31; John 5:22). That is when ["all things"]{.class_s36N} are put ["under his]{.class_s36N} (Jesus')[ feet"]{.class_s36N} by God the Father (vs. 27 cf. Phil. 2:9--11). This is the apex of Christ's exaltation. And while God exalts Christ's name above even His own (Phil. 2:9), the Lord Jesus Himself is never exalted above the Father (vs. 27).

That brings us to verse 28, and given the interpretation above, this would take place [after]{.class_s36S} the White Throne Judgment and [before]{.class_s36S} the New Heavens and New Earth of Revelation 21:1. This would be verified by the fact that Jesus as the Lamb of God is only mentioned three times [apart ]{.class_s36S}from God the Father in Revelation 21:1--22:5 (the description of the New Heavens, New Earth, and New Jeruslaem). In each of these references, the verses speak of something belonging to Christ: Revelation 21:9, ["the Lamb's wife"]{.class_s36N}; Revelation 21:14, ["the twelve apostles of the Lamb"]{.class_s36N}; and Revelation 21:27, ["the Lamb's book of life."]{.class_s36N} The other references to the Lamb in Revelation 21 and 22 directly tie him to God the Father: Revelation 21:22, ["The Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are ]{.class_s36N}(plural) [the ]{.class_s36N}(singular) [temple of it"]{.class_s36N}; Revelation 21:23, ["the glory of God did lighten it, and the Lamb is the light thereof"]{.class_s36N}; Revelation 22:1, 3,[ "the throne ]{.class_s36N}(singular) [of God and of the Lamb."]{.class_s36N}

So what it looks like from these verses is [before ]{.class_s36M}Eternity begins Jesus Christ, the man, ceases to exist, and He goes back into the Godhead in His position as the [Word of God ]{.class_s36M}(John 1:1--2). So God, as the eternal Trinity, is all there is; He is no longer "manifested in the flesh." I know that is hard to comprehend, but when has the Trinity ever been easy to understand?

Now, Clarence Larkin has a different view, and you can read it in his commentary on Revelation and his book on [Dispensational Truth]{.class_s36M} (both available from the Bible Baptist Bookstore). And since Clarence Larkin is [the]{.class_s36M} source for all prophetic exposition among premillennial Fundamentalists and Conservatives in the twentieth (and twenty-first) century, I would be doing you a disservice if I did not try to describe his system to you. So briefly, here is how Clarence Larkin handles verses 25--28.

There is a difference between Christ's Millennial kingdom and the kingdom of Revelation 21--22. Larkin calls the kingdom of the New Heavens and New Earth the "perfect Kingdom." He equates it with ["the dispensation of the fulness of times"]{.class_s36N} in Ephesians 1:10, where God gathers ["together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth; even in him."]{.class_s36N} (See comments on this verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Galatians through Colossians,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1997.)

Going to Isaiah 66:22, he shows that Israel, as a nation, will exist [into ]{.class_s36M}the New Heavens and New Earth. From there he goes to Deuteronomy 7:9 and shows that God will keep His covenant with Israel for ["a thousand generations."]{.class_s36N} Using the age of Jesus Christ on earth as the basis for a generation, he has the "Perfect Kingdom" lasting 33,000 years. After this 33,000 years, he has the "Great Abdication" in which Christ, the Son of Man, [merges back into the Godhead as solely the Son of God. Larkin does not believe Jesus surrenders His human nature.]{.class_s36M}

After the "Perfect Kingdom" has ceased with the "Great Abdication," the [Eternal Ages]{.class_s36M} begin, which he calls "the Ages of Ages." Here time ceases, and God's servants (Rev. 22:3) will reign with him then (Rev. 22:5).

Now, brethren, if there is any explanation beyond those two, I do not know what it is, and it will take someone who has put more prayer and study into it than I have to figure the thing out.

Verse 29, ["Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? Why are they then baptized for the dead?"]{.class_s36N}

This is a Mormon verse. The Mormon reads it this way: "Else what shall they do which get baptized [in the place of a fellow who dies]{.class_s36M}?" That is not what the verse says.

Even the Scofield notes are a little misleading. It is [not ]{.class_s36M}referring to "being baptized to fill the ranks of a dead brother in Christ." The way you solve this thing is real simple. You just stick with the context. In the context to what does ["the dead"]{.class_s36N} refer? Does it refer to a fellow who just died? [It hasn't one time in twenty-seven verses.]{.class_s36M} Look at ["the dead."]{.class_s36N} Verse 12, the ["resurrection of the dead"]{.class_s36N}; verse 13, ["the dead"]{.class_s36N}; verse 15, ["the dead"]{.class_s36N}; verse 16, ["the dead"]{.class_s36N}; verse 20, ["the dead."]{.class_s36N} Every time you find ["the dead"]{.class_s36N} mentioned there, it is referring to [everybody ]{.class_s36M}who ever died. It is referring to [all the people]{.class_s36M} that are dead. It is not a reference to [one individual.]{.class_s36M} In verses 12--13 it is not even [saved ]{.class_s36M}dead. It's a reference to [anybody ]{.class_s36M}who is dead. So the expression ["the dead" ]{.class_s36N}in verse 29 refers to [anybody who is dead.]{.class_s36M}

Simon Peter said baptism was a [figure ]{.class_s36M}of the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ (1 Pet. 3:21). When I take a man and put him down under the water, I am baptizing him ["for the dead."]{.class_s36N} When that fellow goes down I am saying, "You are dead, you are buried, you're down with all the rest of them." [You're all dead.]{.class_s36M} I bring them back up and say, "The dead are going to come up." See that? There isn't a man who was baptized, [at least in a Bible-believing Baptist church,]{.class_s36M} that wasn't ["baptized for the dead."]{.class_s36N} But he wasn't baptized for some fellow that just kicked the bucket. [The verse doesn't say that.]{.class_s36M}

15:30 And why stand we in jeopardy every hour?

31 I protest by your rejoicing which I have in Christ Jesus our Lord, I die daily.

32 If after the manner of men I have fought with beasts at Ephesus, what advantageth it me, if the dead rise not? let us eat and drink; for to morrow we die.

33 Be not deceived: evil communications corrupt good manners.

34 Awake to righteousness, and sin not; for some have not the knowledge of God: I speak this to your shame.

Verse 30, ["And why stand we in jeopardy every hour?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}What is the sense in sticking your neck out for Christ and making a fool out of yourself for Christ and sacrificing your health and time and money and talent if there is no resurrection?

["I protest by your rejoicing which I have in Christ Jesus our Lord, I die daily"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 31).[ ]{.class_s36P-0}There's quite a statement, "You talk about dead people! [I die every day!"]{.class_s36M} Notice there is a doctrinal death and a practical death. In Romans 6:1--6 it is [doctrinal]{.class_s36M}; you're dead because of your position in Christ. You don't [have ]{.class_s36M}to ["die daily."]{.class_s36N} A dead man can't die [daily]{.class_s36M}; he is [already dead.]{.class_s36M} Romans 6:1--6 is your doctrinal position. That is your [standing]{.class_s36M}. Your state is that flesh keeps kicking up. Right? All right, then daily you have to die. The practical part is ["mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth"]{.class_s36N} (Col. 3:5), ["reckon ye also yourself to be dead"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 6:11), ["die daily"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 31), "[if any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 16:24).

That's the practical position. [Doctrinally ]{.class_s36M}you're dead. The Christian is always dead. According to Romans 6, [he is dead and buried with Christ.]{.class_s36M} According to Colossians 2, he is cut loose from the flesh, and he is still in a corpse, and he carries the corpse until he dies. When you find passages like ["mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth,"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}that is talking about a [practical ]{.class_s36M}thing. That is talking about [you fighting against the flesh and putting the flesh down.]{.class_s36M} But as far as the position of your flesh is concerned, [your flesh is dead.]{.class_s36M}

Paul said, ["I am crucified with Christ: nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 2:20).[ ]{.class_s36N}The way that works is before you got saved your flesh was connected, on the inside, to your soul. When the Lord saved you, [He made an operation]{.class_s36M} (Col. 2), and that soul was cut loose from the flesh by the word of God (Heb. 4:12). That's an [operation]{.class_s36M}, but you didn't see how it took place. It was a genuine miracle.
