After my soul was cut loose like that, [all around inside my body my soul doesn't touch my flesh at any one single point. ]{.class_s36M}You say, "How can that be?" I don't know how it can be, [but that's how it is]{.class_s36M}. So that soul is loosed in there. It's like an ice cube in an ice tray that has been cut loose and is just rattling in the tray; whereas, before, it was stuck to it. That soul, being cut loose,[ is in Christ,]{.class_s36M} and the [spirit ]{.class_s36M}in that soul is joined to Christ. That soul is in Christ and saved, but [that soul is still inside a body of death.]{.class_s36M} Paul said in Romans 7:24, ["O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}It's an active warfare. The Bible says, ["abstain from fleshly lusts, which war against the soul"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 2:11).[ ]{.class_s36P-0}So this flesh is after the soul. It can't [get it]{.class_s36M}, but it [wars against it.]{.class_s36M} You would think this flesh is alive because you are engaged in combat with it, [but the Bible says it's dead.]{.class_s36M} The Bible says it is nailed, and if you want victory, you reckon the flesh dead. When you reckon it [dead]{.class_s36M}, you have the victory, and when you[ don't]{.class_s36M} reckon it dead, you don't have the victory. Like I said, it will put up a holler.

The ["beasts"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 32) are not literal animals with which Paul fought in the arena at Ephesus. Notice that he said ["after the manner of men."]{.class_s36N} So the ["beasts"]{.class_s36N} were men. He warned the Philippians (Phil. 3:2) to ["beware of dogs." "Dogs,"]{.class_s36N} in the New Testament, are unsaved Gentiles (Matt. 15:21--27). The Lord used a vision of [unclean beasts]{.class_s36S} to get Peter to witness to unsaved [Gentiles ]{.class_s36S}(Acts 10:9--22). Christ called the Pharisees[ "ye generation of vipers."]{.class_s36N}

["What advantageth it me, if the dead rise not? let us eat and drink; for to morrow we die."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}Paul is saying[, ]{.class_s36M}"If the dead don't come up, why am I subjecting myself to possible death and going through all this persecution and all this hell on earth? If the dead don't come up, then live it up! That is the hedonistic teaching of eat, drink, and be merry, for tomorrow you are dead like a dog!

Verse 33 is another of those great verses in 1 Corinthians that should be committed to memory. It shows that the [wrong company]{.class_s36M} will corrupt you. Proverbs 13:20 says, ["a companion of fools shall be destroyed."]{.class_s36N} Hanging out with the wrong crowd will corrupt a Christian. ["Manners"]{.class_s36N} here is more than just politeness. It's all conduct, and especially in regard to witnessing. Look at verse 34.

["Awake to righteousness, and sin not; for some have not the knowledge of God: I speak this to your shame."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}It is to the Christian's [shame]{.class_s36M}. The reason some folks don't have[ "the knowledge of God"]{.class_s36N} is because of [Christians]{.class_s36M}, and they have been corrupted, verse 33, by ["evil communications."]{.class_s36N} That is why they are not witnessing and telling people about the Lord. So he says, ["Be not deceived: evil communications corrupt good manners."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}There are some folks that you cannot hang around and come out clean. There are some folks you can't hang around and come out on fire for the Lord.

There are going to be people with whom you associate in the ministry that will [kill your zeal for the Lord.]{.class_s36M} I make it a habit in my own personal life (and I don't know whether you will in yours or not, or whether you should or not) to cut off communications with every Christian who in [any way]{.class_s36M} tends to drag me away from the word of God or prayer or witnessing or preaching the truth. I've done that for fifty-three years. As a result, I don't have too many friends. There will be some Christians with whom you may want to stay in fellowship, and they may be [likeable ]{.class_s36M}and [loveable ]{.class_s36M}and sweet and nice and [wonderful]{.class_s36M}, but they will kill your zeal for the Lord. [I drop those kind of Christians.]{.class_s36M} I drop them and go right on forward. Consequently, I have [lost ]{.class_s36M}a good many friends.

Spend your time with the Christians that are doing something for the Lord. If a man wants to be wise, he had better be a [companion ]{.class_s36M}of wise men. The way to learn how to win souls is to hang around [soul winners.]{.class_s36M} The way to learn the Bible is hang around with the folks [that know it. ]{.class_s36M}The way to learn how to pray is hang around the folks [that pray. ]{.class_s36M}The way to learn how to backslide is to hang around the [backsliders]{.class_s36M}. That is all there is to that. Birds of a feather flock together.

["I speak this to your shame."]{.class_s36N} Isn't it a shame when unsaved people don't know the Lord because of Christians? It's bad enough when they don't know the Lord because of the world, the flesh, and the devil, but because of a [Christian]{.class_s36S}! The Christian should clean up and quit sinning so he can be an effective witness for Christ.

15:35 But some man will say, How are the dead raised up? and with what body do they come?

36 Thou fool, that which thou sowest is not quickened, except it die:

37 And that which thou sowest, thou sowest not that body that shall be, but bare grain, it may chance of wheat, or of some other grain:

38 But God giveth it a body as it hath pleased him, and to every seed his own body.

39 All flesh is not the same flesh: but there is one kind of flesh of men, another flesh of beasts, another of fishes, and another of birds.

40 There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial: but the glory of the celestial is one, and the glory of the terrestrial is another.

41 There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars: for one star differeth from another star in glory.

42 So also is the resurrection of the dead. It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption:

43 It is sown in dishonour; it is raised in glory: it is sown in weakness; it is raised in power:

44 It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body. There is a natural body, and there is a spiritual body.

45 And so it is written, The first man Adam was made a living soul; the last Adam was made a quickening spirit.

46 Howbeit that was not first which is spiritual, but that which is natural; and afterward that which is spiritual.

47 The first man is of the earth, earthy: the second man is the Lord from heaven.

48 As is the earthy, such are they also that are earthy: and as is the heavenly, such are they also that are heavenly.

49 And as we have borne the image of the earthy, we shall also bear the image of the heavenly.

50 Now this I say, brethren, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God; neither doth corruption inherit incorruption.

Now, for sake of argument, Paul posits the question that an infidel would ask:[ "How are the dead raised up? and with what body do they come?"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 35). Paul's response to such a question is ["Thou fool"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 36). Isn't that something? Evidently, he's not worried about [name calling]{.class_s36M} (Titus 1:12). He also must not be too worried about violating Christ's words from the "Sermon on the Mount" (Matt. 5:22); [Christ Himself certainly wasn't ]{.class_s36M}(Luke 24:25).

Watch out for these people who are always trying to drag you back under a pre-crucifixion, pre-resurrection passage. The Gospel (vss. 3--4) changes all kinds of things in regards to salvation and conduct.

In verse 36, Paul repeats a principle of nature which Christ stated in John 12:24, ["Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit."]{.class_s36N} In verses 36--38, Paul uses the illustration of planting grain to show what happens to our bodies in the resurrection.

First, a new body won't come without [death]{.class_s36M}. The seed dies in the ground, and then it is able to grow up into a new plant. Next, the body that comes out of the ground is always [more ]{.class_s36M}than what went in.

Corn is one of the most remarkable examples I've ever seen. Put a kernel of corn, no bigger than the end of your fingernail in the ground, and pretty soon that plant pops up and goes higher and spreads out---they grow so fast you think you can almost hear them growing. You end up with an ear of corn with about [fourteen rows ]{.class_s36M}of [thirty kernels]{.class_s36M} in a row on that ear. That is a miracle. Well, he said the resurrection is like [that]{.class_s36M}.

In the same way, our bodies have to go into the ground and rot. But when they come up, they come up different and better than when they went in. So the body that goes in, the one right now, is [not]{.class_s36M} the one that comes up.

The resurrection body is [a new body]{.class_s36M}. It is a body [like ]{.class_s36M}the Lord Jesus Christ had at His resurrection (Phil. 3:21). It is the body of a 33-year-old, [sinless male]{.class_s36M} that can become [visible ]{.class_s36M}and [invisible ]{.class_s36M}at will and move [faster than light.]{.class_s36M} All the science fiction movies and all the science fiction novels are counterfeits of the real thing.

["All flesh is not the same flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 39). The old-time scientists thought it was, that all flesh came from a basic substance called "protoplasm." But they finally caught up with the Bible after a while. Just give them time. ["But there is one kind of flesh of men, another flesh of beasts, another of fishes, and another of birds."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}Notice those four groups: men, beast, fish, birds. That is laid out in the major groups in Genesis 1, where the fishes show up first and the birds who are kin and then the beasts and then man.

Verse 40 goes a step further. The ["terrestrial bodies"]{.class_s36N} are bodies on earth. The ["celestial bodies"]{.class_s36N} are bodies in the heavens. This includes the third heaven. So angels, cherubim, and seraphim all have ["celestial bodies."]{.class_s36N} [The devil has a celestial body.]{.class_s36M} An earthly body is a wonder of God. David says, ["I am fearfully and wonderfully made"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 139:14). But the heavenly bodies we will one day inhabit have their own special features and wonders.

To illustrate this, Paul uses the ["celestial bodies"]{.class_s36N} which we can see in the heavens: the sun, moon, and stars (vs. 41). The ["glory"]{.class_s36N} of each differs from the others. The sun puts out more light and is greater in size than any other body in our solar system. The moon reflects the light of the sun, and God gave each a purpose (Gen. 1:14--18). Each star differs from the other stars in the amount of light it puts out and its size. There are stars bigger, sometimes much bigger, than our sun. Stars also differ in color: yellow, blue, white, and red.

The Mormons try to use verse 41 as a prooftext for different hierarchies and degrees of resurrection in glory, where you have some Christians reigning over others and some are more holy than others in eternity. But verse 42 simply said ["So also."]{.class_s36N} So it is an illustration.

["So also is the resurrection of the dead"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 42). OK, now here we start. Here's the body. ["It is sown in corruption."]{.class_s36N} When you die, you go in the ground and rot. ["It is raised in incorruption."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}The first thing about the resurrection body is it is [incorruptible]{.class_s36M}. It can't rot. The body you get in the resurrection can never decay and decompose again.

Isn't it strange how much a person gets in salvation for which he hadn't bargained? People think that Christianity is just some [religion ]{.class_s36M}that somebody accepts. It's the answer to the whole thing. Here's a fellow trying to develop, in a test-tube, a body that will never die and make life and all this junk. [Why, the whole thing is in the Book.]{.class_s36M}

The Lord has ways of confounding people. Everything you want and everything scientists are trying to get a man gets[ in Christ.]{.class_s36M} When I got saved I didn't know anything. I just knew I was going to Hell and didn't want to go. That's all I knew. I trusted Christ and got saved and was glad I wasn't going to Hell. That's all I knew about it. I never knew I was going to get [a sinless body]{.class_s36M} that will travel [faster than the speed of light]{.class_s36M} and become [visible ]{.class_s36M}and [invisible ]{.class_s36M}at will and have a reserved mansion in New Jerusalem. I didn't know all that.

Verse 43, ["It is sown in dishonour."]{.class_s36N} It's obviously [dishonorable]{.class_s36M}. What are you going to do? Your old naked body lies there in the casket and can't even get up. ["It is raised in glory."]{.class_s36N} It comes up out of the tomb a [glorious body]{.class_s36M} worthy of honor. ["It is sown in weakness." ]{.class_s36N}It collapses, falls to pieces. ["It is raised in power."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}It comes out of the grave in a rush and travels off 150 million light years a second, a lot faster than you have ever travelled before.

Verse 44, ["It is sown a natural body." ]{.class_s36N}That is, earthly, made of the [dust]{.class_s36M}. ["It is raised a spiritual body."]{.class_s36N} Notice it is not raised a [spirit body]{.class_s36M}. It is raised a [spiritual body]{.class_s36M}. ["There is a natural body."]{.class_s36N} There it is. ["And there is a spiritual body."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}You haven't seen the [spiritual ]{.class_s36M}body yet. ["It doth not yet appear what we shall be"]{.class_s36N} (1 John 3:2). But ["there is a spiritual body."]{.class_s36N} Notice it is not a spirit. When Christ rose from the dead, He said, "[A spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 24:39).[ ]{.class_s36P-0}[He wasn't a spirit.]{.class_s36M} He had a body. It had flesh and it had bones. [But it wasn't a natural body.]{.class_s36M}

["And so it is written, The first man Adam was made a living soul"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 45 cf. Gen. 2:7). ["The last Adam"]{.class_s36N} is a reference to Christ. So Adam is the first man. Christ is the last man. ["The last Adam was made a quickening spirit."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}Not only was He raised from the dead, but He is able to raise [others ]{.class_s36M}from the dead (John 5:28--29).

Verses 46 and 47 lay down a great principle which is illustrated throughout Scripture. When God sends two men along who are associated with each other, the first is the [natural man]{.class_s36M}, the [worldly man]{.class_s36M}; the second is the [spiritual man]{.class_s36M}, the [heavenly man.]{.class_s36M}

So in the Scriptures, the [natural man]{.class_s36M} is Cain, the firstborn. Abel is the [spiritual man]{.class_s36M}. The child of God's promise is Isaac, not Ishmael. The carnal man is Esau; the spiritual man is Jacob. Even with Jacob, it is not [his first name,]{.class_s36M} Jacob---supplanter, that is good; it is his [second name,]{.class_s36M} Israel---a prince with God, that is given by God. Moses can't get anyone into the promised land, so it has to be [Joshua]{.class_s36M}. So it is not Adam's body that we want, but Christ's.

Just as your spirit bears either the image of Adam or the image of Christ (vss. 21--22), in the same way your body will either be in [Adam's image]{.class_s36S} or [Christ's image.]{.class_s36S} Right now, your body is in Adam's image. It is an earthly image that will die and return to dust. But when the Lord returns, He will finish what He began at your salvation. Romans 8:29, ["For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son."]{.class_s36N} [That ]{.class_s36S}is your destination! And you are predestinated. [Your destination is fixed.]{.class_s36S}

When you trusted Christ as your Saviour, the Lord [fixed your destination,]{.class_s36M} and your final destination is to be just exactly like Jesus Christ, 1 John 3. This brings up the problem: Are there any women in heaven? First John 3:1,[ "Behold, what manner of love the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called the sons of God."]{.class_s36N} Are you ladies ["the sons of God"]{.class_s36N}? Sure you are. ["But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God"]{.class_s36N} (John 1:12). Did you believe on Him? Did you receive Him? Well, you're sons. How's [that ]{.class_s36M}for women's lib?

Now, the Bible is very specific on the resurrection body of the Christian. First John 3:2 says,[ "when he ]{.class_s36N}(Christ) [shall appear, we shall be LIKE HIM; for we shall see him as he is."]{.class_s36N} Philippians 3:21 nails it down,[ "Who shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto his glorious body, according to the working whereby he is able even to subdue all things unto himself."]{.class_s36N}

So it is not general; [it is specific as to anatomy]{.class_s36M}. A Christian is going to end up with a body [just like the resurrected body of Jesus Christ]{.class_s36M}; the glorified body of [a 33-year-old male.]{.class_s36M} Now, it won't have the scars: the nail prints in the hands and feet and the spear thrust in the side. Those are the marks of His atonement for sinners that He will show to the nation of Israel as evidence that He is the same Messiah they crucified (Zech. 12:10, 13:6). But in every other aspect, including gender, the Christian's new body will be like Christ's.

I know some of the women find this objectionable. They say, "Who wants to be a man?" and all that kind of business. But who would want to be a [woman ]{.class_s36M}in heaven? The woman has always been a secondary creation. She was made after the man, to be a help to the man. That is [not]{.class_s36M} the result of the Fall. The woman is a part of the man, so she has no name of her own.

Moreover, everyone in heaven is a [male]{.class_s36M}. God the Father is a male, God the Son is a male, God the Holy Spirit is a male, the angels are male. When God made Adam, He did not make the woman until Adam noticed that all the male animals had a [partner]{.class_s36M}, and he did not (Gen. 2:19--20).

The woman is a "womb-man," [a man with a womb.]{.class_s36M} She was made to reproduce the species. Well, there won't be any reproduction in heaven (Matt. 22:30), so why be a woman?

You see, people have not thought the thing out. If the ladies remained ladies in heaven, and you went with your family on earth, pretty soon all the families would divide off out of self-interest, and you would have Hell in heaven. It would never work.

By the same token, [what age ]{.class_s36M}would you decide to be in heaven? You may love your saintly old grandmother and remember her in her old age, [but what woman would want to be 80 or 90 years old forever?]{.class_s36M}

A little baby dies and goes home to heaven. Now, you want to see your baby again. How old is the baby going to be in heaven? [Do you want to nurse a baby in]{.class_s36M} [heaven forever?]{.class_s36M} You say, "The baby will grow up." The baby will grow up to [what]{.class_s36M}? Folks don't think.

If you ladies had an age to be in heaven, what age would you pick? You wouldn't want to be [forty ]{.class_s36M}forever in heaven. What would you do with the competition?

When you get to heaven you are going to be just like Jesus Christ. The problem comes up then, what age would a [man ]{.class_s36M}like to be forever? The Lord has already decided for you. The Lord has decided that if you are under thirty you are too [young]{.class_s36M}. It's over thirty. It is thirty-three and[ one half]{.class_s36M}. In the Bible that is the prime of life.

The next question that arises is "will everyone from Adam on have a resurrection body like Christ's"? Will they be sons of God just as the Christian is? I don't know all about that. All I can tell you is this. Only Adam is individually called ["the son of God"]{.class_s36N} in the Old Testament (Luke 3:38), and that was because of [a direct act of creation]{.class_s36M} that took place before the Fall. Israel is corporately, as a nation, called God's son (Exod. 4:22). There is one prophecy in Hosea 1:10 that says that in the future they will be called ["the sons]{.class_s36N} (plural) [of the living God."]{.class_s36N}

Some of the Old Testament saints have already arisen (Matt. 27:52--53), and at least one (Moses---Rev. 11:7--10) will die again. So unless Moses is the exception to the rule, the resurrection bodies of these saints are [not ]{.class_s36M}like Christ's.

We also know that there is a post-Tribulation rapture of the Tribulation saints. If any of the Old Testament saints come up here, there is no indication in Scripture. These saints go into the Millennium and die. Since [keeping the commandments]{.class_s36M} was part of their salvation, they get their eternal life off a [tree ]{.class_s36M}(Rev. 12:17, 14:12 cf. 22:14). But they are not given access to the ["tree of life"]{.class_s36N} until Eternity starts. So they do die again.

In Ezekiel 37, you have the physical resurrection of all the saints in the nation of Israel. The question is when. Look at Ezekiel 37:24,[ "DAVID my servant shall be king over them."]{.class_s36N} The verse didn't say the seed of David, so it can't be Christ in the Millennial reign. During Eternity, Christ is in the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:22--23), which leaves David free to rule Israel on earth. He is said to be ["their prince for ever" ]{.class_s36N}(Ezek. 37:25).

Look next at Ezekiel 37:26. The Lord makes ["an everlasting covenant"]{.class_s36N} with Israel. Those words[ "for ever," "everlasting,"]{.class_s36N} and ["for evermore"]{.class_s36N} in verses 25--28 of Ezekiel 37 point to Eternity. So what it looks like is, at the Great White Throne Judgment, when God brings up the unsaved dead, He also brings up the saved from [Old Testament Israel ]{.class_s36M}that did not get up in Matthew 27.

This is exactly what the Jews of Christ's day were taught. Martha replied to the Lord, in John 11:24, ["I know that he]{.class_s36N} (Lazarus)[ shall rise again in the resurrection at the last day."]{.class_s36N}

The Old Testament Jew believed in a General Resurrection at a General Judgment. When Christ came along, He revealed that those who believed in Him would get up [before]{.class_s36M} that time (John 11:25--26).

This resurrection "at the last day" was believed by Old Testament Jew [and]{.class_s36M} Gentile alike. Job said, before Moses ever wrote, ["For I know that my redeemer liveth, and that he shall stand at the latter day upon the earth: And though after my skin worms destroy this body, yet in my flesh shall I see God: Whom I shall see for myself, and mine eyes shall behold, and not another; though my reins be consumed within me"]{.class_s36N} (Job 19:25--27). So at the Great White Throne, the [saved Gentiles ]{.class_s36M}from Adam to the death of Christ are also raised.

As to whether all of them are like Adam before he sinned and need to eat of the ["tree of life"]{.class_s36N} in order to get eternal life, I am not sure about that. The New Jerusalem and the ["tree of life"]{.class_s36N} seem to be connected with the Gentiles, not Israel: ["the kings of the earth"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 21:24), ["the nations"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 21:26),[ "the nations"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 22:2).

So that is where we will have to leave it.

Verse 50, ["Now this I say, brethren, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God; neither doth corruption inherit incorruption."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}There are [seven ]{.class_s36M}important things about the verse. Notice, first of all, if a man is [postmillennial ]{.class_s36M}he is teaching fantasy. If a man teaches that we are bringing in the kingdom, he is teaching [fantasy]{.class_s36M}. You can't bring in the kingdom because ["flesh and blood"]{.class_s36N} can't ["inherit the kingdom."]{.class_s36N} All of you are flesh and blood. Well, you can't bring in any kingdom. [If you brought it in, you couldn't get it because you can't inherit it.]{.class_s36M}

When a man teaches that we are all in "kingdom work spreading the kingdom," he is a [Bible heretic]{.class_s36M}.[ ]{.class_s36P-0}The combination that [cannot ]{.class_s36M}get in the kingdom is a combination of flesh and blood. The combination that can get in is flesh and [bone]{.class_s36M}.

When the Lord produced Eve out of Adam, Adam said, ["This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh"]{.class_s36N} (Gen. 2:23). He said nothing about [blood ]{.class_s36M}at all. Christ comes up from the dead, and He says, ["A spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 24:39). He[ ]{.class_s36P-0}said nothing about [blood ]{.class_s36M}at all. Your blood is corruptible. It is the part that transmits to you the old Adamic nature and death.

Christ said to a certain bunch of people in flesh and blood, ["Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 25:34).[ ]{.class_s36P-0}But again, you are in a Tribulation setting, Matthew 25. There will be people that will go out of the Tribulation and go into the kingdom of God and get in the kingdom of God that are [flesh and blood]{.class_s36M}, but that isn't the context of 1 Corinthians 15. The context of 1 Corinthians 15 is Church Age saints: "[Now this I say, brethren."]{.class_s36N} The people that go into the Millennium undoubtedly will die in the Millennium because their [blood ]{.class_s36M}is no good.

Now, look at that word ["inherit"]{.class_s36N} a moment. Every time you find the word ["inherit"]{.class_s36N} in the Bible in relation to the kingdom, it is always a reference to an [earned reward]{.class_s36M}. It is never a reference to [salvation ]{.class_s36M}by grace. Colossians 3:24 says, ["Knowing that of the Lord ye shall receive the reward of the inheritance: for ye serve the Lord Christ."]{.class_s36N} So an inheritance is a [reward ]{.class_s36M}for serving Christ. If you serve the flesh, you don't get any reward. Ephesians 5:5 says, ["For this ye know, that no whoremonger, nor unclean person, nor covetous man, who is an idolater, hath any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God."]{.class_s36N} Here in 1 Corinthians 15:50, the emphasis is on the natural, earthly body:[ "neither doth corruption inherit incorruption."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}I have a body that is corruptible, and it can't get into that kingdom.[ It has to be changed.]{.class_s36M}

15:51 Behold, I shew you a mystery; We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed,

52 In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump: for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed.

53 For this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality.

54 So when this corruptible shall have put on incorruption, and this mortal shall have put on immortality, then shall be brought to pass the saying that is written, Death is swallowed up in victory.

55 O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory?

56 The sting of death is sin; and the strength of sin is the law.

57 But thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.

Now we have reached that great Rapture passage here in 1 Corinthians 15. This passage, along with 1 Thessalonians 4:13--18, make up the two definitive passages in the New Testament on the Rapture of the Church. In both passages, Paul expected the Rapture to take place in his lifetime. ["Then we ]{.class_s36N}(Paul includes himself)[ which are alive and remain shall be caught up"]{.class_s36N} (1 Thess. 4:17). Here in 1 Corinthians 15:51, Paul says, ["We ]{.class_s36N}(again, Paul includes himself)[ shall not all sleep."]{.class_s36N}

The word ["sleep"]{.class_s36N} here is the Bible's way of describing death; it's speaking about ["the dead in Christ"]{.class_s36N} (1 Thess. 4:16 cf. 4:13--15). When Lazarus died, Christ said,[ "Our friend Lazarus sleepeth; but I go, that I may awake him out o sleep\...Howbeit Jesus spake of his death"]{.class_s36N} (John 11:11, 13).

Since Paul was expecting the Rapture in his lifetime, we say that the Rapture is [imminent]{.class_s36M}; i.e., it could take place any moment. Now, I have an idea from Scripture as to when I think it will be. Song of Solomon 2:8--13 [seems ]{.class_s36M}to indicate that it will take place [in the springtime, probably at the Feast of Pentecost.]{.class_s36M} But I'm not waiting till then for Him to show up. [I'm expecting Him now!]{.class_s36M}

The "dearly beloved brethren" make fun of us here and call us a "cult" and "datesetters" because I have hazarded a few [guesses ]{.class_s36M}on the date of the Rapture. Of course, that is all they have ever been is guesses; I have never laid down a firm date for the Rapture a day in my life. I have always been very careful to preface it with "[if ]{.class_s36M}our calendar is right." By now (2002), it seems pretty evident that our calendar is [not]{.class_s36M} right. Quite frankly, right now, I have no more guesses to make.

People ask, "Well, what did you do to get ready for the Rapture back in 1989?" I guess they thought we were going to sell our property and dress in white sheets and go up on a mountain somewhere. I tell them, ["I scheduled my meetings for the next year."]{.class_s36M} The Lord said, ["Occupy till I come"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 19:13). When the Lord returns, He expects to find us doing what He called us to do. If He calls you to go to Bible school and He comes before you have had a chance to finish, [that]{.class_s36M} is where He expects to find you.

Then again, people ask, "How did you feel when He didn't come at the time you thought?" I felt very [disappointed]{.class_s36M}; that's how I felt---but [not]{.class_s36M} because He didn't come back when I expected. I was disappointed because I didn't get to see Him! I don't care if He doesn't come back when I guess He will, as long as He comes back soon. I'm looking for [Him!]{.class_s36M}

Now, back to verse 51, ["Behold I shew you a mystery."]{.class_s36N} Then what he is going to show you is [something that has not been revealed before.]{.class_s36M} If you wanted any verse in the Bible to prove a pre-tribulation rapture, [that ]{.class_s36M}would be the verse, because the post-tribulation rapture was [not ]{.class_s36M}a mystery. It is found in the Old Testament. I'll give you the references. Psalm 50:4--5, the catching up of Jewish saints: ["He shall call to the heavens from above, and to the earth, that he may judge his people. Gather my saints together unto me; those that have made a covenant with me by sacrifice."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}[This is found throughout the Old Testament. ]{.class_s36M}Paul knew about it and preached about it. What Paul is going to show us is a [mystery ]{.class_s36M}that hasn't been revealed.

[Isaiah 26:16--17, ]{.class_s36K}"Lord, in trouble have they visited thee, they poured out a prayer when thy chastening was upon them. Like as a woman with child, that draweth near the time[ ]{.class_s36P-1}of her delivery, is in pain, and crieth out in her pangs; so have we been in thy sight, O [Lord]{.class_s36Z-1}.[ ]{.class_s36P-1}[Verses 19--21,]{.class_s36K} "Thy dead men shall live, together with my dead body shall they arise. Awake and sing, ye that dwell in dust: for thy dew is as the dew of herbs, and the earth shall cast out the dead. Come, my people, enter thou into thy chambers, and shut thy doors about thee: hide thyself as it were for a little moment, until the indignation be overpast. For, behold, the [Lord ]{.class_s36Z-1}cometh out of his place to punish the inhabitants of the earth."

[That's]{.class_s36M} the Second Advent. There's that post-tribulation rapture and that second advent right in there, back in Isaiah, back in the Psalms. [That's]{.class_s36M} no mystery. It's clearly given.

All right, 1 Corinthians 15 is a [mystery]{.class_s36S}, something that isn't revealed in the Old Testament. It's like the Church Age. The church may be found there, but it wasn't [revealed ]{.class_s36S}until it was revealed to Paul. So what is this ["mystery"]{.class_s36N}? First, ["we shall not all sleep."]{.class_s36N} [Not every Christian will have to die. ]{.class_s36S}That was a mystery. Enoch is an Old Testament example of that, but unless the Holy Spirit had told you that by revelation to Paul, you would have never known how to make the application.

["We shall all be changed."]{.class_s36N} That is part of the mystery. The corruptible body of the Christian will be changed into an incorruptible body like Christ's. There is going to be a transformation of [living Christians,]{.class_s36S} and they are not all going to die. They are going to be transformed [while they are alive.]{.class_s36S}

Verse 52, ["In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}The thing will be quicker than the snap of a finger. It will be faster than the blink of an eye. Won't that be a sensation, man? Notice this carefully, ["at the last trump."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}It is not [trumpet]{.class_s36M}, but ["at the last trump."]{.class_s36N} The trump is the [sound ]{.class_s36M}that a trumpet makes. [Trump one ]{.class_s36M}is the first blast and trump two is the second blast. [But it is made by a trumpet.]{.class_s36M}

Notice something else about that. It doesn't say the [seventh trumpet]{.class_s36M} in the book of Revelation. Notice something else about it. It doesn't say an [angel's trumpet.]{.class_s36M} It just says, ["at the last trump: for the trumpet shall sound."]{.class_s36N}

People who teach one post-tribulation rapture of [all ]{.class_s36M}the saints teach that the Christian will go into the tribulation, Daniel's Seventieth Week. In Revelation 8--9 and 11:15, you read about a series of [seven trumpet]{.class_s36M} judgments. That [seventh trumpet]{.class_s36M} goes off, and they say, "Well, that's the [last ]{.class_s36M}trumpet," although it didn't say last trumpet. It said "trump." The last trumpet, that seventh trumpet, is blown by an [angel]{.class_s36M}. The seventh trumpet of Revelation 11:15 is not ["the last trump"]{.class_s36N} of 1 Corinthians 15:52. In Revelation 11:15, there is the seventh [angel ]{.class_s36M}blowing the seventh [trumpet]{.class_s36M}, ["And the seventh angel sounded; and there were great voices in heaven]{.class_s36N}[, ]{.class_s36P-0}[saying, The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord."]{.class_s36N} The seventh trumpet of Revelation 11:15 ends the Tribulation and begins the Millennium. [It doesn't catch up anybody.]{.class_s36M}

Moreover, the seventh trumpet comes after a rapture. Notice in Revelation 11:12, ["Come up hither."]{.class_s36N} Moses and Elijah are raptured [before]{.class_s36S} the last trumpet. [So the seventh trumpet has nothing to do with any rapture.]{.class_s36S}

There is another way we know this. Turn to 1 Thessalonians and notice that the trumpet which calls you up is not a trumpet blown by an [angel]{.class_s36M}. I'm showing you fellows this because when you get out in the work you are going to run into a heresy called [post-tribulation Rapture]{.class_s36M} which teaches that the Christian will go through the Tribulation. If the Christian goes through the Tribulation, what should you be looking for? [You should be looking for the Tribulation.]{.class_s36M} Right? Well, when did Paul ever tell you to look for the Tribulation? [He told you to look for the Lord, not the Tribulation.]{.class_s36M}

First Thessalonians 4:16, ["For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with THE trump of GOD."]{.class_s36N} There was no [angel ]{.class_s36M}in the passage; it was the [archangel]{.class_s36M}. In the Bible there is only one archangel, and that is Michael. And besides that, the verse says that ["the last trump"]{.class_s36N} of 1 Corinthians 15:52 is ["the trump of God."]{.class_s36N}

So what you have here is this. When the Lord comes back, He will give a shout [which will sound like a trumpet. ]{.class_s36M}Revelation 4:1, ["After this I looked, and, behold, a door was opened in heaven: and the first voice which I heard was as it were OF A TRUMPET talking with me; which said, Come up hither."]{.class_s36N}

The world won't hear it that way. To the unsaved world it is like [thunder]{.class_s36M}. Turn to Job 37. ["The noise of his voice\...the sound that goeth out of his mouth"]{.class_s36N} (Job 37:2) is associated with ["his lightning"]{.class_s36N} (Job 37:3). ["After it a voice roareth: he thundereth with the voice of his excellency\...God thundereth marvellously with his voice"]{.class_s36N} (Job 37:4--5). When that happens, ["he will not stay them when his voice is heard"]{.class_s36N} (Job 37:4): somebody is going to take off!

Again, look at John 12:29. When the Father spoke to Jesus, those that stood by ["said that it thundered: others said, An angel spake to him."]{.class_s36N} So as we hear a trumpet call in the shout, everyone left behind hears a thundercrash so loud that it sounds like an atomic war has started.

When the Lord calls you up, not only does He call you up, but He calls you up[ "by name."]{.class_s36N} John 10:3, ["the sheep hear his]{.class_s36N} (the Good Shepherd's)[ voice: and he calleth his own sheep BY NAME, and LEADETH THEM OUT."]{.class_s36N} Out where? [Out of this world, brother!]{.class_s36M}

At the same time, Michael the archangel accompanies the Lord down to this earth. Why? Because the Rapture of the Church signals the beginning of Daniel's Seventieth Week. Daniel 12:1 says,[ "And at that time shall Michael stand up, the great prince which standeth for the children of thy people."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}That verse indicates that when that [trump ]{.class_s36M}goes off and you are caught up, Michael the archangel shows up, and, boy, when he shows up, God begins to renew His dealing with Israel.

There are other ways that you know that the Rapture of the Church is [pre-Tribulational.]{.class_s36M} The first, of course, was that the seventh trumpet of Revelation 11:15 was not even connected with ["the last trump"]{.class_s36N} of 1 Corinthians 15:52. The second reason that we believe in a "Pre-Trib" rapture for the Church is because in the Tribulation, [there is a faith-and-work system of salvation]{.class_s36M} (Rev. 12:17, 14:12). A Christian has eternal security under "grace-through-faith," but in the Tribulation, [a person can lose his salvation.]{.class_s36M} The foolish virgins of Matthew 25 "run out" of the Holy Spirit. The slothful servant of Matthew 25 is cast into ["outer darkness."]{.class_s36N}

Another way we know that the Rapture is before the Tribulation starts is that, in the Church Age, all believers are in [one body]{.class_s36M}---the Body of Christ. A saved Jew is no longer a Jew; he is a member of the ["church of God."]{.class_s36N} A saved Gentile is no longer a Gentile; he is a member of the ["church of God"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 3:28). But in the Tribulation [there is one body of Jewish believers and another, a separate, body of Gentile believers ]{.class_s36M}(Rev. 7). So no Christian goes through the Tribulation.

Back in 1 Corinthians 15:52, the order of the Rapture is the same as that of 1 Thessalonians 4: it is the dead believers from Acts 2 to the present come up in their new resurrection bodies, and then the living Christians are changed and caught up. Of course, we don't know the interval between the two. But the way 1 Thessalonians 4 is worded, it won't precede it by much.

We call this a rapture although the word "rapture" doesn't occur anywhere in the Bible. It's from a Latin word [rapere ]{.class_s36M}which means to [seize by force]{.class_s36M}, and that is exactly what the Lord does at the Rapture. He enters the strong man's house and binds him and [steals the best thing in the house.]{.class_s36M}

Both Peter and Paul said ["the day of the Lord"]{.class_s36N} was like a ["thief in the night"]{.class_s36N} (1 Thess. 5:2; 2 Pet. 3:10). Christ said to those who came to take Him in the garden,[ "Are ye come out, as against a thief?"]{.class_s36N} (Mark 14:48). He was crucified between two thieves. Paul, his main disciple in the New Testament, said,[ "I robbed other churches"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 11:8).

Of course, we know He is just stealing [what is His own.]{.class_s36M} But it is the devil's too, isn't it? Isn't the devil ["the god of this world"]{.class_s36N}? The Lord comes down to the [devil's house]{.class_s36M} and takes the valuables out by [force]{.class_s36M}. He kidnaps you.

There are two classes of Christians in verses 53--54 based on their order in the Rapture. The first is the ["corruptible."]{.class_s36N} It is the [dead Christians]{.class_s36M} whose bodies are rotting in the ground. They come up with a body [that cannot decay.]{.class_s36M}

The second class is the[ "mortal."]{.class_s36N} That is the Christian who is still alive but [is subject to death]{.class_s36M}. When Christ returns at the Rapture, those Christians who are still alive will be given [a body that cannot die.]{.class_s36M}

There are two Scripture quotations Paul uses in verses 54 and 55. The first is ["Death is swallowed up in victory"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 54). It is a quote from Isaiah 25:8, ["He will swallow up death in victory."]{.class_s36N} But look at the rest of the verse:[ "the Lord God will wipe away tears off all faces."]{.class_s36N} That verse isn't fulfilled until Revelation 21:4. You talk about taking a verse out of context! [That is pure devotional preaching.]{.class_s36M}

But you see, that is often what the Holy Spirit does with Paul. He already told you that the Rapture of the Church was a [mystery]{.class_s36M}, something not revealed in the Old Testament.

But what the Holy Spirit will do is lead Paul to a verse that has a [general application]{.class_s36M} to his topic (in this case, victory over death), and He will lead Paul to apply [that verse]{.class_s36M} to the Church so that he will have Scriptural authority for what he is saying. If the Holy Spirit hadn't revealed that application to Paul, you would never see it.

For example, look at the next verse, 55, ["O death where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory"?]{.class_s36N} That is a quote from Hosea 13:14. Actually, it is what you would call a "free quote"; in other words, it is the Holy Spirit, as Author, who is quoting the verse in the way He wants in order to make His own application. The actual verse says,[ "O death, I will be thy plagues; O grave, I will be thy destruction."]{.class_s36N} It is not even a question, and [it has nothing to do with the Church]{.class_s36M}. The verse says, ["I will ransom them from the power of the grave."]{.class_s36N} Who is the ["them"]{.class_s36N}? It is the children of Ephraim, [the northern kingdom of Israel, in the context of Hosea]{.class_s36M} (Hosea 13:12--13).

You have to be careful if you try doing what Paul did. There are some wonderful [spiritual applications]{.class_s36M} which you can make in the Old Testament, especially in typology, but you can't press some of those things doctrinally. The first purpose of Scripture is ["for doctrine"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 3:16). Paul was operating under the direct leadership and inspiration of the Holy Spirit. So you have to be very careful when you try to apply Scripture [devotionally]{.class_s36M}.

Now, verse 55 is a tremendous verse.[ ]{.class_s36N}That verse says that if Jesus Christ were to come right this minute I would never know the sting of death. I wouldn't know what it would be like to die, because this mortal would ["put on immortality."]{.class_s36N} It means if I were dead and my body was in the grave and rotting in that grave and maggots eating that body, that it puts on incorruption.

I have never buried a Christian that I didn't think about John 11:25--26. I have looked down at many graves where they were burying a Christian, and they begin to put in the dirt. They have a green mat over the grave to make it nice and respectable. Everybody these days are just so "nice," while being just as corrupt as they always have been. They keep the bulldozer out of the way until everybody gets out of the cemetery. Then they take up that green mat and take the bulldozer and push the dirt in. It's still dirt. It [isn't]{.class_s36M} pretty green grass. It's [dirt.]{.class_s36M} I've stood by there many times after the family left and looked down at the grave, and I've thought to myself (and sometimes I have said it out loud, talking to the dirt): "You can't keep him, you can't keep him." And that grave cannot keep the body of a dead saint. You can't keep a good man down.

In John 11:25, you have the promise of Jesus Himself that you don't have to wait until the Great White Throne Judgment to come up.[ "I am the resurrection, and the life."]{.class_s36N}

You have the word of someone who has already come up from the grave never to die again.[ "He that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live."]{.class_s36N}

Do you have a dead loved one who was saved? You will see them again! ["And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die."]{.class_s36N} That is a reference to the Church Age Rapture. It won't fit anywhere else. That is a rapture of the living saints in the Church Age. ["Believest thou this?"]{.class_s36N}

So death is not going to "sting" every Christian (vs. 55). And death will not be victorious over the dead Christian. One way or the other, you are going to cheat death of its prize.

Verse 56, ["The sting of death is sin."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}That's what hurts. What hurts about dying is sin. Dying wouldn't be so bad if it weren't for sin. [Sin ]{.class_s36M}is the thing that makes a man die; it is [sin ]{.class_s36M}that makes death painful. ["And the strength of sin is the law."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}That is, the law is what makes sin [operate ]{.class_s36M}and shows you what sin is. Paul says, in Romans 7:7, ["I had not known sin, but by the law."]{.class_s36N} When you know what is wrong, the condemnation of the wrong is greater than when you don't.

Verse 57, ["But thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}The Christian is able to have victory over sin and death because the Lord Jesus Christ has fought the battle for us and has won. As we yield ourselves more to Him and identify ourselves more with Him in His death and resurrection, we can have victory over sin (Rom. 6). Victory over death is freely given to us because of our position in Christ (Rom. 8).

15:58 Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye stedfast, unmoveable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, forasmuch as ye know that your labour is not in vain in the Lord.

Verse 58 is another of the great verses in 1 Corinthians which should be committed to memory. The ["Therefore"]{.class_s36N} connects the verse with those before it. In other words, because Christ, in His resurrection, has gained the victory over death for [us]{.class_s36M}, then we should use our lives to work more diligently for [Christ]{.class_s36M}.

["Be ye stedfast, unmoveable."]{.class_s36N} Old timers used to call it "stick-to-ittiveness." I don't suppose it is the in the dictionary, but it is a good word. It means to persevere at something, to stick to the job until it is done. Paul says in Galatians 6:9, ["Let us not be weary in well doing."]{.class_s36N}

But not only are you to keep on working for Christ, you are to "abound" in the Lord's work. You will find out that, in the ministry, if you are faithful in doing what God wants you to do, then God will give you more to do. Pretty soon, you'll have so much to do that you don't know where you'll find the time. That is why most of the work in a church is done by only a few. Those are the few that are faithful, and you, as a pastor, know you can depend on them, so you give them another job to do.

["Forasmuch as ye know that your labour is not in vain in the Lord."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}What you do for the Lord and to the Lord, and in the Lord, is never[ "in vain,"]{.class_s36N} no matter what you think about it. And you will think a lot of things about it. I don't know how many times a year I get up and preach and give an invitation and nobody gets saved. You fellows will get out in the ministry and find things very discouraging.

The modern picture of the ministry is Satanic. It is not [real]{.class_s36M}. If you take the [Sword of the Lord,]{.class_s36M} you get the idea the ministry is an attendance record-breaking, \$20,000,000 revival, etc., etc. It is not like that. You get out there in the work struggling along with fifteen people, and then one of them will go all to smithereens and split your congregation. You will get up to twenty-five members, and you'll find one of them getting drunk every other night. You will get up to about thirty-five members, and you will get to know their personal lives, and you will wonder if God called you to be a sheep herder or a hog caller. That's right, brother. You get about fifty members, and they'll be talking about each other and cutting each other down. You will get up to about one hundred members, and three of them will show up on visitation night and twenty of them on prayer meeting night. It is discouraging.

There will be times that you will be tempted to turn hyper-Calvinist. When those times come, don't subscribe to the [Pilgrim Press.]{.class_s36M} When those times come, don't start reading John Calvin. [Get out and start dealing with people.]{.class_s36M} When you don't get results and don't get results and don't get results, for goodness sakes don't start saying, "Well, it's the will of God, it's the will of God." Pretty soon you'll alibi [everything ]{.class_s36M}as the will of God when it[ ain't ]{.class_s36M}the will of God.

Now, it's not the will of God for you to be discouraged. ["Be strong and of a good courage"]{.class_s36N} (Josh. 1:9). It isn't the will of God for you to blame your failures on the Lord. It is the will of God for you to be ["stedfast, unmoveable, always abounding in the work of the Lord."]{.class_s36N} If you can't "abound" in one kind of work then abound in another.

If I was one of these guys in a small church out in the country, I wouldn't stay stuck in just a small church out in the country. If I got in a small church out in the country, I would call all the phone numbers I could call (without calling long distance) and start dealing with people on the telephone. I would put part of the church revenue in postage stamps and mail out tracts within twenty-five miles of the church.

I wouldn't "call it quits" right there. If I was stuck in some country church that had been there twenty years and knew everybody's business, I would get in there and beat the bushes up one side and down the other. I would say, "You folks have got crops, you've got land, you're taking care of your families. There are people overseas who are starving. What have you done for the folks overseas?"

I would put a mission program on them that would break their backs. If you can't get them out winning souls and everybody in the community knows everybody's business and two families have run the church for thirty-five years and they've done nothing, just hit them on [missions]{.class_s36M}. Say, "What have you done for the folks overseas?" "Well, everybody around here professes to be a Christian." All right, get them to working [overseas]{.class_s36M}. Doing "the work of the Lord" includes more than just baptizing a few people into a local church.

["Forasmuch as ye know that your labour is not in vain in the Lord."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}You will think that it is. I've thought that many times. Many times I just thought it was hopeless. I've come nearer to losing my religion in dealing with church people than anything I've ever done. But once a week I get a call from somebody somewhere of whom I never heard. "Hello there. Is this Doctor Ruckman?" Yes. "Well, this is Bob Wilson. I'm up here in Wyoming. I just wanted to tell you what a blessing I'm getting out of your tapes." The next week, "Hello there. Is this preacher Ruckman?" Yes. "Well, I'm up here in North Carolina. I ain't had much book learning, and I ain't read much, but ah learnt how to read by readin' one of your books." That's a blessing.

But problems come with them. I've made a tape for WMEZ, and they'll say it's too scratchy. I've made another tape, and the radio station doesn't get it in time. I've made another tape, and the radio station will go off the air. I've made another tape, and the telephone will ring in the middle of the tape. I've made another tape, [and a jet will break the sound barrier]{.class_s36M} right in the middle of the tape, or I forgot the timing and got it too long. I've made another tape, and the [theme ]{.class_s36M}gets left off. I've made another tape, and the thing will start too late, and it will go off the air [before the tape is finished.]{.class_s36M} There isn't any radio program in this town that has that much trouble. Not one.

One time I bought a \$600.00 machine to make tapes on their kind of equipment, and do you know what they did down there?[ They changed equipment]{.class_s36M}. I get going sometimes, and I say, "Look here, Lord, there's PCC putting out that junk twenty-four hours a day with no bite in it and no fire in it and no unadulterated word of God in it, and they just go right on everyday and never have any problems." You get out in the work, and you will get the same thing. You'll start feeling sorry for yourself and crawl up under a juniper tree. "I'm the only one left, and they seek my life." You can't do that. Always [abound]{.class_s36M}.

"Be not weary in well doing: for in due season we shall reap, if we faint not" (Gal. 6:9).

[]{#c1TU.xhtml}

## Chapter 16 {.heading_s39}

16:1 Now concerning the collection for the saints, as I have given order to the churches of Galatia, even so do ye.

2 Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him, that there be no gatherings when I come.

["Now concerning the collection for the saints"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1). How's that for a comedown? He's been talking about the Rapture and the new body, and then he comes right down to earth. Paul is never [impractical]{.class_s36S}. He can get you right up to the heavenlies and then all of a sudden land you right back on the church pew.

In verse 2, we have a disappointment for our Seventh-day Adventist friends. The [church]{.class_s36M} at Corinth never did meet ["in the synagogue every sabbath"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 18:4). That was Paul when he first started evangelizing ["the Jews and the Greeks"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 18:4). When the Corinthians [did ]{.class_s36M}start getting saved, they met in the house of one of the members (Acts 18:7--8), and they did [not]{.class_s36M} meet on the sabbath day. They met ["upon the first day of the week"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2)---Sunday.

["Let every one of you lay by him in store."]{.class_s36N} The quantity here is ["as God hath prospered him."]{.class_s36N} Come to 2 Corinthians for more on this.

My teaching on "tithes and offerings" is since Abraham tithed [before ]{.class_s36S}the law, which he [did]{.class_s36S}, and Jacob tithed [before ]{.class_s36S}the law, which he [did]{.class_s36S}; under grace, then, [a tithe is the minimum.]{.class_s36S} A Christian ought to be able to do just as much as an Old Testament Jew. The trick is to give as much [above a tithe]{.class_s36S} as you possibly can. Second Corinthians 9:6--7,[ "But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully. Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver."]{.class_s36N}

So the thing is (1) give cheerfully, (2) give as you purpose in your heart, and (3) give as God has prospered you. [That's the statement in the New Testament.]{.class_s36M} And if you do, then here is the promise. Second Corinthians 9:8[ ]{.class_s36N}is the greatest promise in the Bible outside of Romans 8:28. The two greatest promises in the Bible are Romans 8:28 and 2 Corinthians 9:8.[ ]{.class_s36N}You don't realize the breadth of that thing until you look at it.

["And God is able to make all grace abound toward you."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}Wouldn't you like to have all the grace you can get? ["That ye always."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}No letdown. ["Having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}Do you realize what a mouthful that verse is? That says that God will give you [all the grace]{.class_s36M} you need [all the time ]{.class_s36M}and [all the supply ]{.class_s36M}at [any time]{.class_s36M} you need to do [anything ]{.class_s36M}you should do. That is a mouthful. But that is conditioned upon [your generosity]{.class_s36M}, verses 6--9.

It's strange how a man will trust God with his [soul ]{.class_s36M}but not with his [pocketbook]{.class_s36M}. If you can trust God with your soul, you should sure trust him with your [pocketbook]{.class_s36M}. But it's strange how carnal we are. We always think the [money ]{.class_s36M}is more valuable than the [soul]{.class_s36M}. You don't really believe the money is more valuable than the soul, [not really]{.class_s36M}, but when it comes to turning it over to God\...it's weird, isn't it?

["That there be no gatherings when I come."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}There's a collection taken up. When Paul is coming through, he wants to take some money on down to Jerusalem for the poor saints. So he tells them to lay it by them, so that when he comes through, there doesn't have to be a running around to pick it up. It would all be ready for him when he comes through.

16:3 And when I come, whomsoever ye shall approve by your letters, them will I send to bring your liberality unto Jerusalem.

4 And if it be meet that I go also, they shall go with me.

5 Now I will come unto you, when I shall pass through Macedonia: for I do pass through Macedonia.

6 And it may be that I will abide, yea, and winter with you, that ye may bring me on my journey whithersoever I go.

7 For I will not see you now by the way; but I trust to tarry a while with you, if the Lord permit.

8 But I will tarry at Ephesus until Pentecost.

9 For a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries.

In verse 3, he is saying that he will come down to Corinth, and then whoever [they ]{.class_s36M}recommend he will take with him and take the collection on down and give it to the poor saints at Jerusalem. The collections in the New Testament are for two things: [the poor saints,]{.class_s36M} that's found here and in Romans 15 and once again in Acts[ ]{.class_s36N}11:29; and [the support of the ministry,]{.class_s36M} found in 2 Corinthians 9[.]{.class_s36M} So in the New Testament, the collections are taken for support of poor saints and for support of the ministry.

When you speak of the [support of the ministry,]{.class_s36M} there is a lot that comes under that heading, and it depends upon what you are going to do and how you are going to do it. I'm not going to go into a long thing, but I have never been in favor of spending the Lord's money lavishly on what I consider to be nonessentials. As far as I'm concerned the only thing that is wrong with this place right here for a church now is that it is too small. We don't have enough room. During Blowouts we have so many people from out of town come in that we have to put chairs in the aisles and set up a closed circuit television room. Even then there are still people standing in the foyer. So right now, we have [a need]{.class_s36M}.

As far as I'm concerned money that is spent on luxuries, like fancy indirect lighting and fancy organs, fancy, high class things ("Nothing but the best for the Lord's servants," you know) like robes and stained glass windows and high class parking lots, is wasted money. As far as I'm concerned that money should go into [missions]{.class_s36M}. I figure there's a missionary on the foreign field that needs that money more than I need it and more than [you ]{.class_s36M}need it. Because of that, you will never see anything very expensive around here. I consider money for the ministry to be primarily [for the direct spreading of the teaching and preaching of the Authorized Version of the word of God.]{.class_s36M} To me [that ]{.class_s36M}is the first and prime essential.

Verses 4--8 is basically a travel itinerary of Paul and his companions. He planned on going up to Macedonia (vs. 5---probably the churches at Philippi and Thessalonica) and then swinging by to get the Corinthian's offering and representatives. Winter was a bad time to travel in the Mediterranean (Acts 27:9--12; 2 Tim. 4:21), so he was planning to stay with them during that time (vs. 6). Verse 8 gives us an idea of when this took place in the book of Acts; it was between Acts 18:19 and Acts 20:16.

["For a great door and effectual is opened unto me, and there are many adversaries"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). That is probably a reference to Acts 19:9[ ]{.class_s36N}when he had a chance to dispute in the school of Tyrannus and all the people in Asia heard the word of God. A ["door,"]{.class_s36N} in the Bible, [spiritually]{.class_s36M}, stands for an opportunity to preach, an opportunity for service. In the Book of Revelation the Lord told the church at Philadelphia, ["I have set before thee an open door, and no man can shut it"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 3:8). When the Lord Jesus Christ gave Simon Peter the "keys," those keys were to a [door]{.class_s36M}; it was a [preaching opportunity.]{.class_s36M} When you fellows start in the ministry and want a place to preach, ask the Lord to open a door for you.

Bob Jones had some things to say about that. I quote him a good bit because I have a great deal of respect for him. I have a lot of respect for him because I observed that when all was said and done, he could [produce ]{.class_s36M}what he [preached]{.class_s36M}. That man had a lot of wisdom. He said, "You ask the Lord to open you a door, and when the Lord opens you a door, you go through it. It may not be the door opened that you wanted to open, or it may not open exactly the way you wanted it to open, but when you pray for God to open a door, you are to go through when He opens it. I don't say they won't try to keep you from going through it, and never said somebody won't spit on you while you are going through it, but it is [open]{.class_s36M}. It is an [open door]{.class_s36M}, and[ 'no man can shut it.']{.class_s36N}"

["And there are many adversaries." ]{.class_s36N}The Lord opened the door for some of you to come down here and study.[ He was the One who opened it.]{.class_s36M} You might think [you ]{.class_s36M}opened it, but if you knew how many things could go wrong, you wouldn't think that. [He opened it.]{.class_s36M} But in going through that door somebody is going to kick you going through. Somebody will spit on you; somebody will slap you going through. You won't just [slide ]{.class_s36M}through, but it is opened.

In these verses, Paul discusses two people: Timothy and Apollos. Timothy was Paul's son in the faith (2 Tim. 1:2) and his closest helper in the ministry (Phil. 2:19--20). Since Timothy was a relatively young man, Paul told the Corinthians ["Let no man therefore despise him"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). He wrote to Timothy and said, ["Let no man despise thy youth"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 4:12).

The other man Paul mentions is [Apollos ]{.class_s36S}(vs. 12). He first shows up in Acts 18:24, and like Paul, once he was saved and taught right, he went out among the Jews publicly preaching Christ (Acts 18:28). For a time he followed Paul's ministry to Corinth (Acts 19:1 cf. 1 Cor. 3:6). He had made such an impression on some of them that they sided with him. Notice here, though, that there was no hierarchal "command structure" among the apostles, elders, and evangelists. Paul didn't tell Apollos where to go and what to do as the Catholic Church does to their "ministers."

16:13 Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong.

14 Let all your things be done with charity.

Verses 13 and 14 contain [five commands]{.class_s36M} for the Christian. The first four are in verse 13. First of all, every Christian should ["watch."]{.class_s36N} The Christian should watch for [the return of His Lord]{.class_s36M} (Matt. 24:42; Titus 2:13). He should watch the [actions of a minister]{.class_s36M} to see whether he is God's minister or a "wolf in sheep's clothing" (Acts 20:29--31).

A Christian should ["watch and pray, that ye enter not into temptation"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 26:41). Peter never forgets that and says in 1 Peter 4:7, ["But the end of all things is at hand: be ye therefore sober, and watch unto prayer."]{.class_s36N} So the nearer we get to the Second Coming, the more we should ["watch and pray."]{.class_s36N} Paul says much the same thing in Colossians 4:2, ["Continue in prayer, and watch in the same WITH THANKSGIVING."]{.class_s36N}

Paul also connects [being sober]{.class_s36M} with watching: ["Therefore let us not sleep, as do others; but let us watch and be sober"]{.class_s36N} (1 Thess. 5:6). So the Christian is to keep his eye out against the Devil working in his life to destroy him (1 Pet. 5:8). A pastor is to watch for [the souls of his congregation]{.class_s36M} (Heb. 13:17). And if that were not enough of a job to fulfill, a pastor is to ["watch\...in all things: ]{.class_s36N}(2 Tim. 4:5).

I have a sermon called "The Watch Words of the Bible." In that sermon, I give [five things]{.class_s36M} a Christian should watch and keep an eye on: A Christian should watch his [Words]{.class_s36M}, his [Actions, ]{.class_s36M}his [Thoughts]{.class_s36M}, his [Companions]{.class_s36M}, and his [Heart]{.class_s36M}. And brother, if you can keep all five of those under control, you will have done [something]{.class_s36M} in your Christian life, I kid you not.

Next, the Christian is to ["stand fast in the faith."]{.class_s36N} Paul says in Ephesians 4:14, ["That we henceforth be no more children, tossed to and fro, and carried about with every wind of doctrine."]{.class_s36N} Every Christian should know [what ]{.class_s36M}he is to believe from the Bible, and he should be able to defend that belief from [Scripture]{.class_s36M}. The problem with most Christians is that they never grow up to where thy should be in their Christian lives.

Concerning this matter of [standing]{.class_s36M}, a Christian should ["stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free, and be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage" ]{.class_s36N}(Gal. 5:1). Every Christian is commanded to ["put on the whole armour of God, that ye may be able TO STAND against the wiles of the devil"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 6:11). Part of that is to ["stand therefore, having your loins girt about with truth, and having on the breastplate of righteousness"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 6:14). A church is to ["stand fast in one spirit, with one mind striving together for the faith of the gospel"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 1:27).

In 1 Thessalonians 3:8, Paul says, ["For now we live, if ye stand fast in the Lord."]{.class_s36N} Now, the "standing fast" here is not a [positional ]{.class_s36M}thing. It is a matter of daily fellowship. And when Paul says,[ "Now we live,"]{.class_s36N} he isn't talking about [physical ]{.class_s36M}or [spiritual life.]{.class_s36M} What Paul is saying in 1 Thessalonians 3:8 is that when the Thessalonians live in fellowship with the Lord, [his life]{.class_s36M} is full of joy (vs. 9). He says the same thing in Philippians 4:1, ["My brethren dearly beloved and longed for, my joy and crown, so stand fast in the Lord."]{.class_s36N}

A New Testament Christian is also to ["stand fast"]{.class_s36N} in the things Paul taught in his epistles (2 Thess. 2:15). And a Christian is to stand in ["the true grace of God"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 5:12) and not ["frustrate"]{.class_s36N} it by hitching it back up to the law (Gal. 2:21).

The [third ]{.class_s36M}commandment in verse 13, of 1 Corinthians 16, is ["quit you like men."]{.class_s36N} The word ["quit"]{.class_s36N} here doesn't mean to stop doing something. It is an archaic usage that means "to do your part, to behave yourself, to bear yourself." So when the Bible says ["quit you like men,"]{.class_s36N} it is saying that you are to do your part like a man, to behave yourself like a man, to bear yourself in the manner of a man; in other words---ACT LIKE [A MAN!]{.class_s36M}

Now, was that meaning truly that hard to get? It is places like this that the apostate grabs and uses to change your Bible in 500 to 36,000 places. I dare say that you could have [guessed]{.class_s36M} pretty close to the correct meaning if you had tried. If not, five minutes (if that much) in a good dictionary would have given you the meaning, but that would have required something the average Christian is unwilling to do---a little WORK! But if that were not sufficient, the Bible student could have gone to 1 Samuel 4:9 [and compared Scripture with Scripture]{.class_s36M} and could have figured out what it meant. The trouble is that the average Christian expects to be spoon-fed like a toddler when it comes to matters of Bible study.

First Samuel 4:9 is a great verse in explaining Paul's command in 1 Corinthians 16:13. ["Be strong, and quit yourselves like men, O ye Philistines, that ye be not servants unto the Hebrews, as they have been to you:" quit yourselves like men, and fight."]{.class_s36N}

Its matchmeet is in 2 Samuel 10:12, where Joab says, ["Be of good courage, and let us play the men for our people, and for the cities of our God: and the LORD do that which seemeth him good."]{.class_s36N} The first reference is by a bunch of [unsaved heathen]{.class_s36M}, and that last reference is by a [self-serving Jew]{.class_s36M}, but they both mean the same thing. "The odds are stacked against us, and we're probably going to die any way. But let's go down [fighting ]{.class_s36M}like a bunch of men and see what happens." I wish God would take that sentiment and stuff it into the backbone of some Christians.

Back in World War II, when the Russians counterattacked after Stalingrad, there was a case of a young German lieutenant at Minsk, Russia. The Russians had killed all six of his guncrew. He collected his dead comrades' weapons and fired all of [their ]{.class_s36M}ammunition at the advancing Russians. Then he fired all of [his ]{.class_s36M}ammunition until it was all gone. When the Russians found him, he was sitting in the snow with corpses all around him, and he was just about frozen. One of his arms was in a sling, and there was blood all over him. The Russians put their bayonets on him and told him to put his hands up. That kid (he was about twenty years old) stood up and walked over to them and [spit on them]{.class_s36M}. Of course, he died, but what a gesture!

Now, my question is this: if an [unsaved German]{.class_s36M} can do that for a [demon-possessed dictator]{.class_s36M} (Hitler), why can't Christians ["quit" ]{.class_s36N}themselves like men for the Lord Jesus Christ?

The fourth of Paul's commands in 1 Corinthians 16:13 is ["be strong."]{.class_s36N} When I sign a Bible for a man or boy, I usually give Joshua 1:9, ["Be strong and of a good courage."]{.class_s36N} The Lord tells Joshua that[ four times]{.class_s36M} in that first chapter (vss. 6, 7, 9, 18). Paul tells the Ephesians, ["Be strong in the Lord, and in the power of his might"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 6:10). Nehemiah told the Jews, ["The joy of the LORD is your strength"]{.class_s36N} (Neh. 8:10). So if a Christian is to ["be strong,"]{.class_s36N} he needs the Lord working His power through him, he needs the joy that only the Holy Spirit can produce in his life (Gal. 5:22), and he needs the courage to use what strength the Lord gives him.

The final command given by Paul, here, is in verse 14.[ "Let all your things be done with charity."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}That's the balance. Be strong, but don't be [ungracious]{.class_s36M}. Be strong, but don't be [unthankful]{.class_s36M}. Be strong and be a man, but don't be [hateful ]{.class_s36M}or [vicious]{.class_s36M}. Be strong and be a man, but let[ "things be done with charity."]{.class_s36N} Paul said one time in Ephesians 4:15, ["Speaking the truth in love."]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36M}Fundamentalists tend to speak the truth, but not[ in love.]{.class_s36M} Liberals speak in love, but not in [truth.]{.class_s36M} Now, it is better to speak the truth [without love]{.class_s36M} than to speak in love [without the truth]{.class_s36M}. But the ideal is [both]{.class_s36M}: speak in truth and love.

16:15 I beseech you, brethren, (ye know the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and that they have addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints,)

16 That ye submit yourselves unto such, and to every one that helpeth with us, and laboureth.

17 I am glad of the coming of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus: for that which was lacking on your part they have supplied.

18 For they have refreshed my spirit and yours: therefore acknowledge ye them that are such.

In this passage, Paul lists, by name, some of the Christians at Corinth who have been a blessing to him and his ministry. He lists Stephanas first (vs. 17) because his household was the first to be saved. That is why he calls them ["the firstfruits of Achaia."]{.class_s36N} You will remember back in chapter 1 that his was the only household which Paul could recall [baptizing ]{.class_s36M}in Corinth (1 Cor. 1:16).

Paul points out that particular family as an example for the other Corinthians to follow (vs. 16). The reason was that they ["addicted themselves to the ministry of the saints"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15). They weren't addicted to dope, alcohol, cigarettes, sex, or food; they were addicted to[ helping out other Christians]{.class_s36M}. An addict is someone hopelessly given up to something. That bunch was hopelessly given up to [helping the saints out.]{.class_s36M} Boy, I would like to see some churches like that! There's a passage about which I have often wondered:[ "Let each esteem other better than themselves"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 2:3). Can you imagine a church were everybody is esteeming the other person better than [himself]{.class_s36M}?

That is the kind of person to whom others in the church should look (vss. 16, 18). Those that help a missionary out are just as important as the missionary.

16:19 The churches of Asia salute you. Aquila and Priscilla salute you much in the Lord, with the church that is in their house.

20 All the brethren greet you. Greet ye one another with an holy kiss.

21 The salutation of me Paul with mine own hand.

22 If any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema Maranatha.

23 The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.

24 My love be with you all in Christ Jesus. Amen.

Paul ends his first letter to the Corinthians in these last six verses. The postscript says that ["the first epistle to the Corinthians was written from Philippi."]{.class_s36N} If that was so, then it was after Paul had just returned from Ephesus (Acts 19 cf. 20:1). So Paul sends greetings from the churches he had founded [while at Ephesus]{.class_s36M} (Acts 19:9--10). ["The churches of Asia"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 19) were no more of a denomination than the ["churches of Christ"]{.class_s36N} were (Rom. 16:16).

["Aquila and Priscilla"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 19) were Paul's ["helpers in Christ Jesus"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 16:3). He first met them at Corinth after they had been exiled from Rome (Acts 18:2). They would either stay behind after Paul and help establish a church (Acts 18:26), or they would go ahead of him and begin evangelistic efforts (Rom. 16:3--5).

Note in verse 19 that the churches in the book of Acts met in [houses]{.class_s36M}. Church buildings didn't show up until A.D. 213. So when Paul wrote to the early Christians, they did not confuse the church (the called out assembly of born-again believers) with [a building.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 20, ["All the brethren greet you, Greet ye one another with an holy kiss."]{.class_s36N} You don't find many assemblies that practice [that]{.class_s36S}. It's oriental: greeting a man by kissing him on the cheek and women kissing each other on the cheek. The Orientals are much more demonstrative about that than we are; not the Chinese and Japanese, but Asia Minor: Turkish people and [Greek people.]{.class_s36S} That holy kiss is called "the kiss of peace" in the [New English Bible]{.class_s36S} in preparation for the Antichrist. The "kiss of peace" is what Absalom, the "father of peace," gave to men in 2 Samuel 15:5 in order [to betray his father David.]{.class_s36S}

In verse 21, Paul points out to the Corinthians that he is greeting them [in his own handwriting]{.class_s36S}. Normally, he used a scribe who wrote the words he dictated (Rom. 16:22 cf. Jer. 36). But occassionally, because of the importance of the message, he did write some things personally (Gal. 6:11).

In verse 22, the apostle uses two words that appear nowhere else in Scripture in this form: ["Anathema Maranatha." ]{.class_s36N}The word ["Anathema"]{.class_s36N} is a Greek word that has been [transliterated]{.class_s36M} into [Latin ]{.class_s36M}and then into [English]{.class_s36M}. It is [translated]{.class_s36M} elsewhere in Scripture as ["accursed"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 9:3; 1 Cor. 12:3; Gal. 1:8--9). It is the favorite word at the Council of Trent (1545--1563) for all Bible-believing Protestants. Modern apologists for the Catholic Church, like Karl Keating of "Catholic Answers," like to play little word games to cover up the Catholic Church's foul mouth (Rom. 3:14). They say it simply means "kicked out of the church." (Karl Keating's actual words were "no longer part of our club.") But that is [not]{.class_s36M} what it means in [Scripture]{.class_s36M}.

Paul says, ["I could wish that myself were accursed FROM CHRIST for my brethren"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 9:3). [In other words, he was willing to go to Hell for them.]{.class_s36M} The cross reference to 1 Corinthians 16:22 is 2 Thessalonians 2:10--12, where those who ["received not the love of the truth\...might be DAMNED."]{.class_s36N} So ["Anathema"]{.class_s36N} is not a light term to be tossed around, like the Council of Trent does. Paul uses it of someone who preaches another gospel (Gal. 1:8--9) and (here) of someone who does not have Christ at His return.

The second word ["Maranatha"]{.class_s36N} is an [Aramaic ]{.class_s36M}word meaning "our Lord returns." So Paul says if anyone does not love the Lord when He comes back that he will be [accursed]{.class_s36M}.
