At the Bible Baptist Bookstore, I have a tract booklet called [Millions Disappear---Fact or Fiction?]{.class_s36M} In that booklet, I tell a person how to be saved in the Tribulation. But let's just face it, if someone has rejected a clear presentation of the Gospel in this Age (which is in the first part of that booklet) when it is [free,]{.class_s36M} there is slim chance to none that he will [work]{.class_s36M} like he [has]{.class_s36M} to do in the Tribulation to be saved. In that passage over in 2 Thessalonians 2:10--12, God sends those who have rejected the Gospel during the Church Age ["strong delusion, that they should believe a lie"]{.class_s36N} for the sole purpose of [damning them.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 23 is Paul's standard closing for all of his epistles, including Hebrews. In one form or another, Paul always prays for God's grace, ["the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ," ]{.class_s36N}to be bestowed upon those to whom he writes. The big thing with Peter and John is [peace ]{.class_s36M}(1 Pet. 5:14; 3 John 14), but with Paul it is [grace]{.class_s36M}. He started off this epistle in just that way (1 Cor. 1:3). He gives an abbreviated definition of grace in 1 Corinthians 1:5, ["That in every thing ye are enriched by him."]{.class_s36N} But for a more thorough definition of that grace, turn to 2 Corinthians 8:9,[ "For ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich."]{.class_s36N} That is why preachers like to use this acrostic to define grace: "God's Riches At Christ's Expense."

And finally, Paul closes with his love in verse 24. In fact, chapter 16 has been a fairly [positive ]{.class_s36M}chapter, except for a minor warning about giving, in verse 2. But my goodness, you don't get chapter 16 until you have gone through nearly [fifteen chapters of ripping, tearing, and blasting.]{.class_s36M} So when Paul says, ["My love be with you all,"]{.class_s36N} he is telling them that even though he had to get on them so harshly that he still loves them.

This modern generation doesn't understand that. They think that if you yell at them and preach against their dirty, rotten sins that you don't [love ]{.class_s36M}them. It's "kissey, feeley, touchey" with this generation. But Solomon, way back in the Old Testament, said, ["He that spareth his rod hateth his son: but he that loveth him chasteneth him betimes"]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 13:24). So a good pastor will take [the rod ]{.class_s36M}(Psa. 23:4) to the sheep when they need it.

"The first epistle to the Corinthians was written from Philippi by Stephanas, and Fortunatus, and Achaicus, and Timotheus." Paul didn't write all of the epistle. Paul dictated it. ["Holy men of God SPAKE as they were moved by the Holy Ghost"]{.class_s36N} (2 Pet. 1:21). So he [speaks ]{.class_s36M}and those guys he named [wrote ]{.class_s36M}down the letter.

[]{#c1W7.xhtml}

# Second Corinthians {.heading_s1WD}

## Introduction {#c1W7.xhtml_a34X .heading_s2U}

The book of 2 Corinthians was written not long after the Corinthians had received and responded to Paul's [first ]{.class_s36M}epistle. So the book was written anywhere from a few months to a year after Paul completed 1 Corinthians. The date for the writing is somewhere between A.D. 58 and A.D. 60.

The [theme ]{.class_s36M}of the book is the Christian ministry. Dr. William T. Brunner at Bob Jones University (1950) said, "This is the minister's book; it is THE book for the Christian ministry." Like many others, I had already taken for granted (back in 1949--1950) that the book of Acts was the book describing the ministry, but Dr. Brunner was right.

Acts only dealt with the ministry of [the apostles]{.class_s36M} while God was still dealing with [Israel ]{.class_s36M}as a nation. Second Corinthians goes way beyond that (see chaps. 5, 6, 8, 9, 11, and 12, for example). It deals with the personal aspects of the Christian ministry [worldwide ]{.class_s36M}as it related to the "brethren," the churches, the enemies of the gospel (2 Cor. 11), and the treatment of a true witness for Jesus Christ and his Christian responses to that treatment (2 Cor. 2:14, 12:9--10, 4:17).

Second Corinthians is Paul describing [his own ministry,]{.class_s36M} and it is a testimony as to what God called him to do and what God did through him. What you will find out really quick is that Paul's picture of the ministry is nothing like the modern picture of the ministry as presented in periodicals like [Sword of the Lord.]{.class_s36M} Paul's description of the ministry reads like an omnibus of [negativity]{.class_s36M}: ["anguish, perplexity, devour, smite, bondage, weak, forsaken, faint, persecuted, sorry, grieved, down, perish, burden, cast down, groaning, poor, chastened, hunger, beaten, perils, pain, infirmity, stoned, reproaches, offended, weariness, mourning, trembling, fear, poverty, affliction, despair, necessities, imprisonments, distressed, destruction, tumults, killed, tribulation, dying, suffering, terrified."]{.class_s36N} [Those ]{.class_s36M}are the words Paul chose to describe the [Christian ministry ]{.class_s36M}here in 2 Corinthians.

Second Corinthians has 13 chapters, 256 verses, and 6,092 words. If you want to find out what to expect in a life of ministry to and for Christ, this is the book to study.

[]{#c1WP.xhtml}

## Chapter 1 {.heading_s39}

1:1 Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timothy our brother, unto the church of God which is at Corinth, with all the saints which are in all Achaia:

Paul begins this epistle with ["an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God." ]{.class_s36N}You will remember that there was a faction in Corinth questioning his authority as [an apostle]{.class_s36M} (see 1 Cor. 9:2 and comments). In this second epistle he defends his work as [an apostle ]{.class_s36M}among the Corinthians (chap. 10). So as he begins the epistle, he lets the Corinthians know that he is not like those that John wrote about in Revelation 2:2, those ["which say they are apostles, and are not,"]{.class_s36N} but that he is an apostle ["by the will of God."]{.class_s36N}

The epistle is written ["unto the church of God which is at Corinth."]{.class_s36N} The Baptist Briders have a funny idea about the church at this point. They say that the church at Corinth was not a [part]{.class_s36M} of the Body of Christ, but actually [the]{.class_s36M} Body of Christ (cf. 1 Cor. 12:27). So what you end up with are actually hundreds and thousands of "Bodies" of Christ running around. If each local church is the ["one body"]{.class_s36N} of 1 Corinthians 12 and Ephesians 4, then there are hundreds of Holy Spirits, Lords, Faiths, Baptisms, Gods, and Fathers, (Eph. 4:4--6). Who is fool enough to believe that? But that is exactly where you end up if you take the Baptist Brider doctrine seriously.

["The church of God"]{.class_s36N} is [one ]{.class_s36M}body (Eph. 1:22--23). Paul persecuted [that church]{.class_s36M} before he was saved (1 Cor. 15:9) and before any Corinthians were saved. The Ephesian Christians were a part of [that ]{.class_s36M}church (Acts 20:17, 28). So the expression ["which is at Corinth"]{.class_s36N} makes it very clear that he is speaking to that [part]{.class_s36M} of ["the church of God."]{.class_s36N} The "universal church," then, is just as much a Bible doctrine as the "local church."

["With all the saints which are in all Achaia."]{.class_s36N} Corinth was the main city of the region of Achaia. Paul's practice was to start a church in an influential city of a region, and from there he would reach the rest of that region. This is what he did in Ephesus and Asia Minor (Acts 19:8--10). So the epistle to Corinth was also the epistle to [all ]{.class_s36M}the Christians in the region of Achaia.

1:2 Grace be to you and peace from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ.

3 Blessed be God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort;

4 Who comforteth us in all our tribulation, that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble, by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God.

5 For as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ.

6 And whether we be afflicted, it is for your consolation and salvation, which is effectual in the enduring of the same sufferings which we also suffer: or whether we be comforted, it is for your consolation and salvation.

7 And our hope of you is stedfast, knowing, that as ye are partakers of the sufferings, so shall ye be also of the consolation.

Verse 2 is Paul's standard salutation to all the Gentile churches and pastors to whom he wrote (Romans--Philemon). See the comments on 1 Corinthians 16:23 on grace.

Regarding [peace]{.class_s36M}, this has nothing to do with nations ceasing to fight each other. The poor, stupid Pope gets up every Christmas and prays for "peace on earth," and the Lord never answers his prayer. Instead, two new wars break out somewhere [every year]{.class_s36M}. Doctrinally speaking, [the peace]{.class_s36M} of Luke 2:14 is when Jesus Christ [sits down on the throne of David as the Prince of Peace]{.class_s36M} (Isa. 9:6--7) [and rules with a rod of iron]{.class_s36M}. The peace of 2 Corinthians 1:2 is a [personal peace]{.class_s36M} given to a Christian, positionally, when he receives Jesus Christ by faith (Rom. 5:1). Peace in the daily life of a Christian is part of the fruit which the Holy Spirit produces in the life of a Christian who follows His leadership (Gal. 5:18, 22).

Note in verse 3 that the Lord is ["the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort."]{.class_s36N} That is a keynote to the entire epistle. With all the troubles in the ministry, you will need God's mercy and God's comfort. If you look at verse 4 you see that God comforts you when you are in trouble so that you can comfort other folks when [they ]{.class_s36M}get in trouble. You are able to use the comfort God gave to [you ]{.class_s36M}to comfort [them]{.class_s36M}.

Verse 4 says, ["in ALL our tribulation"]{.class_s36N} and ["them which are in ANY trouble."]{.class_s36N} So if you are any kind of minister at all, God will send you trouble. He sends that trouble your way so that you can minister to [others ]{.class_s36M}in their trouble. If [you ]{.class_s36M}have no trouble, then you can't minister.

[There is nothing deader in this world than a man who has had no trouble.]{.class_s36M} When I want to talk to somebody interesting I go to somebody that has been through something. Sam Levenson's mother was once asked to define love. She said, "Love is something you have been through with somebody." She was referring to raising her family in the ghettos of New York for twenty-five years.

You may not have noticed this, but the difference between a preacher whose message reaches you and affects you and a preacher whose message just goes over your head is that the former has been through some trouble in his life and the latter hasn't been through enough trouble to lend any weight to his [message]{.class_s36M}. For a message to bear any weight, [the messenger]{.class_s36M} has to go "through the mill." I don't envy anyone who has a message like that. I'll pray for him and his trouble and hope that he will pray for me.

When the Lord makes a diamond, He takes a lump of ugly coal and puts it through intense [heat]{.class_s36M} and [pressure.]{.class_s36M} Then some jeweler comes along and [cuts]{.class_s36M} that diamond to perfection. Then it is polished to brilliant splendor. The Lord is going to make some of you reading this page wonderful gems for His crown, but before He does that you are going to go through a fiery trial and the pressures of life and have all the rough edges cut off, brother. Then the Lord can polish you up and make something useful of you.

When I first began to preach, I used to think it was a contest between me and the congregation. I had been a DI in hand to hand combat for years, before I was saved, and naturally, I was rough and crude. In hand-to-hand you are always challenging the fellows you teach; [comforting ]{.class_s36M}is out of the question. My job was to make them miserable, to antagonize them until they were ready to kill. So when I got saved and called to preach, I mounted the platform, and there were my "troops" in front of me. I must have been preaching for two years before it suddenly dawned on me that I was talking to people who had diabetes, cancer, and leukemia. They had to bury dead babies; they went bankrupt; their houses burnt to the ground. Their homes were busting up, they were losing their jobs, and God knows what else. So I toned it down and got another "perspective." But you know, you will only be able to minister to the proportion that God has ministered to you, and God will only minister to you in proportion to your need. Your need will be in proportion to the trouble through which you go. That trouble, then, becomes a part of [you]{.class_s36M}.

Reputation won't do you a bit of good. Your ministry will depend on what you have [actually]{.class_s36M} been through, not what folks [think]{.class_s36M} you have been through. It will be dependent of the pain you have been able to take in your life, not what folks [think ]{.class_s36M}you have taken. Reputation has very little to do with your ability to minister the word of God.

There are men in this country who have reputations for being great preachers, powerful preachers, but you will be amazed at how disappointed you will be when you hear them. It will be a real letdown. Then there will be some obscure fellow whom hardly anyone knows. When he starts to preaching, you will think, "Wow! Where did that come from? Why haven't I heard about this guy before?" The difference is that the first group of preachers are living on their [reputations]{.class_s36M}, and that last fellow is going on [his character]{.class_s36M}.

Like I said, I don't envy anyone who is like that, because for him to be able to minister like he does, he has had to go through sorrow and trouble in his life. That's why Paul was such a great minister; he had been into nearly every kind of trouble a fellow can get into.

You make a good [soldier ]{.class_s36M}by persecuting him. That's how you also make a good [Christian]{.class_s36M}: you persecute him; [you turn the Devil loose on him.]{.class_s36M}

Before leaving this passage, note how many times Paul has mentioned Jesus Christ: verse 1, ["Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 2, ["the Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}; verse 3, ["our Lord Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N} The outstanding mark of a man who is filled with the Holy Spirit is that he magnifies Jesus Christ, [not]{.class_s36M} the Holy Spirit (John 16:13--14).

Verse 5, ["The sufferings of Christ abound in us."]{.class_s36N} These are not only the things Christ suffered while here on earth, they are also the things [Paul suffered,]{.class_s36M} for Christ's sake, in the ministry. Christ was cursed; Paul was cursed. Christ was shipped; Paul was whipped. Christ was thrown in jail; Paul was thrown in jail. One time Paul said, ["I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 6:17). Paul wanted to know ["the fellowship of his sufferings, being made conformable unto his death"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 3:10).

["So our consolation also aboundeth by Christ."]{.class_s36N} As a man goes through suffering for Christ, the Lord pays him back with the exact comfort and consolation he needs to come out of the trouble victoriously. The key, though, is to [suffer ]{.class_s36M}abundantly for Christ if you want abundant [comfort]{.class_s36M}. Don't expect God's comfort for suffering for [your flesh]{.class_s36M} or the [world ]{.class_s36M}(1 Pet. 4:14--19).

In verse 6, notice how the word ["salvation"]{.class_s36N} is used. The people to whom Paul is writing are already ["saints"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1), so it is not talking about salvation in an eternal sense. Paul sometimes uses the word in relation to one's life, ministry, and work. In writing to Timothy about the ministry, he says, ["Take heed unto thyself, and unto the doctrine; continue in them: for in doing this thou shalt both SAVE thyself, and them that hear thee"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 4:16). In the same epistle (1 Timothy), Paul tells the women in the church,[ "notwithstanding she shall be SAVED in childbearing"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 2:15). There he is referring back to verses 12--14 and saying that, during that time in a woman's life, [she will be saved from being deceived.]{.class_s36M}

In this chapter, deliverance (vss. 9--10) is in three tenses, so salvation (as sanctification) is in [three tenses.]{.class_s36M}

A man's [soul]{.class_s36M} was saved ([past]{.class_s36M}) when he accepted the finished work ([past]{.class_s36M}) of Calvary. This is past sanctification (Heb. 10:10, 14). He was saved from the PENALTY of sin. A Christian's [spirit]{.class_s36M} is being saved daily from the POWER of sin as he grows in the Lord, yields himself to Christ, and reckons his "old man" dead (a [present ]{.class_s36M}sanctification). At the Rapture, his [body]{.class_s36M} will be saved from the PRESENCE of sin, and then his salvation will be complete ([future ]{.class_s36M}sanctification---1 Thess. 5:23).

In 2 Corinthians 1:6, he is referring to the Corinthians' Christian lives. He is saying that if he and Timothy are ["afflicted"]{.class_s36N} it is so the Corinthians can save their own Christian lives for Christ's service by ["enduring\...the same sufferings which we also suffer."]{.class_s36N} So when Paul said, ["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 11:1), it was [not]{.class_s36M} just in reference to his doctrine. Paul was an example to the Corinthians of how to suffer for Christ and be comforted [in the midst of that suffering.]{.class_s36M}

Now, I don't like those verses any more than you do. I don't like verses like Hebrew 12:11, ["Now no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grievous: nevertheless afterward it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness."]{.class_s36N} I don't care too much for verses that say, ["For our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 4:17), or ["For I reckon that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:18).

Flesh is flesh. Nobody likes [to hurt]{.class_s36M}. The old saying is, "Nobody is happy on the rack." But the fact of the matter is, if we are going to be saved from a barren, fruitless Christian life then we are going to have to suffer and come through it victoriously. And that is the example Paul set for us in verse 6.

In light of that, Paul's [hope ]{.class_s36M}in verse 7 is this (and remember that in the New Testament, hope is [never]{.class_s36M} an unsure thing): that when the Corinthians suffered for Christ, they would also be [consoled ]{.class_s36M}by Christ. No consolation without suffering, but at the same time, no suffering without consolation. I think sometimes that the only way a Christian can really get to know the Lord is just to hurt like the devil. I think people like Richard Wurmbrand and Harlan Popov got to know the Lord really well when they were undergoing persecution at the hands of the Communists.

There used to be a lady in a rest home here in Pensacola named Mrs. Salter. She had to lay flat on her back in a bed for over twenty-five years because of infirmities. I never met that woman but that she had the victory. I would say, "Mrs. Salter, let me hear you say 'Praise the Lord'." And at the top of her voice she would say, "Well, Praise the Lord!" I'll tell you what, she said it a lot [louder ]{.class_s36M}than some Christians I know that have good health and can get around just fine.

Now, I don't like [that]{.class_s36M}, and my flesh doesn't appreciate it one bit. [But it sure seems like it is so.]{.class_s36M}

1:8 For we would not, brethren, have you ignorant of our trouble which came to us in Asia, that we were pressed out of measure, above strength, insomuch that we despaired even of life:

9 But we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves, but in God which raiseth the dead:

10 Who delivered us from so great a death, and doth deliver: in whom we trust that he will yet deliver us;

Whatever Paul went through in Asia Minor in these verses, is not recorded in the book of Acts. It could be a reference to one or more of those events he mentions in 2 Corinthians 11:23--27. But whatever it was, it was a situation where Paul thought he was going [to die, ]{.class_s36M}and he thought it could be any minute.

Sometimes when you get terribly sick and think you are going to die, and you get all tore up and think it's all over, the Lord is just doing that so you will trust in Him more. Sometimes the Lord will give you a sickness, a sorrow, or a threat to such a degree that you know that the [only]{.class_s36M} way out of it is for God to do something.

An old Quaker said, "Go out in the backyard and dig yourself a hole about four feet deep. Then lie down in that hole flat on your back and look straight up in the sky for about five minutes. Just think for about five minutes that that is how you will be when you are dead. From there, get out and start to live life again. Most men would hit the ground running and start ['redeeming the time']{.class_s36N} instead of loafing."

Verse 10 has already received comment under verse 6.

1:11 Ye also helping together by prayer for us, that for the gift bestowed upon us by the means of many persons thanks may be given by many on our behalf.

12 For our rejoicing is this, the testimony of our conscience, that in simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God, we have had our conversation in the world, and more abundantly to you-ward.

13 For we write none other things unto you, than what ye read or acknowledge; and I trust ye shall acknowledge even to the end;

14 As also ye have acknowledged us in part, that we are your rejoicing, even as ye also are ours in the day of the Lord Jesus.

In Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, he spent nearly the whole letter [rebuking ]{.class_s36M}and [correcting ]{.class_s36M}a carnal church. Paul's approach in this letter is a little different. He commends them for something they had done right (vs. 11). The Corinthians had heard about his trouble in Asia, and many of them had gotten together in prayer for him. Then they took a special offering and sent it to him. The result was that a lot of people thanked God for what was done. When you give in response to a need the Lord has shown you, then you cause [others ]{.class_s36M}to give thanks to God. Some thank God that the need was met; others thank God that He used you to help someone out. The important thing is that God gets [the thanks]{.class_s36M} and God gets [the glory.]{.class_s36M}

Because of what happened in verse 11, Paul is able to rejoice, and the reason he is able to rejoice is ["the testimony of our conscience"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12). In other words, Paul was able to look back on the things he had said and taught the Corinthians without any regrets of a bad conscience. He had done his best. He had used the right methods: ["simplicity and godly sincerity, not with fleshly wisdom."]{.class_s36N} He had not tried to [trick ]{.class_s36M}them with false science (1 Tim. 6:20) or philosophy (Col. 2:8). Instead, he lived out ["the grace of God"]{.class_s36N} before them in his daily life.

In verse 13, Paul lets the Corinthians know that they have received the letter that he had written to them and that it was indeed from him. Some of the Corinthians merely ["read"]{.class_s36N} the epistle. They knew it was from Paul but did not recognize him as speaking from God or being an apostle and did not appreciate what he said.

Back in 1 Corinthians 14:37, Paul said, ["If any man think himself to be a prophet, or spiritual, let him acknowledge that the things that I write unto you are the commandments of the Lord."]{.class_s36N} So those who were prophets and spiritual among the Corinthians knew that what Paul was writing was the words of God. So Paul is going to have "get on them" again, and he wants the Corinthians to stick with him and acknowledge that everything he writes is from God.

In verse 14, the ["acknowledged us in part"]{.class_s36N} could refer to the fact that, from a human standpoint, they recognized that Paul had a part in their salvation. God had used [him ]{.class_s36M}to lead them to the Lord, and they rejoiced that God had sent him. Or the ["in part"]{.class_s36N} could mean that they had responded [to part of what Paul wrote in 1 Corinthians,]{.class_s36M} and they rejoiced that God used Paul to teach them right.

["Even as ye also are ours in the day of the Lord Jesus."]{.class_s36N} At the Rapture, we will rejoice in each soul which he had a part in leading to Christ. Paul said in 1 Thessalonians 2:19--20, ["For what is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing? Are not even ye in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming? For ye are our glory and joy."]{.class_s36N}

[Christians shouldn't be ashamed of one another.]{.class_s36M} If you see a brother who is going through a real trial of affliction, don't avoid him like the plague; acknowledge him. Be his friend; let him know you are praying for him. Some Christians look at a brother or sister going through bad trouble, and they automatically attribute it to sin without getting to know that person. You had better watch out for an attitude like that. The Devil is likely to take [your ]{.class_s36M}case to God and see how well [you ]{.class_s36M}take it.

Part of the Corinthian church accused Paul of being rude in his speech (2 Cor. 11:6). When the apostates talk about "Ruckmanism," they love to bring up Ruckman's "crude speech." They figure that because they don't like the [way ]{.class_s36M}a thing is said that they can reject the [truth]{.class_s36M} of what is said. That is the way some of the Corinthians were treating Paul. They said, ["His speech is contemptible"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 10:10).

His enemies were telling Paul's converts that ["his bodily presence is weak"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 10:10). Whippings (195 stripes), beatings (with rods), and stonings had undoubtedly left the apostle's body bent and frail. He probably limped from broken bones that had not healed straight. I've often wondered what he looked like. He was probably a little, old, hunchback Jew with a big nose. He probably had welts all over his body and scars all over his face. And what Paul's enemies couldn't stand was that God would give His power to someone who looked like that and talked the way he did.

But Paul still had many friends in Corinth who loved him and stood up for him before his enemies. The world would say they bragged about Paul and were proud of him. I understand what they are saying, but to the Christian it is not a matter of [pride]{.class_s36M}. It is being [thankful ]{.class_s36M}to God for someone who has given you the truth, and it is rejoicing in the truth God has given you through them. At the Rapture, ["the day of the Lord Jesus"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14), we are going to rejoice over every soul we have led to Christ. At the Judgment Seat of Christ, they will be precious jewels in our crown. At the same time, your converts are going to be rejoicing in you because you loved them enough to give them the Gospel and lead them to Christ. So we are all going to have us "a time"!

1:15 And in this confidence I was minded to come unto you before, that ye might have a second benefit;

16 And to pass by you into Macedonia, and to come again out of Macedonia unto you, and of you to be brought on my way toward Judaea.

From the expression ["a second benefit,"]{.class_s36N} in verse 15, the Holiness brethren have constructed the teaching about a "second work of grace." They couple this verse with Romans 1:11, ["For I long to see you, that I may impart unto you some spiritual gift, to the end ye may be established."]{.class_s36N} From there they go to 2 Corinthians 8:6, ["Insomuch that we desired Titus, that as he had begun, so he would also finish in you the same grace also."]{.class_s36N}

The teaching runs like this: when a person is saved, he receives the Spirit of God, and the Spirit of Christ, but he does [not ]{.class_s36M}receive the[ Holy Ghost.]{.class_s36M} When a person receives the baptism of the Holy Ghost (sometime [after]{.class_s36M} salvation), [he will speak in tongues.]{.class_s36M} Sometimes this version has you receiving the Holy Ghost and talking in tongues at salvation. Another version of this teaching is when you receive Christ as your Saviour, you [do]{.class_s36M} get the Holy Ghost but you still have sin in you. At the [baptism ]{.class_s36M}of the Holy Ghost (which you pray for), your old, sinful nature is [eradicated ]{.class_s36M}(or sanctified as one group teaches). When that happens then you [cannot sin any more. ]{.class_s36M}First John 3:9 is the verse they use: ["Whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin; for his seed remaineth in him: and he cannot sin, because he is born of God."]{.class_s36N}

In Moody's day, this "second blessing," as it is also sometimes called, was the extraordinary power which God bestowed upon you at [the baptism of the Holy Ghost.]{.class_s36M} Normally you prayed for this baptism at some point after salvation.

In the Catholic and Episcopal churches in which I was raised, those things would be dealt with at [baptism ]{.class_s36M}and [confirmation]{.class_s36M}. That is two separate works, two sacraments.

Now, in regards to all of this, there are certain things of which you can be sure and certain things of which you cannot be. To begin with, 2 Corinthians 8:6 refers to a [monetary offering,]{.class_s36M} not any baptism or spiritual gift. The ["spiritual gift"]{.class_s36N} of Romans 1:11 was so the saved people in Rome could be established, not [sanctified.]{.class_s36M}

But if there is a second work of grace, it certainly does [not]{.class_s36M} do away with the old, Adamic nature. Paul had his until the day he died. Neither sanctification, eradication, nor confirmation removes "the old man." The [old man ]{.class_s36M}is still there until the day he goes to the grave. Until then he will give you a fit.

What you can be certain in all of this is that [after ]{.class_s36M}you get saved there is something [more ]{.class_s36M}God has for you besides just going to heaven. The Lord wants your spirit to grow to be more like Him, and He wants your [body ]{.class_s36M}for His service alone. You can call it whatever you like, but that is God's will for you above and beyond the salvation of your soul.

Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "Some folks are so worried about what to [call]{.class_s36M} the baby that they forget the important thing, and that is [having the baby."]{.class_s36M} Christians get to arguing and fussing about what [to call]{.class_s36M} what God does to you after you are saved. They call it consecration, the filling of the Spirit, the baptism of the Holy Ghost, eradication, sanctification, confirmation, and God knows what. That is not the important thing. The important thing is---HAS IT [HAPPENED ]{.class_s36M}TO YOU? That's the important thing. The important thing is now that you are saved, [does God have all of you that there is to have and do you have all of Him there is to have? ]{.class_s36M}That's the thing. I don't care what you call it, [just get it.]{.class_s36M}

I still think that that point in your life is the result of a crisis. I know that there is a [daily filling]{.class_s36M} of the Spirit, a daily selling out to the Lord, a daily submitting to the Lord, and a daily yielding to the Lord; but those things don't happen until you reach a place in your life where there is [a crisis.]{.class_s36M} Nine times out of ten, that crisis is a place where the Lord deals with you about [giving up your plans for His]{.class_s36M}. That decision is usually a crisis situation in your life. After that, then it is a daily thing, but if you don't overcome [that crisis,]{.class_s36M} the work can't be done. The idea is that the Lord brings you down to the bottom so that there is no direction to go but [up]{.class_s36M}.

The Lord will start you in a place where you are sweating out bills on a continual basis; then when you begin to minister to folks that are having bill trouble, you will know what they are up against. You won't charge through the congregation like they do at Bob Jones and [Faith for the Family]{.class_s36M} magazine. You won't be strutting your high and mighty stuff like, "God wants only the best for His people," and "God has to have the best of you," and "If you are in the will of God you will have the best." You'll have better sense than that because of what God has put you through.

When students come down to the Pensacola Bible Institute, they always complain about trailers, the cockroaches, the humidity, and the low-paying jobs. But PBI is God's "boot camp" for Christian service. The Lord puts the students through some things here in preparation for the ministry.

The car breaks down, the kids get sick, the dog messes up the carpet, and you get laid off from work. When that stuff happens, look at it this way: what's three years of [that ]{.class_s36M}compared to three years in solitary in a Communist prison? You know, brethren, if the Lord didn't let some of you go through that, what will you tell some of the brethren when you get to heaven? They'll ask you, "What did [you ]{.class_s36M}go through?"

Do you know that sometimes it is easier to [die]{.class_s36M} as a [martyr]{.class_s36M} than it is to [live]{.class_s36M} as a [Christian?]{.class_s36M} Every Christian should be willing and ready to die for Christ, but the problem is living [for ]{.class_s36M}Christ while you pay the light bill, gas bill, grocery bill, and the IRS.

Compare verse 16 with 1 Corinthians 16:5 and see comments under that verse. Our "stops" (Phil. 1:12--13) are directed by God as well as our "steps."

1:17 When I therefore was thus minded, did I use lightness? or the things that I purpose, do I purpose according to the flesh, that with me there should be yea yea, and nay nay?

18 But as God is true, our word toward you was not yea and nay.

19 For the Son of God, Jesus Christ, who was preached among you by us, even by me and Silvanus and Timotheus, was not yea and nay, but in him was yea.

20 For all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him Amen, unto the glory of God by us.

Verse 17,[ "When I therefore was thus minded, did I use lightness?"]{.class_s36N} Some of the Corinthians thought Paul was insincere when he said he was coming. But in verse 17, Paul assures them that he wasn't kidding, he hadn't changed his mind, he wasn't lying by saying that he was when he wasn't or that he wasn't when he was. Paul's yes meant "yes" and his no meant "no" (James 5:12).

There was no double-tongued flimflam with Paul (vs. 18). He didn't say "yes" and "no" at the same time about the same subject. He was just as clear about his intentions to visit the Corinthians (and straighten things out in person, if need be---see chapter 13) as he was when he preached the Gospel to them (vs. 19).

["For all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him Amen"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 20). Before the "Pollyanna" Christians and the "Power-of-Positive-Thinking" preachers like Robert Schuller and Norman Vincent Peale claim this verse for their own, note that the [positive]{.class_s36S} promises of God are IN CHRIST! [Outside ]{.class_s36S}of Him, the promises run like this: ["He that believeth not the Son shall not see life; but the wrath of God abideth on him"]{.class_s36N} (John 3:36). ["For when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction cometh upon them\...and they shall not escape"]{.class_s36N} (1 Thess. 5:3). ["The wicked shall be turned into hell, and all the nations that forget God" ]{.class_s36N}(Psa. 9:17). ["And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 20:15). All of those are just as much promises as Philippians 4:13, 19; they are just promises to people who are [not in Christ!]{.class_s36S} Jesus Christ is the ["Amen"]{.class_s36N} to all of God's promises (Rev. 3:14), but if you want the ["yea"]{.class_s36N} ones, you had better be ["in him."]{.class_s36N}

21 Now he which stablisheth us with you in Christ, and hath anointed us, is God;

22 Who hath also sealed us, and given the earnest of the Spirit in our hearts.

These two verses are great verses on the position of the believer. When you received Christ, the Holy Spirit put you [in]{.class_s36M} the Body of Christ (["stablisheth us...in Christ,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 21), and the Holy Spirit came to live in [your]{.class_s36M} body (["anointed us,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 21). [That anointing is PERMANENT ]{.class_s36M}(1 John 2:27), regardless of what the Clownsville Charismatics teach. He sealed you ["unto the day of redemption"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 22 cf. Eph. 4:30), and He is the security God puts down on you---["the earnest."]{.class_s36N}

In finances, earnest money is a secure down payment that shows you are serious about a purchase. Well, God is serious about saving all of you (1 Thess. 5:23), and the Holy Spirit living in you is God's "proof of purchase" until the day He claims it all. God has [purchased you]{.class_s36M} (1 Cor. 6:20) and [sealed ]{.class_s36M}His property. Moreover, He has signed a [written contract]{.class_s36M} (the New Testament). If He could welch on that contract, you would be in trouble, but verse 20 already told you that God's promises to you are "yea" and "Amen." He won't back out of His contract.

I like the story about the saved colored boy. He had gotten into the wrong crowd, and a razor fight broke out. He began to pray and say, "Lord, yo' property is in danger!"

23 Moreover I call God for a record upon my soul, that to spare you I came not as yet unto Corinth.

24 Not for that we have dominion over your faith, but are helpers of your joy: for by faith ye stand.

Paul obviously had wanted to come earlier, but he would have had to come in with some pretty stiff rebukes. Everything he had [written]{.class_s36M} in 1 Corinthians, he would have had to [say in person.]{.class_s36M} That would have been a bit too much. He wanted to spare them some grief. So he put off his visit hoping that the Corinthians would correct some of the problems on their own after [reading his epistles.]{.class_s36M}

Note verse 24, ["Not for that we have dominion over your faith."]{.class_s36N} Peter put it this way, ["Neither as being lords over God's heritage"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 5:3). How different that is from the Roman Catholic hierarchal system or even the modern "pastoral authority" practised among some Baptists today. Paul chose to describe his job as ["helpers of your joy."]{.class_s36N} By what follows in chapter 2, the joy of verse 24 is the joy they would have once the [sin ]{.class_s36M}and [false doctrine]{.class_s36M} had been [removed from the congregation.]{.class_s36M}

[]{#c1ZM.xhtml}

## Chapter 2 {.heading_s39}

2:1 But I determined this with myself, that I would not come again to you in heaviness.

2 For if I make you sorry, who is he then that maketh me glad, but the same which is made sorry by me?

3 And I wrote this same unto you, lest, when I came, I should have sorrow from them of whom I ought to rejoice; having confidence in you all, that my joy is the joy of you all.

4 For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote unto you with many tears; not that ye should be grieved, but that ye might know the love which I have more abundantly unto you.

["But I determined this with myself"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1). Paul talked the matter over with himself. The world says that talking to yourself is a sign that you're going crazy. If that is true, then just about every Christian is half nuts. But it really all depends on what you talk to yourself [about]{.class_s36M}. The rich fellow in Luke 12 was a fool (Luke 12:19--20). The prodigal in Luke 15, on the other hand, finally had a conversation with himself ["when he came to himself"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 15:17--19). You may even get in a situation where the best advice you can get, from a human perspective, is your own (Neh. 5:7). It's like the colored fellow said one time, "I talks to myself because I likes to hear a smart man talk, and I likes to talk to a smart man."

The ["heaviness"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1) that Paul had was a sorrowful burden like Christ had in the Garden of Gethsemane (Matt. 26:37--38). Paul had a genuine burden for the [spiritual condition ]{.class_s36M}of his converts. His sadness was over the sin so openly manifest in the Corinthian church. He had no problem with making the Corinthians sorry over their sins, but his goal was to see the Corinthians get right. What made him ["glad"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2) was to see the Corinthians sorry over their own sins to the point of [getting right. ]{.class_s36M}That was a blessing to him.

Paul wanted to come to a church that was [acting right]{.class_s36M} and [teaching right]{.class_s36M} and be able to rejoice with them over[ the victories]{.class_s36M} God had given them (vs. 3). He didn't want to have to come to them and give them grief at his coming by [rebuking them ]{.class_s36M}and [throwing some of them out of the church.]{.class_s36M} So Paul poured out his grief and his tears for the Corinthians spiritual state on paper, hoping they would react the right way and get right (vs. 4). As Paul said in 1 Corinthians 16:24, his epistle was written out of a heart of love: ["that ye might know the love which I have more abundantly unto you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4).

The old saying is "you always hurt the one you love." But Paul didn't write the Corinthians with the motive of causing them grief and sorrow. He was concerned about them, and he cared about them.

That is often true in a marriage where a husband corrects his wife. Some modern women think that they are above correction, but normally a wife will cry and say, "If you [loved ]{.class_s36M}me, you wouldn't talk to me in that tone of voice." But the truth of the matter is, sister, if he wasn't concerned enough to tell you, he wouldn't bother to tell you. The trouble in a marriage is when the husband no longer cares whether the wife pays attention to him or not. That's when it gets really bad.

When you love your kids, [you correct them]{.class_s36M}. You correct them so that when they grow up they won't need someone who [doesn't]{.class_s36M} love them correcting them. I think kids know that you love them when you correct them. The trouble with a lot of children in this country is that momma and daddy don't correct them enough, and the kids know it, [and they miss it]{.class_s36M}. So they keep doing wrong to get their parents' attention until they just don't care anymore.

2:5 But if any have caused grief, he hath not grieved me, but in part: that I may not overcharge you all.

6 Sufficient to such a man is this punishment, which was inflicted of many.

7 So that contrariwise ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him, lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow.

8 Wherefore I beseech you that ye would confirm your love toward him.

The ["man"]{.class_s36N} Paul mentions in this passage is the man who was having sexual relations with his [stepmother]{.class_s36M} (1 Cor. 5:1--2). Paul recommended that the man be put out of the congregation and spiritually turned over to Satan ["for the destruction of the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 5:5).

In verse 5, Paul lets the Corinthians know the man's actions had only hurt him a little bit (["in part"]{.class_s36N}). That man caused more grief to his own congregation than he had caused the apostle Paul.

Evidently the congregation had done what Paul said, for he says, ["Sufficient to such a man is this punishment, which was inflicted of many"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6). Apparently that man had taken the actions of the congregation to heart, for Paul says in the next verse, ["so that contrariwise ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him, lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with overmuch sorrow."]{.class_s36N}

So here is a fellow who was "churched" (Southern term for kicking someone out of the church) and turned over to the Devil for a whipping. He took his whipping like a man and straightened up and [got right]{.class_s36M}. Since that was the case, Paul tells the church to restore him. The sinning brother is allowed to come back on the basis of CONFESSION (1 John 1:9) and REPENTANCE.

The modern "All-Grace" crew (mainly the "Bereans" and "Dry Cleaners") have pretended that 1 John 1:9 only applies to [lost people]{.class_s36M}. That is Roman Catholicism, pure and simple. Sinning Christians are to repent of their sins (2 Cor. 12:20--21) and confess them (1 Cor. 11:31). The rules for confession are these:

1\. Sins committed against [God ]{.class_s36M}should be confessed [only to God.]{.class_s36M}

2\. Sins against [another person]{.class_s36M} should be confessed [to that person.]{.class_s36M}

3\. Sins against [the congregation]{.class_s36M} should be confessed [to the church]{.class_s36M} (Acts 5 cf. Matt. 18:16--17).

God's forgiveness is not a substitute for man's forgiveness. Sometimes we need [both]{.class_s36S}. Moreover, to restore a repentant brother, he not only needs the forgiveness of the congregation, he needs the congregation to show him comfort (vs. 7) and love (vs. 8). Don't be like David when Absalom returned in 2 Samuel 14:21--24, "Well, I'll forgive you if I have to, but don't get anywhere near me." ["Confirm your love toward him."]{.class_s36N}

2:9 For to this end also did I write, that I might know the proof of you, whether ye be obedient in all things.

10 To whom ye forgive any thing, I forgive also: for if I forgave any thing, to whom I forgave it, for your sakes forgave I it in the person of Christ;

11 Lest Satan should get an advantage of us: for we are not ignorant of his devices.

When Paul wrote 1 Corinthians (vs. 9), he wanted to see if the Corinthians would do what he told them to do. They did! And because they did, the fellow who sinned got right. And because he got right, Paul was able to extend the forgiveness of God to a repentant brother (vs. 10).

The idea of verse 10 is that when a Christian forgives, [he is actually standing in Christ's place forgiving. ]{.class_s36M}The Roman Catholic Church reserves this right exclusively for [their priests]{.class_s36M}, claiming it was passed down from Peter (Matt. 16:19) and the apostles (Matt. 18:18). But in John 20:23, when Jesus gives the power to remit and retain sins, he gives it to ["the disciples"]{.class_s36N} (John 20:20), [not just the eleven apostles as the Scofield note says before Luke 24:36]{.class_s36M}. There were more disciples there than ["the eleven"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 24:33).

This is part of the doctrine of the "priesthood of the believer." Every Christian is a [priest]{.class_s36M}. Paul said, ["we pray you IN CHRIST'S STEAD,]{.class_s36N} [be ye reconciled to God"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 5:20). You are Christ's ambassador; you are His [representative]{.class_s36M}. That position is not relegated to a few ordained bachelors in an ecclesiastical system. In fact, the German word "Christi" itself means "little Christ."

So whenever you lead a person to Christ, [you ]{.class_s36M}are authorized by God to tell him that his sins are forgiven. Whenever a person turns Christ down, [you ]{.class_s36M}can tell them that their sins are [unforgiven]{.class_s36M}---no matter how much they confess them.

Of course, the new Bibles have carefully and judiciously altered verse 10 in order to knock out the doctrinal material found in Acts 13:38--39 (where Paul actually does what was discussed in the previous paragraph) and John 20:23. Instead, the [NIV, NASV,]{.class_s36M} and similar depraved corruptions have you forgiving someone "IN CHRIST'S PRESENCE." [The fact that He is always present throughout the universe]{.class_s36M} (Eph. 1:22--23; Acts 17:27--28; Psa. 139:7--8) [never occurred to the Bible-perverting "Evangelicals."]{.class_s36M}

In order to take the Christian's priestly power (1 Pet. 2:5) from him, the [NIV]{.class_s36M} says: "I have forgiven IN THE SIGHT OF CHRIST." [That means absolutely nothing at all.]{.class_s36M} It is a fairyland excursion into "virtual reality" by someone wearing a gas mask wired to a [boombox.]{.class_s36M}

Notice the remarkable placement of Satan's ["devices"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11) right where Satan took ahold of the translators of the [ASV, NASV, RSV, NRSV,]{.class_s36M} and [NIV]{.class_s36M} and duped them into lying about sound doctrine.

Verse 11, ["Lest Satan should get an advantage of us: for we are not ignorant of his devices."]{.class_s36N} You'll remember that part of the punishment for the man's behavior in 1 Corinthians 5 [was to be turned over to Satan. ]{.class_s36M}When he had repented and gotten right, he was no longer under that punishment. Now it was up to the congregation to forgive and restore that brother. But if they didn't, notice that Satan could ["get an advantage"]{.class_s36N} of [them.]{.class_s36M} So if the Devil cannot shove you one way to ruin you, he will shove you the other way.

If you are angry at another Christian, you had better forgive them for two reasons. First of all, you had better forgive him for [his sake.]{.class_s36M} If you hold something against another Christian, you can cause them to become depressed and bitter. The next thing you know, they will be saying, "Well, if that's the way a Christian is, you can have it," and then they will quit serving the Lord.

Second, and even more important to you, you need to forgive that brother or sister for [your]{.class_s36M} sake. If you don't, that thing will eat [you ]{.class_s36M}out like a cancer.

A woman came down the aisle at a Dwight L. Moody meeting. She got on her hands and knees and prayed to be saved. After she finished, Moody asked her, "Are you saved now?"

She said, "No."

"Did you ask the Lord to save you?" he asked.

"Yes."

"Did you ask Jesus to come into your heart?"

"Yes."

"You believe God, don't you?"

"Yes."

"Well, why aren't you saved?"

She said, "I don't know. I just don't feel anything."

"What is the trouble?" he asked.

"I don't know what the trouble is," she replied.

He said, "OK, let's pray. You follow me in prayer. ['Our Father, which art in heaven, hallowed be thy name'."]{.class_s36N} She repeated that prayer all the way through to the phrase ["And forgive us our trespasses, as we forgive those who trespass against us."]{.class_s36N} Then there was silence. Again, he said, ["Forgive us our trespasses, as we forgive\...."]{.class_s36N}

All of a sudden, that woman cried out and said, ["I'll never forgive that woman as long as I live!"]{.class_s36M}

Moody said, "Well, if you won't forgive her, then God won't forgive you."

Shocked, the woman said, "Do you mean after what that woman did to me that I've got to forgive her or I won't be forgiven?"

Moody replied, "No, I don't mean to tell you anything of the kind. I mean to tell you that [God said,]{.class_s36M} ['If ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses']{.class_s36N} (Matt. 6:15)."

A grudge will do more damage to your testimony than if the Devil himself jumped on your back. Nothing in the world will eat you out like holding a grudge or being envious. If you find yourself with a grudge against someone or envious of another or bitter with a brother, you had better go to that person and make it right, if at all possible. If you can't make it right like that, [which sometimes you can't, ]{.class_s36M}then you had better get as far from that situation as possible. And pray for that person. If it is something personal which they have done to you, pray that God will [bless them]{.class_s36M}. Nothing will kill a grudging, envious, bitter spirit better than forgiving a person and [praying for them.]{.class_s36M}

2:12 Furthermore, when I came to Troas to preach Christ's gospel, and a door was opened unto me of the Lord,

13 I had no rest in my spirit, because I found not Titus my brother: but taking my leave of them, I went from thence into Macedonia.

As we commented in 1 Corinthians 16:9, ["a door"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12) is an opportunity to preach the gospel (see also Col. 4:3, Acts 14:27; Rev. 3:7--8). The "keeper of the keys" to those doors (Rev. 3:7) also holds the keys that open the doors ["of hell and of death"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 1:18). The Pope no more has those keys than Princess "Di" or Elizabeth Taylor.

The ["Titus"]{.class_s36N} of verse 13 shows up [nine times]{.class_s36M} in the text of 2 Corinthians and once in the subscript at the end of the book; he is never mentioned in the book of Acts. Titus was Paul's messenger to the Corinthians when he wrote this second epistle (2 Cor. 8:6, 16, 23). Although Paul missed him at Troas, Titus finally caught up with Paul in Macedonia later on (2 Cor. 7:5--6).

Another thing to note in these verses is that the trip mentioned here is not spoken of at all in the book of Acts. In 2 Corinthians 13:1, Paul says that the trip in these verses is the ["third time I am coming to you."]{.class_s36N} [Acts only gives two trips:]{.class_s36M} Acts 18:17 and Acts 20:2.

2:14 Now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ, and maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place.

15 For we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ, in them that are saved, and in them that perish:

16 To the one we are the savour of death unto death; and to the other the savour of life unto life. And who is sufficient for these things?

Verse 14 is one of the greatest promises in the Bible for a Christian (cf. Rom. 8:37) and, therefore, should be memorized. The promise is that the Lord will give you the victory in [every ]{.class_s36M}circumstance, and He will give you that victory in Jesus Christ. You can't do it in your own strength. You have to ["be strong in the Lord, and in the power of his might"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 6:10). So the Lord will cause you to rejoice when no one else can. And He will cause you to rejoice when there is no [cause ]{.class_s36M}for rejoicing.

Not only will He give you the victory, but He will use you to let others know about him. ["And maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place."]{.class_s36N} So everywhere you go, speak up for Jesus Christ. Pass out tracts, go street preaching, give a testimony, sing about Jesus, do door-to-door work, read your Bible in the work place, invite someone to church; but SPEAK UP! You have God's promise that He [will]{.class_s36M} use you.

The ["savour"]{.class_s36N} of verses 14--16 is not only the [taste ]{.class_s36M}of a thing; it is the [smell ]{.class_s36M}(see Gen. 8:21). The things that come from our [flesh ]{.class_s36M}stink: breath, sweat, spit, feces, urine, wax, pus, etc. These are not ["the things of the Spirit"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:5; Gal. 5:16--17). So when Paul says, ["we are unto God a sweet savour of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15), he is not talking about us in our natural state.

Positionally, when we are in Christ, God has accepted the sacrifice of Christ on the cross (Isa. 53:10--12); so just as Noah's burnt offerings sent up ["a sweet savour"]{.class_s36N} to God, in like manner, when we are in Christ and are part of His [death ]{.class_s36M}(Rom. 6:3--8), the Lord smells the sweet savour of Christ's sacrifice, not our flesh.

But the verse is much more [practical ]{.class_s36M}than [doctrinal]{.class_s36M}. When we are doing right and fighting against sin and making the Lord known to others (vs. 14), then we are a sweet smell to the Lord: [we smell good to Him.]{.class_s36M}

Isn't it strange how much the Lord brags about us and how little we brag about Him, at times? Did you ever read the Song of Solomon where the bridegroom gets to bragging about the bride? There's the Lord telling you how beautiful the bride is; He's bragging about [us]{.class_s36M} like we are beautiful! Doesn't that get you under conviction?

When a Christian goes out and does something for the Lord, [it smells good to Him.]{.class_s36M} We might find, when we get to heaven, that the only good thing we ever did, which the Lord really enjoyed, was our street preaching and some of our wild witnessing. Maybe the rest of it, to Him, is just "pretty good" and "par for the course."

That's our [savour ]{.class_s36M}before the Lord, but that is not necessarily how we smell to everyone else. I guarantee you that if you had been downwind of the bull burning on the altar ["outside the camp"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 13:11--12; Exod. 29:14) on a hot day in the Sinai wilderness, you would not have thought it was [a sweet smell.]{.class_s36M} So the service a Christian renders to the Lord, in the right way, with the right motive, smells one way to the ["saved"]{.class_s36N} and another way to ["them that perish"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15).

To the lost who are ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 2:1), those things done for Christ are [the stench of death.]{.class_s36M} It is the smell, to them, of a rotting corpse. If we were to live our lives like the apostle Paul, then we would be a WRETCHED STINK in the nostrils of the world and the flesh. People who are unsaved and ungodly can't stand you if you are [right]{.class_s36M}. To [the world,]{.class_s36M} YOU STINK!

But when a Christian sees another Christian living for the Lord, that is the smell of life. Folks that know the Lord have the right response.

James McGinley was going up an elevator one day, and a lady on the elevator with him, when she smelled his cologne, she said, "I see you wear perfume." He replied,[ "I see you don't."]{.class_s36M}

Now, the "brethren" think I'm crude and vulgar, and they get upset with my preaching and language. But those same people will sit around and watch that television; it is just too hypocritical for words. They have commercials for underarm odor, post nasal drip, mouthwashes, underwear (both genders!), women's personal items, toilet smells, and all kinds of crude matter. People will sit around and watch that stuff and then have the audacity to talk about "good taste" in preaching. Like someone said, "They may have gotten some of the violence off television, but it's just as smelly as it ever was."

["Who is sufficient for these things?"]{.class_s36N} Who can figure out that [savour thing]{.class_s36M} he has talked about in verses 14--16? Without Christ, nobody. You couldn't have figured the thing out unless God had revealed it to you in a Book!

But naturally speaking, there is no accounting for the thing. A company puts up a billboard with some naked woman in a bikini advertising suntan lotion, and everything's just fine. But let a Christian put up one of Bro. Dickman's Scripture signs in front of his yard and suddenly the Catholics, the atheists, and the town council are all trying to pass a city ordinance [against it.]{.class_s36M} God takes us and turns us around backwards so that the folks with whom we used to get along now think we stink, and the folks we used to think [stank ]{.class_s36M}we now get along with them.

The passage is an absolute condemnation of twenty-first-century Christianity in America. Every "Christian celebrity" is splitting his britches to stay in good with the world system by not offending them in his SPEECH or ministry. [It is this cowardice that causes the replacement of the Holy Bible in all major colleges, universities, and seminaries with fictional myths like "the original autographs," ]{.class_s36M}outright lies like "THE Greek text," and 100--2,000 "variant readings" that create nothing but [total confusion in the Body of Christ.]{.class_s36M}

Galatians 1:10 is the other side of the coin. Only [one side ]{.class_s36M}of this coin is promoted by higher Christian education. You cannot serve Christ correctly while craving the world's approval. So the modern Christian educator avoids these passages: 2 Corinthians 11:13, 4:2; Galatians 2:6; James 4:4; Luke 16:15; 1 John 2:15--17; John 12:42--43; Romans 16:18; and twenty more just like them.

2:17 For we are not as many, which corrupt the word of God: but as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ.

As though sensing the outcome of verses 15 and 16---that the real Christian is the stink of a [dead man]{.class_s36M} in the nostrils of the "global community" in which they live---Paul purposely pens a conclusion to this comparison which has been neatly [erased ]{.class_s36M}by every translator of every "updated" version since 1880.

The [RSV, NASV, NIV,]{.class_s36M} and even the ["New" KJV]{.class_s36M} all change the corrupters of God's words to "peddlers" of God's words. Of course that is a condemnation of every Bible publisher on the market today. Every one of them "peddle" Bibles, and they all do it ["for profit"]{.class_s36M} ([NIV]{.class_s36M}). The publishers of the new Bibles do not even [believe ]{.class_s36M}the Bibles they [sell]{.class_s36M}. Who would be stupid enough to believe that if you sell a Bible and make a profit on it that that is against God (["but\...as of God"]{.class_s36N})? Certainly not Zondervan, Thomas Nelson and Sons, the Lockman Foundation, and Tyndale Publishers.

Verse 17 permanently marks the apostate scholars in the Laodicean "community" who wanted to be a "sweet smelling savour" to[ the WORLD]{.class_s36M}, instead of [Jesus Christ. ]{.class_s36M}They are easy to spot. They are Bible corrupters. So the word ["corrupt"]{.class_s36N} [has been removed from every modern English text by every Bible corrupter who knew the verse was aimed at HIM (or her)!]{.class_s36M}

These corrupters are identified, discussed, analyzed, criticized, and condemned in [The Last Grenade]{.class_s36M} (1990), [The Christian's Handbook of Biblical Scholarship]{.class_s36M} (1988), [The Anti-Intellectual Manifesto]{.class_s36M} (1991),[ The Christian Liars' Library]{.class_s36M} (1997), [The Scholarship Only Controversy]{.class_s36M} (1996), and many more works by James Son, Alan O'Reilly, Gail Riplinger, Dennis Lloyd, Sam Gipp, Kevin James, Edward Hills, Paul Heaton, and other works, all available from the Bible Baptist Bookstore here in Pensacola, Florida.

There is no connection, whatsoever, between your modern apostate Fundamentalist (1800--2001), who sits in judgment on the Holy Bible, and the "soldiers of the cross" who went ["OUTSIDE THE CAMP"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 13:10--15) to bear "His" reproach (A.D. 33--A.D. 201).

Now, the corruption of God's words was [already]{.class_s36M} taking place [in Paul's day]{.class_s36M}. When Paul said that the New Testament was not even [half written. ]{.class_s36M}So the reference is to the corruption of [the Old Testament. ]{.class_s36M}The earliest corruption was the addition of the [Apocrypha ]{.class_s36M}to the Old Testament canon. That took place in Alexandria, Egypt. It is no coincidence then to find Origen, the first "textual critic" of the Scriptures, at work incorporating earlier Egyptian corruptions, as well as his own changes, into the New Testament Greek manuscripts in Alexandria, Egypt.

Moreover, Paul said it was ["many,"]{.class_s36N} not just a [few]{.class_s36M}. So the "canon" of textual criticism that says the "oldest manuscripts are the better manuscripts," is not necessarily true at all. There were people messing with Paul's letters as far back as A.D. 55 (2 Thess. 2:2). Just as God works to preserve His Bible, you can bet that the Devil was working just as hard to preserve his.

[]{#c22G.xhtml}

## Chapter 3 {.heading_s39}

3:1 Do we begin again to commend ourselves? or need we, as some others, epistles of commendation to you, or letters of commendation from you?

2 Ye are our epistle written in our hearts, known and read of all men:

3 Forasmuch as ye are manifestly declared to be the epistle of Christ ministered by us, written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God; not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart.

4 And such trust have we through Christ to God-ward:

5 Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think any thing as of ourselves; but our sufficiency is of God;

In verse 1, Paul asks the Corinthians, "Did we recommend [ourselves]{.class_s36M}? Did we need to get letters of recommendation to bring to you in order to come visit you? Did we ask [you ]{.class_s36M}to recommend us?" And of course, the answer to all of these is no.

The cross reference to verse 1 is 2 Corinthians 10:12--18. Paul was not self-promoting. He let the Spirit of God "recommend" him.

But from a human perspective, the Corinthians themselves were Paul's recommendation (vs. 2). [A man's converts and a man's fruits are his commendations. ]{.class_s36M}The people you win to Christ will speak as to what kind of Christian you are, and the people you train and teach in the ministry will speak as to what kind of minister and teacher you are.[ "By their fruits ye shall know them."]{.class_s36N}

I think of one of the young men who came through PBI and went into evangelism. His name was Bruce McDowell. Bruce McDowell is what I consider to be a textbook example of how to enter into evangelism. He began by preaching on the streets, everywhere he could preach, until he was invited into a church to preach. Then he got into another church and then another. [He has had the pastorates of three churches offered to him,]{.class_s36M} and he did not take any of them. He said he didn't feel called to be a pastor, yet; he didn't think he would have enough patience yet to deal with the people. The Lord had taken care of him just fine on the evangelistic trail.

To get into evangelism, all you have to have is about three good meetings. If the Lord wants you in evangelism, He will give you the [good meetings]{.class_s36M}. One of the pastors of those good meetings will recommend you to another pastor. Those things begin to build up. When you have a meeting, other preachers will come to hear you preach. Preachers get hungry to hear good preaching. Preachers circulate around and look for good preachers to hold meetings.

Now, every Christian is [a letter]{.class_s36M} written by God to the world (vss. 2--3). Of course, that letter is a [spiritual letter]{.class_s36M}, not a pen and ink letter. And the Lord uses preachers of the Scriptures to minister the Holy Spirit in the hearts of believers, where that [letter ]{.class_s36M}is written by Him. Somebody once said, "All of us are not apostles, but all of us are 'epistles.'"

Those "living epistles" in verse 3 (see ["lively stones"]{.class_s36N} in Eph. 2:20--22 and 1 Pet. 2:5) are like letters:

a\. Written on [prepared]{.class_s36M} material.

b\. Bearing the [expressions]{.class_s36M} of the writer.

c\. Bearing the [signature]{.class_s36M} of the writer.

d\. Should be [legible]{.class_s36M} (readable).

e\. Should be free of [blots]{.class_s36M} and [smudges.]{.class_s36M}

f\. Can be known by the writer's [handwriting.]{.class_s36M}

(Some of you preachers reading this commentary are free to try that for a sermon. It has been sitting in my Bible for fifty-two years. I have just never had the time to "put the meat on the bones.")

In 2 Corinthians 2:16, Paul asks, ["Who is sufficient for these things]{.class_s36N} (speaking of a Christian's testimony before others)[ ?"]{.class_s36N} In verse 5 of this chapter, he answers that question. We are [not ]{.class_s36M}["sufficient of ourselves." "Our sufficiency is of God."]{.class_s36N} That verse should be memorized and hung on the wall of your dining room, living room, bedroom, bathroom, and car. In Philippians 3:3, Paul writes, ["We\...rejoice in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh."]{.class_s36N}

3:6 Who also hath made us able ministers of the new testament; not of the letter, but of the spirit: for the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life.

7 But if the ministration of death, written and engraven in stones, was glorious, so that the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance; which glory was to be done away:

8 How shall not the ministration of the spirit be rather glorious?

9 For if the ministration of condemnation be glory, much more doth the ministration of righteousness exceed in glory.

10 For even that which was made glorious had no glory in this respect, by reason of the glory that excelleth.

11 For if that which is done away was glorious, much more that which remaineth is glorious.

Any preacher's ability to minister depends on the All-sufficient God of verse 5 (vs. 6). And when a preacher ministers [the word,]{.class_s36M} he is not just reading the bare letter of paper and ink. I can get up and read a verse in such a way that it helps no one. A preacher should read and preach the Scriptures in such a way that those who hear him know he [believes ]{.class_s36M}it.

The Scriptures have to have the right spirit behind them, and in the verse, the reference is [not]{.class_s36M} to the Holy Spirit. The idea of verse 6 is that the [bare form]{.class_s36M} of a thing has no life-giving properties, but when the thing is [energized ]{.class_s36M}and given [in the right spirit]{.class_s36M}, it does something.

You ladies understand that. Your husband can put his arm around you and say, "Honey, I love you"; and it is a lot different from when he says indignantly, "You know I love you; shut up"! "I love you" is on both those statements, but it is the spirit, [the attitude,]{.class_s36M} with which one is given that brings life to a relationship.

["The ministration of death"]{.class_s36N} is the Old Testament Law. When Moses came down from the mount with the Ten Commandments in his hands, 3,000 [died ]{.class_s36M}because of that Law. The Law brings the knowledge of sin and accountability for sin (Rom. 5:14, 20, 7:12--13).

Without a doubt, the giving of the Law was a glorious thing (vs. 7). The Ten Commandments were ["written with the finger of God"]{.class_s36N}) Exod. 31:18). When Moses came down with the second set of tablets, ["the skin of his face shone"]{.class_s36N} from the glory of God (Exod. 34:29--35). The children of Israel ["were afraid to come nigh"]{.class_s36N} Moses because of this. But that glory did not last.

That phrase in verse 7, ["which glory was to be done away,"]{.class_s36N} is a good verse to put on a Seventh-day Adventist. The glory of the engravings on stone is gone. When you get to the New Testament, ["the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, (and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father,) full of grace and truth"]{.class_s36N}[ ]{.class_s36P-0}(John 1:14). He received a glorified body at His resurrection; He suffered so He could ["enter into his glory"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 24:26). At His ascension, He was ["received up into glory"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 3:16). So the glory of the New Testament is Jesus Christ, not the Law.

Verse 8, ["the ministration of the spirit be rather glorious." "The spirit"]{.class_s36N} of verse 8 is ["the spirit"]{.class_s36N} of verse 6. It is the ["ministers of the new testament"]{.class_s36N} delivering their message in the [right spirit and attitude.]{.class_s36M} The message itself is already ["glorious"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 4:4); how much more so then when it is preached [correctly]{.class_s36M}.

If there was glory in the giving of the Law, which brought [condemnation ]{.class_s36M}to those who received it, how much more glory is there at the giving of the Gospel, ["the ministration of righteousness"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). When the Law was given, 3,000 people died (Exod. 32). When the Holy Spirit was given at Pentecost, 3,000 people were [saved]{.class_s36M}.

["For even that which was made glorious]{.class_s36N} (Old Testament)[ had no glory in this respect by reason of the glory that excelleth]{.class_s36N} (New Testament)["]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10).

There is such a contrast between the Old and New Testaments that some Christians don't even bother to study the Old Testament. Liberals point out the difference between the "vengeful God" of the Old Testament and the "loving God" of the New Testament. Those are mistakes, but they show you that anyone who knows anything about either Testament knows that the New Testament is better. It is like seeing the moon in daylight. You can see it, but you do not perceive it as giving any light. The light of the sun overwhelms it.

Now, verses 10--11 are very clear about the influence of the Old Testament over the New Testament Christian. The condemnation of the Law has been "done away." To the Seventh-day Adventist, [this means that]{.class_s36M} [there is no Sabbath in this age.]{.class_s36M} God Himself put away the Sabbath in Isaiah 1:10--15 and Hosea 2:11.

The problem with the Seventh-day Adventist is that he keeps searching for an Old Testament alibi to [reject ]{.class_s36M}New Testament truth. For instance, a Seventh-day Adventist will go back to Isaiah 56 and drag it into the Church Age, [when the chapter is obviously a reference to the Sabbaths of the Millennium]{.class_s36M} (Ezek. 45--46). These Sabbaths are mentioned by Paul in Colossians 2:16--17. (That last verse has been altered in the [NIV]{.class_s36M} to make you think those [Millennial Sabbaths]{.class_s36M} won't show up.)

What the Seventh-day Adventist can't see is that there are[ two gatherings]{.class_s36M} of the Gentiles to God (Acts 15:14, 17): one to His Son in [this age]{.class_s36M} and one to His nation in the [Millennium ]{.class_s36M}(Isa. 64--66). But let's not be too hard on the Seventh-day Adventists; after all, all of the major commentators missed all of the references thumbs down. [Not one Bible teacher at Liberty University, BBC, Tennessee Temple, BJU, or PCC could expound the passages.]{.class_s36M}

3:12 Seeing then that we have such hope, we use great plainness of speech:

13 And not as Moses, which put a vail over his face, that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished:

14 But their minds were blinded: for until this day remaineth the same vail untaken away in the reading of the old testament; which vail is done away in Christ.

15 But even unto this day, when Moses is read, the vail is upon their heart.

16 Nevertheless when it shall turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away.

There is one thing certain about the way Paul talked: he never used ["great swelling words"]{.class_s36N} (Jude 16; 2 Pet. 2:18), nor did he use ["wisdom of words"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 1:17). When Paul preached the Gospel, it was with ["great plainness of speech"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12).

Paul was so plain spoken that he said, ["Though I be RUDE in speech\..."]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 11:6). Apparently Paul was not quite as cultured in his speaking habits as the modern Fundamentalist. He was liable to call someone a "suck-egg hound" ([Sam Jones]{.class_s36M}) when he preached. He might have even called a top city official a "hog-jowled liquor head" ([Billy Sunday]{.class_s36M}). He might have even gotten along well with Peter Cartwright, who, one night, when he was told that General Andrew Jackson was in his congregation, said loud enough for all those around him to hear: "You tell General Jackson that if he don't get saved, that he'll go to hell, just like any guinea-stealing n\-\-\-\--." (But I forbear on the "original" for all American readers who no longer have the [freedom of speech]{.class_s36M} promised to them in their Constitution and Bill of Rights.) Bible preaching has always had the distinction of being plain and "to the point."

Note one more time in verse 13 that the demands of the Law and of the Old Testament on the New Testament Christian are ["abolished."]{.class_s36N} You do [not]{.class_s36M} have to keep the Ten Commandments to be [saved]{.class_s36M}. You never hear a Seventh-day Adventist preach 2 Corinthians 3. There are some passages in the Bible from which a heretic won't preach; he doesn't even dare to touch them. I would like to hear a Campbellite preacher preach on Romans 4:5,[ "But to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness."]{.class_s36N} [That]{.class_s36M} will be the day!

I thank God that I can open that Book [anywhere]{.class_s36M}, blindfolded, and if I had to preach a chapter, I could preach any chapter in it.

Verse 13 takes the reader back to verse 7 and Exodus 34:29--35. Since the children of Israel were afraid to be anywhere near Moses while the glory of the Lord shone on his face, Moses would put on a vail to cover his face while he talked to them. Whenever he would go back to speak to God, he would take off the vail.

The blundering blockheads who put out the [NIV]{.class_s36M} have altered the passage in Exodus (34:33, 35) to read that Moses put the vail on his face [after]{.class_s36M} he had spoken to the children of Israel. The [NIV]{.class_s36M} is the same piece of rubbish that has [Elhanan]{.class_s36M} killing Goliath and [all]{.class_s36M} of the Jews who left Egypt dying before Joshua crossed Jordan (2 Sam. 21:19; Heb. 3:16--17).

It is a genuine miracle in this century (and the twentieth) that any professing Christian of any intelligence at all---say, with an IQ of 90---could possibly recommend the [NIV]{.class_s36M} as a "Bible" to [anyone]{.class_s36M}. The fact that they [do]{.class_s36M} only emphasizes the spiritual insanity of the modern Christians in America.

In verses 14--16, Paul figuratively applies the vail Moses wore to the blindness of Israel. This "spiritual" vail acts as a blinder to a Jew; [He cannot see Christ when he reads the Old Testament.]{.class_s36M} He reads Isaiah 53 and he thinks it speaks of [Israel]{.class_s36M}. He reads Psalm 22 and thinks it is talking about [David]{.class_s36M}.

That "vail" is there because the Jews, [as a nation,]{.class_s36M} rejected Christ at His first coming. Look at Acts 28:17, ["their]{.class_s36N} (the Jews') [eyes HAVE THEY CLOSED; lest they should see with their eyes\...and should be converted."]{.class_s36N} Paul is quoting Isaiah 6:10. But note that the verse in Isaiah has someone else shutting the eyes of Israel. That "Someone" is in John 12:40, ["He]{.class_s36N} (God) [hath blinded their eyes."]{.class_s36N} So God blinded Israel to their Messiah [after they had willfully rejected Him.]{.class_s36M}

Paul says in Romans 11:7,[ "What then? Israel hath not obtained that which he seeketh for; but the election hath obtained it, and the rest were blinded (According as it is written, God hath given them the spirit of slumber, eyes that they should not see, and ears that they should not hear;) unto this day."]{.class_s36N} And again in Romans 11:25, ["Blindness in part is happened to Israel, until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in."]{.class_s36N}

So the rule is that, [as a nation]{.class_s36M}, Israel is blind during the Church Age. But [individually]{.class_s36M}, a Jew has the ability to turn his heart to the Lord (vs. 16).
