When that happens, and it doesn't happen very often, God takes the vail away and lets him see Christ, and he ends up getting saved. That is why a saved Jew can see Christ all through the Old Testament, both in prophecy and typology.

If you want to see how the Holy Spirit operates in the life of a Jew, turn to Ezekiel 14. Look at verses 3--4, the children of Israel had set up idols in their hearts. When that happens, the Lord says two things: verse 3, ["should I be inquired of at all by them?"]{.class_s36N}---The Lord won't listen to or answer their prayers, and verse 4, ["I the LORD will answer him that cometh according to the multitude of his idols"]{.class_s36N}---verse 9 says God will deceive them through their prophets.

That is Jehovah God who said that, not a Gentile, Baptist, Christian preacher.[ The Lord will use the Jews' own ministers, the rabbis, to damn them.]{.class_s36M}

The Lord says in Ezekiel 14:5, ["That I may take the house of Israel IN THEIR OWN HEART, because they are all estranged from me through their idols."]{.class_s36N} But in the next verse He says, ["Repent, and TURN YOURSELVES from your idols."]{.class_s36N} So the Jew [can ]{.class_s36M}turn back to God and is [obligated ]{.class_s36M}to do so.

If you apply that lesson from Ezekiel 14, then it is this: A Christian will only grow in the Lord as much as he keeps the channel clear between them. As long as you keep the Lord first, the Lord will give you more and more. "Nothing between my soul and the Saviour," brother.

3:17 Now the Lord is that Spirit: and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty.

18 But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord.

Verse 17 is a great verse and should be committed to memory. It is a verse proving the[ Deity of the Holy Spirit]{.class_s36M}: ["the Lord is that Spirit."]{.class_s36N} There is no such thing as having the ["Spirit of the Lord"]{.class_s36N} without having [the Lord.]{.class_s36M} There is no such thing as having the Lord without having [the Holy Spirit]{.class_s36M}. In verse 3, He is called ["the Spirit of the Living God."]{.class_s36N}

["Where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty."]{.class_s36N} In the context, the Holy Spirit gives the Christian liberty from the bondage of the Law (Gal. 5:1--5). It is the liberty to follow God's precepts without the Law condemning you before the Lord (Psa. 119:45).

When we talk about [liberty ]{.class_s36M}in a church service, we understand what we mean. We mean that the Holy Spirit is in charge and things are loose and easy. The Lord leads and guides, and nothing feels tight and artificial. When we speak about liberty in Christ, we are saying that the Holy Spirit is in charge, and He leads and guides. The new man, then, is free to live for Christ and enjoy the Lord Jesus without constraint of any kind.

In verse 18, the Bible likens itself to a mirror. James 1:22--25 says that when a man comes to the Bible, [the Scriptures show him what he is like on the inside.]{.class_s36M} There are only two reactions any person can have to a situation like that. He can either correct the problems he sees or he can[ ignore it. ]{.class_s36M}Too many Christians today don't even bother to [look ]{.class_s36M}in the Mirror.

When I look at this Book, I don't really see the Lord as He is, but I get an image of Him, [a reflection of Him.]{.class_s36M} The more I expose myself to that Book, the more His image reflects back at me; and the more of His reflection I receive, the more I am changed into [that image.]{.class_s36M}

They say that the longer a husband and wife live with each other, the more they look like each other.

Nathaniel Hawthorne once wrote a story called "The Great Stone Face." In that story, a legend was told about a famous New Hampshire landmark called the "Old Man of the Mountain," a geological formation at the top of a mountain that looked like the profile of a man's face. The legend went that someday a man would come who [looked ]{.class_s36M}like the face on the mountain. As the story goes, a young boy would look at the stone face on the mountain everyday, and he would look at everyone who came through town waiting for the legend to come true. Some famous people, like Abraham Lincoln, came through the town in his lifetime, but none of them fulfilled the legend.

Then one day when he was an old man, he was walking down the street and a child cried out, "Look, the Great Stone Face." He realized that they were talking about [him]{.class_s36M}. He had looked at that stone face on the mountain for so long that his face took on [its features.]{.class_s36M}

That's the way it should be with a Christian. The more time that you spend in that Book, the more it is going to show in your character and your face and every other way. There is something that [comes off that Book ]{.class_s36M}and goes [into you]{.class_s36M} when you expose yourself to it. Right now, it isn't clear, but some day you will see ["face to face"]{.class_s36N} as Paul said back in 1 Corinthians 13:12. There is a [transaction ]{.class_s36M}that takes place; the glory of God in the Book rubs off on you. That glory rubbed off on Moses' face until the Israelites could not even look at him. When people look at you, can they see Jesus Christ; [can they tell you have been spending time in the Book?]{.class_s36M}

To the preachers who are reading this commentary, verse 18 has some great sermonic material associating the Bible with a mirror:

1\. A [broken ]{.class_s36M}mirror gives a false image.

2\. You need a [light ]{.class_s36M}to see your face in a mirror.

3\. You should [start each day]{.class_s36M} with a look in the mirror.

4\. A mirror does not [lie]{.class_s36M}; we lie when we look into it.

5\. Unlike a picture, which reproduces an image of the time it was taken, [a mirror is alive]{.class_s36M}, giving an up-to-date image of yourself every time you look in it.

6\. No matter how many mirrors you have, they won't do you any good [if you don't look in them.]{.class_s36M}

[]{#c253.xhtml}

## Chapter 4 {.heading_s39}

4:1 Therefore seeing we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we faint not;

2 But have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully; but by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God.

Chapter 4 is a great passage. It discusses the ministry from both its doctrinal [and ]{.class_s36M}practical aspects.

["This ministry," ]{.class_s36N}from verse 1, refers to the preceding verses back in chapter 3, verses 17 and 18. It is the ministry of grace through faith, [plus nothing.]{.class_s36M} In the context of chapter 3, the ministry is [not]{.class_s36M} the ministry of the Old Testament. It is to the ministry of ["Thou shalt"]{.class_s36N} and ["Thou shalt not."]{.class_s36N}

People who are trying to get saved by [works ]{.class_s36M}are still under the Old Testament Law. The ministry in this passage is exactly the opposite of that. The ministry here is [that you don't deserve to be saved, you can't earn salvation, and you don't keep the Law; but God will save you if you come to Him as a sinner and trust His work to save you instead of your own works.]{.class_s36M}

["As we have received mercy, we faint not;"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1). [That ]{.class_s36M}is what God wants to bestow on the sinner---mercy. God wants to have [mercy ]{.class_s36M}on the sinner. The trouble is that we [prevent ]{.class_s36M}the Lord from having mercy on us. I wasn't saved until I was twenty-seven. If you were over twenty before you got saved or if you were a Catholic before you got saved, then you know how it goes. You [prevent ]{.class_s36M}the Lord from having mercy on you by insisting that if you follow some [religion ]{.class_s36M}or some [belief]{.class_s36M}, then you can make it. You try keeping the Golden Rule, taking the sacraments, "finding the church Christ founded," and all of that baloney. You just keep trying to do good works. What God wants is you [as you are.]{.class_s36M} He doesn't want you to prove to Him what a fine fellow you are.[ He wants you to own up to what a dirty, low-down rascal you are.]{.class_s36M} That's the difference.

Back in 2 Corinthians 2:17, there were ["many, which corrupt the word of God."]{.class_s36N} In verse 2 of chapter 4, there are those who are ["handling the word of God deceitfully."]{.class_s36N} So when Paul wrote his epistles, there were those who [changed ]{.class_s36M}the Scriptures, and there were those who were [using ]{.class_s36M}the Scriptures to teach and preach things that were not so. Things are no different today. The Bible revisers ["corrupt the word of God,"]{.class_s36N} and the modern, apostate Fundamentalists and Laodicean Nicolaitans handle it deceitfully.

The word of God is handled deceitfully in four ways:

1\. When a man [adds ]{.class_s36M}a word to a verse, he is trying to prove something that is not so. Solomon said, ["Add thou not unto his words, lest he reprove thee, and thou be found a liar"]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 30:6).

2\. When a man [subtracts ]{.class_s36M}from the words, then he is trying to prove something that is not so. The last chapter of the Bible says, ["And if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city"]{.class_s36N} (Rev. 22:19).

3\. [By removing a verse from its context]{.class_s36M}, the Bible can be made to prove anything.

4\. The final way to handle the Scriptures deceitfully is to outright [change the words.]{.class_s36M}

I'll give you some examples. A Campbellite will add to Mark 16:16. They will read the second part of the verse like this: "He that believeth not and is not baptized shall be damned." The Campbellite proves baptismal regeneration by [adding ]{.class_s36M}to the Scriptures. Either that, or he will go to 1 Peter 3:21 and quote it like this: ["Baptism doth also now save us." ]{.class_s36N}What was subtracted from the verse? ["The like figure."]{.class_s36N} If [adding ]{.class_s36M}or [subtracting ]{.class_s36M}from the Scriptures won't do, the Campbellite will go to John 3:5, and say[ "Born of water"]{.class_s36N} means baptism by ignoring the context in verse 6:[ "That which is born of the flesh is flesh; and that which born of the Spirit is spirit."]{.class_s36N} [Baptism isn't within sixteen verses of John 3:5.]{.class_s36M}

And that is how the thing goes, and it goes on [twenty-four hours a day]{.class_s36M} over the TV and radio. You'll turn that radio dial, and you will hear, ["'And they were all filled with the Holy Ghost, and began to speak with other tongues, as the Spirit gave them utterance']{.class_s36N} (Acts 2:4). You see there, [every ]{.class_s36M}Christian ought to speak in tongues." [There is not a Christian anywhere in Acts 2, but does that matter?]{.class_s36M} No, there will always be someone stupid enough to believe it. You turn the dial again, and you hear, ["If ye love me, keep my commandments"]{.class_s36N} (John 14:15). If you don't go to church on Friday night and Saturday morning, you are lost!" You turn the dial a little further: "If you don't endure to the end, you can lose your salvation." Do you see what a mess Americans are in?

God has that Bible set up so that if you are not zealous for the truth, [you won't find it.]{.class_s36M} If a man doesn't know his Bible, he is a sucker and chump for the Devil. He has to be [diligent]{.class_s36M}, he has to be fanatical, he has to be [zealous]{.class_s36M}. He has to [search those Scriptures]{.class_s36M} as if he were looking for [gold]{.class_s36M}. What that Bible [says ]{.class_s36M}has to be more important to you than the [money you earn]{.class_s36M} and the [food you eat,]{.class_s36M} or else you will end up being deceived.

Don't flatter yourself. You are no different from anyone else. If some guy gets up and quotes Scripture to prove a lie and you won't take the time to[ check him out]{.class_s36M}, you will swallow that garbage like a cow licking up black strap molasses. If you are not going to make up your mind to find out what the truth is [and go after it,]{.class_s36M} you would do just as well for yourself to get drunk or blow your brains out!

Now, if you come here to the Pensacola Bible Institute or the Bible Baptist Church, you will never find that mess going on in any class, whether it is our three-year-old "Little Lambs" or our third-year graduating students. We won't change 1 Timothy 3:16 to some inane nonsense like "who was manifest in the flesh," instead of ["God was manifest in the flesh."]{.class_s36N} If that Bible says ["wist"]{.class_s36N} or ["I trow no,"]{.class_s36N} that is how it is going to be. We are not going to change it or wrest the Scriptures from their context. And it is the obligation of every God-called preacher to do likewise.

A minister is also called to renounce ["the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness\...but by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2). He is to walk openly and honestly before men. There should be [nothing ]{.class_s36M}"undercover." Everything should be above board, out in the open, the whole deck---face up. In the matter of questionable things, like that discussed back in 1 Corinthians 8--9, the minister should submit himself to the majority judgment of the community---"[commending ourselves to every man's conscience."]{.class_s36N}

The minister is to ["provide things honest in the sight of all men"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 12:17). Second Corinthians 8:21 says, ["Providing honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men."]{.class_s36N} What it amounts to is an open life without trickery or guile (Acts 20:18), where you are no longer concerned enough about your reputation to take care of it. Let people think what they will think, but be GENUINE. A minister should expose himself to God and to the people with whom he deals; no subterfuges, no pretenses, no "put-ons," [no calculated and controlled speeches to produce the right impression or get the desired results.]{.class_s36M} In other words, DON'T BE A HYPOCRITE.

4:3 But if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost:

4 In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.

When it comes to witnessing, you are not going to "bat a thousand" (vs. 3). There are always going to be a majority of sinners who "don't get it," when it comes to the Gospel. You have to remember that while you are sowing the seed of the word of God, ["the fowls of the air"]{.class_s36N} (Satan---Mark 4:4, 15) are snatching the word away, and "the sun of adversity" is scorching those with no "roots" in the Gospel.

Now, it is easier for some people to "see" the Gospel than others. A poor person is not trusting in his riches (1 Tim. 6:17), so it is easier for [him ]{.class_s36M}to see the great deal he is getting in the Gospel. Generally speaking, the worse a man is,[ the more he sees his need]{.class_s36M}; the better (morally) a man is the less he sees his need. It's a lot easier to reach a Publican than a Pharisee, because the Publican knows he has no righteousness to commend himself to God, while the Pharisee believes [he does.]{.class_s36M} It was a lot easier for blind Bartimaeus to get saved than Saul of Tarsus. As a matter of fact, Paul was so beyond salvation [that the Lord had to knock him down and blind him to get him to see Christ.]{.class_s36M} If you had to pick out one sin, then, above all others that keeps a man from seeing the Gospel, it would have to be [pride]{.class_s36M}: pride in a man's accomplishments, possessions, righteousness, station, etc.

Verse 4 has a lot in it, doctrinally. First, note that ["the god of this world"]{.class_s36N} is the Devil. The ["world"]{.class_s36N} in that verse is not the physical creation; that belongs to God (see 1 Cor. 10:26). The world in verse 4 is the [world system]{.class_s36M} as described in 1 John 2:15--17 and Luke 4:5--6. It is the [world system]{.class_s36M} of Daniel 2 that will be overthrown one day by the smiting stone from heaven (Dan. 2:44--45). The old [Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s36M} has an excellent note on this [world system ]{.class_s36M}under Revelation 13:8.

"In the sense of the present world-system, the ethically bad sense of the word, refers to the 'order,' 'arrangement,' under which Satan has organized the world of unbelieving mankind upon his cosmic principles of force, greed, selfishness, ambition, and pleasure\....This world system is imposing and powerful with armies and fleets; is often outwardly religious, scientific, cultured, and elegant; but, seething with national and commercial rivalries and ambitions, is upheld in any real crisis only by armed force, and is dominated by Satanic principles" (pg. 1342, old [Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s36M}).

This [world system]{.class_s36M} is described by Paul as ["evil"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 1:4). While in it, we, as Christians, are to ["live soberly, righteously, and godly"]{.class_s36N} (Titus 2:12). We are not to be conformed to it (Rom. 12:2), no matter how seductive it is (2 Tim. 4:10). It's cares can choke a Christian's spiritual life, making it unfruitful to the Lord (Mark 4:19).

Satan is the ["god"]{.class_s36N} over this world system, so Paul says in 1 Corinthians 8:5, ["For though there be that are called gods, whether in heaven or in earth, (as there be gods many, and lords many."]{.class_s36N} Satan is a ["god"]{.class_s36N} exactly as the ["gods that have not made the heavens and the earth"]{.class_s36N} (Jer. 10:11).

They are called ["gods"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 82:6), and before Genesis 1:2, they are called ["sons of God"]{.class_s36N} (Job 38:4--7).

So in the Bible you have ["God"]{.class_s36N} and ["gods,"]{.class_s36N} [the]{.class_s36M}[ "Son of God"]{.class_s36P-0} and ["sons of God,"]{.class_s36N} and [the]{.class_s36M} "Devil" and "devils." Never mind the Fundamentalist cliché, "There are many demons but only one devil." [Correct all commentators on the subject with John 6:70, ]{.class_s36M}1 Corinthians 8:5, and John 1:12.

As ["the god of this world,"]{.class_s36N} it is the Devil's job to keep as many of his subjects as possible blinded to the saving knowledge of Jesus Christ. If a man rejects the Gospel and dies and goes to Hell, there is one person who will rejoice in that, because that was his [goal ]{.class_s36M}for that soul all along---[that will be the Devil]{.class_s36M} (Ezek. 32:31--32). That is why Paul described his job as a missionary this way: ["To open their eyes, and to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins, and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in me" ]{.class_s36N}(Acts 26:18).

And before we leave verse 4, note that ["the image of God"]{.class_s36N} is not anything that you find written in any of the major commentaries on that subject.[ "The image of God"]{.class_s36N} is Jesus Christ (cf. Heb. 1:3; Col. 3:10)---period! Devotional excursions into "perfect righteousness, holiness, etc." are just hot air.

[Adam was made in God's image]{.class_s36M} (Gen. 1:26). After Adam fell, [not one man since was made in that image]{.class_s36M} (Gen. 5:3); that is, until Jesus Christ showed up---["the last Adam"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 15:45). He was what Adam lost: ["the image of God."]{.class_s36N} When a man receives Christ as his Saviour, the Lord sends His Holy Spirit to live in that man and resurrects his old, dead spirit (Eph. 2:1), thus making a ["new man"]{.class_s36N} in Christ Jesus. With this ["new creature,"]{.class_s36N} the man is once more in ["the image of God"]{.class_s36N}: a live body, a live soul, and a live spirit. Someday God will remove us from these bodies of sin and give us bodies exactly like His, and we will be completely like Christ.

4:5 For we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord; and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake.

6 For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts, to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.

7 But we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us.

["For we preach not ourselves]{.class_s36N} \[I hope you don't!\][, but Christ Jesus the Lord"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). An old time divine once said, "He preaches most excellency who preaches Christ." Every preacher should have at least twenty good sermons on Jesus Christ. If you are a preacher reading this, you ought to preach on His hands, His feet, His eyes, His death, His burial, His resurrection, His birth, His prayer life, His soulwinning techniques, His denunciation of religious hypocrites like the Pharisees, and His compassion toward sinners. Preach Christ!

Learn how to preach [Christ]{.class_s36M}; learn how to present [Christ]{.class_s36M}. Take all the powers and abilities of which you are capable and all the talent and eloquence you have and all the oratorical powers and vocabulary you can muster and all of your education and learning and consecrate the whole thing to presenting [Christ]{.class_s36M}. There is no better objective in life than that.

A preacher came up to his pulpit one day and saw a sign there that said, "Sir, we would see Jesus." After he had gotten right and straightened out his preaching, he found another anonymous note that said, ["Then were the disciples glad when they saw the Lord."]{.class_s36N}

Now, there is nothing wrong with using [personal illustrations]{.class_s36M} in sermons, but some preachers spend all of their time giving personal testimonies. Preach Christ first and bring yourself in second or even[ last.]{.class_s36M}

Notice, in verse 5, that Paul uses the full title of Jesus Christ [before]{.class_s36M} he says, just ["Jesus."]{.class_s36N} The name is ["Christ Jesus,"]{.class_s36N} not just the ["Lord"]{.class_s36N}; it is the[ "Lord Jesus Christ,"]{.class_s36N} not merely Jesus of Nazareth or the lowly Galilean. It is not just "Jesus, dear Jesus, sweet Jesus, precious Jesus." Those are personal, private, devotional things that lie between you and the Lord. We have a kind of Christianity today that makes Jesus into a pet doll or a teddy bear. He's ["the Lord of glory,"]{.class_s36N} brother, and don't you forget it!

The Charismatics are great ones for concentrating on Christ's [human name.]{.class_s36M} They called themselves "the Jesus people" back in the 70s. My wife and I were driving back once from Disneyland and saw a sign for a "Jesus '77 meeting." I told my wife, "I'll bet that is a charismatic movement."

And of course, the Liberal and Modernistic scholars have a yearly get together they call the "Jesus Seminar." [They don't believe in the Deity of Christ anyway,]{.class_s36M} so they meet and vote out of the Bible anything they don't [think]{.class_s36M} Jesus said. So every reference to Christ's [Deity ]{.class_s36M}is removed from the Gospels.

Now, Paul uses Jesus' name alone from time to time (see the end of vs. 5), but watch out for anyone who dwells on it [to the exclusion of His full title.]{.class_s36M} First and foremost, He's the Lord (God). Then He's Jesus (the man). After that, He's the Christ (God's anointed).

Verse 6 goes all the way back to Genesis 1:[ "For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness."]{.class_s36N} That is the God who said, ["Let there be light: and there was light"]{.class_s36N} (Gen. 1:3).

That is why the historical accuracy of the Bible is so important. If the Genesis account of creation is not true, then what Paul is about to say about your [salvation ]{.class_s36M}is not true either. The Bible is a[ history book]{.class_s36M} (see [1 in 23,000,000,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, 2001), and that sets it apart from all the other "holy scriptures" of other religions. The Bible uses the [facts]{.class_s36M} of history to illustrate the great spiritual truths of faith, and if the [facts]{.class_s36M} aren't true, then your [faith ]{.class_s36M}is so much floss candy.

Back to verse 6. The same God who said, ["Let there be light,"]{.class_s36N} said "Let there be light" down in your heart, if you are a Christian. Christ said, ["I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life"]{.class_s36N} (John 8:12).

I lived twenty-seven years as an unsaved man, and I know the emptiness and desolation in which I used to live. There was not enough women, liquor, money, travel, fame, fortune, art, or music to fill the void that used to exist in me. [It was a vacuum that was insatiable.]{.class_s36M} As a saved man, I consciously sense that there is an Occupant in my body that was not there before. When I received Jesus Christ, He began to fill up that void. It was very much like turning on a light in a dark room: the light takes over everywhere the darkness was.

The light which Jesus gives is ["the light of the knowledge of the glory of God."]{.class_s36N} God revealed His glory ["in the face of Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N} Paul saw that glory when he saw Christ on the road to Damascus (Acts 22:9--11); he described it as ["above the brightness of the sun"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 26:13). That is the light that ["hath shined in our hearts"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6).

The ["treasure"]{.class_s36N} of verse 7 is the light of verse 6. That light, of course, is Christ (John 1:1--9). Colossians 1:27 is more direct in describing this treasure: ["To whom God would make known what is the RICHES of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles; which is CHRIST IN YOU, the hope of glory."]{.class_s36N} And of course, we know that Christ is in us in [the person of the Holy Spirit ]{.class_s36M}(Rom. 8:9). So the Holy Spirit is the treasure.

The ["earthen vessels"]{.class_s36N} are our [bodies]{.class_s36M}. They are made out of the clay, the mud (Gen. 2:7). The Lord told Adam, ["In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for OUT OF IT WAST THOU TAKEN: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return"]{.class_s36N} (Gen. 3:19).

Back in the days when pirates roamed the seas, they used to take the gems they had looted and cover each one in a little clay ball. They would then take those little clay-covered gems and hide them in a hole of a rock near the beach. Every once in a while you'll hear of a beachcomber finding some of those little clay balls, and not knowing what they are, he will walk along the beach pitching them into the ocean like stones. Then later on he will find out what he had and kick himself for what he did.

[Now that's you.]{.class_s36M} You're a little ball of clay covering up a valuable treasure. [You are not worth anything,]{.class_s36M} but that treasure is. That's why Paul says, ["that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us."]{.class_s36N} So in ourselves, we are nothing. So Paul says, ["For we\...rejoice in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh."]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 3:3).

4:8 We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed; we are perplexed, but not in despair;

9 Persecuted, but not forsaken; cast down, but not destroyed;

10 Always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body.

What you have in verses 8--10 is a comparison between what is happening on the outside to the flesh and the state of the treasure on the inside.

Verse 8, ["We are troubled on every side."]{.class_s36N} That's the flesh. ["Yet not distressed."]{.class_s36N} There's the new man on the inside. ["We are perplexed."]{.class_s36N} That's the [earthen vessel.]{.class_s36M}[ "But not in despair."]{.class_s36N} There's the Lord inside, taking care of them. Verse 9, ["Persecuted."]{.class_s36N} On the outside, the flesh. ["But not forsaken."]{.class_s36N} The Lord will never leave you (Heb. 13:5).

["Cast down, but not destroyed."]{.class_s36N} That's the contradiction of the Christian life. A Christian may be troubled, perplexed, persecuted, and cast down; but he is not distressed or in despair or forsaken or destroyed.

In verse 10, a Christian is alway to bear ["in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus Christ."]{.class_s36N} [Doctrinally ]{.class_s36M}that means this: The Lord is inside of you, and you are inside of Him. You are part of His life, and that life goes back to Calvary, [where He died]{.class_s36M} and was buried. So Romans 6:8 says we are ["dead with Christ."]{.class_s36N} So in a sense, you are carrying around your [corpse ]{.class_s36M}day by day. Paul said, ["I am crucified with Christ: nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 2:20). So if you have a part in His death and burial, then you also have a part in His resurrection.[ ]{.class_s36N}\
["That the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10). That is your position in Christ [doctrinally.]{.class_s36M}

[Practically ]{.class_s36M}speaking, verse 10 is "exposure to the same kind of death" constantly (vs. 11). So if someone is going to see Christ ["in you"]{.class_s36N} (["might be made manifest in our body"]{.class_s36N}), the test will not be your dress or your conduct or your studied speech or your nice manners or your clean-cut face or your polite conversation or your handshake; it is [how well you take suffering and opposition and persecution.]{.class_s36M} A reenactment of Christ's life for Paul meant bearing whip marks (Gal. 6:17), sustaining ridicule, arrests, beatings, and imprisonments (2 Cor. 11), while praising God through the whole mess [without complaining.]{.class_s36M} He is making no reference at all to "training champions for Christ," "high academic standards," "reverend Biblicists," "Sword of the Lord" conferences, Fundamental Congresses, chapel services, or "secondary separation." All of that bunk is just so much modern, twenty-first century, Laodicean [gas]{.class_s36M}.

4:11 For we which live are alway delivered unto death for Jesus' sake, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh.

12 So then death worketh in us, but life in you.

13 We having the same spirit of faith, according as it is written, I believed, and therefore have I spoken; we also believe, and therefore speak;

14 Knowing that he which raised up the Lord Jesus shall raise up us also by Jesus, and shall present us with you.

The cross-reference to verse 11 is Romans 8:36--37. In the Christian life, the way up is down; the way to be a master is to serve; and the way to live victoriously for Christ is [to die to self.]{.class_s36M}

In verse 12, Paul applies everything he has been saying in verses 7--11. Why had Paul gone through all that trouble back in verses 8--10? It was for the benefit of the Corinthians. All of the perplexity, persecution, distress, and despair was to help the Corinthians and work good for them. It affected the [spiritual life]{.class_s36M} of his converts.

So when you, as a Christian, reckon yourself dead to sin (Rom. 6:11), it produces [spiritual life]{.class_s36M} in other Christians. Note verses 12, 14, and 17. The same persecutions and trials which worked life in [the converts]{.class_s36M} also worked for Paul (note: ["worketh for us,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 17).

In verse 13, every Christian has ["the same spirit of faith"]{.class_s36N} no matter what his situation. Now, the [amount]{.class_s36M} of faith may differ from one believer to another, but the basic [spirit ]{.class_s36M}behind that faith is the same. That is because the [source ]{.class_s36M}of a Christian's faith is the word of God (Rom. 10:17).

Paul then quotes Psalm 116:10 and applies it to the Christian. ["We also believe, and therefore speak."]{.class_s36N} That is a good motto for a preacher. In Romans 10:14--17, the order is the word of God produces [faith]{.class_s36M}; that faith produces [preaching]{.class_s36M}; and the preaching results in the [salvation of sinners.]{.class_s36M}

In verse 14, there is a great promise for the soulwinner. One of these days, the same Lord who raised His Son from the grave is going to raise [you ]{.class_s36M}up. When that happens, He is also going to raise up those whom [you ]{.class_s36M}have led to the Lord. When you get to heaven, [your converts]{.class_s36M} will be there with you. And then the Lord Jesus is going to take all of us and bring us to the throne and present us to the Father. Won't that be something?

Now, Paul said that his converts were his ["crown of rejoicing"]{.class_s36N} (1 Thess. 2:19). Jesus said that ["joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 15:7). So every time a sinner gets saved, the presses in heaven put out an "extra" in "WAR" type: SINNER RECEIVES CHRIST! The bells start ringing, and the saints start whooping and "running the bases"! And some day when you get to heaven and see those you have led to Christ [that ]{.class_s36M}is what you are going to run into. There'll be back-slapping, "Hallelujah, glad to see you here, brother!" tears of joy; and your converts will be saying thank you for making it possible for them to be there. And then you'll find the person who led you to Christ, and the whole thing will start all over again! You will see that scene repeated all the way back to the disciples who were called out in Matthew 10. The first disciple ever to lead someone to Christ was Andrew: ["He first findeth his own brother Simon\...And he brought him to Jesus" ]{.class_s36N}(John 1:41--42).

I do not know how it is going to be with you, but I have had the fortune of leading some people to Jesus Christ. I know the number is not a lot compared with other preachers, but I thank God for what He has given me.

I suppose the reason that I have not led as many to Christ as other well-known preachers is because I've always laid more emphasis on [training the Christian]{.class_s36M} than I have on winning someone to Christ. I [do]{.class_s36M} believe that [every ]{.class_s36M}Christian ought to win people to Christ. I belive every Christian at least ought to [try]{.class_s36M}, whether he actually wins anyone or not. I believe that. But what I have seen up and down this country in my fifty-three years in the ministry is a bunch of Christians who aren't worth the power and shot it would take to blow them to heaven. So I concentrate on rooting and grounding [the Christians]{.class_s36M} in the word of God.

But the Lord has been very good to me with the converts I have had. Most of them have turned out to be soulwinners. As of 1970, at least 137 of them have turned out to be preachers; I don't know how many more after that date. About forty-eight of the young men I have trained here at PBI are on the mission field right now, winning souls.

I led one fellow to the Lord in California who has led 500 people to Christ every year for fifteen years. Those are people he has [personally ]{.class_s36M}led to Christ, not counting what his church is doing. At least two of his converts are now in the ministry winning souls. Those converts are my spiritual grandchildren and great grandchildren.

Some day, Hugh Pyle, the man who led me to Christ, is going to see his sons, grandsons, great grandsons, great-great grandsons, and great-great-great grandsons in the faith. Did you ever stop to think of the generations of sons D. L. Moody must have? And how about the apostle Paul?

The moral of this story is simple. Make sure that, when you get to heaven, you have "replenished the earth" and "brought forth seed [after your own kind.]{.class_s36M}" If you are a Christian, you ought to be reproducing other [Christians]{.class_s36M}.

4:15 For all things are for your sakes, that the abundant grace might through the thanksgiving of many redound to the glory of God.

16 For which cause we faint not; but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day.

Again, Paul says in verse 15 that everything that happens to him is for the sake of the Corinthians, so [they ]{.class_s36M}will grow in the Lord. By growing, the Corinthians can give thanks to God, and when thanks is given to God, the Lord gets glory. The more people who give thanks, the more glory God gets.

Verse 16 is a great verse. No matter how decrepit this old body gets, the "new man," inside, is [new ]{.class_s36M}every day. That new man never wears out; it just gets better every day. It is the [old man,]{.class_s36M} the flesh, that grows worse every day; that is the part of you that is subject to the Second Law of Thermodynamics. But that new man is [renewed ]{.class_s36M}by the Holy Spirit, and the Holy Spirit is [eternal ]{.class_s36M}and [omnipotent]{.class_s36M}. If you want to benefit from that daily renewal then you have to refresh that new man through Bible reading and prayer. Communication with the Lord will make that renewal [real]{.class_s36M} to you.

4:17 For our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory;

18 While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen: for the things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal.

These verses are one of those passages which should be committed to memory by every Christian. Sooner or later you will [need ]{.class_s36M}them.

All the trouble which Paul has described in this chapter is called, in verse 17, ["our light affliction."]{.class_s36N} Whenever the trials of this life burden you down, and you don't think you can go another step, remember that. Paul says in Hebrews 12:4, ["Ye have not yet resisted unto blood, striving against sin."]{.class_s36N} I dare say that few people reading this commentary have gone through what the apostle Paul went through for the faith. Now, you [can]{.class_s36M} go through it, but it will be in Catholic countries like Mexico where the Catholic priest still leads the village people to stone missionaries like Weldon Jones. It will be in Moslem-controlled countries like the Sudan and Indonesia, where Christian are mutilated and sold into slavery for not converting to Islam. But Christians here in the USA and even in most European countries don't really have it that bad.

Brethren, let me tell you something. When you get depressed and feeling sorry for yourself, go on down to the [cancer ward]{.class_s36M} and make some visits. Go to the [nursing home,]{.class_s36M} go to the spastic clinic, go to the [retarded children's home, ]{.class_s36M}go down to the [burn clinics,]{.class_s36M} and visit some people who have some [real ]{.class_s36M}problems. You will find out really quick that [your ]{.class_s36M}problems aren't so big. You will thank God for the ["light affliction"]{.class_s36N} that you [do]{.class_s36M} have.

The next phrase in verse 17 is the one that really gives us the problems and the heartaches and the headaches that we have. ["Which is but for a moment." ]{.class_s36N}It sure doesn't seem like [a moment]{.class_s36M}, does it? If the Lord had shown up in chapter 3 of Job, then Job would have made it through his trials victoriously. What wore Job down was chapters 3--32. You may make it OK with one night of back pain and only two to four hours of sleep. But let that one night stretch into [years ]{.class_s36M}of that same thing, and your attitude will change, brother, I kid you not.

The problem is time drags on for you, but with God, who is eternal, it is just [a moment]{.class_s36M}, a handbreadth. A little boy once was talking to God in prayer, and he asked the Lord, "Lord, what's a thousand years like with you?" To his amazement, the Lord answered back, "A thousand years is but [a moment.]{.class_s36M}" Then the little boy asked, "And what is a million dollars like?" And the Lord answered, "A million dollars is like a penny." So the little boy said, "Well, Lord, can I have a penny?" And the Lord replied, "In a moment!" And that's exactly how we feel sometimes.

[I have never known the Lord to be in a hurry about anything.]{.class_s36M} He always takes His time. You would think it would mess up His timetable, but it never does. [We ]{.class_s36M}are the ones who are always in a hurry, and we think that if something is not done on our schedule that our world will cave in. But the Lord often lets your deadline come and go and answers your prayer when He sees fit. So what may be to us a period of seventy years may only be [a moment]{.class_s36M} to him.

In Hebrews 12:10, the Bible speaks about the chastening of a father, ["For they verily for a FEW DAYS, chastened us after their own pleasure."]{.class_s36N} That ["few days"]{.class_s36N} is a period of sometimes[ sixteen years]{.class_s36M}! So when things don't seem to be going your way, and you are "confessed up" before the Lord, and you've prayed about it and maybe even fasted over a thing, and it doesn't seem that the Lord is answering, the best you can do is claim Romans 8:28 and hold on.

Now, what is the purpose of this ["light affliction, which is but for a moment"]{.class_s36N}? It ["worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory."]{.class_s36N} In plainer words, what you are going through right now is on one side of the scale, and what you are going to get in eternity is on the other side. Anything you can do for the Lord [right now]{.class_s36M} goes on the scales, and it weighs out in [eternity]{.class_s36M}. You put the blood, sweat, and tears down here on the scales, and in eternity it is weighed out in gold, silver, and precious stones. When folks start cussing you out, let them cuss. Don't stop them. Every time they open their mouth, the computer up in heaven puts down some more to[ your account.]{.class_s36M}

The greatest tragedy on this earth is for a man or woman to live and die, having done nothing that will last for [eternity]{.class_s36M}. You may as well be a dog. If all you do is come into this world, grow up, eat a few thousand chickens, pigs, and cows, raise a family, pay your bills, and die, what have you done that any dog, cat, or skunk hasn't done. Any [animal ]{.class_s36M}eats, reproduces, and tries to live as comfortably as possible until the day it dies. There's nothing to that.

If a man doesn't do something [eternal]{.class_s36M}, his life isn't worth living. I would hate to go through this life, as a Christian, and only have 500 people know that I had been here. As you live, may your footprints leave a mark for Christ on at least 10,000 lives.

You say, "I won't have that chance." Sure you will have that chance. If you go out [street preaching,]{.class_s36M} you will hit fifteen to twenty people every time you open your mouth. Even you ladies can affect at least 100 people if you carried a [Scripture sign]{.class_s36M} or banner during an hour of street preaching. You can get on the radio and reach 100 to 200 listeners every time. If the average Christian would pass out only [three tracts a day, ]{.class_s36M}he would reach 1,000 people in one year. If the Lord puts a preacher in a town of 10,000 people, and if that preacher does what he ought to do for the Lord, there will not be anybody in that town that doesn't know his name, address, and telephone number.

Hugh Pyle was like that. He was the type of character that, after he had been in town for[ a year]{.class_s36M}, everybody in town knew his name. He would go to the [barber shop]{.class_s36M} and put out a tract, to the [gas station ]{.class_s36M}and put out a tract, ride[ the bus]{.class_s36M} and give a tract to the bus driver, take a [taxi ]{.class_s36M}and give one to the cabby, go to the [grocery store ]{.class_s36M}and give one to the cashier. He would get on the phone and witness over [the phone.]{.class_s36M} He would go to the[ radio station ]{.class_s36M}and witness to everyone coming in and going out. He would go down to[ the bank]{.class_s36M} and witness to the tellers going in and the desk clerks coming out. He would walk down the street and put tracts in all of [the cars]{.class_s36M}. By the time that fellow was in Pensacola three years, [there was not one place in this town you could go that didn't know Hugh Pyle, pastor of Brent Baptist Church.]{.class_s36M} They knew who he was and from where he was.

If you're a housewife and can't get out much, you can still do something that will last for eternity. Certainly you can raise children who will love and serve God. From time to time you can even hold a Children's Bible Club in your home after school. And when you go shopping, you can certainly be faithful in passing out tracts. But if you can do nothing else, you can [support a man who is doing something for God.]{.class_s36M} If that seems kind of dull, remember this: God made you to [complete ]{.class_s36M}that man. When you free him from daily tasks to serve the Lord, [then you have a part in his reward.]{.class_s36M} Look at 1 Samuel 30:24.[ "But as his part is that goeth down to the battle, so shall his part be that tarrieth by the stuff: they shall part ALIKE."]{.class_s36N} If you stay home and take care of the house and wash the dishes and do the laundry and take care of the kids and scrub and clean and tarry by the stuff, when your husband comes back from doing the work of the ministry, [then you both split the reward.]{.class_s36M}

Verse 18 is one of the greatest verses in either Testament. It shows you that everything that [can be seen]{.class_s36M}---the basic root creed of all atheists, humanists, pragmatists, phenomenalogists, existentialists, and "New Agers"---is going to perish. It will all burn up (2 Pet. 3:12; Rev. 20:11). The only [permanent things ]{.class_s36M}are those that are [invisible now;]{.class_s36M} note, in particular, the Godhead (Rom. 1:20; Heb. 11:27).

Too many Christians have their windows open toward Sodom and Gomorrah (Jude 7) or Babylon (Rev. 18:8) when they should be opened toward New Jerusalem (Col. 3:2). Lot forever stands as a picture of a Christian who [looked ]{.class_s36M}in the wrong direction (Gen. 13:10 cf. 2 Pet. 2:7--8).

["While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 18). Now, there is a contradiction if you ever saw one. [The whole Christian life is a contradiction. ]{.class_s36M}We are to look at the things which [cannot ]{.class_s36M}be seen. Paul said in Romans 1:20, ["For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen."]{.class_s36N} He said in Hebrews 11:27 that Moses ["endured, as seeing him who is invisible."]{.class_s36N}

Every Christian should be [farsighted]{.class_s36M}. He ought to have the [long look]{.class_s36M}. He should live in the light of eternity. Instead of getting up in the morning and seeing the house, the car, the kids, and job and living in light of those things; get up every morning and live life in the light of [the Judgment Seat of Christ]{.class_s36M}. Say, "I am going to live this day so that I am ready to face the Judgment Seat of Christ." That is where the eternal things are settled for the Christian, and that is where they start.

Look at 2 Corinthians 5:10. No sooner has the Lord said, ["the things which are not seen are eternal,"]{.class_s36N} than the [Judgment Seat of Christ]{.class_s36M} shows up within ten verses. You have never seen it before, but it is [there, ]{.class_s36M}waiting for you.

There is only one [temporal ]{.class_s36M}thing which God has put before your eyes that is [eternal]{.class_s36M}. That is the Bible. It's only a book; it isn't indestructible. It can be destroyed. But its words are [eternal]{.class_s36M}. ["For ever, O LORD, thy word is settled in heaven"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 119:89). ["Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall not pass away"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 24:35).

["The things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 18).

Every minister should remember [that ]{.class_s36M}when the church building program begins. Every dollar invested in souls will last. Every dollar invested in a [monument ]{.class_s36M}is fuel for the fire. Don't build too big of a mausoleum. One day the big, beautiful campuses at Pensacola Christian and Bob Jones will burn. "Liberty Mountain" will be ashes. Oral Roberts' "Prayer Tower" will be gutted by flames. It's all going up in one great big [inferno]{.class_s36M}.

The colored folks sing, "O my lovin' brother, when the world's on fire, don't you want God's bosom to be your pillow?"

[Live in light of eternity.]{.class_s36M} Don't pin your hopes too much on things down here.

[]{#c28K.xhtml}

## Chapter 5 {.heading_s39}

5:1 For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens.

2 For in this we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed upon with our house which is from heaven:

3 If so be that being clothed we shall not be found naked.

4 For we that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burdened: not for that we would be unclothed, but clothed upon, that mortality might be swallowed up of life.

The ["tabernacle"]{.class_s36N} of this passage is the body (2 Pet. 1:13). It is an ["earthly house,"]{.class_s36N} like a mud hut, that will one day be ["dissolved"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1). When that happens, Paul says, ["we know\...we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens."]{.class_s36N} We move out of this house of [clay]{.class_s36M}, and we move into a[ new house]{.class_s36M}, built by God, that will last forever. The transition is immediate at death (Phil. 1:21--23), and that ["house"]{.class_s36N} is preferable, according to Paul (vs. 8). So this house is not the resurrection body of 1 Corinthians 15. Rather this house is a "soulish" body, awaiting the resurrection. God has some kind of a covering for me up in heaven [between ]{.class_s36M}death and the resurrection.

Verse 2 should be the desire of every Christian. Paul says, ["having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is FAR BETTER"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 1:23). Just think of being able to live in a place where you never have to worry about what you think. Anything you want to do is all right. You can go anywhere you want to go. You never have to check yourself to see if you are in the wrong. You are finally in a place where the World, the Flesh, and the Devil can no longer hurt you or affect your relationship to the Lord. No wonder Paul said, ["we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed upon with or house which is from heaven"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2).

Verse 3 implies that a Christian [could]{.class_s36M} be found naked. Christ tells the Laodicean Christians to get ["white raiment"]{.class_s36N} from Him in order to cover their nakedness (Rev. 3:18). In Revelation 16:15, the Lord says, ["Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked."]{.class_s36N} I read in Revelation 19:8 that ["fine linen is the righteousness of saints."]{.class_s36N}

Now, I don't profess to understand all of that. I've heard all kinds of expositions on it, and I've read all kinds of commentaries on it. What it looks like is that at the time you get your [spiritual body]{.class_s36M} in heaven, that it is clothed with [fine linen]{.class_s36M} made from your own good works [after ]{.class_s36M}salvation. While you are down here working for the Lord, [you are sewing you a suit of clothes for your spiritual body]{.class_s36M}. Some Christians are going to be better clothed than others.

I know, [doctrinally]{.class_s36M}, that when you are saved you are given [God's righteousness]{.class_s36M}, and I know that, before salvation, ["all our righteousnesses are as filthy rags"]{.class_s36N} (Isa. 64:6). But [after ]{.class_s36M}salvation, you are ["created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 2:10). We are commanded to ["be careful to maintain good works:]{.class_s36N} (Titus 3:8). One passage says, ["he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever."]{.class_s36N} Do you know where that is found? It is in 2 Corinthians 9:9, a New Testament, Pauline epistle under grace. So in the life of a Christian, you not only have your [doctrinal standing ]{.class_s36M}in the righteousness of Christ, but you also have a [practical righteousness]{.class_s36M} which you practise [in this body.]{.class_s36M} When you get to heaven, it will be clothing for your spiritual body. How nice your clothes are depends on what you do, down here, out of a heart of love for Christ and His saints.

In verse 4, Paul is saying, "I don't wish to die, if that is all there is to it, but there is more to death than just dying. I get a new suit of clothes that won't wear out." Paul said he groaned for that new body; he was burdened about it. Imagine having no more backaches, headaches, kidney stones, or gall stones; no more glasses, hearing aids, or artificial limbs; no more pills, therapy, or treatments. You won't have to worry about getting old or sick. If you are a genuine Christian, that is why you are groaning; that is why you are burdened. [You want to get out of this mess!]{.class_s36M} Sometimes you just want to cry out like Paul, ["Who shall deliver me from the body of this death? I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 7:24--25).

A certain Dr. Stearns, while dying, said, "I'm homesick for heaven; quit praying and let me go."

Compare that attitude with poor Bertrand Russell who wrote, "The life of man is a long march through the night, tortured by weariness and pain, toward a goal that few can hope to reach and where none may tarry," [Man's Free World.]{.class_s36M}

[He was one of the world's most tragic and disgraceful, mentally handicapped nuts.]{.class_s36M} Too many goofballs.[ He was nuttier than a pecan pie.]{.class_s36M}

If you want to see the difference between a sane Bible believer and a cockeyed, British, atheistic [nut]{.class_s36M}, compare Bertrand's description of life with the one Paul gave in 2 Corinthians 2:14 and Romans 8:31--39. My, my, my, what a contrast!

5:5 Now he that hath wrought us for the selfsame thing is God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the Spirit.

6 Therefore we are always confident, knowing that, whilst we are at home in the body, we are absent from the Lord:

7 (For we walk by faith, not by sight:)

8 We are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body, and to be present with the Lord.

9 Wherefore we labour, that, whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him.

The Lord made you for the new body he has prepared for you (vs. 5).[ A Christian's destination is fixed.]{.class_s36M}

I have already discussed ["the earnest of the Spirit"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5) under 2 Corinthians 1:22. But in relation to your new body in heaven, let me just add that the Holy Spirit [in you]{.class_s36M} is God's proof that He will complete His work [in you]{.class_s36M}. The new spirit which He birthed in you at your conversion will result in a new [spiritual ]{.class_s36M}body in heaven and eventually a new [physical ]{.class_s36M}body at the resurrection. The Holy Spirit constantly bears witness to the fact that death is not the end for a Christian.

The longer that you have been saved, the clearer this becomes. If an older Christian stays in fellowship with the Lord, he has a tremendous advantage in that the closer he gets to death, the brighter the light on the other side becomes and the clearer he sees. The older you may get on the outside, the younger you get on the [inside]{.class_s36M}. The renewal on the [inside ]{.class_s36M}just keeps picking up and gathering momentum until someday, BLAM!; you're in a new body, brother. The Lord just gives you a foretaste. It is like Eliezer (a type of the Holy Spirit) giving the jewels of silver and gold to Rebekah. That was the "earnest" of the riches of Isaac.

Note in verse 6 that the Christian is confident of both his present standing in Christ, by faith, with the Holy Spirit living inside him, never to leave (vss. 5--7), [and ]{.class_s36M}his future state, in person, before the Lord, forever (vs. 8). Philippians 1:6 [combines ]{.class_s36M}both of these states: ["Being confident of this very thing, that he which hath begun a good work ]{.class_s36N}\[present state\][ in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ ]{.class_s36N}\[future state\][."]{.class_s36N} A Christian can [know]{.class_s36M} where he is going when he dies because he has the Holy Spirit living in him and telling him so.

In regards to verse 7, I am reminded of the words of Christ to Thomas in John 20:29, ["Because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed: blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed."]{.class_s36N} Peter says, ["Whom having not seen, ye love; in whom, though now ye see him not, yet believing, ye rejoice with you unspeakable and full of glory]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 1:8).

I wish sometimes when I give an invitation that I could actually place the Lord right in front of the congregation so that they could [see]{.class_s36M} Him. But I can't. You will either see Him by the Holy Spirit's revelation through His Book or you will not.

Now, when we are in the body, we are [physically ]{.class_s36M}separated from the Lord. [Spiritually]{.class_s36M}, we are never apart. ["He that is joined unto the Lord is one spirit"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 6:17). And Ephesians 5:30, ["For we are members of his body, of his flesh, and of his bones."]{.class_s36N} But [geographically]{.class_s36M}, Christ's body is in heaven seated at the right hand of God the Father, and my body is down [here ]{.class_s36M}on earth. We don't walk hand in hand down the street and all of that Satanic stuff. The Christian walks ["by faith, not by sight."]{.class_s36N}

But if we had our choice, we would have to say with Paul that we would ["rather to be absent from the body, and to be present with the Lord."]{.class_s36N} Then every Christian has [natural]{.class_s36M}, innate, suicidal tendencies. Every born-again child of God would rather go home to be with his Lord than stay down here in this mess.

Paul said, ["For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 1:21). For a true Christian, death is a [benefit]{.class_s36M}; in fact Paul said it was ["far better"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 1:23). In Philippians 1:23, Paul was torn between staying alive and helping his Philippian converts and going home to be with the Lord.

If a man doesn't [know ]{.class_s36M}that he is saved, he can't say that. You take some numbskull who is always running to Acts 2:38 and keeps trying to "Repent, Believe, Confess, and be Bup-tized," the fellow doesn't have breathing sense. He goes through all of that and [still ]{.class_s36M}doesn't know he is saved. One poor woman at the "Brownsville Revival" got "saved" six times and still had no assurance of heaven when she got through.

To people like that, death isn't a gain; it's a [gamble]{.class_s36M}. They may or may not make it. You have never heard a Roman Catholic say, ["To die is gain,"]{.class_s36N} unless he is believing[ contrary to what his religion teaches.]{.class_s36M} "Purgatory" is no gain!

I don't mean to be critical of folks' religion. I'm just saying that unless you are born again, a saved child of God, [you can't say what Paul said. ]{.class_s36M}Only a saved, born-again child of God can say that and tell the [truth]{.class_s36M}. If there is some professing Christian reading this commentary who does not know[ for sure]{.class_s36M} that you have a home in heaven when you die, you had better take whatever Scripture you are using to convince yourself you can lose it and leave it alone and start learning some real Bible.

[A Christian knows where he is going when he dies. ]{.class_s36M}Paul knew, Peter knew, John knew. If you [don't,]{.class_s36M} then you are not following the three greatest apostles in the New Testament. And not only does a Christian know where he is going, [he wants to go]{.class_s36M}, and he is in a hurry to get there. It is an improvement over [anything]{.class_s36M} down here.

Now, until that time, ["we labour, that\...we may be accepted of him"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). He doesn't mean ["accepted"]{.class_s36N} in the sense of salvation. He is saying that the Christian wants God to accept [his works ]{.class_s36S}for the Lord. ["For to me to live is Christ."]{.class_s36N} While we are down here on earth, we, as Christians, are to be ["always abounding in the work of the Lord"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 15:58) so that one day we can hear Him say, ["Well done, thou good and faithful servant."]{.class_s36N}

5:10 For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ; that every one may receive the things done in his body, according to that he hath done, whether it be good or bad.

11 Knowing therefore the terror of the Lord, we persuade men; but we are made manifest unto God; and I trust also are made manifest in your consciences.

12 For we commend not ourselves again unto you, but give you occasion to glory on our behalf, that ye may have somewhat to answer them which glory in appearance, and not in heart.

13 For whether we be beside ourselves, it is to God: or whether we be sober, it is for your cause.

["The judgment seat of Christ"]{.class_s36N} is the same judgment as Romans 14:10. Note there that the judgment is a judgment of brothers in Christ, so it is [not ]{.class_s36M}the Great White Throne Judgment. The new versions change that judgment in Romans 14:10 to "the judgment seat of God," [which is a reference to NOTHING]{.class_s36M}. The Lord Jesus Christ is just as much God at the Judgment of the Nations as He is at the Great White Throne Judgment. So all the new Bibles do is spread confusion.

This judgment of the Christians is described in 1 Corinthians 3:11--15, and it determines the Christian's reward for his life of service for Christ. In verse 10 in the passage before us, the judgment is made so ["that every one may receive the things done in his body."]{.class_s36N} If a work is ["good" ]{.class_s36N}(vs. 10), then it will ["abide,"]{.class_s36N} and ["he shall receive a reward"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 3:14). That reward will be purified gold, silver, and precious stones (1 Cor. 3:12) and will be given to him in the form of crowns.

If the work is ["bad"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10), then it will be ["burned,"]{.class_s36N} and ["he shall suffer loss: but he himself SHALL BE SAVED"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 3:15). So [bad works]{.class_s36M} in this life are [lost rewards]{.class_s36M} for the Christian at the Judgment Seat of Christ. It will be the equivalent of coming home and finding your house burned to the ground and you have no insurance. You're alive, but everything that gave life joy is gone.

There are Christians, throughout America, who will get home to heaven, only to receive [a bonfire]{.class_s36M} at the Judgment Seat of Christ. Motive of service will have a lot to do with it. Their church attendance was to please the family or to soothe their consciences. They gave for the tax deductions from the government. They have a whole shelf of Bible translations and don't [believe ]{.class_s36M}one of them. They kept their worldly companions and never led a soul to Christ the whole time they were here. All that those Christians are headed for is a [bonfire]{.class_s36M}.

If every Christian since the time of Christ had done what he should have done and led someone to Christ every year, and then those new Christians had done the same thing, the whole world would have been converted before Christopher Columbus. Why do we have to have paid ministers to go out and win souls? [Every Christian ought to win souls!]{.class_s36M}

Christianity is an awful lot like the great orange festival that was held once in South Florida. The purpose of the festival was to pick the biggest orange crop ever. It was advertised all over town on posters and billboards and on radio and television. The goal was to break all of the attendance records for [picking oranges.]{.class_s36M}

When the day came, the road to the orange grove was jammed with cars. Thousands of people came out and ushers would greet them and seat them. A big band paraded around, and pretty soon the Master of Ceremonies mounted the platform. He led in prayer, and then an offering was taken up for orange picking. Behind the platform was a grove of hundreds of orange trees with thousands of sun-ripened oranges just waiting to be picked. Then with a blast of trumpets and fanfare from the band, the M.C. stepped up to the first tree and very solemnly [plucked an orange off the limb.]{.class_s36M}

A cheer went up from the crowd. Everybody hooped and hollered and clapped and shouted. Then the M.C. dismissed in prayer, and [everyone went home.]{.class_s36M}

Now, that's just a story, but there's a lot more truth than fiction to it. When the fields are "ripe to harvest," you don't have to spend all of that money just to pick [one orange]{.class_s36M}. Billy Graham held a crusade, one time, that cost about \$157,000. About twenty people got saved. [That is \$7,850 to save one soul.]{.class_s36M} I'm sorry, but any time you need to spend \$7,850 to get [one soul]{.class_s36M} saved, you should be locked in a looney bin. One of the street preachers who went out from Bible Baptist, Bill Eubanks, has had better results with tract evangelism crusades wherever he goes, and the cost has come nowhere near \$157,000.

Note again that the context of this passage is the Judgment Seat of Christ. ["The terror of the Lord,"]{.class_s36N} then, is [not ]{.class_s36M}a warning to [unsaved sinners]{.class_s36M} about hell. It is a warning to Christians to live right. The modern commentators and expositors don't believe that, but then again, they are rather backslidden themselves. No wonder they want to pass the ["terror"]{.class_s36N} on to [someone else.]{.class_s36M}

The Judgment Seat of Christ is going to be a terror to some [Christians]{.class_s36M}. Paul prayed for Onesiphorus, in 2 Timothy 1:18, that God would show him [mercy ]{.class_s36M}at the Judgment Seat of Christ because Onesiphorus had helped him while he was imprisoned at Rome. So though you are not in danger of going to Hell at the Judgment Seat of Christ, there is something about it that brings terror, and when that time comes you will wish to God that there was some good work to which God could look and show you [mercy ]{.class_s36M}because of it.

Knowing that, Paul persuaded men to live right. Contrary to what the Charismatics say about us, we Baptists do [not]{.class_s36M} believe "once saved, always saved; so go out and live like the devil" or "I'm saved so I can live any way I want to." Baptists teach that if a man is saved by grace through faith in the shed blood of Jesus Christ on the cross that God will get him to heaven [no matter what happens.]{.class_s36M} The journey home can be good or rough. If he doesn't live right and behave himself, then he can get [weak ]{.class_s36M}and [sickly]{.class_s36M}, and the Lord can [kill ]{.class_s36M}him ahead of time. He can lose his testimony, his character, his possessions, his life, his inheritance, and his rewards.

The terror of the Judgment Seat may be the shame of going through it. I have painted it for the [Apocalypse,]{.class_s36M} but still, we have no idea, for sure, exactly what it will be like. Can you imagine getting to heaven and having the Lord reel out your life just like an unsaved man? Out would come everything you have done, said, or thought since you got saved. Then the Lord would begin detailing everything you had done wrong, and you would have to stand there in humiliation knowing what you [could ]{.class_s36M}have done and [should ]{.class_s36M}have done, but didn't do. When Simon Peter denied the Lord the night before the crucifixion, all the Lord did to him was turn and look at him, and Peter ["went out, and wept bitterly"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 26:75). If that is all it took for Peter, think about how you will feel before Christ during the Judgment Seat. That will be bad enough.

["For we commend not ourselves again unto you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12). Back in verse 11, Paul said, ["we are made manifest unto God; and I trust also are made manifest in your consciences."]{.class_s36N} In other words, God saw that what Paul and his companions did was right, and the consciences of the Corinthians should have told them the same thing. Given that, then, he was not going to try to recommend themselves all over again to the Corinthians. Anything Paul said on his own behalf was so that they could answer those heretics and hypocrites who attacked the Corinthians by bad mouthing Paul (2 Cor. 11:4--19). To those who ["glory in appearance,"]{.class_s36N} Paul had the [real life experience]{.class_s36M} to back up what he said. So when Paul seems to be bragging, it is only to give the Corinthians what they need to shut the mouths of those to whom [appearance ]{.class_s36M}was so important.

[Appearance is nothing. ]{.class_s36M}Big buildings and nice school grounds aren't proof of spirituality. If you are spiritual, then you are spiritual whether you are rich or poor as Job's turkey. Saul's big church service in 1 Samuel 15:31 was only to [hide ]{.class_s36M}the fact that he had [disobeyed God]{.class_s36M} (1 Sam. 15:22--23). ["Man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart"]{.class_s36N} (1 Sam. 16:7).

When it came to what the Corinthians thought of him, Paul could care less, as long as they realized that what he did, he did out of love for the Lord and for them as his converts. So whether they thought he was crazy (["whether we be beside ourselves"]{.class_s36N}---vs. 13) or whether he was serious as a heart attack, ["it is to God\...it is for your cause."]{.class_s36N} Paul does just as Jesus did when dealing with the unprofitable servant in Luke 19:22---he allows for false accusation and then slams home the truth on top of it.

5:14 For the love of Christ constraineth us; because we thus judge, that if one died for all, then were all dead:

15 And that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them, and rose again.

16 Wherefore henceforth know we no man after the flesh: yea, though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we him no more.

17 Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new.

Now, what Paul did, he did because Christ loved him and those to whom he preached and because he loved Christ for what He had done for him. ["The love of Christ constraineth us"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14). Note that the love of God is directly connected with the atonement:[ "one died for all."]{.class_s36N} The whole world was ["dead in trespasses and sins"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 2:1). So acting out of love for His creation, ["Christ died for us"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 5:8; John 3:16). The problem is that sinners think that God loves them "unconditionally" (I believe that is the modern, psychological "buzz word"), but the only place you will find the "unconditional" love of God is [at Calvary]{.class_s36M} (see [God is Love,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, 1996). And if you won't go [there ]{.class_s36M}and fall at the foot of the cross, then you are under the unsheathed [wrath of God]{.class_s36M} (John 3:36).

Verse 15 is one of those verses in the New Testament that proves Calvin's doctrine of [limited atonement]{.class_s36M} (Christ died only for the elect) is so much bunk. ["He died for all;"]{.class_s36N} that's [universal.]{.class_s36M}[ "They which live"]{.class_s36N}---not everyone will receive Him. So Christ died not only for those who receive Him, but for those who reject Him as well.

["Henceforth know we no man after the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16). The fellowship between Christians should never be of a fleshy nature. Christians may get together and eat, drink, and chat; but that is not true Christian fellowship. True Christian fellowship revolves around the [person of Christ]{.class_s36M} and the [Scriptures ]{.class_s36M}(1 John 1:3--4). The fellowship of Christians is not even in buildings, buses, brochures, quartets, choirs, curriculum, numbers, etc. That is still flesh. It is the physical creation under a curse and the Christian life is not about the physical. The measure of a man is [not]{.class_s36M} the outward appearance.

The physical body will die. [The new life in Christ is spiritual.]{.class_s36M} There is no need to get to know a corpse. So the local assembly exists not for entertainment or socializing, but ["for the perfecting of the saints, for the work of the ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 4:12).

["Though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we him no more."]{.class_s36N} The Bible-believing Christian is no longer occupied with the life of Christ in the flesh. "WWJD?" (Charles Sheldon's "What Would Jesus Do?") is the mark of a [carnal Christians]{.class_s36M} who is trying to decide with his own brains and "feelings," instead of Scripture, what a Christian should do.

Now don't get me wrong. I thank God for [anyone ]{.class_s36M}who truly gets saved through the [Jesus]{.class_s36M} film; some of our own missionaries have used it with some success. I [preach]{.class_s36M} from the Gospels. We teach the Life of Christ class at PBI to acquaint the students with the principal facts of Christ's [earthly ministry]{.class_s36M}. That's Bible teaching; that's Bible preaching; that's evangelism. But we are not occupying ourselves with the Jewish Messiah who went around working miracles as signs to Israel. [That ]{.class_s36M}is where the Charismatics waste their time. We aren't fooling around with Jesus of Nazareth, the lowly Galilean, the Man from Galilee, baby Jesus, or the Sermon on the Mount.

We have a [living Lord in heaven]{.class_s36M}, seated at the right hand of God the Father, interceding on our behalf, waiting for the day when He will [receive us to Himself.]{.class_s36M} He is in us and we are in Him. We are part of His Body, and He is the Head. [We are bone of His bones and flesh of His flesh.]{.class_s36M} His Spirit reached down through the universe, past the galaxies and constellations and star clusters and planets, and reaches into my body.

He is not walking beside me, holding my hand, along side the Sea of Galilee. [He lives in me. ]{.class_s36M}He walks [in]{.class_s36M} me. He leads me from the [inside.]{.class_s36M} I am His and He is mine. The Bible says, ["Christ in you, the hope of glory"]{.class_s36N} (Col. 1:27).

I must admit that I am guilty of relating to people ["after the flesh."]{.class_s36N} Either I am looking at people as a soldier would look at them or I look at them through the eyes of an [artist]{.class_s36M}. As a [trained infantryman,]{.class_s36M} I see men as targets. I am always sizing people up as to their potential threat. I have never had anyone jump me yet, but if they [do]{.class_s36M}, I'm ready!

As an artist, I have a hard time seeing somebody in any other way than the way he looks. An artist is preoccupied with [how to draw what he sees.]{.class_s36M} I never pass by anybody without making a decision about them based on the way they look. It's a bad habit into which to get. Nine times out of ten you make the wrong decision anyway. So I go by the maxim that a thing is almost always the [opposite ]{.class_s36M}of the way it [appears]{.class_s36M}. You won't miss too many times that way.

But a Christian can't look at things that way. When dealing with people, a Christian should remember that each person is [a soul ]{.class_s36M}for which Christ died. That's what makes a good soulwinner. A good personal worker sees people as [souls ]{.class_s36M}instead of [persons]{.class_s36M}. He doesn't get sidetracked by a person's weight, height, build, sex, or color. That is what made Jack Hyles so successful as a soulwinner. He took a personal interest in [souls]{.class_s36M}; He was [soul-conscious. ]{.class_s36M}If you are soul-conscious, you will win souls.

Verse 16 is essential to understanding verse 17. The ["old things"]{.class_s36N} that ["passed away"]{.class_s36N} at conversion has to do with the acquaintance to those things. At salvation, a believer should have a new outlook on things, a [spiritual outlook]{.class_s36M}, that he did not have before. The natural man is ["in the flesh,"]{.class_s36N} and he minds ["the things of the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:5--8), but the Christian is ["in the Spirit,"]{.class_s36N} because He is indwelt by the Holy Spirit of God (Rom. 8:9).

We come now to verse 17. It is one of those verses that every Christian should memorize. It is a great verse to use in personal work to check out the time of a man's salvation. It is excellent in dealing with Catholics who don't have the "foggiest" idea about WHEN they became ["a new creature"]{.class_s36N} in Christ.

If you ever want to hear something ridiculous, get some pope to tell you WHEN [he]{.class_s36M} became ["a new creature"]{.class_s36N} and [knew it]{.class_s36M} by the way [his whole attitude changed]{.class_s36M} toward the Bible, real prayer, tract distribution, wine, nuns, prestige, humility, telling the truth, street preaching, preaching the Gospel, or lying to the press. [That]{.class_s36M} would be the day!

Now, what does it mean to become ["a new creature"]{.class_s36N} in Christ. Well, it does [not]{.class_s36M} mean that [all]{.class_s36M} of your [tastes ]{.class_s36M}and [desires ]{.class_s36M}have changed. Just because you liked iced tea before you were saved doesn't mean that you can't like iced tea after you have been saved. Not even all of the [sinful attitudes ]{.class_s36M}change. I have known Christians who still had a battle with cigarettes and alcohol after they got saved.

Before I was saved, I used to smoke cigars. I can take you to the very street corner in downtown Pensacola where I smoked my last cigar. [I haven't smoked another one since.]{.class_s36M} But I must admit that, after a big smorgasbord dinner, I have gone by the cash register and have looked down at the case of cigars, and the old desire comes back. I haven't bought one yet, but they still appeal to the flesh.

So becoming ["a new creature"]{.class_s36N} doesn't mean that the old, fleshy desires are all [gone]{.class_s36M}. It doesn't mean that you [stop sinning]{.class_s36M}. And it doesn't guarantee that you will always do right (Rom. 7:14--18).

What it does mean is that there is something in you that was not there before. It's a new nature, a new way of looking at things. [It's a spiritual nature.]{.class_s36M}

All of a sudden you have a stronger[ conscience about your sin. ]{.class_s36M}What used not to bother you a hundred times before, suddenly torments the life out of you. [That is a good proof that you are saved.]{.class_s36M} That is the new man inside of you resenting what's going on.
