If a man can't point to a place in his life, somewhere, where he began to love the things that he[ didn't]{.class_s36M} love before and to [hate ]{.class_s36M}the things that he did [love ]{.class_s36M}before, that is pretty good evidence that he is not ["a new creature."]{.class_s36N} I don't say that it is infallible, but it is pretty good evidence.

If you are saved, there should have been a time in your life, somewhere, when you began to appreciate Bible study and when you began to [enjoy ]{.class_s36M}going to church and singing hymns. There should have been a time in your life when you developed a [distaste]{.class_s36M} for tobacco, liquor, and wild women. Some of that stuff may flare up once in a while, and it will be bad or worse with you, depending on how far you get from the Lord. But if you can point to a time in your life when those things began to [change]{.class_s36M}, that is pretty good evidence that you are saved.

One of the best ways to deal with people like Roman Catholics, who have been raised in the system, is with 2 Corinthians 5:17. When a Catholic says, "I receive Jesus Christ every Sunday"; then you answer back, "Fine, can you tell me of a time in your life when [old things passed away]{.class_s36M} and all things became [new]{.class_s36M}"? [There will not be one Catholic out of a hundred who could point to a place in his life when he stopped loving bingo and started loving the King James Bible.]{.class_s36M}

5:18 And all things are of God, who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation;

19 To wit, that God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them; and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation.

20 Now then we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God did beseech you by us: we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God.

The ["all things"]{.class_s36N} of verse 18 are the ["all things are become new"]{.class_s36N} of verse 17. So all of the [new things]{.class_s36M} in the Christian life, the spiritual things, are from God.

The theme of this passage is [reconciliation]{.class_s36M}. Reconciliation is to bring two disputing parties together. Before salvation, we are "at odds" with the Lord. The Lord wanted reconciliation between us and Him, so He sent His Son Jesus Christ to make that reconciliation.

Jesus was 100 percent man, so He understood man's weaknesses and frailties. He was 100 percent God, so He understood God's holiness and righteousness.

The story is told of a man and his son who were on the "outs" with each other. The conflict became so bad that neither one would talk to the other, and finally the son left home. One day the son received a telegram that his mother lay dying, and if he ever wanted to see her alive again, he needed to return home right away. So the son came to the bedside of his dying mother, and on the other side of the bed stood his father. Still neither of them would speak to the other. The wife pleaded with them to make things right, and though she begged them with tears, both of them stood beside the bed and said nothing. Finally, with her dying breath, she took the hand of her husband on one side and the hand of her son on the other and brought them both to her bosom and died. The two stood there a moment with their hands clutched to the breast of the dead woman. Then they burst into tears and got right with each other.

[That is a picture of what Christ did for us.]{.class_s36M} On the cross, He reached out to God with one hand and to us with the other, and He brought them together in Himself at His death.

Verse 19 says, ["God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them."]{.class_s36N} If God had charged all of this world's sins to it,[ this world would be burned to a crisp. ]{.class_s36M}But God gives the peoples of this world the chance to be saved.

Now, the Christian has a part in that each of us has been ["given\...the ministry of reconciliation"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 18), and ["the word of reconciliation"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 19) has been ["committed unto us."]{.class_s36N} At Calvary, Christ did everything possible to reconcile sinful man to God. Upon returning to heaven, He left the job of telling the world [how]{.class_s36M} to be reconciled to God. That is why we are called ["ambassadors for Christ."]{.class_s36N}

The Christian stands in [Christ's place]{.class_s36M} in this world. Each of us who have trusted Him are His representatives here on earth. We have no need of some rascal in a Halloween costume to tell us that he is forgiving in the person of Christ. Every Christian is a "vicar of Christ," a substitute for Christ. The very word "Christian" means "little Christ." That is a legitimate usage as long as the Lord Jesus Christ does indeed live in you.

Now, note: if we are to ["pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 20), then the reconciliation has been accomplished, but it is not [automatic]{.class_s36M}. Back in 1968, the Presbyterian assembly put up a new creed of confession where they said that the death of Christ on the cross [automatically ]{.class_s36M}reconciled all institutions and people to God; now all we have to do is work it out. [That is not true. ]{.class_s36M}If it were automatic (unconditional election, limited atonement, irresistible grace!), then there would be no need for anyone to ["beseech"]{.class_s36N} anyone else.

5:21 For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.

This is a great verse. Not only did Christ [bear ]{.class_s36M}our sins on the cross so that we ["should live unto righteousness"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 2:24), but God actually made the Lord Jesus ["to be sin for us."]{.class_s36N} So closely was Christ identified with sin on the cross that He likens Himself to Satan,[ the serpent on the pole]{.class_s36M} (John 3:14).

On the cross, Christ not only suffered humiliation, shame, and agony, [but God turned Him into a sin offering. ]{.class_s36M}He became sin [personified]{.class_s36M}, and the wrath of God was poured out on Him as it will be poured out on Satan. Jesus paid for every sin that has ever been committed (including the Devil's) from eternity to eternity.

[That is the heart of the atonement.]{.class_s36M} John R. Rice and Curtis Hutson missed it. Dear, old, sweet, "godly" Dr. John decided that Jesus Christ did NOT drink ["the cup"]{.class_s36N} in Gethsemane or later (John 18:11), because ["the cup"]{.class_s36N} was defined in Psalm 75:8 and Jeremiah 25:15--17 as God's wrath poured out on sin. [Christ certainly did drink it]{.class_s36M} (John 18:11)! Both Rice and Hutson were evangelists! When it came to Biblical "scholarship," neither of them even made good STUDENTS.

Christ became ["a curse for us"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 3:13) and drank the wrath of God, poured out against sin, to its fullest!

[]{#c2BT.xhtml}

## Chapter 6 {.heading_s39}

6:1 We then, as workers together with him, beseech you also that ye receive not the grace of God in vain.

2 (For he saith, I have heard thee in a time accepted, and in the day of salvation have I succoured thee: behold, now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation.)

In verse 1, a Christian is a [worker]{.class_s36M}. We are not saved to [sit]{.class_s36M}; we are saved to [serve]{.class_s36M}. ["We are labourers together with God:]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 3:9); we are fellow- labourers with each other (1 Thess. 3:2; Phil. 4:3). Although a Christian is saved by grace through faith, [after ]{.class_s36M}salvation he ought to work. In the New Testament, the Christian worker is likened to a husbandman who keeps a vineyard (2 Tim. 2:6) and a builder who lays a foundation (1 Cor. 3:10).

Verse 1 goes on to say, ["we\...beseech you also that ye receive not the grace of God in vain."]{.class_s36N} Remember that when Paul wrote to a local church, he addressed both the [saved ]{.class_s36M}and the [lost ]{.class_s36M}in a congregation. If verse 1 is addressed to the lost, then he is saying, "I hope that you truly received the grace of God and are really saved." If the verse is aimed at the saved. Then he is saying, "I hope that now that you are saved, that it is going to do something for you." It is possible for a fellow to be saved, and that is it. A lot of Baptists are that way. They get saved; they receive the grace of God, but they never amount to anything for God. So they receive ["the grace of God in vain"]{.class_s36N} in that it never results in anything in this life.

Verse 2 is a quote from Isaiah 49:8. But notice the free application which Paul makes from the verse. The promise of Isaiah 49:8 was made to Jesus Christ, but Paul applies it[ to sinners]{.class_s36M}. He does the same thing again down in verses 17--18: he takes an Old Testament prophecy aimed at Israel and applies it to the Christian.

The Bereans (Stam and the [Searchlight]{.class_s36M}) and the rest of the Dry Cleaners (Hyperdispensationalists) never "got on" to this method of teaching and preaching. Consequently, they ceased to be "Pauline" or to follow Paul in their [actual ministries.]{.class_s36M}

Now, verse 2 is typically used by personal workers to show an individual sinner that the time to get saved is [right now.]{.class_s36M} That is true, and there is nothing wrong with using the verse that way. But verse 2 is actually saying that this current dispensation is ["the day of salvation"]{.class_s36N} in a peculiar sense that it has never been at any other time.

This dispensation, in which you are living, cannot properly be called the Dispensation of Grace. Grace, [as a period of time]{.class_s36M}, stretches from Genesis 6:8 to Revelation 22:21. Yet, this dispensation is marked more by [grace ]{.class_s36M}than any other thing. It is the dispensation where a man is saved by grace through faith apart from works. There is a real sense in which the period of time from Pentecost to the Rapture is ["the day of salvation,"]{.class_s36N} because it is[ the one period of time]{.class_s36M} when you don't have to do [anything]{.class_s36M} to get saved or stay saved. [There is no other period like it.]{.class_s36M}

If you lived before the time of Christ, you would be in a sweat morning, noon, and night, wondering whether you were saved or not. You really wouldn't know until you were dead. But ["the day of salvation,"]{.class_s36N} now, is ["the accepted time"]{.class_s36N} of 2 Corinthians 5:20--21. Get saved now while the grace of God is being freely offered.

6:3 Giving no offence in any thing, that the ministry be not blamed:

4 But in all things approving ourselves as the ministers of God, in much patience, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses,

5 In stripes, in imprisonments, in tumults, in labours, in watchings, in fastings;

6 By pureness, by knowledge, by longsuffering, by kindness, by the Holy Ghost, by love unfeigned,

7 By the word of truth, by the power of God, by the armour of righteousness on the right hand and on the left,

8 By honour and dishonour, by evil report and good report: as deceivers, and yet true;

9 As unknown, and yet well known; as dying, and, behold, we live; as chastened, and not killed;

10 As sorrowful, yet alway rejoicing; as poor, yet making many rich; as having nothing, and yet possessing all things.

["Giving no offence in any thing, that the ministry be not blamed"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3). The idea here is that, in our service for the Lord, we should give no real offense so that the ministry will be [legitimately ]{.class_s36M}blamed. Now, the ministry [will be blamed.]{.class_s36M} Look at verse 8: ["dishonour\...evil report\...deceivers."]{.class_s36N} The world hates you and will find any excuse to criticize you (1 John 3:13), but make sure that you ["suffer as a Christian"]{.class_s36N} for doing right and not ["as an evil doer"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 4:15--16).

Starting with verse 4 Paul gives the job description for a Christian minister. A minister should have a lot of patience (["in much patience"]{.class_s36N}). He should have gone through some trouble (["in afflictions"]{.class_s36N}). He should be able to go through life without always having everything he needs (["in necessities"]{.class_s36N}); someday he will be ministering to someone in the same predicament (2 Cor. 1:4). He should be able to deal with [real]{.class_s36M} crises (["in distresses"]{.class_s36N}).

Verse 5 gets a little rougher: ["In stripes, in imprisonments."]{.class_s36N} Well, I haven't got any stripes, and I have never gone to jail for Jesus Christ. Lester Roloff did and Pastor Sileven out in Nebraska did, but most preachers in America have not gone to jail or prison for the cause of Christ.

What if a fellow was only ordained to the ministry [after]{.class_s36M} he had been beaten for Christ. You might have a lot of pastors in places like China, Sudan, and the former Soviet Union; but that sure would cut down the ministry here in America.

Did you ever stop to think what it would be like if Paul walked in Monday morning to the local ministerial association? All of those lame ducks would be sitting there with their starched collars, pressed suits, and polished shoes. Then in walks this fellow soaked in [blood]{.class_s36M}, with [bruises ]{.class_s36M}and [stripes ]{.class_s36M}all over his body. His clothes are sticking to him, and some of his [teeth ]{.class_s36M}have been knocked out. He takes one look at that room of [stuffed shirts]{.class_s36M} and says, "Where did you birds preach yesterday?!"

Now don't worry! [Our time is coming]{.class_s36M}. We've got "hope." If the Lord tarries, the axe is going to fall in America sooner or later. But in the meantime, if you really want to fulfill that part of verse 5, I know of some really choice places you can go where you won't have to put forth much effort.

Verse 5: ["In tumults."]{.class_s36N} Those are violent arguments and riots like the apostle went through at Thessalonica (Acts 17) and Ephesus (Acts 19). ["In labours."]{.class_s36N} A minister should be able to work to support himself if need be (1 Cor. 4:12). But also, that could refer to laboring in the word (1 Tim. 5:17) and in prayer (Acts 6:4). Real prayer is labor. It caused Christ to sweat great drops of blood in Gethsemane (Luke 22:44).

["In watchings."]{.class_s36N} See the comments under 1 Corinthians 16:13. ["In fastings."]{.class_s36N} There is nothing wrong with fasting. It is a New Testament practise. If you're overweight, then it is probably a good idea to take a couple of days off from eating.

All of these things are characteristics of [the ministry]{.class_s36M}. The ministry consists of keeping yourself pure (vs. 6 cf. 1 Tim. 5:22). It's getting the right kind of knowledge: about the Lord, about yourself, and about others. It is putting up with things (["by longsuffering"]{.class_s36N}---vs. 6); it is showing kindness to those who mistreat you. It is ministering by the power and authority of the Holy Spirit. It is loving people without pretense (["by love unfeigned:]{.class_s36N}---vs. 6). It is [preaching ]{.class_s36M}the[ word of God]{.class_s36M} ["by the power of God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). It is putting ["on the whole armour of God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7 cf. Eph. 6:13).

[The ministry]{.class_s36M} is also a series of contrasts. ["By honour and dishonour"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 8). You will have people in your ministry who love you and respect you. Then there will be those who have no use for you at all. Just be careful about [who ]{.class_s36M}gives you honor. Respect from the wrong crowd doesn't speak well of your ministry.

["By evil report and good report."]{.class_s36N} You will have those who bad mouth you and those who speak well of you. Don't brood over the former, and don't get puffed up by the latter. ["As deceivers, and yet true."]{.class_s36N} Your goal is to be true and honest towards others, but not everyone will see [you ]{.class_s36M}that way. Thanks to modern TV evangelists, there will always be those who view every preacher as an "Elmer Gantry."

["As unknown, and yet well known"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). Now, don't be like R. L. Hymers out in California. Your goal is not to "hot dog" it so that you're grabbing the limelight every chance you get. Don't go around looking for publicity. If you are doing what you are supposed to be doing, don't worry; people know who you are! (See our comments about Hugh Pyle under 2 Cor. 4:17.)

["As dying, and behold, we live."]{.class_s36N} That ["dying"]{.class_s36N} is giving up worldly pleasures; it is sacrificing the desires of self. In [the ministry]{.class_s36M}, you will be making sacrifice after sacrifice. When you do that, remember this: God is keeping track of every sacrifice you make for Him, and He won't let any sacrifice go unrewarded.

["As chastened, and not killed."]{.class_s36N} When the chastening comes, you have to take solace in Hebrews 12:5--11. God chastens out of love; He chastens because you're His son; He chastens so you will submit to Him and live; He chastens ["for our profit"]{.class_s36N}; He chastens so you will bear fruit.

["As sorrowful, yet always rejoicing"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10). The same apostle who wrote ["we\...rejoice in hope of the glory of God"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 5:2), also wrote, ["I have great heaviness and continual sorrow in my heart"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 9:2). The Christian life is a series of extreme paradoxes (Phil. 4:12). And it is not always just between the old nature and the new nature. Sometimes that [new nature]{.class_s36M} is torn between two things (Phil. 1:23--24).

If you have ever lost a loved one in Christ to death, you know the reality of that thing. Part of you sorrows and weeps because you miss that person, and then, down underneath, part of you is rejoicing because he is safe in glory with Jesus and you [will]{.class_s36M} see him again.

There are two sides even to the [new nature]{.class_s36M}. That new nature will be terribly burdened to see sinners saved; it will be terribly burdened to get an answer to prayer. At the same time, it will be rejoicing in its salvation and rejoicing in the glory of God and rejoicing over God's sufficient grace.

So in [the ministry]{.class_s36M}, you may be poor physically, but the message you preach takes those who believe to a city with jasper walls, jeweled foundations, pearl gates, and golden streets (vs. 10). You may have nothing; and yet ["all things are yours; Whether Paul, or Apollos, or Cephas, or the world, or life, or death, or things present, or things to come; all are yours; And ye are Christ's; and Christ is God's"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 3:21--23).

Who owns the title deed to the earth? God. Are you His? Then you know whose property it is, don't you? It's yours. First Timothy 6:17 says that God ["giveth us richly all things to enjoy."]{.class_s36N}

I have often thought about [that]{.class_s36M}. The Lord has put me in some weird situations from time to time. One time I got on a plane going to New Orleans. It was a night flight that left Pensacola about 10:30 P.M., and I was [the only passenger on the plane. ]{.class_s36M}It was a DC7 that would seat eighty or ninety people, and there were two stewardesses. As I sat on that plane all by myself, I thought, "Ain't this wild? I've got to get somewhere to preach, and the Lord [charters me a plane]{.class_s36M}." I don't believe that there is a millionaire in the world that had better service than I had that night.

6:11 O ye Corinthians, our mouth is open unto you, our heart is enlarged.

12 Ye are not straitened in us, but ye are straitened in your own bowels.

13 Now for a recompence in the same, (I speak as unto my children,) be ye also enlarged.

In verse 11, when Paul says, ["Our mouth is open unto you,"]{.class_s36N} he was telling the Corinthians that he was being completely honest with them. His heart was enlarged in that his love encompassed them all.

["Ye are not straitened in us"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12). So Paul had no problem loving them, but they were finding it hard to love Paul inside of them:[ "ye are straitened in your own bowels."]{.class_s36N} Notice that ["bowels"]{.class_s36N} refers to [anything inside a person.]{.class_s36M} Only medical terminology limits the word to a function of the lower viscera. That is not the correct definition here. When we speak of the bowels of a ship, we are speaking of the [inside compartments.]{.class_s36M} So when he says, ["ye are straitened in your own bowels,"]{.class_s36N} the inside of the Corinthians, where their feelings and emotions were, was so narrow and tight that they could find no room to love Paul as they ought.

The ["recompence"]{.class_s36N} of verse 13 is a payback of love. He is saying that as the one who led them to the Lord, their spiritual father (see comments on Timothy under 1 Tim. 1:2 in [Pastoral Epistles,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, 1989), he had a large heart of love for them; all he wants in return is for his spiritual children to love him the same way.

6:14 Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers: for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? and what communion hath light with darkness?

15 And what concord hath Christ with Belial? or what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?

16 And what agreement hath the temple of God with idols? for ye are the temple of the living God; as God hath said, I will dwell in them, and walk in them; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.

17 Wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing; and I will receive you,

18 And will be a Father unto you, and ye shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty.

This whole passage should be committed to memory and practised in the life of every Christian. The theme of this passage is separation.

["Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14). In other words, don't mess around with the [wrong crowd]{.class_s36M}. The Corinthians had a terrible time with this. In 1 Corinthians, they are carnal and get all messed up with the gifts. In 2 Corinthians, they get messed up with false teachers. Look at 2 Corinthians 11:4---["another Jesus," "another spirit."]{.class_s36N} In 2 Corinthians 10:7--10, they are hanging around a bunch of people who are cutting down Paul. Paul is forced to justify himself and talk to them about it.

Verse 14 is the main verse to prove that a saved person should [not]{.class_s36M} marry an unsaved person. A yoke is a crossbar that harnesses two animals together so that they have to move [at the same time.]{.class_s36M} If you are yoked to another person, then where he goes, you go. Amos asks, ["Can two walk together, except they be agreed?"]{.class_s36N} (Amos 3:3).

If the person you marry is lost, then you are in one big mess. Look at the contrast in 2 Corinthians 6:14--16. You are considered righteous before God; the other spouse is considered unrighteous. You live in [light]{.class_s36M}; the lost spouse lives in [darkness]{.class_s36M}. You belong to [Christ]{.class_s36M}; the spouse belongs to [Satan]{.class_s36M}. You're a believer; the spouse is an infidel. You are the temple of God; the spouse is the temple of idols. You are headed to [Heaven]{.class_s36M}; the spouse is headed for [Hell]{.class_s36M}!

Young lady, don't let the Devil tell you that you will win that lost young man to Christ [after]{.class_s36M} you are married. That very [seldom]{.class_s36M} happens. You will either end up in a Hell on earth for a marriage, where every time you try to obey God he fights you, or you will give up serving God all together in order to get along with him, or the marriage will end up in a divorce. [That goes for a saved young man pursuing a lost girl, too.]{.class_s36M}

If you are yoked together with unbelievers in a situation like the military, then you are to obey orders as long as those orders are not [contrary ]{.class_s36M}to the clear, revealed word of God (Acts 4:19--20 cf. Rom. 13:1--5).

Now, you can take separation to unreasonable extremes. Paul had told the Corinthians back in 1 Corinthians 5:9--10,[ "I wrote unto you in an epistle not to company with fornicators: Yet not altogether with the fornicators of this world, or with the covetous, or extortioners, or with idolaters; for then must ye needs go out of the world."]{.class_s36N}

In this world you will have to [work ]{.class_s36M}with unsaved people, [buy ]{.class_s36M}from unsaved people, and [converse ]{.class_s36M}with unsaved people in all kinds of ways. Some Fundamentalists get into ultra-separation, where you can't have fellowship with people who have fellowship with people who have fellowship, etc. You can get so separated that, after a while, you are no cotton-pickn' good to anybody.

If you try to eat just in restaurants that don't serve alcohol, you will find that you are buying your [groceries in stores that sell beer.]{.class_s36M} If you just buy gasoline at stations that don't sell beer, then they probably will be selling [cigarettes]{.class_s36M}.

So complete separation is impossible. What you want to avoid is being joined to either a person or group that causes you to sin [whenever they make a move in the wrong direction.]{.class_s36M}

Note in verse 16, that the temple of God is more than just [your]{.class_s36M} body. See the ["ye"]{.class_s36N} and ["them."]{.class_s36N} They are plural. The reference is to the Body of Christ, as a corporate body, being ["the temple of the living God."]{.class_s36N}

The Scripture quotation in verse 16 is Leviticus 26:11--12. Again, the Old Testament reference is to Israel, but the Holy Spirit gives a free quote and makes spiritual application to the Church. Doctrinally, Leviticus 26:11--12 is a conditional promise, fulfilled when Israel keeps the Law (Lev. 26:3). In verse 16, it is an accomplished fact that leads to the commandment in verse 17: "Since you are the temple of God and God lives in [you, ]{.class_s36M}then come out."

Verse 17 is [the]{.class_s36M} command for the Church. It is a quote from Isaiah 52:11, where it is a command to the Old Testament priests who bore the vessels of the temple. Every Christian in the Body of Christ is a priest, so, [spiritually]{.class_s36M}, the command is to [every]{.class_s36M} Christian:[ "Depart," "come out."]{.class_s36N} Don't join! "Unjoin"! The Church is a [called out ]{.class_s36M}assembly.

The world bids the Christian, "Come in." The Bible says, "Come out!" It is isolationism; Christians are supposed to be [separatists]{.class_s36M}.

["Be ye separate"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 17). There has to be [spiritual]{.class_s36M} separation as well as [physical]{.class_s36M} separation. I have been in Christian day schools where all the boys' hair was cut right and all the girls' dresses were the right length, and yet, their hearts were harder than "lifers" in the prisons where I have preached. [Outward separation does not guarantee spirituality.]{.class_s36M}

I wouldn't hesitate to join the local ministerial association [if]{.class_s36M} it meant an open door to preach in jails, nursing homes, etc. Once I joined, I couldn't guarantee how long it would last. The first time they asked me to do something [contrary to Scripture]{.class_s36M} it would be all over. Bob Jones Sr. said, "You can go as far as you can on the right road with anybody. The right road leads out at the right place. It is always a compromise to go any distance on the [wrong road]{.class_s36M} with anybody."

To the young preacher who may be reading this, I will give you some good advice. Preach wherever the Lord opens the door for you to preach. Preach in a Southern Baptist church, a Methodist church, a Presbyterian church, a Holiness church, or even a Catholic church, [if the Lord opens the door.]{.class_s36M} Preach anywhere---[as long as you don't have to compromise or cut corners. ]{.class_s36M}Now, don't be stupid. Don't offend on purpose. Use some wisdom. But if there are any "strings" attached, don't go.

At the first sign of [compromise]{.class_s36M}, be a gentleman, but be firm, and say, "I'm sorry, but the Bible says this, and I [must]{.class_s36M} do what the [Bible]{.class_s36M} says." Then let the chips fall where they may.

The[ "unclean thing" ]{.class_s36N}of verse 17 is ["all filthiness of the flesh and spirit"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 7:1). That includes tobacco, alcohol, pornography, nearly everything in the newspaper, [90 percent of all television,]{.class_s36M} and the National Council of Christian Churches.

The cross reference to verse 18 is Isaiah 43:6--7. The ["sons and daughters"]{.class_s36N} are a [corporate reference]{.class_s36M} to Israel. Doctrinally speaking, in this age, [no one is made a son or a daughter of God by acts of separation.]{.class_s36M} Moreover, doctrinally speaking, there is no such thing as a New Testament ["daughter of God"]{.class_s36N} in the Body of Christ. When you ladies receive Christ, you are ["sons of God"]{.class_s36N} (John 1:12).

But [practically speaking]{.class_s36M}, in your present condition, you give a testimony to an unsaved world as to whose child you are when you separate from them. The key is to get cleaned up on both the inside [and]{.class_s36M} the outside.

I don't lay down a lot of hard, fast rules, although a lot of Fundamentalists do. I don't do it because I am afraid of making a bunch of Pharisees. The word "Pharisee" means "separated."

My standards are these: get saved, believe the Book, spend time in the Book, spend time on your knees in prayer, [and surrender when God deals with you about something.]{.class_s36M} If you observe those things right there, nobody will have to worry about your dress or your hair or companions or your church or your Bible or your habits.

If there is a controversy between you and the Lord and you are not surrendered on something, then all the separation in the world is not going to do you one bit of good. If you are not going to spend time in that Bible and believe it, then the fact that you don't drink and don't smoke and don't dance just means that you are [a dead man in the cemetery.]{.class_s36M} No corpse drinks, dances, or smokes. If you are not going to spend time in prayer, there will be no spiritual power in your life. You might as well be following the crowd.

[]{#c2E7.xhtml}

## Chapter 7 {.heading_s39}

Chapter 7 follows hard on the heels of the theme of chapter 6---separation. The great word in America today is [integration,]{.class_s36M} which is [not]{.class_s36M} a Bible word. [It is a blasphemy to correct Bible teaching.]{.class_s36M}

The nonsense that everybody should [get together]{.class_s36M} is communistic ecumenism. It has nothing to do with the word of God.

In the word of God, the [Jew ]{.class_s36M}is separate from other people. In the word of God, the [animals ]{.class_s36M}that boarded Noah's ark were separated from all the other animals on earth by the flood. In the word of God,[ the tribes of Israel]{.class_s36M} are separated from each other in the land of Canaan.

When God saved you, [He separated you from other people by a new birth.]{.class_s36M} You are not even born like other people are born.

The whole business about "getting together" and unity is due to two things. The first is [fear]{.class_s36M}. People are afraid to "go it" alone. Cowardice is the major factor in it.

Second is [political expediency]{.class_s36M}. People have found that they can make more money by getting a bigger clientele. So the great opposition to Christian separation is from people who are [cowardly ]{.class_s36M}and [worldly]{.class_s36M}. Christians are in that condition today, so not only do you get opposition from the unsaved, you also get it from Christians.

But in the Bible, the standard is ["not unequally yoked,"]{.class_s36N} no fellowship with unrighteousness, no communion with darkness, no concord with Satan, no part with unbelievers, no agreement with idols (2 Cor. 6:14--16), and no conformity to the world (Rom. 12:2).

People don't want to be [separated]{.class_s36M}. They don't want to be [isolationists]{.class_s36M}; they don't want to "go it" alone. [They are scared and they are lazy.]{.class_s36M}

As a nation, America has that problem. Ever since the Civil War, we have stuck our nose into everybody else's business, and we have come home with a bloody nose every time.

The Christian's basic makeup is such that he is [completely different from the world around him.]{.class_s36M} The Christian is born again; [the world is not]{.class_s36M}. The Christian's home is in heaven; [the world's home is not]{.class_s36M}. The Christian belongs to Christ;[ the world does not]{.class_s36M}. The Christian believes the Book; [the world does not. ]{.class_s36M}The fact that many people in the world go to church and profess to be religious is immaterial. The counterfeit always acts or [appears ]{.class_s36M}like the genuine. As a Christian, you are commanded to ["come out\...and be ye separate"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 6:17).

With that in mind, let's begin chapter 7.

7:1 Having therefore these promises, dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God.

The[ "promises"]{.class_s36N} of verse 1 refer to Isaiah 52:11, quoted in verse 17 of the last chapter; i.e., if you separate yourself, God will receive you and be a Father to you (2 Cor. 6:17--18). Paul claims Old Testament promises, which [doctrinally ]{.class_s36M}apply to Israel, and [spiritually ]{.class_s36M}applies them to the Church.

Now, Paul did that under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit. You have to be a lot more careful. The general rule of thumb is that you "rightly divide" the word and interpret a passage in its doctrinal context. However, [if]{.class_s36M} the Lord gives you the liberty to claim an Old Testament promise for yourself in a particular situation, go ahead and claim it. John Paton claimed Psalm 2:8 for his missionary work in the New Hebrides islands of the South Pacific, and the Lord blessed him and let him win a whole island to Jesus Christ. But anyone who reads Psalm 2 knows that it is a [Messianic Psalm ]{.class_s36M}that prophetically points to Jesus Christ in the [Millennium]{.class_s36M}. In the gospels, a Syro-Phonecian woman got the Lord to cross dispensational lines and answer her prayer. So don't play fast and loose with Scripture, but at the same time don't be so [dispensational ]{.class_s36M}that you talk yourself out of a blessing or an answer to prayer.

["Let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit."]{.class_s36N} In the verse, two types of uncleanness are mentioned:[ "filthiness of the flesh"]{.class_s36N} and ["filthiness of the\...spirit."]{.class_s36N} Now, I am not going to spend a lot of time telling you what the ["filthiness of the flesh"]{.class_s36N} is and what it isn't, but wouldn't tobacco fit the bill beautifully?

I heard about a fellow who had been preaching about smoking, and one of his parishioners said to him after the sermon, "I agree that smoking is a sin. I believe it's a sin to burn up anything that tastes that good." [He had been chewing it.]{.class_s36M}

Do you know that if you chew tobacco and spit it out, a fly will not land on it? Have you ever seen some of the things on which a [fly ]{.class_s36M}will land? Did you ever stop to think about putting something in your mouth that a [fly ]{.class_s36M}will not touch? I think that that is one of the most filthy things a fellow could do.

I'll guarantee that you can take your cigarettes and leave them on the porch, and a dog, cat, or pig wouldn't touch them. How is it that a "dumb" animal is more intelligent than a human when it comes to cigarettes?

Now, if Jesus Christ loved you enough to clean you up on the inside, shouldn't you love Him enough to clean yourself up on the [outside]{.class_s36M}? A fellow said one time at a meeting where Dr. Bob Gray was preaching, "I'm going to give my cigarettes to God"; and Bob Gray replied, "He won't take them."

I will give you[ six reasons]{.class_s36M} why smoking or chewing tobacco comes under the ["filthiness of the flesh."]{.class_s36N}

1\. Smoking or chewing tobacco is a [bad example.]{.class_s36M} I don't care how much you enjoy it, it is a bad example. In the New Testament, Jesus Christ and Paul are the [examples ]{.class_s36M}for the Christian. Would Jesus Christ smoke if He were here? Would Paul have a big wad of "chaw" in his mouth? Some folks say, "They might." You haven't thought that over. Jesus Christ wouldn't set a bad example.

Why would you take cigarette smoke into your lungs? God made your lungs to inhale [air]{.class_s36M}, not tobacco smoke. You inhale enough corruption through traffic without stoking your own.

2\. It is [a bad habit]{.class_s36M}. Anything that gets to the point where it masters you is a bad habit. Folks say, "It's no worse than coffee or coke." Then quit your coffee; quit your coke!

3\. It [cultivates an unnatural desire]{.class_s36M}. It is not natural for a man to want to breathe smoke. Nobody goes to a bonfire or brushfire to breathe[ the smoke.]{.class_s36M}

A fellow sat in a train compartment across from a woman who smoked and kept blowing the smoke toward him. He finally said to her, "Lady, I wish I had a chaw of tobacco, because everytime you blew that smoke in my face I would [spit ]{.class_s36M}in yours."

4\. It [causes disease]{.class_s36M}. You can get lung cancer, lip cancer, or mouth cancer. You will grant that that is possible, won't you? When I was a boy, we used to call cigarettes "coffin nails." We knew what they did to your body.

Somebody said, "Well, I can smoke and still go to heaven." Yeah, you can, and you will probably make it there before the rest of us.

5\. Smoking is [a wasteful habit]{.class_s36M}. What do you store up by smoking? You burn the paper; [that's]{.class_s36M} a waste. You burn the tobacco; [that's]{.class_s36M} a waste. You burn out your lungs; [that's]{.class_s36M} a waste. Is there anything about tobacco that isn't destructive?

Somebody said, "Well, the Lord made tobacco." The Lord made it for [rat poison.]{.class_s36M} Tobacco is one of the greatest rat poisons there is.[ It will kill them dead]{.class_s36M}. It is not meant for folks to inhale.

6\. Smoking is [an offense to others.]{.class_s36M} People have complained about "second-hand" smoke so much that restaurants and airlines have eliminated the separate smoking sections which were originally set up because people complained about having to sit and smell somebody else's smoke. Now most restaurants and airlines are completely smoke-free.

Did you know that you can walk into a convenience store, and when you come out, you stink like cigarette smoke? One fellow said to a smoker, "I didn't say that you were going to Hell. I just said that you [smelled]{.class_s36M} like you were."

Now, it is not just ["filthiness of the flesh,"]{.class_s36N} it is also ["filthiness of the\...spirit."]{.class_s36N} The Pharisee was clean in the [flesh]{.class_s36M}, but he wasn't clean in the [spirit]{.class_s36M}. There are Christians that don't drink and don't smoke and don't dance. They have three-quarter length sleeves, and the ladies have hair down to their waist. They don't wear make-up or any type of gold or jewelry; they don't go to the movies or bowl. But they live like hell on wheels, because they are [proud, lazy, stubborn, rebellious, self-righteous, gluttonous, and indifferent.]{.class_s36M} They have no burden for souls, no concern for unsaved people, and[ no faith in the word of God.]{.class_s36M}

Cleaning up only the outside isn't what Paul said in verse 1: it's both the flesh [and]{.class_s36M} the spirit. You are to keep yourself separate from the world of demoniac spirits and unclean spirits. You are not to let the devil work on you in the spirit world.

The problem arises as to what qualifies as ["filthiness of the flesh and spirit."]{.class_s36N} The Supreme Court of the United States would answer, "Nothing." Years ago, the Supreme Court decided that [nothing is pornographic.]{.class_s36M} Every time a case on pornography was brought before the High Court, the judges position was that [if]{.class_s36M} it was [artistic]{.class_s36M} and had any point to get across, besides arousing lust, then it didn't qualify as [pornography]{.class_s36M}.

Therefore, if you got a beautiful color camera shot with the right [balance ]{.class_s36M}and the right [tone ]{.class_s36M}and the right [blend ]{.class_s36M}with the right composition, it is "art." You would be amazed at what you can get away with in the name of "art."

Those operas are just stories about murder and adultery; 90 percent of the operas are just that. But you know, if you're [singing]{.class_s36M}, then it is different, and of course, it is beautiful to watch. So if you go into "art," you can get away with murder, [literally]{.class_s36M}.

A good rule of thumb for deciding what is ["filthiness of the flesh and spirit"]{.class_s36N} is "if it is doubtful, it is dirty." If you can pray over it and ask God to bless it and then do it with a clear conscience, then it is all right. If you [can't ]{.class_s36M}pray over it and ask God's blessings on it before you do it, [then it is not right.]{.class_s36M}

Now, I know that back in the Dark Ages, the Catholics would pray over the instruments of torture at the Inquisition. But that is only when a fellow (or a nation or society) has rejected the Bible for so long that he is [demon-possessed]{.class_s36M} and his [conscience is seared]{.class_s36M} until it is no good.

If you are in that Book and you are reading that Book and you are trying to serve God, your conscience is still [awake]{.class_s36M}. It will guide you right. You won't pick up a glass of beer and say, "Lord, bless this for your glory; I'm doing this in Jesus' name and for your glory," and then drink it down. Your conscience would say, "HEY, BOY!" And it is the same way with other things. Movies, ball games, cards, etc.

Christians do have a time. They are always trying to find out [how much they can get away with before they sin. ]{.class_s36M}Why don't you see how much you can give up to please God?

The most [negative people]{.class_s36M} in the world are these positive thinkers. You start talking about sin, and they say, "Oh, we're not going to talk about that. You criticize other folks' faith. I don't believe in talking about other peoples' religion." Then these meatballs go around asking, "What's [wrong ]{.class_s36M}with this? What's [wrong ]{.class_s36M}with that?" [That is negative.]{.class_s36M} The question isn't what is [wrong]{.class_s36M} with it; the question is what is [right]{.class_s36M} with it.

The trick, then, in the Christian life is to see what you can give up for the Lord,[ not what you can get away with.]{.class_s36M}

["Perfecting holiness in the fear of God."]{.class_s36N} Holiness is for the Christian. The Christian should endeavor to live a [holy ]{.class_s36M}life. I am not what would be called a "Holiness" by doctrinal affiliation, but I [do]{.class_s36M} believe in [holiness]{.class_s36M}. I am not a Pentecostal or a Charismatic, but I believe Baptists ought to pay more attention to it than they do.

There is nothing wrong with being holy, and there is nothing wrong with [holiness]{.class_s36M}. After you get saved, your ambition, as a Christian, should be to clean up your life and to keep it as clean as possible until the Lord comes.

7:2 Receive us; we have wronged no man, we have corrupted no man, we have defrauded no man.

3 I speak not this to condemn you: for I have said before, that ye are in our hearts to die and live with you.

4 Great is my boldness of speech toward you, great is my glorying of you: I am filled with comfort, I am exceeding joyful in all our tribulation.

Paul point blank commands the Corinthians:[ "Receive us"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2). He shouldn't have had to be that blunt. Paul and his companions (Titus and Timothy) had done nothing wrong to the Corinthians to cause them to reject him. Now, every minister ought to be able to say what Paul said: ["we have wronged no man, we have corrupted no man, we have defrauded no man."]{.class_s36N} Paul told Timothy and Titus,[ "A bishop then must be blameless"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 3:2; Titus 1:7).

Paul's purpose in saying what he said was not to condemn the Corinthians (vs. 3). He loved the Corinthians: he loved them enough to [die]{.class_s36M} for them; he loved them enough to [live]{.class_s36M} for them. But the way the Corinthians had been treating Paul forced him to speak as he did.

What the Corinthians didn't realize (or wouldn't recognize) was that Paul had been bragging about them. He thought a lot of them. That is why he didn't mind using ["boldness of speech"]{.class_s36N} toward them (vs. 4). He wanted them to live up to what he had said about them. He counted all the trouble which he had gone through for them as ["exceeding joyful"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4).

7:5 For, when we were come into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest, but we were troubled on every side; without were fightings, within were fears.

6 Nevertheless God, that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus;

7 And not by his coming only, but by the consolation wherewith he was comforted in you, when he told us your earnest desire, your mourning, your fervent mind toward me; so that I rejoiced the more.

The first time Paul came into Macedonia (vs. 5) can be found in Acts 16. There in Philippi, he was hounded by a demon-possessed girl, dragged into court, beaten, and put in stocks. He had passed through Macedonia again in Acts 20:1 and Acts 20:3. Apparently Macedonia was not exactly a "fun" place for Paul.

In verse 6, Titus finally shows up. Paul had hoped to meet up with him in Troas (2 Cor. 2:12--13), but they missed each other until Macedonia. As Paul was going through so much trouble in Macedonia, Titus showed up, and that was a real comfort to Paul. So a Christian showing up at the right time can be a great help to another Christian. Are you an encouragement to other Christians when you show up, or does a groan go up when you come in the door? Would they just as soon not attend another recital of all your aches and pains and problems and gripes?

["By the consolation wherewith he was comforted in you, when he told us of your ernest desire, your mourning, your fervent mind toward me"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). Whenever Paul gets in trouble and gets thrown in jail, he worries about how his converts will take the news. He worries that they will stop obeying God because of what had happened to him.

But when Titus came, he brought all the good news that there was till a "core" of Corinthians who still wanted to see him; they still were on fire for the Lord; and they still thought highly of him (vs. 7). Paul found comfort in that and rejoiced. It is always a comfort and a blessing when your friends stand by you.

I remember when I went through some deep water several years back. When I look back, it wasn't too deep, but it seemed deep at the time. Everything was going to pieces, and psychiatrists and lawyers were getting involved. One of my German buddies came up from Elberta, Alabama, and brought five of the brethren with him. He took me out in the back yard of a little, old farmhouse in Bay Minette, Alabama, and he put five chairs in a circle. Then he sat down with his friends and said, "Now, Brother Ruckman, we've heard everything about what you are going through. Now we want to hear [your side]{.class_s36M}." That was a real blessing to me. It is a blessing to know that, when you get in trouble, [some of your friends]{.class_s36M} are interested in your viewpoint, more than somebody else's.

7:8 For though I made you sorry with a letter, I do not repent, though I did repent: for I perceive that the same epistle hath made you sorry, though it were but for a season.

9 Now I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry, but that ye sorrowed to repentance: for ye were made sorry after a godly manner, that ye might receive damage by us in nothing.

10 For godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of: but the sorrow of the world worketh death.

11 For behold this selfsame thing, that ye sorrowed after a godly sort, what carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves, yea, what indignation, yea, what fear, yea, what vehement desire, yea, what zeal, yea, what revenge! In all things ye have approved yourselves to be clear in this matter.

The[ "letter"]{.class_s36N} of verse 8, of course, was 1 Corinthians. When the Corinthians read it, they were sorry for what they had done to the point of being sorrowful over their sin. In this context, ["sorry"]{.class_s36N} means "sorrowful." When he wrote the letter, Paul thought he was being too hard on them, and he [was sorry]{.class_s36M} that he had written it (["I did repent"]{.class_s36N}). But when he saw how the Corinthians reacted to it, he was [glad ]{.class_s36M}that he had written it.

First Corinthians had resulted in the Corinthians repenting of their sin (vs. 9). They straightened up, and they got right. So Paul wasn't happy that they were sad, but because they got right.

["For ye were made sorry after a godly manner, that ye might receive damage by us in nothing"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). This is obviously a reference to the way the church reacted to Paul's instructions in 1 Corinthians 5 and 6 in regards to the man who was committing fornication with his father's wife. He is [glad ]{.class_s36M}that they got upset and did the right thing about it. In that way, when he got there, he wouldn't have to blame the church in person and take care of the problem himself.

Verse 10 is one of those great verses in 2 Corinthians that should be committed to memory. Observe that there are two kinds of sorrow that result in two kinds of repentance. There is ["godly sorrow"]{.class_s36N} and ["the sorrow of the world." "Godly sorrow"]{.class_s36N} is sorrow over [what you are]{.class_s36M} (Job 42:6; Isa. 6:5). ["The sorrow of the world"]{.class_s36N} is sorrow for [what you have done]{.class_s36M} (Judas---Matt. 27:3; Pharaoh---Exod. 9:27; Balaam---Num. 22:34). It is usually [regret]{.class_s36M} over being [caught]{.class_s36M} in your sin.

These two sorrows are perfectly illustrated by the lives of [Judas ]{.class_s36M}and [Simon Peter]{.class_s36M}. Both Simon Peter and Judas repented. Both of them confessed. Both of them made restitution. If you put those two fellows side by side, you would swear that both of them were saved---[but they weren't]{.class_s36M}. One ends up hanging himself, and one ends up back at the feet of Jesus Christ. One ends up in the [bottomless pit,]{.class_s36M} and the other ends up in [heaven]{.class_s36M}.

They were both [apostles]{.class_s36M}; they both [healed]{.class_s36M}; they both ministered with the Lord; they both stayed with the Lord three and one-half years; they were both [baptized with the baptism of John.]{.class_s36M} The difference in those two was that when Judas got caught, ["the sorrow of the world"]{.class_s36N} worked on him. Peter repented with ["godly sorrow."]{.class_s36N}

Judas went to the priest and said, "I'm sorry I got caught; I made a mistake." He was sorry because he had made a mistake in betraying innocent blood. Well, everybody makes a mistake about something, sometime; moreover, everybody is usually sorry they made the mistake. You can lie in bed at night and wet your pillow with tears all night long over the messes you've made in life. That does nothing; [it changes nothing.]{.class_s36M} I've made every mistake a man can make and several he [couldn't]{.class_s36M} make.

But repentance is not just being sorry for what you have [done]{.class_s36M}; real repentance means being sorry for [what you are. ]{.class_s36M}Our churches are filled with religious people who every now and then have a little spell of "crying in the chapel" and "God please forgive me." But that is not [repentance]{.class_s36M}. That is not[ "godly sorrow."]{.class_s36N}

I'll show you the difference. ["The sorrow of the world"]{.class_s36N} is "I've made a mess of things, I've ruined my life, my life is messed up, I'm doomed, I can't do anything anymore, I'm a failure, I've never been anything, I wish I was dead"; and then you blow your brains out. That is ["the sorrow of the world."]{.class_s36N} Sometimes the suicide is much slower; sometimes it is through drugs and/or alcohol. But it always ends up in [death]{.class_s36M}.

["Godly sorrow"]{.class_s36N} is "I never have been any good; [I'm no good right now]{.class_s36M}; I'm never going to be any good; at my very [best ]{.class_s36M}I'm not worth shooting or blowing myself to Hell; I shouldn't even blow my brains out because I didn't have any to begin with; unless you do something [for]{.class_s36M} me, God, and [to]{.class_s36M} me and [with]{.class_s36M} me, I'll never be worth a dime; from now on I'm going to trust You, Lord, to do it."[ That's the difference.]{.class_s36M}

When a man simply sees that his sins are wrong and bad and terrible, and he tries to straighten things out himself, that is [worldly sorrow. ]{.class_s36M}When a man sees his sins are against God and His divine justice and holiness, then he starts waking up and flees to God alone for his help.

Now, the Corinthians ["sorrowed after a godly sort"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). They were careful to do right; they cleared themselves by showing their disapproval of sin. [They became angry over sin ]{.class_s36M}(["what indignation"]{.class_s36N}) and [feared God.]{.class_s36M} They showed a [fierce desire ]{.class_s36M}to do right and carried out their desire with zeal. The ["revenge"]{.class_s36N} they showed was legitimate church discipline upon a man who had messed up the whole church. So the vengeance here was not wrong; [it was not a personal thing.]{.class_s36M}

Because they reacted in this way, Paul says, ["ye have approved yourselves to be clear in this matter."]{.class_s36N} Because they had disapproved of the sin and would not put up with the fellow who was doing, they had cleared themselves of the charges Paul had brought against them in 1 Corinthians.

7:12 Wherefore, though I wrote unto you, I did it not for his cause that had done the wrong, nor for his cause that suffered wrong, but that our care for you in the sight of God might appear unto you.

Paul did not write 1 Corinthians 5--6 to get on the fellow who was fornicating, nor did he write it to avenge whoever was hurt by the man's sin. Rather it was to show the Corinthians the true love he had for them ["in the sight of God."]{.class_s36N} That is a mighty high motive, but it is true in Paul's case. It is hard to convince people that you are giving them good advice and orders [because you love them. ]{.class_s36M}You may have noticed that if you have children.

I discussed this once with one of my boys. He said, "Well, the older generation is always trying to keep the younger generation down and prevent them from getting ahead."

"Is that the kind of baloney they are teaching you at school?" I asked.

He said, "Well, that's just life, Dad."

I said, "Well, did it ever occur to you that maybe one reason why they do it is to [keep the young folk from getting in a lot of trouble]{.class_s36M}? Maybe some of the older folk have been through some troubles, and they're trying to do that [because they love you]{.class_s36M}. Have you ever thought of that?"

He replied, "No."

I said, "Well, think about it for a while. [See you around."]{.class_s36M}

Paul wrote to the Corinthians because he was concerned about them. If somebody loves you enough to correct you, [then they love you.]{.class_s36M} Of course, you will always have these chronic correctors and chronic gripers and chronic complainers. [They don't love you.]{.class_s36M} They just gripe and complain to keep in practise. They just want to make their voice heard. But a good mamma or daddy will correct the children because they are concerned about them and love them.

7:13 Therefore we were comforted in your comfort: yea, and exceedingly the more joyed we for the joy of Titus, because his spirit was refreshed by you all.

14 For if I have boasted any thing to him of you, I am not ashamed; but as we spake all things to you in truth, even so our boasting, which I made before Titus, is found a truth.

15 And his inward affection is more abundant toward you, whilst he remembereth the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling ye received him.

Now, Titus had come to encourage the Corinthians. He found them doing right, and they treated him right, and that made him happy. When he told Paul about it all, that made Paul happy (vs. 13).

So Paul wasn't ashamed of bragging about the Corinthians to Titus (vs. 14). They had done everything Paul had said they would do, and Titus found what Paul said to be true.

Titus' love for the Corinthians had grown when he had visited the Corinthians (vs. 15). Titus went there after they had received the first epistle. When he showed up, they knew who he was and whom he represented. They were scared Paul had sent him to straighten things out in Paul's place. But Titus saw that they were already obeying Paul, and he loved them all the more for what they had done.

Because of what Titus had told him, Paul was joyful that he could ["have confidence"]{.class_s36N} in them ["in all things"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 16). He was happy that he could count on them to do everything about which he had written to them.

[]{#c2H8.xhtml}

## Chapter 8 {.heading_s39}

8:1 Moreover, brethren, we do you to wit of the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia;

2 How that in a great trial of affliction the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality.

3 For to their power, I bear record, yea, and beyond their power they were willing of themselves;

4 Praying us with much intreaty that we would receive the gift, and take upon us the fellowship of the ministering to the saints.

5 And this they did, not as we hoped, but first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the will of God.

6 Insomuch that we desired Titus, that as he had begun, so he would also finish in you the same grace also.

["We do you to wit"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1). That is an archaic way of saying, "we want you to know." ["To wit"]{.class_s36N} means to know [exactly ]{.class_s36M}what a thing is. And though it is called archaic, it is still used in twentieth and twenty-first centuries. You can still see it in legal documents. (See Vance's work on [Archaic Words and the Authorized Version, ]{.class_s36M}Vance Publications, Pensacola, FL, 1999.)

Now, ["the grace"]{.class_s36N} of verse 1 is given to a bunch of people who are [already]{.class_s36M} saved. Grace is quite a "gal." She "gets around." Grace shows up in all kinds of situations. Sometimes grace is a period of time. Sometimes, grace is that by which God saves a man. Sometimes, grace is an [ability]{.class_s36M} that God gives. Sometimes, grace is a[ temporary gift]{.class_s36M} which God gives to help you when you are in trouble. So that word ["grace"]{.class_s36N} is a very broad word. Here in verse 1, it has nothing to do with salvation at all. The context, in verse 2, is giving.

Note in verse 2, the giving was not merely out of poverty; it was out of ["their DEEP poverty."]{.class_s36N} The Macedonians were in ["deep poverty"]{.class_s36N}; they were undergoing ["a great trial of affliction"]{.class_s36N}; yet in all of that ["the abundance of their joy"]{.class_s36N} caused them to give [liberally]{.class_s36M}. If you want to see that thing illustrated in the Gospels, turn to Luke 21:1--4 and read the story of "the widow's mite."

Now, there is a lesson there for a Christian. Here in America, we don't know much about ["deep poverty."]{.class_s36N} Almost every American reading this page owns a car. I'll grant you that it might be a rolling junk pile, but it will still run. And when it stops running, it won't be long until you get another.

I have seen people come to church on the back end of a tractor. I have seen people walk barefoot to church: five miles [to church]{.class_s36M} in their bare feet and five miles [back home]{.class_s36M} in their bare feet.

I remember one place in Alabama, there was a widow woman with four children. She was about fifty years old, and a couple of the children were about ten and fourteen years old. The kids were barefoot, she had no job, and the family was just living hand to mouth from day to day. I went there for dinner, and she took back the cloth on the table with yesterday's meal under the cloth. There was corn bread, that looked like a blue paste, and there were black-eyed peas, which I had never seen before. The black-eyed peas and corn bread were going to be the meal.

We sat down and started eating, and she said to me, "Well, Brother Ruckman, did you ever try any of that pepper sauce? Maybe you would like some of that pepper sauce on those peas."

I tried some, and I must have eaten four helpings. [I've been addicted to the stuff ever since.]{.class_s36M}

Now, that woman didn't have much. But she faithfully gave to the church: a quarter one Sunday, a half dollar the next, then a quarter again. That woman gave out of her ["deep poverty."]{.class_s36N}

I believe the worst thing that I have ever heard was related by John Tyner, who used to be a missionary in Ethiopia. They were going to build a new church building. They found that one of the members, a woman, had sold herself into slavery [to get enough money to give to help toward the new building.]{.class_s36M}

I read about a case in Korea where a missionary said he found a tourist laughing about a situation in the village. There was a young man out in the field pulling a plow while an old man ran it. The tourist asked how they got in that condition, and the missionary explained that they had sold the only ox they had to get money to help with the new church building. [So now the boy had to pull the plow instead of the ox.]{.class_s36M} That is giving out of ["deep poverty,"]{.class_s36N} brother.

We might have had that same thing happening in America if the church buildings hadn't gotten so fancy. But you don't feel quite right about selling your car to give to the church building when it is air-conditioned and has a thirty-foot steeple, a \$40,000 pipe organ, and expensive carpet on the floor. You feel more like taking the buttons off the cushions and putting [them]{.class_s36M} in the collection plate.

Now, Paul was able to testify of the Macedonians that they were willing to give not only what they were [able]{.class_s36M} to give, but they were willing to give [beyond]{.class_s36M} what they were able to give (vs. 3). Look at verse 12:[ "For if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not."]{.class_s36N}

That is a great spiritual principle. The principle is that what God wants from us is [willingness]{.class_s36M}. Where you are [willing]{.class_s36M}, the Lord will accept it whether you have it or not, and it will be accepted according to what you [have ]{.class_s36M}and not according to what you [don't have]{.class_s36M}. In plainer words, if the Lord wants you on the mission field, He will call you and supply you and equip you and get you there. But the Lord may deal with you first and see if you are [willing ]{.class_s36M}to go before He will let you know whether you are to go or not.

The Lord is always dealing with us about our [wills.]{.class_s36M} Did you notice that? Christ, in the Garden of Gethsemane, said, ["Not my will, but thine, be done"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 22:42). There is a place where God's will runs one way and your will runs the other way. That is where [the cross]{.class_s36M} comes in. The cross is the place where your will crosses God's will, and you die to your wants and desires and will.

I have gone to the Lord many a time and prayed, "Lord, I am sick and tired of going out to preach and seeing no one saved, or only two or three saved." B. R. Lakin can go to a place and see fifty saved every time he gets up to preach. But when I get up, there are three, two, or sometimes none. Once in a while I will see a bonanza of ten or fifteen in one meeting, but not often. It is usually only two or three.

I will come home from a meeting like that and will fight the Lord about that thing until I am red, white, and blue in the face, but it won't make any difference. The Lord will send me right back into the pulpit and say, "Preach to the Christians! The Christians aren't getting fed. [Preach to the Christians]{.class_s36M}!"

"But, Lord, I want to come home and say that I had four or five saved this time. I can't have four or five saved if I am preaching the ['Seven Sevens']{.class_s36M} or 'Why do the Righteous Suffer?' or 'Romans 8:28.'"

And the answer will come back, "Preach to the Christians! [The Christians aren't getting fed]{.class_s36M}. Preach to the Christians!"

When my will runs one way and God's will runs another, if I have any sense, I will line my will up with His.

["Praying us with much intreaty that we would receive the gift"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). Here were some dirt poor Christians in Macedonia who were begging Paul to take the gift they had collected to help out some other saints. Paul would just as soon have given the money back.

I have seen that in operation with poor Christians overseas. One time, the Bible Baptist Church took up an offering of clean, white socks for a Christian leper colony in Thailand. In return the lepers asked us to send them one more thing. Since they had plenty of time on their hands, [they asked us to send them our prayer requests so they could pray for them throughout the day.]{.class_s36M}

That is a perfect illustration of verse 5, ["And this they did, not as we hoped, but first gave their own selves to the Lord."]{.class_s36N} Those poor lepers in Thailand couldn't give money, but they gave what they could for us.[ They gave us their prayers.]{.class_s36M}

The Christians in Macedonia were like that. They went far beyond Paul's expectations. They not only gave the money, they gave [themselves ]{.class_s36M}first and foremost. If God has [you,]{.class_s36M} then He has [your wallet!]{.class_s36M}

Again, ["the same grace"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 6) which Paul wanted finished in the Corinthians is the grace which was ["bestowed on the churches of Macedonia"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1). It is the grace of giving. So the grace of giving needs to be finished in the life of each Christian.

8:7 Therefore, as ye abound in every thing, in faith, and utterance, and knowledge, and in all diligence, and in your love to us, see that ye abound in this grace also.

8 I speak not by commandment, but by occasion of the forwardness of others, and to prove the sincerity of your love.

9 For ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich.

The Corinthians abounded in[ several things]{.class_s36M} (vs. 7). They abounded ["in faith,"]{.class_s36N} in the ability to witness (["utterance"]{.class_s36N}), in their knowledge of the Scriptures, in their work for the Lord (["in all diligence"]{.class_s36N}), and in the love which they showed to Paul and his companions. What Paul wanted was for them to abound as much in giving as they did in all of these other things. Giving here is called ["this grace also"]{.class_s36N} because 1) there are other graces in the Christian life (vs. 7) and 2) because it takes grace to give.

Paul wants them to give, not because they [have]{.class_s36M} to (["not by commandment"]{.class_s36N}). Paul says later on in 2 Corinthians 9:7, ["Not grudgingly, or of necessity."]{.class_s36N} Paul [does]{.class_s36M} want them to give because of the tremendous giving of ["the churches of Macedonia"]{.class_s36N} (["the frowardness of others"]{.class_s36N}). It is not so much "keeping up with the Joneses" as it is making sure that all of his converts grow alike. He doesn't want the growth of the Corinthians to be stunted by their "tight pockets." Giving also proves ["the sincerity of your love."]{.class_s36N} So Paul wanted the Corinthians to give so that they would get the same grace which the Macedonians had gained by their giving and to show their love for the brethren who would benefit by the gift.

Verse 9 is one of those key verses in the Corinthian epistles which should be committed to memory. This verse defines ["the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N} with which Paul closes nearly all of his epistles.

What is ["the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N}? ["That, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich."]{.class_s36N}[ That]{.class_s36M} is His grace. It took grace for Him to do that. It took [grace ]{.class_s36M}for Christ to leave heaven and to be born in a stable, to be humiliated by men, and to die on the cross. Moreover it took [grace ]{.class_s36M}for Him to do it for a bunch of sinners. So the acrostic used by preachers to describe [grace ]{.class_s36M}is very appropriate: God's Riches At Christ's Expense.

8:10 And herein I give my advice: for this is expedient for you, who have begun before, not only to do, but also to be forward a year ago.

11 Now therefore perform the doing of it; that as there was a readiness to will, so there may be a performance also out of that which ye have.

12 For if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not.

In verse 10, Paul gives ["advice"]{.class_s36N} which ["is expedient"]{.class_s36N} for the Corinthians. What Paul is about to say is not a commandment. When you hit a verse like this, some people tend to dismiss the verse because it is only Paul's "opinion." But you had better watch an attitude like that. In this case, Paul's ["advice"]{.class_s36N} is Holy Spirit-inspired Scripture. Paul calls it ["advice"]{.class_s36N} because giving it is a free-will matter in the Christian life or it does the giver no good (2 Cor. 9:7). It is [good]{.class_s36M} advice to [finish]{.class_s36M} what you [start]{.class_s36M} for Christ as Paul tells the Corinthians in verses 10--11. It is ["expedient"]{.class_s36N} to keep your vows to God. You will be better off, if you made up your mind to do something for Jesus Christ, to go ahead and [do it]{.class_s36M} (vs. 11).

Verse 12 is one of the greatest verses in the Bible to help a Christian to ascertain "the will of God." It should be committed to memory. If Bob Jones Sr. is right, a [successful ]{.class_s36M}Christian is one who finds out the will of God for his life and does it. And if you want to find out if you are [in]{.class_s36M} God's will, then verse 12 is the verse in which you want to live. If you want to see the contrast between true consecration to Jesus Christ and the modern "family-oriented" Christianity, read our comments under 2 Corinthians 8:12 in [Ruckman's Bible References]{.class_s36M} (Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1997).

[Verse 12 is the key to consecration.]{.class_s36M} If you want to find out if you are in God's will, at any point in your ministry, verse 12 is the place to "check your water." God does not require from you want He knows is impossible, but He will test you to see if you are [willing ]{.class_s36M}to try what you [think]{.class_s36M} is impossible. As long as the Christian is [flexible]{.class_s36M}---that is, willing to do a particular thing or willing to go to a particular place---[he is IN the will of God.]{.class_s36M} He is only in danger of getting out of God's will by resisting at some point and [refusing to be willing.]{.class_s36M}

In my preaching, you will quite often hear me refer to a controversy between you and God. I mention that because I notice that that is [the greatest thing I know in the life of any Christian. ]{.class_s36M}We get in trouble, and we reach the point where there is a [controversy ]{.class_s36M}between us and the Lord.[ That's Job.]{.class_s36M}

In your own life you watch those places where you begin to get bitter at God because you run out of money or you get sick or there is a death in the family or you don't get a place to preach or you get a bum grade on an examination. Those are the places you have to watch, because those are the places where the Lord is trying you. "Are you [willing ]{.class_s36M}to take this? Are you [willing ]{.class_s36M}to go through this?"

"Just suppose that after I call you to go through Bible school and you finish the training, I [don't]{.class_s36M} call you to preach. Just suppose I call you to do something else for a living? [Are you willing]{.class_s36M}?" That's it! I have met fellows who never surrendered [not]{.class_s36M} to preach. I've had young men who came through PBI who had their hearts so set on preaching that they were not surrendered [not]{.class_s36M} to preach. I have seen the Lord put a couple [out]{.class_s36M} of the ministry. They never yielded. It is being yielded and being [flexible]{.class_s36M}.

I'm a fine one to talk about that. I feel like a fool talking like that. Yet, knowing my own heart, I know how flexible I am in the hands of the Lord. I just don't [appear]{.class_s36M} flexible to other people.

When I speak of being flexible, moveable, and pliable in the hands of the Lord, I mean that you are ready to yield at any time there is a point between you and the Lord.

Be honest with the Lord. If you are [not ]{.class_s36M}willing, tell Him so, but don't do it with a defiant spirit. [Ask the Lord to make you willing. ]{.class_s36M}Be willing for Him to change your mind, and yield to Him when He does. You get into trouble when you have already made up your mind and are not willing to [change]{.class_s36M}.

8:13 For I mean not that other men be eased, and ye burdened:

14 But by an equality, that now at this time your abundance may be a supply for their want, that their abundance also may be a supply for your want: that there may be equality:

15 As it is written, He that had gathered much had nothing over; and he that had gathered little had no lack.

In the context here, Paul is saying that a year before the Corinthians had made up their minds to give toward the special offering Paul was taking up for the poor saints at Jerusalem. But at the time they didn't have the resources to give. So instead of giving what they could and allowing God to make up the rest, they just stopped giving.

But as Paul wrote 2 Corinthians, the Lord had given them extra resources (["at this time your abundance"]{.class_s36N}---vs. 14). So Paul is urging them to use the [extra ]{.class_s36M}God had given them to make up what they did not have before to give.

Now, Paul was [not]{.class_s36M} trying to ease the plight of the poor saints at Jerusalem to the hurt of the Corinthians (vs. 13). But what he is seeking is an ["equality"]{.class_s36N} between the brethren. Obviously, God had supplied an abundance to the Corinthians so they could help others at their time of need. The time would come when they, too, would need the help of other Christians, and the Lord would supply [their need]{.class_s36M} out of an abundance which He would give to[ another church.]{.class_s36M}

The word ["equality"]{.class_s36N} is found only in this verse in the Bible. It is not like the equality of the French Revolution ("Liberty, Fraternity, and Equality") which resulted in the "Reign of Terror" and the Napoleonic Wars. It has nothing to do with the socio-politico-economic musing of Karl Marx where "some are more equal than others." It is not Robin Hood in Sherwood Forest robbing from the rich and giving to the poor, and it is certainly not President Johnson's "War on Poverty."

[It is a group of Christians using the abundance God has given them to help other Christians in need]{.class_s36M}. It is completely [voluntary]{.class_s36M}. It is letting God use you to be a blessing to others.

Paul uses the story of the manna in Exodus 16 to illustrate his point (vs. 15 cf. Exod. 16:18). God supplied the [needs ]{.class_s36M}of the Israelites, and the "greeds" bred worms. The manna was supplied on a daily basis, ["every man according to his eating"]{.class_s36N} (Exod. 16:18). And when all was said and done, everybody had exactly what he needed to satisfy his hunger.

8:16 ¶ But thanks be to God, which put the same earnest care into the heart of Titus for you.

17 For indeed he accepted the exhortation; but being more forward, of his own accord he went unto you.

18 And we have sent with him the brother, whose praise is in the gospel throughout all the churches;

19 And not that only, but who was also chosen of the churches to travel with us with this grace, which is administered by us to the glory of the same Lord, and declaration of your ready mind:

Titus had come to love the Corinthians as much as Paul (vs. 16). When Paul needed someone to go to the Corinthians and to help them make up their offering before Paul arrived, Titus volunteered (vs. 17).

["The brother" ]{.class_s36N}of verse 18 is not named, but it is probably Silas. Silas was Paul's helper on the missionary journey through Corinth (vs. 18 cf. Acts 18:5). Silas was also chosen by the church at Jerusalem to deliver the decree of the council at Jerusalem to the Gentile churches while accompanying Paul (vs. 19 cf. Acts 15:25--27).

8:20 Avoiding this, that no man should blame us in this abundance which is administered by us:

21 Providing for honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men.

Paul was very careful with the money gathered in this offering. He did not want anyone to point to him or Titus and accuse them of stealing the money or [embezzlement ]{.class_s36M}(vs. 20). So Silas and Titus were a check on each other, and Paul was a [double-check]{.class_s36M} on both of them.

Every church should have a double- and triple-check on the funds of the church. No [one man]{.class_s36M} should have control of the money. A check should be signed by [more than one man. ]{.class_s36M}Always have it that way so there is no underhanded stuff. Even then there may be some underhanded stuff going on.

In some of these Southern Baptist churches, the deacon board gets their own fellows on the finance committee, and they use those men to control the church's funds. Sometimes the constitution of the church will have to be completely rewritten to straighten out that mess.

I have been in them. It must have been about the first week I ever preached in a church. A man came down the aisle at the end of the service and shook my hand, and he left a five dollar bill in my hand.

I asked, "What is this for?"

He replied, "It's for you."

"Why didn't you put it in the plate?" I asked.

He said, "Well, Brother Ruckman, if I put it in the plate in this church, I'm afraid it might not get to [you]{.class_s36M}."

That shocked me terribly. I didn't believe it for about four or five months. Then I ran into another case where a fellow had written a check to me personally. My name had been forged on the back by the leader of the church, and the check had been cashed without my knowledge.
