I have been in churches where the deacons have embezzled \$8,000 a year and have put it in a [separate fund]{.class_s36M} that didn't show up on the book balance: no one knew the fund existed except [the deacons]{.class_s36M} who had set it up. I've been in churches where the pastor left the church with \$13,000 of [bearer bonds ]{.class_s36M}from the church's bond program. The church couldn't get the money back because [anybody ]{.class_s36M}who bore the bonds and turned them in could cash them.

I have been in churches where the pastor went down to the bank and asked for money for somebody else---[then he clipped the bond coupons for himself and cashed them in.]{.class_s36M} I have seen a pastor, who had set up the church, borrow \$300,000 on his church, and the whole property wasn't worth \$85,000. He lied about the bench mark and the property line. [He had made the lender believe that the property had an acre more than it had.]{.class_s36M}

Now, that kind of stuff goes on in [Christian work,]{.class_s36M} day and night, all over this country. If a pastor is going to assure that nobody blames him in the ministry (vs. 20), then he is going to have to provide ["for honest things\...in the sight of men"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 21).

Here at the Bible Baptist Church and the Pensacola Bible Institute, we have a board of trustees, not deacons, that handle the money. We have a certified public accountant check the books every year. Just do right and there won't be any problem. Do everything aboveboard, and there won't be any problem.

8:22 And we have sent with them our brother, whom we have oftentimes proved diligent in many things, but now much more diligent, upon the great confidence which I have in you.

23 Whether any do inquire of Titus, he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you: or our brethren be inquired of, they are the messengers of the churches, and the glory of Christ.

24 Wherefore shew ye to them, and before the churches, the proof of your love, and of our boasting on your behalf.

Paul had great confidence in his missionary companions. They were examples of that maxim of Christ,[ "He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 16:10). Paul was able to say of the first brother, ["whom we have oftentimes proved diligent in many things"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 22). Because he was diligent in smaller things, Paul was confident that he would be diligent concerning the offering of the Corinthians (vs. 22).

Titus had proven to be a true friend to the Corinthians. Paul said of him, ["he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 23). Paul therefore urges the churches of Achaia to show Titus and his companions ["the proof of your love"]{.class_s36N}---love in action---the same way they had treated Paul when he was there. That is why he bragged about them (vs. 24).

[]{#c2M3.xhtml}

## Chapter 9 {.heading_s39}

This chapter is one of the greatest chapters in the Bible on [giving]{.class_s36M}. We tend to think of giving as a practical matter, but in this chapter, Paul is going to give us some great doctrinal truths about giving.

For instance, when we get down to verse 9, we will see how the New Testament doctrine of giving goes completely cross-grain to Dry Cleaner theology. We will discover the [greatest promise]{.class_s36M} ever found in print (vs. 8), and we will learn of the direct relationship of giving to [the love of God]{.class_s36M} (vs. 7).

So with those things in mind, let's begin chapter 9.

9:1 For as touching the ministering to the saints, it is superfluous for me to write to you:

2 For I know the forwardness of your mind, for which I boast of you to them of Macedonia, that Achaia was ready a year ago; and your zeal hath provoked very many.

3 Yet have I sent the brethren, lest our boasting of you should be in vain in this behalf; that, as I said, ye may be ready:

4 Lest haply if they of Macedonia come with me, and find you unprepared, we (that we say not, ye) should be ashamed in this same confident boasting.

5 Therefore I thought it necessary to exhort the brethren, that they would go before unto you, and make up beforehand your bounty, whereof ye had notice before, that the same might be ready, as a matter of bounty, and not as of covetousness.

The word ["superfluous"]{.class_s36N} in verse 1 means "more than enough" or "unnecessary." In other words, when it came to the topic of ["ministering to the saints,"]{.class_s36N} he had said more than enough and anything else would be unnecessary.

["Your zeal hath provoked very many"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2). Christians are to provoke each other to [good works.]{.class_s36M} Provoking each other to do the [right]{.class_s36M} thing is good. Every little bit helps. Instead of getting jealous or upset about it, just thank God for it.

When you stay stirred up, you stir other people up. When you stir other people up, even when they don't get right,[ they pretend to get right.]{.class_s36M} I have kept the Bible issue so stirred up that finally Pensacola Christian had to take a stand for the[ right Greek text.]{.class_s36M}

Now what has been going on in verses 1--5 is this. Paul had come to Macedonia to take up the offering they had. The Macedonians had become all excited when Paul had told them that Achaia had collected their offering over a year ago. Even though they could not afford it, they sacrificed until they too had a good offering to give Paul (2 Cor. 8:1--6).

Paul loved the Corinthians and bragged about their zeal, but he had the sneaking suspicion that they had not followed through on what they had started. So he was sending Titus and Silas ahead to make sure that the Corinthians collected what they had promised to give (vs. 5 cf. 8:23 and 8:16).

Of course, Paul was not concerned about getting his hands on the money (["not as of covetousness"]{.class_s36N}---vs. 5). Rather, he wanted a good offering to give to the poor saints at Jerusalem (["as a matter of bounty"]{.class_s36N}---vs. 5). Also, he wanted the reputation of the Corinthians to stand before visitors.

Paul was afraid that when he came to get the offering that some of the Macedonians would come with him and find out the offering was [not]{.class_s36M} ready. Paul was willing to take the blame for the Corinthians (vs. 4), but he really wanted them to live up to the good things he had said about them.

9:6 But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully.

7 Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver.

8 And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work:

9 (As it is written, He hath dispersed abroad; he hath given to the poor: his righteousness remaineth for ever.

This is a great passage which gives the New Testament standard on [giving]{.class_s36M}. I would definitely memorize verses 6--8. Verse 6 is known as the Law of Harvest. You should compare verse 6 with Galatians 6:7--9. The complete law states this:

1\. You reap [what]{.class_s36M} you sow (Gal. 6:7--8). If you sow to the corrupt flesh, you ["reap corruption"]{.class_s36N}; if you sow to the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of life, you ["reap life everlasting."]{.class_s36N}

2\. You always reap [more]{.class_s36M} than you sow (John 12:24).

3\. You reap according to [how]{.class_s36M} you sow (2 Cor. 9:6). If you only sow a little, you will reap a [small harvest]{.class_s36M}; if you sow a lot, you will reap a [big harvest.]{.class_s36M}

4\. You [will]{.class_s36M} reap (Gal. 6:9). It may not be down here. You may not see a harvest until the Judgment Seat of Christ. But [you will reap!]{.class_s36M}

Now, that's a [spiritual law]{.class_s36M}. Campus Crusade has a famous tract called "Four Spiritual Laws," and not one of them is a [real ]{.class_s36M}spiritual law. But verse 6 [is.]{.class_s36M} If you sow sparingly, you'll reap sparingly; if you sow bountifully, you'll reap bountifully.

That does not mean every seed you sow will bear [good fruit.]{.class_s36M} The crows may get some of the seeds. The worms and caterpillars may get some of the plants. The thorns and weeds might choke some of them. But it stands to reason that the more you sow, the more you will reap. In business, men get rich by investing their money, [not hoarding it.]{.class_s36M}

And now the standard, verse 7: ["Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give."]{.class_s36N} Technically speaking, there is no New Testament [minimum ]{.class_s36M}to give. Now, don't get me wrong. I believe in tithing. I think any New Testament Christian under grace ought to be able to do as much as an Old Testament Jew [under the Law.]{.class_s36M} I think God gave you examples of tithing, [before ]{.class_s36M}the Law, in Abraham (Gen. 14) and Jacob (Gen. 28). The Lord did not start blessing me financially, after my salvation, until I began to tithe.

But in the final analysis, New Testament giving lies between you and the Lord. You have to settle in your heart with the Lord what you are going to do, and He is going to reward you according to how you and He work the thing out. In my way of thinking, the trick is to find out how much you [can]{.class_s36M} give and [get away]{.class_s36M} with it.

One time I got rid of a hundred dollars, just to help someone out, without any tax exempt benefits. Within a week I received a hundred dollars in the mail from some guy of whom I had never heard before or since. Another time I passed off some money on the side, and I thought I was going to get away with it. When I came home from a meeting, the first thing my wife said was, "A check came in the mail today for such-and-such an amount."[ It was the exact amount I had given away!]{.class_s36M}

["Not grudgingly, or of necessity."]{.class_s36N} Pastor friend, reading this commentary, I am going to give you some good advice [from experience]{.class_s36M}. Don't force people to tithe. I pastored one church for twelve years (Brent Baptist) and another now (2002) for thirty years (Bible Baptist), and [I never put any pressure on them to tithe.]{.class_s36M} I have never had to. Somebody told me once, "Feed the sheep, and the sheep will feed you." I have and [they ]{.class_s36M}have!

Now, I have talked to them about giving. I have two sermons on giving that I have preached, maybe, a total of three times each over the last [twenty-seven years.]{.class_s36M} I have never said, "If you don't tithe, you're robbing God," or "Tithe or the Lord will take your kids." I do believe, though, that sometimes when trouble strikes a stingy Christian that God is collecting His tithe, and I have said as much. But I have never [threatened ]{.class_s36M}the non-tithers in my congregation.

I have found that if you preach the spiritual things that you will do more damage than the bare command. In the twelve years in which I pastored the Brent Baptist Church, we managed to pay off a total of[ \$325,000 of bad debts left by the former pastor.]{.class_s36M} When I left, the church was completely [out of debt]{.class_s36M}, and the church property was completely paid. As I said, I never had to force or threaten any member to give.

Brethren, if you can't trust the Lord with your [money]{.class_s36M}, how can you trust Him with your [soul]{.class_s36M}? The whole thing is that we value the money more than our souls. We [say]{.class_s36M} that we value our soul, but when "push comes to shove," [we can see the money]{.class_s36M}. You can buy things with money, you can pay the bills with money, you can see the money. [You can't see your soul.]{.class_s36M}

A fellow said one time, "What if you were offered a hundred dollars for every soul you led to Christ?" If that were so, don't you know there would be some people on fire for God! I know you believe ["For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?"]{.class_s36N} (Mark 8:36). You believe that. You believe the soul is more valuable than anything. But when you put the thing right on the scale and say a hundred dollars per soul, it would mean something more to you, [wouldn't it?]{.class_s36M}

I bet a bunch of you would quit your jobs and become [fulltime soulwinners.]{.class_s36M} You would be beating the bushes from morning to night. You would start at 7:00 in the morning and wouldn't quit until 7:00 in the evening. And yet, souls are worth more than the [money]{.class_s36M}. But that is how we are.

As I travel around to different churches, pastors ask me what I teach about tithes and offerings. I teach my people to find a good, Bible-believing church that they can support [with their tithes]{.class_s36M}. If they can't, then they had better put their money [somewhere else]{.class_s36M}. I would never tell anyone that his tithe should only go to a local church. What if you get into a town where there wasn't a local church worth blowing up? Would you put your money into it?

In Butte, Montana, there are three churches in town: Northern Baptist, Episcopal, and Mormon. What are you going to do? ["Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse"]{.class_s36N} (Mal. 3:10)? Not unless you've got bad sense. You [can]{.class_s36M} go [overboard]{.class_s36M} on the thing.

I teach people to find a [good church]{.class_s36M} that is doing the job and [put their tithe there]{.class_s36M}. After that they support missionaries and evangelists and poor saints and other works [with the offering]{.class_s36M}. I have always taught that the tithe was the [minimum]{.class_s36M}. That is a lot tougher than "storehouse tithing." I say that God is [entitled ]{.class_s36M}to 10 percent as a token gesture, and then, after that, [you start to give.]{.class_s36M}

Folks say, "Well, you've got plenty of money. You can afford to give." Yes, but you see, [I have sat where you are sitting.]{.class_s36M}

During the first year that I went to Bob Jones University, September and October, I didn't tithe. I was too poor to tithe. There wasn't any way that I could tithe. [I had bills]{.class_s36M}. I lived in a plywood trailer that had no bathroom in it; we had to use a communal bathroom. There was no running hot water, no refrigerator; [it had a kerosene stove]{.class_s36M}. I got to the place that I just couldn't tithe.

I finally got down to nothing. I had no food, and I sent my family back home. There was no food for them to eat, and [I had no food for three days.]{.class_s36M} I learned about fasting, but it wasn't a [voluntary ]{.class_s36M}fast. There just wasn't any food in that trailer!

On the third day I found an old, brown box of pancake mix in the back of one of the cupboards which had been left by the previous occupant a year before. I didn't have any milk or butter, so I mixed it with water and cooked it. [It tasted like glue.]{.class_s36M}

Some money came in, about \$3.00, but not enough to do any good. When that money came in, I took [thirty cents]{.class_s36M} and put it in a cup and placed the cup in the back of the cupboard. I told myself, "OK, I'm going to try tithing. [That's the Lord's. If I starve, I don't get that thirty cents."]{.class_s36M} The next day I walked out the door, and there was a cardboard box, filled with canned goods, bread, butter, and a quart of milk, in front of my trailer. So I started tithing, and for about six months everything went well.

Then I was called to go to a meeting, and I didn't have enough money for gas to get there. The car had about [four ]{.class_s36M}gallons of gas in it, and the meeting was 300 miles away. [There was \$10 in the tithe cup.]{.class_s36M} I said to myself, "Well, I've been through this thing before. The only thing to do is just leave the \$10 in the cup and go. If I run out of gas, that's just tough apples."

So I got in the car, and I left for the meeting. [When I left there was no milk in the ice box for the kids, Diana and David.]{.class_s36M} They were young then: Diana was about seven, and David was around a year and a half old.

I started down the road to that meeting, and around fifty miles later, I picked up a hitchhiker. I witnessed to him and found out that he was a Christian. We went on down the road and used up the [four gallons of gas.]{.class_s36M} The hitchhiker said to me, "I'd go on a little bit further, but I see you're getting a little low on gas there. Tell you what, I'll fill your tank up for you." I was able to fill that tank with enough gas to get to the meeting. After the meeting was over, I hurried back to the trailer, and [fifty dollars had come in the mail.]{.class_s36M}

Now, I don't know how many times you have seen those kinds of things, but I know it is something you have to settle between you and the Lord. I'm not going to tell you how to do it. I know what I went through, and I know how I settled it. And [by the grace of God]{.class_s36M} (I had better add that), it is settled.

Verse 8 says, ["And God is able to make all GRACE abound toward you."]{.class_s36N} I intend, [by the grace of God,]{.class_s36M} always to give[ above the tithe]{.class_s36M} until I go home to Glory. Lord willing, I am going to try to work my giving up to [twenty ]{.class_s36M}or even [thirty percent.]{.class_s36M}

["For God loveth a CHEERFUL giver"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). It's hard to give [cheerfully]{.class_s36M}, isn't it? Giving cheerfully is pictured by a man, laughing hysterically, who is throwing his money to the wind. A cheerful giver doesn't throw his money away ignorantly, but he does give it to the Lord [without a care for what he has left.]{.class_s36M}

Have you ever reached into your pocket when the plate comes by and discovered you had several bills in your pocket. You don't know what the denominations are, and God says, "Why don't you just trust me and give the first one that comes out?"

Every once in a while it is good to take some money and give it without it showing up on the books and without getting a [tax exemption]{.class_s36M} for it. Before you give it, ask the Lord where He wants you to give it. You will get a definite impression about where the Lord wants it to go. Then put it out, whether there is a record for it or not, and watch the Lord bless you for it.

["For the Lord LOVETH a cheerful giver."]{.class_s36N} Now, we know that God loves the whole world (John 3:16), but there are [degrees ]{.class_s36M}to God's love. Out of the twelve disciples, Jesus had three that were His "inner circle": Peter, James, and John. Of those three, He had one that was that disciple ["whom Jesus loved"]{.class_s36N} (John 13:23), and that disciple was John. He loved all twelve, but the closer certain disciples got to Jesus, the more of His love they experienced.

Do you want God to love you in a special way? Then start "shelling out." Here is your chance to shine: [start giving thirty percent and work your way up!]{.class_s36M}

Several things take place when you give cheerfully:

a\) You get back more than you give (vs. 6).

b\) You become a [personal blessing ]{.class_s36M}to the person to whom you give (vs. 12).

c\) You supply [their need]{.class_s36M}, and they attribute it to answered prayer (vss. 11, 14). In return, they [pray for you]{.class_s36M} and thank God for you!

d\) God gets the praise for answered prayer (vss. 11--12, 15).

How's that for "dividends"?

I have already told you about Tillman's lepers in Thailand asking for prayer requests from our church members. [That was in response to giving socks!]{.class_s36M} That just about put me out of the ministry. I have seen, on video, some of those lepers crawling 100--200 yards to church, dragging their mutilated legs behind them.

I remember receiving a letter from a "lifer" in prison at Raiford, Florida (2000). [He made twenty cents a day working in prison]{.class_s36M}. He saved up his tithe and sent it to the Bible Baptist Church here in Pensacola. He asked me for the name of a missionary he could support "since \[he\] made so much money": [it was \$80 a year.]{.class_s36M}

And those are the kinds of things that are going on while the [Christian celebrities ]{.class_s36M}are running \$20,000,000 building programs and bringing in \$200,000,000 a year. Brethren, are you [ever]{.class_s36M} going to see a reckoning at the Judgment Seat of Christ! You wouldn't believe it if I were to tell you!

That man may last, but never lives

Who much receives but little gives.

Whom none can love, whom none can thank

---Creation's blot---[Creation's BLANK.]{.class_s36M}

There is a [Christian celebrity]{.class_s36M} here in Pensacola (a typical, apostate Fundamentalist) who is so tight that you can hear him squeak when he walks by. I think his wife must give their cat salted peanuts to eat so it will drink water instead of milk.

Now we have come to one of the greatest promise in the entire Bible. There is only one other rival, and that is Romans 8:28. The difference between the two is that Romans 8:28 is unconditional, and 2 Corinthians 9:8 is conditional.

Verse 8 is saying that God will give you anything you [need]{.class_s36M}, to do [anything ]{.class_s36M}you want to do for Him, at [any time]{.class_s36M} you want to do it (vs. 8). But the trouble with that promise is that it is [conditional]{.class_s36M}. Romans 8:28 is for every Christian. Second Corinthians 9:8 is conditioned upon a Christian giving bountifully and cheerfully.

In verse 9, Paul quotes Psalm 112:9. Now, when it comes to [salvation,]{.class_s36M} Christ Himself is our righteousness (Rom. 10:1--4). But to the dismay of the Bereans, the Christian [has a body of righteousness separate from Christ's righteousness.]{.class_s36M} It is righteousness based upon his [works. ]{.class_s36M}Verse 9 is a prime example. Whenever you help out a poor Christian or a needy Christian, the Lord puts that act down as [your]{.class_s36M} [righteousness]{.class_s36M}. (See comments on 2 Cor. 5:3 in this commentary.)

9:10 Now he that ministereth seed to the sower both minister bread for your food, and multiply your seed sown, and increase the fruits of your righteousness;)

11 Being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness, which causeth through us thanksgiving to God.

12 For the administration of this service not only supplieth the want of the saints, but is abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God;

13 Whiles by the experiment of this ministration they glorify God for your professed subjection unto the gospel of Christ, and for your liberal distribution unto them, and unto all men;

14 And by their prayer for you, which long after you for the exceeding grace of God in you.

15 Thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift.

There are two ways of interpreting verse 10, and [both]{.class_s36M} are right. The first is a literal interpretation. The same God who supplies the farmer with seed to plant can cause that seed to grow and be turned into bread. In turn, he provides that bread to those who faithfully serve Him and prospers the seeds they plant in the ground. When you give to those in need (vs. 9), He can ["increase the fruits of your righteousness."]{.class_s36N} So God gives you the seed to plant so you can feed yourself and the poor who need it, and then He gives you rewards for doing right by what He gave you in the first place.

The [spiritual application]{.class_s36M} is even stronger. The seed is the word of God (Mark 4:14). The sower is Christ (Matt. 13:37). But since you are Christ's ambassador in this age (2 Cor. 5:20), then it is Christ sowing the word of God [through you.]{.class_s36M} So the Lord is providing you with the Scriptures to put out. In turn, He feeds you with His words (Matt. 4:4---["Man shall not live by bread alone, but by EVERY WORD that proceedeth out of the mouth of God"]{.class_s36N}). God takes the word of God that you put out and brings people to Christ and helps provoke other Christians to good works and gives you righteousness that bears fruit. (See comments in chapter 11 of the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Ecclesiastes,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL, 1994.)

["Being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). Christ said, ["Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure that ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 6:38).

Now, the problem with all of that is the Char­ismatics wrest these verses out of their context to prove a "health and wealth," "prosperity" Gospel. If you give in order just to get you are [not]{.class_s36M} giving cheerfully; you are giving [greedily]{.class_s36M}, out of a heart of [covetousness.]{.class_s36M} Moreover, the promise of verse 8 is to ["abound to every good work,"]{.class_s36N} so ["being enriched in every thing to all bountifulness"]{.class_s36N} is the supply for service to Christ, [not personal wealth or prosperity.]{.class_s36M} And, of course, the idea that God has to pay you back on [your]{.class_s36M} time schedule is sheer nonsense. The Lord may give it back to you a little at a time, as you have need.

Again, one of the results of giving is to bring ["thanksgiving to God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). The poor saints who receive your gift thank God for using you to supply their need (vs. 12). When God "pays you back" for giving to Him, then you, too, thank God (vs. 11).

And that is really what heaven is all about. Up there [in heaven]{.class_s36M}, the saints and the angels are engaged in [continuous praise to God.]{.class_s36M}

Every time we come before ["the throne of grace"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 4:16), we have a petition: "Lord, I need this. I want this. I have to have this. Give me this, give me that, give me the other thing." You know, it's a wonder that the Lord doesn't get put out with us. He is very patient, you know.

[But up there it is]{.class_s36M}: "Praise God! Glory to God! Hallelujah to the Lamb! Praise the Lord!" Then all of a sudden I show up.

"Who's that outside?"

"Peter Ruckman."

"Well, send him in! What praises do you have, brother?"

"Well, Lord, I need this, and I've got to have that, and if You don't do this by\...."

Now, He told you to ["obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 4:16). And He told you to ["let your requests be made known unto God"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 4:6). But isn't it a shame that we have to take so much time [asking for things]{.class_s36M} when we ought to be [praising God]{.class_s36M}?

It would be better to step into the throne room, and when the Lord asks you want you want, to say,"I just wanted to stop by and say, 'Hallelujah!'" [You should do that once in a while.]{.class_s36M}

Every Christian ought to have the experience of parking his car in an empty field somewhere and going out there all by himself (making sure that no one else is around to hear him) and start walking around in a circle and praising God until he is hoarse. How's that for good Baptist doctrine?

The whole point is that [you should cause somebody to praise God]{.class_s36M}, and giving to meet someone else's need does that.

Now, in verse 13, the recipients of the Corinthians' gift are glorifying God for two things. They rejoiced in the Corinthians' generosity toward them, and they rejoiced because that generosity was evidence that the Corinthians were in subjection to the Lord.

One of the outstanding marks of a Christian's salvation is his [liberality]{.class_s36M}. He learns how to give.

I hear preachers talk all of the time about the fruits of being saved: "You're not saved if you don't get baptized; you're not saved if you don't do this; you're not saved if you don't do that." Well, I'll tell you, brother; one of the surest marks you have never been saved is a [tight, stingy attitude.]{.class_s36M}

If God has really forgiven you, and [you know it,]{.class_s36M} then you tend to be more liberal with other folks. You tend to go a little easier on folks than you did before you were saved. If you're saved and in subjection to God, you learn to be liberal in [your giving]{.class_s36M}. You're not as tight-fisted as you used to be.

I heard of a missionary who went to a business man one time and asked him for a donation of \$500. The businessman thought about it a while and wrote out a check for \$500. He started to give it to the missionary; then suddenly he yanked it back and tore it up. The missionary's heart sank, but after about five minutes, the businessman handed him a new check for \$5,000. The missionary asked, "What happened?"

The businessman replied, "Well, I wasn't always as liberal as I am now. I was a stingy, tight-fisted man before I was saved. Ever since I got saved I've been trying to be unselfish and give, and the Devil has been working me over. So I made a contract with the Lord that every time the Devil gave me a hard time about a [gift that I would give more just to shut him up."]{.class_s36M}

["Your professed subjection unto the gospel of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13). If a man is in subjection to the Lord, [the Lord runs his pocketbook]{.class_s36M}. Maybe it would be a good idea when a fellow gets baptized that [he leave his wallet in his pants.]{.class_s36M}

When you give, you cause the recipients of your gift to pray for you (vs. 14). I'm mercenary about that. I have given money to an orphanage in Haiti just to get those children there praying for me. I figure God is liable to hear [their ]{.class_s36M}prayers before He will hear some Americans.

Now, all of our giving is based on God's giving to us (vs. 15). The gift is ["unspeakable,"]{.class_s36N} not in the sense that you can't talk about it; but there is no way that you can adequately describe it in words. ["The gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 6:23).

[]{#c2RG.xhtml}

## Chapter 10 {.heading_s39}

10:1 Now I Paul myself beseech you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ, who in presence am base among you, but being absent am bold toward you:

2 But I beseech you, that I may not be bold when I am present with that confidence, wherewith I think to be bold against some, which think of us as if we walked according to the flesh.

3 For though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh:

4 (For the weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds;)

5 Casting down imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ;

6 And having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience, when your obedience is fulfilled.

Up to chapter 10, Paul has been rather mild with the Corinthians, [not]{.class_s36M} like he was in his first epistle. He has commended them for their obedience in the matter of 1 Corinthians 5. He has encouraged them in their giving. But in chapters 10--13, things change.

The main problem with which Paul deals in these chapters is tolerance of false teachers. Note: ["another Jesus," "another spirit" ]{.class_s36N}(2 Cor. 11:4), and ["Satan...his ministers"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 11:14--15). Throughout all four chapters, Paul is contrasting his ministry with theirs.

In verse one, Paul is being sarcastic, but he is quoting the Corinthians (cf. vs. 10). They thought Paul's ["bodily presence"]{.class_s36N} was ["base"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1) and ["weak"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10). They thought Paul was only a "big talker" in his letters, where he didn't have to confront them face to face.

Now, it was not Paul's desire to get "rough" with them. In verse 2 he is begging them, in plain language, so they will get right before He arrives so he will not have to treat them as he did a lot of God's enemies.

In verses 2--3, there are three states of the Christian in relation to his flesh:

1\. ["We walked according to the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2).

2\. ["We walk in the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3).

3\. ["We do not war after the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3).

Physically, every Christian is ["in the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:8). That is why Paul said ["we walk in the flesh,"]{.class_s36N} because, [physically]{.class_s36M}, every Christian, this side of death and the Rapture, [lives in a body of flesh.]{.class_s36M}

But spiritually, no Christian is ["in the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:9). The ["new creature"]{.class_s36N} of 2 Corinthians 5:17 is joined to the Lord (1 Cor. 6:17); he is seated ["in heavenly places"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 2:6). [He is cut loose from the flesh]{.class_s36M} (Col. 2:11--12).

Now, depending on how the Christian reckons the old man of the flesh (Rom. 6:11) and depending on the nature to which he yields himself (Rom. 6:13), he will either have a new walk (Eph. 4:17), or he will ["live after the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:13), which fits ["walked according to the flesh"]{.class_s36N} of verse 2.

The Corinthians thought that Paul was being carnal when he had to get rough with them. But Paul was saying that although he lived daily in a body of flesh, he wasn't going to get "physical" when it came to correcting the Corinthians---["we do not war after the flesh."]{.class_s36N}

When it came to Paul's war with the sins of the Corinthians (specifically the false teachers in the next chapter), everything was strictly on a [spiritual]{.class_s36M} level.

Verses 4 and 5 are some more of those verses in the Corinthians epistles which the Christian should memorize.

Every Christian is a [soldier]{.class_s36M}; every Christian is at [war ]{.class_s36M}(2 Tim. 2:3--4; Eph. 6:10--12). So 2 Corinthians 10:4 applies to every Christian.

["The weapons of our warfare are not carnal."]{.class_s36N} As Christians, we don't fight with clubs, guns, knives, grenades, spears, arrows, or bullets. The weapons of the Christian are [spiritual]{.class_s36M}. They are the word of God (Eph. 6:17) and prayer (Eph. 6:18). Those weapons are ["mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds."]{.class_s36N}

Again, those ["strong holds"]{.class_s36N} are not castles, forts, battery emplacements, or machine gun nests. So what are they? Verse 5, ["imaginations."]{.class_s36N} You need something to get inside of you and get rid of an unclean imagination. Jesus said, ["Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth"]{.class_s36N} (John 17:17), and ["Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you"]{.class_s36N} (John 15:3). The Psalmist said, ["Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way? by taking heed thereto according to thy word"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 119:9).

Another of the ["strong holds"]{.class_s36N} in verse 5 is ["every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God."]{.class_s36N} Anything in this world designed to keep you from knowing God better can be fought with prayer and the word of God.

After the ["strong holds"]{.class_s36N} have been "cast down," then the last step is ["bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ."]{.class_s36N} That is sinless perfection, if you can get it. If you are in [constant prayer ]{.class_s36M}and you are committing [everything ]{.class_s36M}to God (i.e., all thoughts, twenty-four hours a day) in subjection to Christ; i.e., you reckon your body of sin dead, and Christ running the body completely), that is as near [sinless ]{.class_s36M}as you can get. You will still have sin [in ]{.class_s36M}you, you will still have the old nature [in ]{.class_s36M}you, but as far as victory over sin and conquering sin, that is as close as you can get.

I imagine Paul got just as close as anybody ever did. Of all the fellows about whom I've read, I think Paul and John Wesley just about obtained it. But there are not many.

Solomon said, ["Commit thy works unto the LORD, and thy thoughts shall be established"]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 16:3). If you are a Christian who doesn't drink, doesn't smoke, doesn't dance, doesn't chew, and doesn't "go with those who do," you know that the trouble you have is in your head. [Worry ]{.class_s36M}is in your thoughts. You start to lose your temper in your [thoughts]{.class_s36M}. Unclean thoughts lead to sin. If I could just cut off my head, I would get rid of my problems.

But if you are going to [live]{.class_s36M} for Christ, you are going to fight [wrong thinking]{.class_s36M} with the word of God and prayer. There is no other way to do it. No Christian can live a victorious life without a prayer life or without knowing and practising the Scriptures.

I go to meetings in the summer time and deal with young people about various problems. My advice to them is learn Scripture that deals with their problems and use it when tempted. But when I return the next year, many of those same people will come to me still being defeated by their sin.

"Did you learn those verses I gave you?" I'll ask.

And they will look at me and say,[ "What verses?"]{.class_s36M}

They can't give me any verses concerning their problems because they haven't [learned ]{.class_s36M}any. They haven't done what I told them to do.

I tell a fellow, "When you get back home, get in the Bible. Learn the Bible. Get armed, get fortified, get some ammo, [get in the Book]{.class_s36M}!" And the kid goes back, and the Bible sits ignored month after month. The Devil attacks, but the kid has no defense, so he goes down. The next year I'll have that same conversation over again.

Years ago, there was a young man named Johnny Todd who claimed to have gotten saved out of witchcraft. Because of his sensational testimony, a bunch of charismatic churches put him on the evangelistic circuit giving his testimony. That fellow was in a bad situation. He had been given no time to grow or get any victories in his life. Instead, he was thrust immediately into a ministry for which he wasn't prepared.

One day I got a phone call from him from Huntsville, Alabama. "Jack Chick phoned you about me, didn't he?"

"Yes," I said.

He went on, "Well, Jack Chick told me to phone you, and maybe you could help me."

"What is your trouble?" I asked.

He said, "I've been fooling with witchcraft. I've been saved now for five years, and I've been trying to preach. I get up to preach, and the people laugh at me and don't pay any attention to what I say. Something is wrong with my ministry. I had to get out of Los Angeles. There were some contracts out on me from the people in witchcraft. I'm up here on a farm in Alabama, and they haven't bothered me since I got here. But I've got a calendar here of the things that are going to happen, and they're coming through on the money, on time. I know there's a contract on me somewhere, and they still have me under a hex out in California."

I said,[ "Have you learned any Scripture?"]{.class_s36M}

"Probably not as much as you've learned," he said. "Jack Chick said I probably couldn't learn as much as you've learned."

"Did you learn [any]{.class_s36M}?" I asked. "Can you give me a verse on what you're going through?"

"Well, no, I can't," he replied, "but I know these things\...." And he went on and on about all the supposed plans of the Druids, witches, Illuminati, etc.

["Do you know how to plead the blood?"]{.class_s36M} I asked.

He said, "I was with a deliverance group. A pastor in San Antonio led me to Christ, and this deliverance group was a Full Gospel Fellowship."

I said, ["How do you plead the blood?"]{.class_s36M}

"Well, I know how to claim the blood of Christ for my sins," he said, "but there is something wrong with that Full Gospel Fellowship. I got all messed up. I had to get out of it. Those folks aren't right."

"Do you know [why]{.class_s36M} they're not right?" I said.

He said, "Well, no, I don't know [why]{.class_s36M} they're not right."

I said, "That's your trouble. You don't know your Bible. Why don't you come down here and [study the Bible]{.class_s36M} for a while?"

"I'm afraid to leave," he said. "I don't want to do anything anymore unless I know absolutely what I'm doing. I've made so many mistakes. I've been shot at twice. I know they're out after me, and I don't want to do anything unless I'm sure about it."

I said, "If you wait until you're absolutely sure, you won't be living by faith. ['The just shall live by faith.']{.class_s36N}"

I prayed with him a while over the phone and then hung up. Johnny Todd never did learn the Bible here or anywhere else. He ended up making a mess of some churches, his ministry, and his life.

One of the best things that he could have done would have been to have taken that calendar and to have burned it. If you start checking out a thing like that, the Devil will make sure that enough things come true to trap you. You will be going by a thing that is not totally accurate. About the time you begin to make decisions based on it, Satan will spring the trap and destroy you. That Bible says, ["Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof"]{.class_s36N} (Matt. 6:34).

As a Christian you will have to learn when the old imaginations and thoughts rise up to say, "Lord Jesus, I want you to take this thought in my mind and [wash it in the blood of Christ.]{.class_s36M} Soak it in the blood of Christ. Take it and [submerge ]{.class_s36M}it in the blood of Christ. Just fill my head with the blood of Jesus Christ. Wash my soul and spirit in the blood of Christ. I want to [soak ]{.class_s36M}in the blood of Jesus Christ." If you do that, you will watch those things shrivel up and die. Boy, you talk about a laser beam and psychiatric treatment! You just watch [that thing]{.class_s36M} go out.

You say, "Well, I've tried it, and it doesn't seem to work." You didn't try it very hard or for very long. I know how it goes. You get in that place where you just get ready to give in to sin and then you try to stop it [by yourself]{.class_s36M}. Or worse, you get in that place where you toy with it: "I'll stop in a minute," "I'll think about it just for another minute."

And, of course, it is not merely doing right. You need to be ready to correct what you have done wrong when you do right. That is "taking vengeance" on yourself (vs. 6). The old time Methodists called it making restitution for your sin. Paul discusses it in more detail in Ephesians 4:24--32.

10:7 Do ye look on things after the outward appearance? If any man trust to himself that he is Christ's, let him of himself think this again, that, as he is Christ's, even so are we Christ's.

8 For though I should boast somewhat more of our authority, which the Lord hath given us for edification, and not for your destruction, I should not be ashamed:

I have already discussed looking ["on things after the outward appearance"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7) under 2 Corinthians 5:16 (see comments there). What the Corinthians were doing was looking at Paul's ["outward appearance"]{.class_s36N} and saying, "Well, how can he be an apostle, how can he be saved, looking like [that]{.class_s36M}?"

But Paul urges them to look in themselves. If they believe that they belong to Jesus Christ, then the same thing in which they are trusting is the same thing in which he and his companions are trusting. So if the Corinthians are confident of their own salvation, they must acknowledge the salvation of Paul and his companions.

The issue, too, was more than salvation; it was [his authority as an apostle]{.class_s36M}. Paul is getting ready over the next few chapters to defend his apostleship. Of course, whenever you defend your ministry or report successes for your ministry, your enemies will always accuse you of boasting or bragging. So Paul says, "I am going to boast about the authority God has given to me, and I won't be ashamed about doing it." God had given the apostles for ["the edifying of the body of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 4:11--12 cf. vs. 8), but the Corinthians had been told by others that all Paul wanted to do was mess up the church at Corinth (["for your destruction,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 8).

10:9 That I may not seem as if I would terrify you by letters.

10 For his letters, say they, are weighty and powerful; but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible.

11 Let such an one think this, that, such as we are in word by letters when we are absent, such will we be also in deed when we are present.

The problem the Corinthians were having was that there was a group of people working on them that [didn't like Paul]{.class_s36M}. So they would cut Paul down to the Corinthians and follow it up with how good [they were]{.class_s36M}. The Corinthians had listened to them and had taken [them ]{.class_s36M}for apostles, while losing faith in the one who led them to the Lord. So Paul spends the next two chapters "bragging" about his ministry.

Look at 10:17, ["he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord."]{.class_s36N} Look at 12:1--5; he's glorying in [himself]{.class_s36M}. Look at 11:30; look at 11:10; look at 11:18. Paul is forced to boast of his ministry, but he tempers that boasting by saying, ["Yet as a fool receive me, that I may boast myself a little"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 11:16). All he is actually doing is giving his credentials and then comparing them to those of his detractors.

Now, Paul's enemies had one main thing against him; their attacks are in 10:10 and 11:6. Paul's enemies didn't like the way he [talked.]{.class_s36M} They found ["his speech contemptible"]{.class_s36N} (10:10), and they thought him to ["be rude in speech"]{.class_s36N} (11:6). Paul was [rude ]{.class_s36M}and [crude ]{.class_s36M}in his preaching (1 Cor. 2:4). If you don't believe it, look at Acts 26:27 where he interrupts a king, after asking him a question, [and then puts his own words in the king's mouth without the king's permission]{.class_s36M}. That's "bad manners."

You are living in a day and age where people think if a man [talks ]{.class_s36M}nice, he's spiritual. I'm not trying to encourage you to be uncouth, rude, or nasty. If you're not that way by nature, don't put on airs and pretend to be something that you're not. But what you want to remember is this: the [real trouble makers]{.class_s36M} and [church splitters]{.class_s36M} and purveyors of false doctrine always use ["good words and fair speeches]{.class_s36N} \[to\][ deceive the hearts of the simple"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 16:17--18). So although Paul's speech may be rude and contemptible, [he is right]{.class_s36M}, and the guys with the ["good words and fair speeches"]{.class_s36N} are [wrong]{.class_s36M}.

I probably get more criticism for my vulgarity and my slang than anything else in the ministry. But I use that kind of language [on purpose;]{.class_s36M} I do it [deliberately.]{.class_s36M} I do it to turn a man's stomach and turn him the other way. [I do it to make a man get away from the truth and leave it alone and go to hell]{.class_s36M}, or else accept the word of God regardless of the [manner ]{.class_s36M}in which it is presented.

Your acceptance of truth cannot be based upon your [feelings ]{.class_s36M}or your [likes ]{.class_s36M}or your [dislikes]{.class_s36M}. If you accept the truth because you like me or reject the truth because you[ don't ]{.class_s36M}like me, you don't need the truth, and you have no business with it. So I [write ]{.class_s36M}the way that I write to take the guy who doesn't really want the truth, but merely wants to politic and make friends so he will make a name for himself, and drop-kick him into hell if he is lost [and into Rome if he is saved.]{.class_s36M}

[That's my ministry]{.class_s36M}. I didn't say it was [yours]{.class_s36M}. You do what God wants you to do, and I'll do what God wants me to do. And [that's]{.class_s36M} what God wants me to do. What God wants me to do is get rid of those who are just following Christ for "the loaves and the fishes" (John 6:66--69). The Lord wants me to kick and spit so hard that if the guy has any motive for coming to the truth other than[ the truth]{.class_s36M}, he can't stand it. That is the way the Lord wants [me ]{.class_s36M}to do it.

For comments on Paul's ["bodily presence,"]{.class_s36N} see the comments on 2 Corinthians 1:14 and 6:5.

Now, without a doubt, Paul's letters were[ "powerful"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 10). They were so strong that Paul was afraid that what he wrote would ["terrify"]{.class_s36N} the Corinthians (vs. 9). But Paul's enemies told the Corinthians that when Paul arrived they would see the difference between his ["weighty and powerful"]{.class_s36N} letters and his weak body and contemptible speech. But the apostle assures them in verse 11 that, if that's what they want, he will be just as rough with them [in person]{.class_s36M} as he is in his letters. He won't [talk ]{.class_s36M}any different than he [writes]{.class_s36M}.

10:12 For we dare not make ourselves of the number, or compare ourselves with some that commend themselves: but they measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves among themselves, are not wise.

13 But we will not boast of things without our measure, but according to the measure of the rule which God hath distributed to us, a measure to reach even unto you.

14 For we stretch not ourselves beyond our measure, as though we reached not unto you: for we are come as far as to you also in preaching the gospel of Christ:

15 Not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours; but having hope, when your faith is increased, that we shall be enlarged by you according to our rule abundantly,

Verse 12 is one of the greatest verses in the entire New Testament and should be committed to memory. You are a fool if you compare yourself to Christians around you. Your sights are too low.

There is nothing wrong with [following ]{.class_s36M}another Christian. Paul said, ["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 11:1). It is all right to emulate a Christian's good works and good deeds. You can use other Christians as an example to study them and learn from them. But you never will get the value of yourself by [comparing]{.class_s36M} yourself with other Christians.

What people will do is pick another Christian who is [inferior]{.class_s36M} to them in some way so that they can[ justify themselves]{.class_s36M}. They find a Christian who is not as good as [they ]{.class_s36M}are, and by comparison, they look better. That, in turn, makes them [feel ]{.class_s36M}better.

There are Christians who spend all of their lives trying to dig into some other Christian's background to dig up some dirt so they can feel better [themselves]{.class_s36M}. It's a wild scene; I have never understood it. I never could get interested in digging up dirt in the first place. The more you dig into human nature, the more you dig into depravity. You can examine the best person in the world, and if you dig, pretty soon you will uncover [humus]{.class_s36M}. You will find garbage because that is what they are. If you are doing that, just expect on being one of the sorriest Christians alive. There is something about a Christian when he finally has an [alibi to sin ]{.class_s36M}that just makes him worthless.

Now, if you want to compare yourself to someone, I'll tell you how to do it. Get [The Journal of John Wesley]{.class_s36M} and read it through. Then compare your ministry with [his]{.class_s36M}. Next, get a good biography of Martin Luther and read it through. Compare your ministry with [his]{.class_s36M}. That is why a Christian should learn church history. Church history is the most valuable thing there is, outside of Bible study, because in church history you get to compare yourself with some of the [best]{.class_s36M} Christians that ever lived.

Compare yourself with someone like John Paton, the missionary to the New Hebrides Islands in the South Pacific. His wife died overseas, and he buried both of his children. Then he lost the mission station and all of his papers, and his Bible was burned up. But he stayed on the field, and when he finished his ministry there, [every adult on the island was a professing Christian]{.class_s36M}. If you want to compare yourself, try [that ]{.class_s36M}one. Get a [big]{.class_s36M} one.

You ladies, get one like Susanna Wesley. She raised seventeen children. She taught herself Greek and Hebrew. She wouldn't let the children raise their voice around the house. Get a big one: one at which you have to really work to reach. I wouldn't do it any other way.

When I set my goals as a Bible teacher, I go after men like Donald Grey Barnhouse, Clarence Larkin, Pember, and Bullinger. I don't waste my time listening to any [modern ]{.class_s36M}Bible "teacher" on the FM stations.

A famous artist said one time, "I never look very long at a bad picture. I don't want to hurt my style." If you are a musician, listening to [good music]{.class_s36M} will improve your style; listening to [bad music ]{.class_s36M}will hurt your style.

Now, in regards to verse 12, you had better keep your spiritual eyes fixed on Jesus Christ (Heb. 12:2). That will be the hardest thing you have ever had to do in your Christian life. If you want to get in trouble, then compare yourself to others and justify your shortcomings, and you will fit right into Proverbs 30:12. But remember this, one day you will stand [alone ]{.class_s36M}before God (Rom. 14:12). When that day comes, you aren't going to worry about how your neighbor did. The Lord won't care one bit about how you stacked up to anyone but His Son. So you had better be concerned about what God thinks of you than what [you ]{.class_s36M}think of [yourself ]{.class_s36M}(2 Cor. 10:18).

God has given each of us a job to do as a Christian. Moreover, God has set a measure to that job. He knows what you can do at it, and He knows what you can't do at it. Paul says, ["According to the measure of the rule which God hath distributed to us, a measure to reach even unto you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13). So the Corinthians were within [God's measure]{.class_s36M} for what God had given Paul to do (vs. 14).

Paul wasn't going to boast about what God hadn't given him to do. Verse 16 refers to the measure of what God has you to do as a ["line."]{.class_s36N} My line goes to a certain place, and I have to stay in [my line]{.class_s36M}. I don't bother [another man's line.]{.class_s36M} Paul said in Galatians 6:4,[ "But let every man prove his own work, and then shall he have rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another."]{.class_s36N}

Your measure will be limited by what you can [know]{.class_s36M} and what you can [do ]{.class_s36M}and what you are [willing]{.class_s36M} to do.

You will be limited by those three things. So prepare yourself, and the Lord will give you a line. As you start going down that line, [the measure ]{.class_s36M}of that thing God has given you to do will stretch out.

When it came to the Corinthians, Paul's ministry to them was within the boundaries of God's measure (vs. 14). He was the one who had given them the Gospel and started the church there.

["Not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15). In the ministry, a preacher should be careful to give credit where credit is due. I went up to a meeting at Rochester, New York, when Bro. Jim Modlish was pastoring there. In that meeting, we saw 157 conversions. That was [not]{.class_s36M} all due to my preaching at one meeting. Three were saved on a Friday night; twenty-five were saved Sunday morning. Brother Modlish preached in another building and saw forty-five saved. Ninety were saved in four other buildings:[ that was the work of other people in those departments.]{.class_s36M}

When I get in a meeting like that, and the Lord gives a harvest like that, I praise the Lord for it. But the people from that church had been knocking on doors and passing out tracts and putting out circulars and pounding the highways of Rochester for six months prior to that meeting.[ It wasn't all me.]{.class_s36M}

Pastor Modlish stopped a doctor and asked him to come to the meeting. The doctor snapped back, "Would you get off my back."

Modlish said, "Well, this is the first time I've ever talked to you."

"Listen," the doctor said, "Fourteen of your members have been by in the last six months to invite me to come to that meeting."

[Those people were working]{.class_s36M}. That was their [labor]{.class_s36M}. The credit, humanly speaking, for those 157 conversions goes as much to them as it does to me.

John R. Rice was bad about that kind of thing. To listen to what he said and to read what he wrote, you would think that all of these big, Fundamental Baptist works like Hyles', Batema's, Falwell's, and Vick's were the result of his [preaching ]{.class_s36M}and the [Sword of the Lord.]{.class_s36M} I've no doubt that through his tracts and the [Sword]{.class_s36M} he was able to motivate a lot of folks into the Lord's work. But those big churches were built by bus captains and personal workers, week in and week out, making the contacts and bringing the kids in. That's how those churches grew. It wasn't some guy hawking his newspaper, books, and tapes with "These messages changed 10,000 lives!" Horseradish, kiddies!

I'll tell you what it is. It is John 4:38, ["Ye are entered into their labours."]{.class_s36N} Sometimes the Lord will let you [reap]{.class_s36M}; sometimes He will let you [sow]{.class_s36M}. But there are others laboring besides you (1 Cor. 3:8).

["But having hope, when your faith is increased, that we shall be enlarged by you according to our rule abundantly"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15). Paul's hope is that by telling them what he had done as an apostle and what his plans were that their faith would increase and that they would help him to preach the Gospel beyond them.

10:16 To preach the gospel in the regions beyond you, and not to boast in another man's line of things made ready to our hand.

17 But he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord.

18 For not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth.

Verse 16 is a continuation of thought from verse 15. With the Corinthian church living right, that would free Paul to concentrate on taking the Gospel to Rome and then Spain.

A few years ago, Modlish's church in Rochester was the only big church doing anything. Before he left and went out west, he planted two more churches within thirty miles of Rochester. Today there is a Bible-believing church near Buffalo (Lockport), and there are about four more in New York and Long Island.

That is the way Modlish is. He will start a work and build it up. Then he will train some men and send them one at a time to other cities to set up churches in the surrounding region. He isn't going to be like Jack Hyles and try to build a gigantic work by bussing them in from hundreds of miles away. [You spread out several works]{.class_s36S}. That is the way to do it: let it keep spreading.

["Not to boast in another man's line of things."]{.class_s36N} Note the modern usage of the term "handing someone a line," "What's your line?", and "a line of goods." It's from a [King James Bible.]{.class_s36M} So don't be so ready to dismiss that Book as archaic.

What Paul wasn't going to do (vs. 16) was step into another man's work, where the fellow had gotten everything ready for him ahead of time, and claim that work as his own. I have discovered in evangelistic and Bible conference work that a lot of work has been done before I arrive. And if not much work has been done, not much is going to happen in the meeting. [You don't carry revival in your back pocket.]{.class_s36M}

["But he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 17). The real work is done by God anyway, so you had better be careful to give Him credit first. It should be "[The Lord]{.class_s36M} gave me the souls, [the Lord]{.class_s36M} did the work, [the Lord]{.class_s36M} did this or that."

Rice brags, "These sermons changed 10,000 lives." That's a stupid thing to say, no matter [who]{.class_s36M} said it. [There isn't a sermon that anybody ever preached that changed anybody's life.]{.class_s36M}

If some of you who follow my ministry have had your life [changed ]{.class_s36M}during a sermon I have preached or a lesson I have taught, I will tell you [who]{.class_s36M} and [what]{.class_s36M} did it.[ It was the Lord and the Book that did it, and if they didn't do it, it didn't get done]{.class_s36M}. I have preached the same message in different places, and in one place folks will get saved and get right. In another place, no one budges.

The Lord can [use]{.class_s36M} a message, but the message doesn't change people. The [Lord]{.class_s36M} changes people. Folks mistake [the tool]{.class_s36M} for the [Carpenter]{.class_s36M}. Don't ever do that.

Verse 18 is one of those great verses in the Corinthians that should be committed to memory. ["For not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth."]{.class_s36N} You can pat yourself on the back and toot your own horn all you want, and if the Lord doesn't think much of you, you're nothing. If the Lord calls you to do a job, the Lord will put His own commendation on you. When [the Lord]{.class_s36M} commends you, what everyone else says, including [yourself]{.class_s36M}, doesn't make any difference.

C. W. Burpo used to come on the radio with an introduction from his announcer that went like this, "And now we present this [great]{.class_s36M} man of God who brings to you, on our nationwide network, this [fearless]{.class_s36M} message of God's love. ("This [fearless]{.class_s36M} message of God's love?" Why, who's afraid to talk about [love]{.class_s36M}?) And now, Dr. Burpo (he always had the accent placed on the second syllable: Bur-PO)."

"Thank you, George. First, I want to tell you, friends, that God loves you and I love you too (he would always over-enunciate those last two phrases)." Ah, nuts! Give him the raspberry. A[ fearless crusader:]{.class_s36M} what a thing! I don't see how a guy could speak after an introduction like that. Don't ever let anybody write anything like that about [you]{.class_s36M}.

"Now here is a man whom God has mightily raised up to use in these last times; God's man for the hour with the message for the hour\...." You start looking around for one of the members of the Trinity to walk in. That is just a bunch of man-made fluff. Let God commend you.

[]{#c2VC.xhtml}

## Chapter 11 {.heading_s39}

11:1 Would to God ye could bear with me a little in my folly: and indeed bear with me.

2 For I am jealous over you with godly jealousy: for I have espoused you to one husband, that I may present you as a chaste virgin to Christ.

3 But I fear, lest by any means, as the serpent beguiled Eve through his subtilty, so your minds should be corrupted from the simplicity that is in Christ.

4 For if he that cometh preacheth another Jesus, whom we have not preached, or if ye receive another spirit, which ye have not received, or another gospel, which ye have not accepted, ye might well bear with him.

The ["folly"]{.class_s36N} of verse 1 is Paul's own boasting of himself, as in 10:8. When Paul had to brag about himself, he would say, ["I speak as a fool"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 23), because he understood that only a fool would brag.

Paul is getting ready to brag about his position and ministry. The Corinthians were being led astray by a bunch of Paul's enemies. Paul, then, is forced to affirm his [apostolic authority ]{.class_s36M}to them.

Look at chapter 10, verse 2: ["But I beseech you, that I may not be bold when I am present with that confidence, wherewith I think to be bold AGAINST SOME, WHICH THINK OF US as if we walked according to the flesh."]{.class_s36N} Note in that verse that these enemies of Paul were accusing him of leading a carnal life. In verse 10, they accused him of being weak, physically, and contemptible in his speech.

So Paul made up his mind to defend his apostolic authority (10:8) and warned this bunch, who were slandering him to the Corinthians, that he would deal with them just as strongly in person as in letter (10:11).

Paul is about to do what he said he shouldn't do (10:18). He is about to [commend himself]{.class_s36M}. He knows it is foolishness (["folly"]{.class_s36N}), but he wants to show the Corinthians that he is the same in person as he is in his letters.

Every minister should take note of that. Be consistent. Don't be one man [in]{.class_s36M} the pulpit and another [out]{.class_s36M} of the pulpit. You are the same man whether you are in a pulpit or your bedroom, bathroom, living room, dining room, or kitchen. You're the same man whether you're in church or out on the baseball field or in the garden or on the job. So be [consistent]{.class_s36M}. The world can't stand professionalism.

A fellow in Texas wrote me one time and said, "You're what we call a bull shooter out here." I wrote that fellow back, and I said, "Son, I'll be at the Morningside Baptist Church on this date. I'll be there for two services every day, ten in the morning and seven at night. I'll see you when I get there. Bring your Bible, and we'll [see]{.class_s36M} who is bull-shooting and who is not." Do you think he showed up? Why, that little pipsqeak never showed his face. He may have been six feet, four, but he didn't have any guts. If he had, he would have been there!

I get those things all of the time. I had a guy write me from up north. He said, "You've attacked my good friend, Robert Taylor. How does it feel to be the leader of a cult? You've got a cult. You have a cult to follow you. Didn't you know the Greek word meant\...." I would walk right by someone like that looking for a man. Nobody but a [sissy ]{.class_s36M}would write a letter like that. A "sure enough" man wouldn't have time to waste to write such a letter.

Before moving on to verse 2, you should note one thing. The boasting in which Paul is about to engage is [not]{.class_s36M} for the purpose of [self-glorification]{.class_s36M}. He is not commending himself so he can get the Corinthians approval. Rather, he is saying what he has to say in order to keep his converts from being [stolen away ]{.class_s36M}by the enemy. Look at verse 12, ["But what I do, that I will do, that I may cut off occasion from them which desire occasion."]{.class_s36N} These slanderers of Paul are just trying to steal Paul's converts and lead them off into false doctrine. So Paul is willing to be viewed as a fool as long as the Corinthians see these people for what they really are (vs. 13).

One of the biggest criticisms leveled at me is that I get on the case of "good, godly men" and call them all kinds of nasty names and show them no respect. I never say anything about anyone until [they cross that Book]{.class_s36M}. And when they try to steal, change, or pervert the Scriptures, [that's]{.class_s36M} when I "get 'em." Other than that, I leave them alone.

Verse 2: ["I am jealous over you with godly jealousy."]{.class_s36N} There are [two ]{.class_s36M}kinds of jealousy just as there were [two ]{.class_s36M}kinds of sorrow in chapter 7, verses 9--10. The one mentioned in verse 2 is godly and right. The Bible says, ["I the LORD thy God am a jealous God"]{.class_s36N} (Exod. 20:5), and ["For the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God"]{.class_s36N} (Exod. 34:14). James said, ["The spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to envy"]{.class_s36N} (James 4:5). ["Godly jealousy"]{.class_s36N} is the kind given in the Song of Solomon. It is God's concern for His people that they not follow false gods.

If a man loves his wife, he will be jealous at times. He doesn't want to see her in the arms of another man. You wouldn't marry a woman who only agreed to live with you two nights a week, while spending the other five nights with someone else.

The other kind of jealousy is [earthly ]{.class_s36M}and is no good. It is over-jealousy. If there is no trust in a marriage, something is wrong. The wrong kind of jealousy is ["the rage of a man"]{.class_s36N} (Prov. 6:34).

The reason Paul is jealous is because he has engaged the church at Corinth, the saved people in that local church, to ["one husband"]{.class_s36N}---Jesus Christ. The picture of the church here is ["a chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} engaged (["espoused"]{.class_s36N}) to ["one husband."]{.class_s36N} The Bride of Christ is the Lamb's bride in Revelation. She is called [a wife]{.class_s36M} in Ephesians 5, and in 2 Corinthians 11, she is ["a chaste virgin."]{.class_s36N}[ She is never called a woman]{.class_s36M}. So the woman in Matthew 13:33 is not the Church.

The problem that comes up is how can the Church be ["a chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} (pure, undefiled, never touched) and still be bone of his bone and flesh of his flesh. Paul calls it ["a great mystery"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 5:32).

The whole thing is pretty mysterious, anyway, when you think about it. Some of the people in the ["chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} are not even [female]{.class_s36M}. The "Bride" of Christ is made up of both male and female.

Now, what you have is two different pictures of the Bride of Christ. The ["chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} is a chronological picture where the Bride is down here on earth and is [engaged ]{.class_s36M}to be married to Christ up on heaven. The [marriage ]{.class_s36M}takes place in Revelation 19:7--9.

The second picture, in Ephesians 5, is that of the present state of the Body of Christ. Right now, Christ lives in you (Col. 1:27), and you live in Christ (1 Cor. 12:13; 2 Cor. 5:17).[ "He that is joined unto the Lord is one spirit"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 6:17).[ "For we are members of his body, of his flesh, and of his bones"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 5:30). So[ in that sense]{.class_s36M}, the marriage is already [consummated]{.class_s36M}.

The Church has a variety of pictures that are all hard to reconcile to each other. Each Christian is pictured as a part of a man's body (Eph. 4:16). You are a [son ]{.class_s36M}born, here on earth, into a family with God as your Father up in heaven. Moreover you are to come ["unto a perfect man, unto the measure of the stature of the fulness of Christ"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 4:13), so you are pictured as a full grown man. Those are the figures.

[Spiritually speaking]{.class_s36M}, you are married [now]{.class_s36M}. Your spirit is part of His spirit. [Spiritually]{.class_s36M}, you are bone of His bone and flesh of His flesh. [Physically]{.class_s36M}, you haven't been saved yet. Your body is down here; it is getting older and falling apart; it is headed for a hole in the ground where it will rot until the day it is saved. In that sense, you are like the chaste virgin, [waiting to be married.]{.class_s36M}

But there is no way, [physically]{.class_s36M}, of understanding these pictures of the Church. How do millions of men fall in love with the [same man]{.class_s36M}? How do millions of [men ]{.class_s36M}marry the same man? No matter how you try to figure the thing out, it will escape you. It is a mystery. That's all I can do for you. If you can do any better than that, write your own commentary, and I'll read it.

Now we come to verse 3, which presents such an enigma which no recognized commentator dares to exegete. It [implies]{.class_s36M} that a sexual transaction took place between Eve and Satan that resulted in the conception of Cain.

The ["chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} of verse 2 is compared to Eve. And the corrupting of ["a chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} is compared to Satan beguiling Eve in verse 3. One of the meanings of beguile is "to seduce." So you will find that some Catholic books teach that the "original sin" was [adultery]{.class_s36M}---Eve stepping out on Adam.

Moreover, God prophecies in Genesis 3:15 that there would be a "seed" from the serpent. Cain was said to be ["of that wicked one"]{.class_s36N} (1 John 3:12). In reference to Cain slaying Abel, Christ said,[ "Ye are of your father the devil\...He was a murderer from the beginning"]{.class_s36N} (John 8:44). Note that Satan himself could not "murder" anyone; God gave Satan ["the power of death"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 2:14). [Cain]{.class_s36M} was the first murderer.

Eve was obviously deceived as to Cain's origin (1 Tim. 2:14--15), for she thought Cain was from God (Gen. 4:1). You talk about the ["subtilty"]{.class_s36N} of Satan (vs. 3).

There is not much more you can say, [doctrinally]{.class_s36M}, on this subject, because the Scriptures don't say much more about it. About the only thing on which you can comment in regards to Eve's sin is the fact that Satan is connected with a [tree ]{.class_s36M}in the Bible and the fact that whatever corrupted Eve was taken in[ through the mouth.]{.class_s36M}

In regard to Satan being connected with a tree, look at Ezekiel 31:1--9, especially verse 9. The Devil is pictured [as a tree]{.class_s36M} in the Garden of Eden.

Next, the fruit that corrupts Eve comes from a tree in the Garden of Eden which is connected with the serpent (Gen. 3:1--6). She put the fruit of that tree in her mouth. When she did, it affected his [circulatory system]{.class_s36M}: ["the life of the flesh is in the blood"]{.class_s36N} (Lev. 17:11). That oral insemination is connected with [adultery ]{.class_s36M}(Prov. 30:20).

So when Christ comes along, He dies on a ["tree]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 2:24). He is likened to a [serpent ]{.class_s36M}on that tree (John 3:14). When you believe on Him, you receive everlasting life (John 6:47), and Christ likens that belief to eating the flesh that was broken on that tree and drinking the blood that was shed on that tree (John 6:51--63). Moreover that belief, that is said to be like eating and drinking His flesh and blood, puts you in Christ and Christ in you (John 6:56). So you are right back to the ["chaste virgin"]{.class_s36N} of 2 Corinthians 11 and the Bride who is ["of his body, of his flesh, and of his bones"]{.class_s36N} (Eph. 5:30).

Now, I know that no modern Bible teacher or commentator can handle that material, and that they simply ignore the texts. But they do exist, and I gave them to you. I told you what I know, and what I didn't know I left alone.

["The simplicity that is in Christ"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3). That is a great statement. ["The simplicity that is in Christ"]{.class_s36N} is so simple that you wouldn't believe it. There is a famous tract called "God's Simple Plan of Salvation," but those of you who preach the Gospel know, that for all of its simplicity, people don't believe it.

Someone will ask you, ["What must I do to be saved?"]{.class_s36N} And you will tell them, ["Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved."]{.class_s36N} What a thing to tell a fellow! No philosophy, no art, no science, no mediation, no good works, no golden rule, no sacraments, no church membership, no baptism, no feeling, no tongues, no experience, no anointing. ["Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved."]{.class_s36N} It's just that simple.

["The simplicity that is in Christ"]{.class_s36N} is simply this: Christ [paid ]{.class_s36M}for all your sins. He paid for them [forever]{.class_s36M}, and they are all taken care of. You trust Him, and you will go to heaven. It is like [that]{.class_s36M}; it's just that simple.

The Devil will try to talk you out of it. He will [quote verses]{.class_s36M} to you that will contradict that. He will make sure that you find the most complicated, [misunderstood verses ]{.class_s36M}in the Bible to damn your soul. That is the Devil trying to corrupt your mind from ["the simplicity that is in Christ."]{.class_s36N}

Notice in verse 4 the "others." You have ["another Jesus," "another spirit," ]{.class_s36N}and[ "another gospel."]{.class_s36N} In verses 13--15, you have other apostles, other angels, and other ministers. [These are all Satanic counterfeits of the real thing.]{.class_s36M} So we are not surprised to find another [church]{.class_s36M}, which is another [city]{.class_s36M}, which is Satan's bride (Rev. 17), just as the real church is the New Jerusalem, which is [Christ's bride]{.class_s36M}. There is another [Christ ]{.class_s36M}besides ["the Lord's Christ"]{.class_s36N} (Luke 2:26; Acts 4:26 cf. Ezek. 28:14) and another "God" (2 Cor. 4:4). Satan has his own "faith healers" (Rev. 16:14) and apostolic signs (2 Thess. 2:9). In order to be ["like the most High"]{.class_s36N} (Isa. 14:14), Satan would have to have his own [Bible]{.class_s36M}, teachers, church members, "sacraments," [music]{.class_s36M}, prayers, worship, disciples, soulwinning efforts, [holy days]{.class_s36M}, and holy places. If you are going to catch the counterfeits, then you had better know the real things.

First, let's look at ["another Jesus."]{.class_s36N} The Charismatics have a Jesus that is a kind of [playboy lover]{.class_s36M}. They overemphasize His human name. Occasionally they will try to prove they're orthodox by proclaiming boldly, "Jesus is Lord!" That is the confession of a devil! Someday ["every knee should bow"]{.class_s36N} and ["every tongue should confess"]{.class_s36N} that, [including ]{.class_s36M}["things under the earth"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 2:10--11). If all you can say is, "Don't forget that Jesus is Lord," then you must be an unsaved man headed for Hell. The Christian confession is ["Jesus is THE Lord"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 12:3). [The Charismatics can't read plain English]{.class_s36M}, so they think they are speaking ["by the Holy Ghost"]{.class_s36N} (1 Cor. 12:3) and exercising the gifts of the Spirit in 1 Corinthians 12. By ignoring [one little three letter word]{.class_s36M} in the Scriptures, they created the biggest mess you ever saw in your life.

Now, there are all kinds of "Jesuses" on the market. Artists favor a [red-haired Jesus]{.class_s36M} or a [brown-haired Jesus ]{.class_s36M}or a[ blond-haired Jesus]{.class_s36M}. Anything but the Jesus whose ["locks are bushy, and black as a raven"]{.class_s36N} (Song of Sol. 5:11). There is the confused teenager Jesus of "Jesus Christ, Superstar." There is the [revolutionary Jesus ]{.class_s36M}of liberation theology who goes around busting into department stores and stealing television sets. There is the [Mormon Jesus ]{.class_s36M}who was the spirit brother of Lucifer and married three or four wives while here on earth. There is the [Catholic Jesus ]{.class_s36M}who can't be approached except through His mother. There is the liberal, [modernistic Jesus]{.class_s36M} who swishes around in His long robes with His mouth full of butter quoting only the Beatitudes. The colored folk even have their own [black Jesus]{.class_s36M}---just as black as the Ace of Spades. The question is not do you worship or follow Jesus; the question is have you received the Jesus [of the Bible]{.class_s36M} as your personal Saviour.

Then there is ["another spirit."]{.class_s36N} Here are these poor Brownsville charismatics, these clowns from Clownsville, trying to get under some ethereal faucet from heaven to get "more, more, more" of a spirit that makes you dance around like a chicken, utter animal noises, belly dance before men to bring them down the aisle, faint dead away, and jerk uncontrollably (except when you go to the bathroom!). And after getting a spirit like that, they still don't know for sure that they are saved, [and they keep going down the aisle to get saved]{.class_s36M}. (See [The Clownsville Carnival,]{.class_s36M} Bible Baptist Bookstore, Pensacola, FL 1998,) That is [not]{.class_s36M} the Spirit of Romans 8:16.
