I am going to tell you just as plain as I know how to tell you: [if you got any other spirit since you got saved, other than the Spirit you received when you got saved, you got the Devil's spirit.]{.class_s36M} That Bible says that ["the Lord is that Spirit"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 3:17), and ["if any man have not the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 8:9).

When you got saved, whether you knew it or not, the Holy Spirit came [into you ]{.class_s36M}and put you [into Christ]{.class_s36M} (1 Cor. 12:13). If you got some other spirit[ after that,]{.class_s36M} that was something else, but it was [not]{.class_s36M} the Holy Spirit. [You received the Holy Spirit when you were saved.]{.class_s36M}

Over in Milton, Florida, a charismatic church put up a sign that said, "When I got saved, I spoke in tongues! What happened to you?" One of our students went over there and marked through it with a felt marker and wrote, "When I got saved, [I received Jesus Christ!]{.class_s36M} What happened to you?" There [is]{.class_s36M} a difference!

I'm not saying that all charismatics are lost. Some of them are just deceived; [a lot of them are demon-possessed.]{.class_s36M}

That's why they keep insisting that a Christian [can't ]{.class_s36M}be demon-possessed. They keep repeating some preacher's outline that a Christian can be demon-obsessed and demon-oppressed, but never demon-possessed.

But Charismatics are always worried about demon-possession and[ the unpardonable sin]{.class_s36M}. They have a bad conscience. They don't know for sure that they are saved or will stay saved. Then one day they do something they know is wrong. Since they don't know any Bible, they are afraid that it is a [demon ]{.class_s36M}working in them or that they have committed the [unpardonable sin]{.class_s36M} (depending on what they have done). And if they aren't thinking either of those things, then they are busy trying to justify themselves: "It was a 'mistake.' It was a 'fault.' I didn't sin 'wilfully.'"

That Bible says in Ephesians 1:12--13, ["That we should be to the praise of his glory, who first trusted in Christ. In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation: in whom also after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise."]{.class_s36N} That's what happened to you when you believed. There is nothing further waiting to happen to you when it comes to salvation.

Did you ever hear Oral Roberts tell [how]{.class_s36M} he got saved? I have heard Jimmy Swaggart preach dozens of times, and I have never heard him tell [how]{.class_s36M} he got saved. It makes you wonder.

Isn't it strange that a fellow should be that long in the ministry, and nobody knows how he got saved? They will tell you [how]{.class_s36M} they got tongues and [how]{.class_s36M} they got the healing power. They can even tell you [when]{.class_s36M} they got saved, but never [how.]{.class_s36M} Come on, boy. What happened? [How]{.class_s36M} did you get saved?

Nothing is ever said about faith and [shed blood.]{.class_s36M} Nothing is said about a [finished atonement]{.class_s36M} or regeneration by trusting Christ as Saviour---not a word. I've listened to those fellows off and on now for over twenty years, and none of them have ever told anybody [how ]{.class_s36M}they were saved. Maybe they never did get saved.

Back in verse 4, there is not only ["another Jesus"]{.class_s36N} and ["another spirit,"]{.class_s36N} there is ["another gospel."]{.class_s36N} Paul wrote to the Galatians about this (Gal. 1:6). It is a perverted gospel (Gal. 1:7), and those who preach it are accursed (Gal. 1:8).

["Which ye have not accepted, ye might well bear with him"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4). Paul is being sarcastic here. The idea is: "Here are a bunch of folks feeding you a lot of spiritual junk, and you are putting up with it without complaint. If you can do that, you can certainly put up with me (Paul)."

11:5 For I suppose I was not a whit behind the very chiefest apostles.

6 But though I be rude in speech, yet not in knowledge; but we have been throughly made manifest among you in all things.

7 Have I committed an offence in abasing myself that ye might be exalted, because I have preached to you the gospel of God freely?

8 I robbed other churches, taking wages of them, to do you service.

9 And when I was present with you, and wanted, I was chargeable to no man: for that which was lacking to me the brethren which came from Macedonia supplied: and in all things I have kept myself from being burdensome unto you, and so will I keep myself.

10 As the truth of Christ is in me, no man shall stop me of this boasting in the regions of Achaia.

11 Wherefore? because I love you not? God knoweth.

12 But what I do, that I will do, that I may cut off occasion from them which desire occasion; that wherein they glory, they may be found even as we.

In verse 5, Paul begins a defense of his apostleship. Normally Paul wouldn't talk this way. His testimony runs more like 1 Timothy 1:12--16. But because the Corinthians were listening to these heretics preaching ["another Jesus," "another spirit,"]{.class_s36N} and ["another gospel,"]{.class_s36N} Paul was going to show them the difference between a [God-called apostle]{.class_s36M} and a bunch of [religious fakirs.]{.class_s36M}

Now, the Corinthians knew what Paul was like (vs. 6). He was evidently well known for his ["rude\...speech,"]{.class_s36N} which was described by his enemies as ["contemptible"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 10:10). Like Peter, James, and John, he appeared to be ["unlearned and ignorant"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 4:13). He warned against ["good words and fair speeches"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 16:18), because such speech is designed to deceive, mislead, and destroy those who heed it (Prov. 26:25, 20:19, 29:5).

When Paul preached the Gospel to the Corinthians, he took up no collection to pay his legitimate expenses in the ministry (vs. 7). Sarcastically he says, ["I robbed other churches, taking wages of them, to do you service"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 8). Obviously, he didn't "break and enter" and "crack the safe" or "stick up" the ushers and take the money out of the offering plate. He lived off the offerings of other churches.

Paul says, ["When I was present with you, and wanted, I was chargeable to no man"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). He did not beg, borrow, or live off the generosity of any of the Corinthians. The first time he was in Corinth, [he worked]{.class_s36M} and made his living with Aquila and Priscilla making tents (Acts 18:2--3). The second time, the Macedonians brought him a gift, and he lived off that (vs. 9). Paul actually bragged about the fact that he was not a financial burden in any way to the Corinthians (vss. 9--10).

Now, Paul wasn't boasting for his own benefit or to embarrass the Corinthians. God knew it was out of [love for the Corinthians]{.class_s36M} that he was saying these things (vs. 11). Paul's intentions were to get the Corinthians' attention with his boasting so that they would be aware of the trouble and danger from these false teachers, who were making merchandise of them (2 Pet. 2:3) and did [not]{.class_s36M} love them, and get rid of them.

From time to time, in the [Bible Believers' Bulletin,]{.class_s36M} I will give a report on souls saved during a prison junket or special meeting or a pastor's conference on some foreign field. I am sure that my enemies consider that "bragging" about my ministry (of course, if they do it in their publications, it is only a "report"). But one of the reasons that I do that is [not]{.class_s36M} to "toot my own horn," but to show you that [belief in the Book]{.class_s36M} produces more than a dead orthodoxy that talks about soulwinning [but never does it]{.class_s36M}. The enemies of that Book are always saying that belief in a [King James Bible ]{.class_s36M}makes you too "theological" and not "practical" enough. That's not true, so every once in a while I let people know what is going on here. And what my critics can't [stand]{.class_s36M} is the fact that God still uses some "roughneck" like me who is "disqualified" for the ministry and talks the way I talk.

So Paul is bragging here to [undermine ]{.class_s36M}the bragging of the false teachers (vs. 12). He is going to show how they [don't ]{.class_s36M}stack up to a [real]{.class_s36M} apostle.

["For such are false apostles"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 13). That is a direct charge.[ It is absolutely negative.]{.class_s36M} There is nothing good about it. He is preaching [against ]{.class_s36M}their religion.

In Revelation 2:2, Christ calls them [liars]{.class_s36M}. They claim to have the apostolic signs (2 Cor. 12:12), but they don't. They claim to have apostolic succession from Matthew 16, [but they don't.]{.class_s36M}

Notice that they are actually ["deceitful workers"]{.class_s36N} but they transform ["themselves into the apostles of Christ."]{.class_s36N} So they are hypocrites. They are pretending to be something which they are not.

["Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14). Note first how Satan [imitates ]{.class_s36M}the Lord Jesus Christ. Jesus Christ is ["the light of the world" ]{.class_s36N}(John 9:5) and ["the angel of the Lord"]{.class_s36N} (Acts 27:23; Gal. 4:14). Satan is a [god ]{.class_s36M}(2 Cor. 4:4); Jesus is [God ]{.class_s36M}(1 Tim. 3:16). Satan is a [lion ]{.class_s36M}(1 Pet. 5:8); Jesus Christ is a Lion (Rev. 5:5). Both[ quote Scripture]{.class_s36M} (Matt. 4; Luke 4). Both are [anointed ]{.class_s36M}(Ezek. 28:14; Heb. 1:9). Both are [princes ]{.class_s36M}(John 14:30; Isa. 9:6). Both have a [bride ]{.class_s36M}who is a [city ]{.class_s36M}(Rev. 17, 21). Both are [serpents ]{.class_s36M}(Gen. 3:1; John 3:14).

Look again at verse 14. From time immemorial, Satan has been pictured as an angel, [and he never was an angel.]{.class_s36M} Some of the best commentators in the world describe the Devil as a fallen angel. [He has never been an angel, and he is not one right now.]{.class_s36M}

When Satan fell, he was a [cherub ]{.class_s36M}(Ezek. 28). He is now a [dragon ]{.class_s36M}(Rev. 12). He goes about the earth ["as a roaring lion"]{.class_s36N} seeking prey (1 Pet. 5:8). But when the Devil appears to men, he transforms himself ["into an angel of light"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14).

Every [angel ]{.class_s36M}in that Bible is a thirty-three-year-old man, without wings. More than likely, that is how Satan appeared to Eve, although Moses knew his real [identity ]{.class_s36M}was a serpent.

So far in this chapter, we have run into the wrong Jesus, the wrong spirit, the wrong gospel, and the wrong apostles. Now, in verse 15, we see wrong [ministers]{.class_s36M}. The Devil has a counterfeit for everything the Lord has. He has counterfeit [ministers ]{.class_s36M}in verse 15.

No modern Christian would dare believe that any "minister" could belong to the Devil. In modern Christianity, Satan would never sit in on a Bible revision committee. He certainly wouldn't have his own church. [Modern Christianity lives in a fool's paradise.]{.class_s36M}

If you want the Lord's description of these "ministers," read 2 Peter 2 and the book of Jude.

11:16 I say again, Let no man think me a fool; if otherwise, yet as a fool receive me, that I may boast myself a little.

17 That which I speak, I speak it not after the Lord, but as it were foolishly, in this confidence of boasting.

18 Seeing that many glory after the flesh, I will glory also.

19 For ye suffer fools gladly, seeing ye yourselves are wise.

20 For ye suffer, if a man bring you into bondage, if a man devour you, if a man take of you, if a man exalt himself, if a man smite you on the face.

Paul is once more about to brag about his work (vs. 16). Paul considers this to be the mark of a fool (vss. 21, 23). He is not a fool (["Let no man think me a fool,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 16), but he doesn't mind if the Corinthians think him to be one (["yet as a fool receive me"]{.class_s36N}) if they will just take to heart what he has to say.

In verse 17, Paul admits that what he is about to say is ["not after the Lord."]{.class_s36N} When a man boasts, he is a fool. Paul recognizes that. But do not discount what Paul is about to say. It is still inspired Scripture. The Lord is going to use Paul's "foolishness" to show you the difference between a real minister of Jesus Christ and those who are ["transformed as the ministers of righteousness"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15).

Now, what Paul is going to do is tell you what he has gone through, in his body, that makes him a fit apostle and minister of Jesus Christ. His [training under Gamaliel]{.class_s36M} never comes up. He says [nothing ]{.class_s36M}about Barnabas accepting him and bringing him to the other apostles. He won't even refer to his[ calling out]{.class_s36M} in Acts 13. It's all going to be glorying ["after the flesh"]{.class_s36N} from a [negative ]{.class_s36M}perspective. But he is indeed going to recommend himself based on what he has done ["after the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 18).

Paul figures that since the Corinthians put up with a bunch of real fools, they wouldn't mind putting up with his own foolishness for a while (vs. 19). Verse 19 is pure sarcasm. Paul doesn't think the Corinthians are [wise]{.class_s36M}; he already said that he thought that they were being deceived (vss. 1--11).

The ["bondage"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 20) is the same ["bondage"]{.class_s36N} as Galatians 5:1. [It is the adding of rules and regulations to salvation or keeping salvation.]{.class_s36M} They put up with those "ministers" who did that to them. They put up with people who took their money and property (["if a man devour you, if a man take of you"]{.class_s36N}). They put up with a man if he bragged about his ministry (["if a man exalt himself"]{.class_s36N}). Paul then sarcastically says that they would even put up with someone who slapped them in the face (["if a man smite you on the face"]{.class_s36N}). Of course, it probably hadn't gone that far, but they were putting up with a bunch of people who were robbing them blind both spiritually and physically.

11:21 I speak as concerning reproach, as though we had been weak. Howbeit whereinsoever any is bold, (I speak foolishly,) I am bold also.

22 Are they Hebrews? so am I. Are they Israelites? so am I. Are they the seed of Abraham? so am I.

23 Are they ministers of Christ? (I speak as a fool) I am more; in labours more abundant, in stripes above measure, in prisons more frequent, in deaths oft.

24 Of the Jews five times received I forty stripes save one.

25 Thrice was I beaten with rods, once was I stoned, thrice I suffered shipwreck, a night and a day I have been in the deep;

26 In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by mine own countrymen, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among false brethren;

27 In weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness.

28 Beside those things that are without, that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches.

29 Who is weak, and I am not weak? who is offended, and I burn not?

30 If I must needs glory, I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities.

31 The God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, which is blessed for evermore, knoweth that I lie not.

32 In Damascus the governor under Aretas the king kept the city of the Damascenes with a garrison, desirous to apprehend me:

33 And through a window in a basket was I let down by the wall, and escaped his hands.

If Paul was the greatest minister that ever lived (and I believe he was) and an example for the Christian (and the Scriptures say he was), then [this passage]{.class_s36M} describes the [qualifications for the ministry]{.class_s36M}. They are nothing like those in your major Christian periodicals and newspapers. There are no baptisms, no buses, no bond programs, no twenty million tracts, no record attendance, and no "mightiest pen of the twentieth century" or any other self-promoting gas.

The [real ]{.class_s36M}Christian ministry is summed up in one word in verse 21: ["reproach."]{.class_s36N} The more you are doing for the Lord, the more you are going to get it "in the neck." We are commanded to ["go forth therefore unto him]{.class_s36N} (Jesus) [without the camp, bearing his reproach"]{.class_s36N} (Heb. 13:13).

["As though we had been weak"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 21). The things he is about to list, the reproaches he went through, one would supposed to have weakened his ministry. But Paul is ["bold"]{.class_s36N} concerning those things. He'll tell you in the next chapter that ["when I am weak, then am I strong"]{.class_s36N} (2 Cor. 12:10).

Now, you have to be careful saying what Paul said in verse 21. He said he was speaking ["foolishly."]{.class_s36N} He is saying, "Look at me; I can take the reproach of Christ, and I'm bragging about it." Paul could do that because he had gone through the reproach victoriously. [He said that to save his converts from false ministers.]{.class_s36M} But if you do what Paul did and said lightly, the Devil will step before the throne and say, "Hey! Did you hear that, Lord? He said he can take it. You are going to give him a chance to prove it, aren't you?" So be careful before opening your mouth and speaking like a fool.

The first thing is ["Are they Hebrews\...Israelites\...the seed of Abraham? so am I"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 22). Paul could name his tribe (Phil. 3:5). The false teachers had nothing on Paul when it came to the Old Testament.

Verse 23, ["Are they ministers of Christ? (I speak as a fool) I am more."]{.class_s36N} He is saying that he is a better minister than that other bunch. Well, he has already associated them with Satan, as ministers (vss. 14--15). So when he says, ["(I speak as a fool) I am more,"]{.class_s36N} he is saying that he is foolishly saying that he has suffered as a minister of Jesus Christ more than they have.

Again, brethren, you had better be careful saying something like that. You might be able to put up with your own trouble, but would you like to try someone else's trouble?

But Paul could legitimately say, [with the Holy Spirit's approval]{.class_s36M}, that he worked harder than the false ministers (["in labours more abundantly"]{.class_s36N}). He had been whipped more than he had coming to him (["in stripes above measure"]{.class_s36N}), and he had been in prisons more. He had often been at the point of death.

He was beaten by the Jews five times (vs. 24), and each time he had received thirty-nine ["stripes."]{.class_s36N} That works out to a total of 195 whip lashes. He had welts all over him. [He was scarred from head to foot.]{.class_s36M}

In addition to the whippings, he was beaten three more times with rods (like a long stick of bamboo or a sturdy broom handle; vs. 25). He says he was stoned once, and that is probably a reference to the stoning he received at Lystra in Acts 14:19. It comes up again in the next chapter.

He was shipwrecked three times. During one of those shipwrecks he was floating around in the water for twenty-four hours (vs. 25).

He traveled more (vs. 26---["In journeying often"]{.class_s36N}). He was in danger, whether he traveled by boat (["in perils of water"]{.class_s36N}) or on land (["in perils of robbers"]{.class_s36N}). He was hounded by Jews and Gentiles and even professing Christians (vs. 26). Danger just seemed to follow him whether in the city or country or on the ocean (vs. 26).

He was "dog-tired" (vs. 27); he was in constant pain. [He lost sleep]{.class_s36M}; he was hungry and thirsty. He not only went without food because he had none; he also voluntarily went without food (["fastings"]{.class_s36N}). And he didn't have [decent clothes]{.class_s36M} that would keep him warm (vs. 27).

As a minister, you will go through some of that. Men like Lester Roloff and Richard Wurmbrand have been to jail for the Lord's sake. I guess that means they are better ministers than most of us.

You may not be stoned or beaten yet, but you can [labor ]{.class_s36M}for Christ. You can put forth an effort. You can work hard for Him. If you witnessed to everything that breathes in the little town where God puts you, you can travel to another town and witness there. There will be ["false brethren"]{.class_s36N} out there who lie about you, betray you, and sell you out. There will be times you will need to fast about a matter in order to get an answer to prayer. And I guarantee you, you will get tired in the ministry. You might even get tired [of]{.class_s36M} the ministry, but Paul says, ["Let us not be weary in well doing: for in due season we shall reap, if we faint not"]{.class_s36N} (Gal. 6:9).

I think every pastor ought to study the life of Moses. To me, Moses was the greatest [pastor ]{.class_s36M}in the Bible. He ran [two million]{.class_s36M} in "Sunday School"; he would put the [Sword of the Lord]{.class_s36M} crowd out of business. He took two million people "out of the convention" with the Devil after him.

He pastored the same congregation for forty years!

His [sister ]{.class_s36M}got mad at him; his [brother ]{.class_s36M}got mad at him. The [whole congregation]{.class_s36M} gets to griping about him. A bunch of them get a committee together to "throw the pastor out."

He loses his temper and [breaks all Ten Commandments at one shot.]{.class_s36M}

[That's]{.class_s36M} a picture of the ministry.

Moses got tired [in]{.class_s36M} the ministry, and he got tired [of]{.class_s36M} the ministry. If you want to see just [how]{.class_s36M} tired, read Numbers 11:10--15. And when those times came along, the Lord came alongside him and gave him a little shot of encouragement and took some of the pressure off him, and Moses would get up and go on again.

God will call some of you reading this Commentary into the ministry, and any delusions you had about the "glories of the ministry" will be shattered right away. The first problem you will have is [property]{.class_s36M}. You won't be able to get any property. Then when you do, you'll find out that the guy who [sold ]{.class_s36M}you the property doesn't have a clear title deed for it. When you get a clear title to it, there will be a "catch" for the mineral rights or there will be some dispute over "wetlands." If that gets straightened out, the [treasurer ]{.class_s36M}will leave the church and abscond with half the offering. Then the church will split, and the offerings will go down, and you won't be able to keep up the [payments on the property.]{.class_s36M}

Meanwhile, there is the congregational end of it. The [chairman of the deacons]{.class_s36M} gets to fooling around with the Full Gospel Businessmen's Fellowship and begins talking in tongues. You get up and preach, and someone gets [offended]{.class_s36M}. You get winning people to Christ, and the church starts growing; then two of your best members leave because all they wanted in a church was a little social club [for them and their friends]{.class_s36M}. Then the teenage daughter of your best tither comes to church dressed like Jezebel, and pretty soon all the girls in the youth department want to look like [her]{.class_s36M}.

Then, you won't always be in good health. Sometimes you'll preach with a headache, a toothache, a backache, a kidney stone. One time I preached six hours after having a wisdom tooth removed. I was spitting up blood all over the pulpit and stuffing a handkerchief in my mouth while I was preaching. There will be times like that!

When you get in those times, it is a good idea just to get away from people. I know some people won't agree with me about that, but I speak from experience. Go out and work in the garden. Get you a set of golf clubs and go out and knock some balls around the course. Go fishing! Just think about something else besides [people]{.class_s36M}. They will drive you up the wall; they will make you "bugs."

Verse 28: ["Besides those things that are without, that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches."]{.class_s36N} Not only did Paul have to put up with all the things he listed in verse 23--27, and not only did he have to put up with unsaved people (["without"]{.class_s36N} cf. 1 Cor. 5:12), but he has the constant burden of the churches which he established. Half of Paul's missionary journeys was checking up on the churches he had planted before. He worried over them; he prayed over them; he took up collections for them. He wrote epistles to deal with their problems, even if it was just two women fighting with each other (Phil. 4:2). The apostleship was a down-to-earth job.

Now, Paul stops "bragging" in verse 29. If he is bragging, then he is bragging about his trouble. Look at the next verse, ["I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities."]{.class_s36N} Moreover, he is not making anything up (vs. 31). He is like any other Christian (vs. 29): he has his own frailties, and he gets offended. He is tempted and tried just like the Corinthians are.

So in light of all that (vss. 29--31), Paul gives an example of how his ministry began. It wasn't grand and glorious, like the false apostles were making out their "ministries" to be. It was running from the law and having to escape capture in a clothes basket. The incident in verses 32--33 is given in Acts 9:22--25. And whoever ["the governor under Aretas the king"]{.class_s36N} was, he tried to capture and kill Paul at the instigation of the Jewish leaders in the city.

[]{#c2ZM.xhtml}

## Chapter 12 {.heading_s39}

12:1 It is not expedient for me doubtless to glory. I will come to visions and revelations of the Lord.

2 I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago, (whether in the body, I cannot tell; or whether out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth;) such an one caught up to the third heaven.

3 And I knew such a man, (whether in the body, or out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth;)

4 How that he was caught up into paradise, and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter.

5 Of such an one will I glory: yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities.

In verse 1, Paul decides that it is not a good idea for him to keep bragging, so he quits. He stops talking about his experiences ["in the flesh"]{.class_s36N} (see 10:3) and comes to some things that God showed him (["visions and revelations of the Lord"]{.class_s36N}).

I have already commented about verses 2--5 in the [Bible Believers' Commentary on Acts]{.class_s36M} (see comments under Acts 14:19--20).[ "I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 2). The event is probably the stoning at Lystra where Paul was left for dead outside the city. [He actually did die and went to heaven (vs. 4), but was sent back.]{.class_s36M}

["Whether in the body\...or whether out of the body, I cannot tell."]{.class_s36N} Like the rich man in Hell (Luke 16), Paul had eyes, ears, mouth, tongue, etc. He had a complete body, but it was a [soulish body]{.class_s36M}. It was the [invisible shape]{.class_s36M} that fills the physical body here on earth, so he could not see it even though it was there. If you were able to throw a white sheet over it, you would have "Casper the Friendly Ghost" (Rev. 6:11, 20:4). For further discussion on this subject get two books from the Bookstore: [Body, Soul, Spirit]{.class_s36M} and [Where Do the Dead Go?.]{.class_s36M}

Paul's situation in verse 2 is a condition that is counterfeited in Zen Buddhism and in drugs. In Zen Buddhism, there is a practise called "astral projection." You go into meditation and send part of yourself out of your body to another place and then bring it back.

The witch doctors do that in Africa. I recall a particular case where the witch doctor told a missionary that he could tell where he was from. The missionary didn't believe it, but the witch doctor demonstrated it. He went through his ritualistic dance and act and then fell into a trance. When he came to, he described in detail the missionary's former house in Pennsylvania. He had seen it,[ but his body never left Africa.]{.class_s36M}

Drugs will do the same thing. That is why they say someone is "tripping" on drugs. I have heard testimony from people who have fooled with dope that they will get a sensation where an arm or a hand or a foot seems to run off and get away from them. They will reach out and grab the runaway part back. All of those things are [counterfeits ]{.class_s36M}of what happened to Paul in verse 2.

A fellow named Raymond Moody one time wrote a book called [Life After Life]{.class_s36M} about "near death" experiences, but the problem that nut had was that he only examined the [positive]{.class_s36M} experiences. As a consequence, he concludes that everyone is going to some version of Heaven. Beloved, the same God who took Paul to Paradise in 2 Corinthians 12 sent a rich man to Hell in Luke 16. "Life after life" is [not ]{.class_s36M}all positive.

If you want to get a Christian perspective on these life after death experiences, get a book from the Bible Baptist Bookstore by the title of [Beyond Death's Door,]{.class_s36M} by a medical doctor named [Maurice Rawlings]{.class_s36M}. That fellow got saved by resuscitating a man who begged him not to stop the heart massage because each time Dr. Rawlings did the fellow would die and go to Hell. Each time Dr. Rawlings would bring him back to life, the fellow would wake up screaming about [being in Hell.]{.class_s36M} When that Doctor saw how genuinely frightened the man was, it shook him up, and he ended up getting saved. That doctor will give you [both sides ]{.class_s36M}of life after death.

Before leaving the verse, note that Paul was ["caught up"]{.class_s36N} (see also vs. 4). Normally there would be nothing special about that phrase, but in the last thirty years the Fundamentalists have started following Einstein and teaching that ["up"]{.class_s36N} is a "relative" term. But in spite of 500 college professors [without a brain between them all]{.class_s36M}, you can locate ["up,"]{.class_s36N} and it is NORTHWARD towards Alpha Draconis (Isa. 14:13; Psa. 75:6--7).

Note also that there are three heavens (see comments under Gen. 1:1--6 of that commentary), not the "seven heavens" of Muslim mythology. It is the third heaven that contains ["paradise"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4).

Verse 4: ["How that he."]{.class_s36N} Paul is describing his experience in the [third person]{.class_s36M}, as if it had happened to someone else. In Zen Buddhism that is what it is like. When you obtain Prajna (enlightenment---Nirvana or Samadhi in Hinduism), when you come back from the trip, you can't say, "It was me." It's not like that. It is like you are observing [another person]{.class_s36M}. That is the pagan counterfeit of Paul's experience.

Notice that [now]{.class_s36M}[ "paradise"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 4) is ["up."]{.class_s36N} It changed positions since its last mention in Luke 23:43. There, it was [down ]{.class_s36M}(Eph. 4:9--10). At present, ["paradise"]{.class_s36N} is in Heaven (Rev. 2:7).

["And heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter."]{.class_s36N} So Paul saw and heard things in Heaven which God wouldn't let him write down or speak of. It must have been a [trial]{.class_s36M} for him; it must have taken a lot of grace for him to keep his mouth shut.

The Lord showed him those things, though, to encourage him. If you had been through everything Paul had been through in 1 Corinthians 11, you would need a "shot in the arm," too. He got up there, and the Lord said, "I know it's rough down there, but look here. This is what is waiting for you. Now get back down there and finish the job. And by the way, don't say anything about what you've seen up here. It's a secret." I think that is why Paul acted for all the world like a suicidal maniac. [He was always trying to get himself stoned or killed in order to get back up there.]{.class_s36M} Look back at Acts 14. He gets up from under a pile of rocks, dusts himself off, [and marches right back into the city that had just stoned him]{.class_s36M}. He was determined to make it back up there!

Later on, John writes about what Paul saw. So when Paul says ["it is not lawful for a man to utter,"]{.class_s36N} he is referring to the ["man"]{.class_s36N} back in verse 2. It is himself. God makes it lawful for [John]{.class_s36M}, whereas He did [not ]{.class_s36M}make it lawful for Paul or Daniel (Dan. 12:9).

["Of such an one will I glory: yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). Now, if Paul is [really]{.class_s36M} going to brag, he wants to brag about the man who went to Heaven. That ["man"]{.class_s36N} is with each Christian in a sense, because each Christian is seated already with Christ in Heaven (Eph. 2:6). But when it comes to the "earthly" Paul, he is not going to brag about anything but its troubles.

12:6 For though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool; for I will say the truth: but now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me.

7 And lest I should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I should be exalted above measure.

8 For this thing I besought the Lord thrice, that it might depart from me.

9 And he said unto me, My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness. Most gladly therefore will I rather glory in my infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest upon me.

Paul wanted to brag about what God had showed him in Heaven (["I would desire to glory,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 6), but he was not going to act like a fool. It would have been ["the truth,"]{.class_s36N} but Paul was going to quit for the Corinthians' sakes. He did not want them to get the wrong idea about him; he did not want them to think he was [better ]{.class_s36M}than he actually was. Paul knew where he was short and where he wasn't.

People will either build you up too high, or they will knock you down too low. If you follow my ministry, the brethren will call you a "Ruckmanite" and accuse you of following a man. "Oh, you just think he's the Lord. You believe everything he says; you don't think he can do anything wrong." They say those kinds of things because you won't follow [them]{.class_s36M}. Don't' let it bug you.

At the same time, if you ever get any highly exalted ideas about me above what you ought to have, you didn't get it from [me]{.class_s36M}. Somebody else made up that stuff. I have always been very careful in teaching students to make it clear that I was a human being and a sinner. I [don't]{.class_s36M} have all of the answers. There are many things about which I am very dumb. There are still many things in the Bible about which I can only hazard a guess. I am very clear about those things with every class that comes through our school. I don't want my students or anyone else to get any exalted ideas about [me]{.class_s36M}.

I have seen that thing go on in other Christian circles. In one of John R. Rice's "Scrapbooks," he published some poem by Bill Harvey in which Rice's horse is bragging about how lucky he is to carry such a "great man of God." I wouldn't let a thing like that be published about me [before ]{.class_s36M}or [after ]{.class_s36M}I died, if I could do anything to stop it.

The present editor of [The Sword of the Lord]{.class_s36M} has his picture published in his own paper [six to ten times every issue]{.class_s36M}. That fellow must kiss the mirror every time he sees himself in it. Our Bookstore manager, Ron Forte, played a dirty trick on me one time. He printed a mock issue of the [Bulletin]{.class_s36M} and put my picture in it [fourteen times]{.class_s36M}. I thought that was one of the most terrible things that could happen to the [Bulletin]{.class_s36M}!

You shouldn't let others think too highly of [you ]{.class_s36M}either. You have to watch it when others put you up on a pedestal. [Just go ahead and do something stupid in front of them]{.class_s36M}. They'll get everything straightened out; they won't think so highly of you, then.

Now, God had shown Paul quite a bit in that vision of heaven (["the abundance of the revelations,"]{.class_s36N} vs. 7). And to keep him from being puffed up with pride about the thing, the Lord turned the Devil loose on Paul and let him knock him around a while (["the messenger of Satan to buffet me"]{.class_s36N}). God's purpose for doing this was to keep Paul humble.

I think of several well-known pastors whom God has used. Every single one of them had something wrong with him. John Rawlings nearly had a nervous breakdown one time and finally got ulcers. Harold Henniger was doing a great work until he had [three ]{.class_s36M}heart attacks. He had surgery and had to slow down. Dallas Billington had [three ]{.class_s36M}heart attacks, and the fourth one got him. I guess J. Frank Norris was one of the greatest preachers that ever lived, but in his latter years, he got in some of the biggest messes you ever saw. A lot of it was his own fault.

For further comment on the ["thorn in the flesh"]{.class_s36N} see the comments under Galatians 4:14--15 in the [Bible Believers' Commentary on Galatians--Colossians.]{.class_s36M}

Down in Florida, we have a little grass burr called a "sand spur" which is a perfect example of that[ "thorn in the flesh."]{.class_s36N} It's a small thing, but if it ever sticks in your skin it is just painful enough to "aggravate the fire" out of you.

It is not a big thing, like losing an arm or a leg. And that is the way most of those "thorns" are, which the Lord sends our way. Thank God that most of them [are ]{.class_s36M}small. The Lord knows that most of us could not handle a big one. But it is the little irritations that wear us down. It is sometimes easier to get over a big thing than it is a little thing. Take headaches, for example. There are few things in this world more distracting and nerve-racking than just a pain in your head that throbs and goes on all day. You don't have a temperature, you're not bleeding, no bones are busted, but that headache just goes on and on. That will drive you half crazy. [A headache that will last two days and two nights]{.class_s36M} will just about put you in the insane asylum. It is little things like [that ]{.class_s36M}that will just about take you down.

Now, Paul prayed three times for God to take that thing away. That was a guy that had such faith in prayer that he could [raise dead people]{.class_s36M}, and the Lord wouldn't take that thing away from him.

Instead, this is what the Lord told him, ["My grace is sufficient for thee"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 9). [The Lord left it stuck in him and then gave him the grace to bear it]{.class_s36M}. Country folk say, "Well, the good Lord won't put any more on you than you can bear." The Bible doesn't say that, but verse 9 is the verse from which they get that.

What the Bible does say in explanation is ["my strength is made perfect in weakness"]{.class_s36N}; that is, [God's]{.class_s36M} strength in your life is perfected in [your]{.class_s36M} weakness. Even if you don't realize it, [that]{.class_s36M} is a blessing. Bob Jones Sr. used to say, "Blessed is the man that can make stepping stones out of his stumbling blocks." The idea is that if you have a defect or weakness in your character that hinders you in serving the Lord, that God may let that thing stay right there. By fighting that thing all of your life, the[ Lord's strength is perfected in you]{.class_s36M}, and you manifest to others that it is not [you ]{.class_s36M}who is getting the victory, [but the Lord]{.class_s36M}. When that thing is done, they will acknowledge that it is God in your life, [and then He gets the glory.]{.class_s36M}

So Paul glories, he brags about, his infirmities, because the more infirmities he has the more power he will get from Christ to overcome those infirmities and accomplish the ministry God has given him.

Note that Paul said ["infirmities."]{.class_s36N} That is a word for sicknesses. Paul was not only sick all of his life, he had multiple illnesses. I heard Oral Roberts preach on that text one time. He fought that text like a salmon swimming upstream. He said that the ["thorn in the flesh"]{.class_s36N} was [persecution]{.class_s36M}. But Paul mentions that in verse 10. Then he changed it to "the inability to do something." But the Bible says, ["infirmities."]{.class_s36N}

These ["infirmities"]{.class_s36N} must have been a humiliating thing for Paul. He could heal people with [handkerchiefs ]{.class_s36M}and [aprons]{.class_s36M}, but he couldn't heal [himself]{.class_s36M}. He carried a [medical doctor]{.class_s36M}, Luke, with him wherever he went (Col. 4:14). When it came time for him to die, he said, ["Only Luke is with me:]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 4:11). [At the end of his ministry, Paul could not heal Timothy]{.class_s36M} (1 Tim. 5:23) or [Trophimus ]{.class_s36M}(2 Tim. 4:20). The Acts of the Apostles was OVER. You can cancel the entire charismatic movement, from Azusa to Brownsville, without a second thought. (See [Modern Charismatic Revival Doctrines Examined,]{.class_s36M} 1998.)

12:10 Therefore I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake: for when I am weak, then am I strong.

11 I am become a fool in glorying; ye have compelled me: for I ought to have been commended of you: for in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles, though I be nothing.

12 Truly the signs of an apostle were wrought among you in all patience, in signs, and wonders, and mighty deeds.

Paul had just gotten through bragging, in chapter 11, about all the things he lists in verse 10: ["infirmities," "reproaches," "necessities," "persecutions,"]{.class_s36N} and ["distresses."]{.class_s36N} He takes ["pleasure"]{.class_s36N} in those things because when he is weak physically, he is strong spiritually.

That is a great truth which I don't like to admit. I don't like to read that, and I don't appreciate it being in that Book. I would "prefer" that it wasn't there, but there it is.

Peter said, ["He that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin"]{.class_s36N} (1 Pet. 4:1). If you want to try that out, try fasting about three days and notice the difference in your inner life, your spiritual life, with the Lord at that time. About the third day you are just as weak as a dish rag. You watch how much less hold the world and flesh have on you than they had when you started.

I'm not going to say that fasting is going to make you spiritual because you might get proud of your ability to fast. You might go around boasting and saying, "I fasted for seven days and seven nights." Well, then the Devil's got you anyway, see. But as a matter of fact, just try that out and notice that when you get weak [physically]{.class_s36M}, the Lord sustains you; He hold you up. If any of you have been really sick or have been through any serious problems, then you know what that verse mean. As you get up in age, you really get to know what that verse means.

["I am become a fool in glorying"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 11). Now, Paul has made a fool out of himself because he has spent the last two chapters bragging. It is true that he has bragged about things about which no one else would brag. Nevertheless, he [has ]{.class_s36M}bragged, and he considered [that ]{.class_s36M}the mark of a fool.

Moreover, he blames the Corinthians (["ye have compelled me"]{.class_s36N}). Indirectly, that was true. They kept bragging about that false bunch that was leading them astray, so he had to brag to keep up with the false bunch in their eyes.

Now, [they ought]{.class_s36M} to have recommended him. They should have been the ones bragging about [him]{.class_s36M}. After all, he was an apostle. And he says, ["in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles."]{.class_s36N} How's that for a claim? He wasn't behind the three apostles who were the closest to Christ: Peter, James, and John.

P.S.:[ "Though I be nothing."]{.class_s36N} He didn't want them to get the wrong idea.

Nevertheless, Paul did have all of the apostolic signs, and he performed them before the Corinthians ["in all patience, in signs, and wonders, and mighty deeds"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12). So the signs are apostolic, and when the last of the apostles die, the signs disappear.

12:13 For what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you? forgive me this wrong.

14 Behold, the third time I am ready to come to you; and I will not be burdensome to you: for I seek not yours, but you: for the children ought not to lay up for the parents, but the parents for the children.

15 And I will very gladly spend and be spent for you; though the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved.

In verse 13, Paul reverts back to sarcasm.[ "What is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you? forgive me this wrong."]{.class_s36N} In other words, Paul is saying, "Why are you feeling badly about the way that I treated you? Is it because I wouldn't let you support me? Is it because I made my own living and took support from other churches? Well, then, forgive me for doing that; I should have taken your money for myself." That's all said [sarcastically]{.class_s36M}.

If you doubt that verse 13 is sarcasm, look at the next verse: ["Behold, the third time I am ready to come to you; AND I WILL NOT BE BURDENSOME TO YOU."]{.class_s36N} He is going to do exactly as he did the first two times: [make his own living and take support from other churches.]{.class_s36M}

He doesn't want their money; he wasn't looking for their financial support. He wanted [them]{.class_s36M}! He was after their hides: ["I seek not yours, BUT YOU!"]{.class_s36N} If the Lord gets you, then He'll get your money along with you.

Did you know that every problem in the church is a [love problem]{.class_s36M}? Do you know why folks don't give generously?[ It is because they don't love the Lord.]{.class_s36M} They don't witness [because they don't love the Lord.]{.class_s36M} They don't attend the services [because they don't love preaching. ]{.class_s36M}They don't study the Bible [because they don't love that Book]{.class_s36M}. If you get that [love ]{.class_s36M}thing fixed, the rest of that mess will straighten out.

David said, ["I had rather be doorkeeper in the house of my God, than to dwell in the tents of wickedness"]{.class_s36N} (Psa. 84:10). Sam Jones preached that verse one time, and he said this: "A man will always do what he'd [rather ]{.class_s36M}do. If I could get your 'rathers' fixed, I'd get you fixed."

If a fellow would [rather]{.class_s36M} to go church than watch TV, then he will go. If a fellow would [rather]{.class_s36M} get saved than go to Hell, then he will get saved. If a fellow would [rather]{.class_s36M} read the Bible than the newspapers, then he will read the Bible. The problem is the "rathers."

["For the children ought not to lay up for the parents, but the parents for the children."]{.class_s36N} The illustration is that of a parent taking care of his children. Since Paul led them to the Lord, he is their spiritual [father]{.class_s36M}. Therefore he is taking care of them, instead of making [them ]{.class_s36M}take care of [him]{.class_s36M}.

Some of the brethren get really radical in these matters. One group takes Matthew 6:34 (["Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself"]{.class_s36N}) and "lives by faith." They make absolutely no preparation for anything, and they leave everything completely to the Lord and chance and the Devil. That is not right. Paul said, ["If any provide not for his own, and specially for those of his own house, he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 5:8). So a man should make some provision for his children. The question comes up: "How much?"

That is the other side of the radicals. They are radical the other way---[toward the world]{.class_s36M}. They are slaves to medical insurance, life insurance, car payments, college funds, etc. Sam Jones said, "There is no point in leaving a kid a whole lot of money, because if he is no good it will ruin him and if he is any good he won't need it." That's his thinking. I tend to agree with him, but I think that you should leave them something. You better hadn't leave them a whole lot.

A kid can learn something by knocking his head against the world, coming up and working his way through, that he can't learn any other way. If you dump too much in that kid's lap you will ruin him.

If I died, I've got enough junk that my family could split it up and keep things going for about three years. I think that is long enough for anybody to be out of work. In fact, I believe if you are in [good health ]{.class_s36M}that it is way too long.

So the Lord leaves it to your discretion as to handle that thing. Don't just leave your family to run around like a pack of wild dogs, but don't bat your brains out trying to overdo it the other way. There is no use to accumulate a big pile.

Brethren, what if the Lord should come? Suppose you had a big bundle in the bank when the Lord returned. [Who would get it?]{.class_s36M} I'll guarantee you it won't be a missionary! It will be the [Devil ]{.class_s36M}and the government of the Antichrist that get it.

Now, what Paul did for the Corinthians, he did ["gladly"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 15). He did not mind spending his time, energy, and money on them. But it seemed to him that the more love he showed them, the less they loved [him]{.class_s36M}. Some folks are like that. The more that you do for them, the more they [hate ]{.class_s36M}you. You will help some people, and they will turn right around and cut your throat. I don't know what that is; I never could really understand that.

I have appreciated anything that anybody ever gave me. I thank God for it. People give me all kinds of junk. I've got all kinds of junk, and I thank God for every bit of it. I do something with all of it. I eat it, and if I can't eat it, I give it to my kids. If they can't eat it, I give it to the dogs or cat or bunny rabbits. I wear it, and if I can't wear it, I'll use it for work clothes.

If you knew how much folks have given me that I didn't earn myself, just because they love the Lord and love the Book, you just wouldn't believe it. If you are a faithful minister, there will be people that love the Lord and love that Bible that will help you out and give you things. Go ahead and take them. Don't have a bad conscience about it.

When you take a gift, make sure you have the right attitude about it. [Don't minister for gifts]{.class_s36M}. Never feel obligated to someone just because they give you a gift. I mean, a gift is [a gift]{.class_s36M}. There are no strings attached. You should never compromise your preaching or convictions because someone gives to you.

When you give, make sure [you ]{.class_s36M}give the same way. You should not be politicking when you help someone out. [You don't do a favor to get a favor in return.]{.class_s36M} There are some people who have ulterior motives behind everything they do. Make sure that when you give that you give with a heart of love [with no strings attached.]{.class_s36M} And when you take a gift, be thankful for it, but don't get [hooked]{.class_s36M}.

12:16 But be it so, I did not burden you: nevertheless, being crafty, I caught you with guile.

17 Did I make a gain of you by any of them whom I sent unto you?

18 I desired Titus, and with him I sent a brother. Did Titus make a gain of you? walked we not in the same spirit? walked we not in the same steps?

In verse 16, Paul is once more being sarcastic. ["Being crafty, I caught you with guile."]{.class_s36N} He used trickery and deception, is what he is saying. Of course, he didn't. The reference is to the fact that he did not take any money or support from them when he was with them. He is sarcastically saying that he purposefully did not take anything from them in order to [trick them]{.class_s36M} into receiving Christ and serving Him. The sense in which that was true was that Paul did it on purpose with that goal in mind, but it was by no means a trick or deception. Paul did not do wrong to win the Corinthians. He simply did not do it the same way he did at other places.

There will be times in the ministry when "Standard Operating Procedure" (SOP) will not work. You will have to be as wise as a serpent and as harmless as a dove (Matt. 10:16) in order to get the job done right. What works in one place, may not work in another, so you are going to need to pray for wisdom.

Now, not only did Paul not ["make a gain"]{.class_s36N} of them, but none of his helpers did either (vs. 17). Titus loved the Corinthians the same as Paul (2 Cor. 8:16). He had not taken any money from them to support himself. Neither had Titus' companion (vs. 18). They were of the ["same spirit"]{.class_s36N} as Paul; they walked ["in the same steps."]{.class_s36N} They weren't mercenary.

12:19 Again, think ye that we excuse ourselves unto you? we speak before God in Christ: but we do all things, dearly beloved, for your edifying.

20 For I fear, lest, when I come, I shall not find you such as I would, and that I shall be found unto you such as ye would not: lest there be debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults:

21 And lest, when I come again, my God will humble me among you, and that I shall bewail many which have sinned already, and have not repented of the uncleanness and fornication and lasciviousness which they have committed.

Paul was making no alibi to the Corinthians (["think ye that we excuse ourselves to you?"]{.class_s36N} vs. 19). God knew Paul's heart, and the things that Paul did (not living off their support, specifically) were to build up the Corinthians in their faith (["for your edifying,"]{.class_s36N} which was the purpose of an apostle---Eph. 4:11--12).

When I read verse 20, I often read it [as Christ Himself writing to the Christian]{.class_s36M}.[ "For I ]{.class_s36N}\[Jesus\][ fear, lest, when I come ]{.class_s36N}\[the Rapture\],[ I shall not find you ]{.class_s36N}\[the Christian\][ such as I would ]{.class_s36N}\[living the way I want you to\][, and that I shall be found unto you such as ye would not ]{.class_s36N}\[ashamed to see Christ because of sin\][."]{.class_s36N} And then the things that he lists are common problems in a local congregation: ["debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults."]{.class_s36N} Now ask yourself this: When Christ returns, is [that]{.class_s36M} the way you want Him to find you and your church?

["And lest\...my God will humble me among you"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 21). What Paul didn't want was to come to Corinth and find the Corinthians living the things he lists at the end of verse 20. You have to admit that would be awfully humiliating. The Holy Spirit would be pricking your heart and saying, "Look at[ your converts,]{.class_s36M} Paul. How are they doing? Pretty rough shape, don't you think?"

I guess that every Christian who has done personal work has seen that sometime. You lead a drunk to Christ, and he gets back on the bottle. You lead a fellow to Christ, and he goes back into the world. You start bragging about a convert that looks like he will do something for God, and about that time, down he goes. That's a humbling experience.

[]{#c32J.xhtml}

## Chapter 13 {.heading_s39}

13:1 This is the third time I am coming to you. In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established.

2 I told you before, and foretell you, as if I were present, the second time; and being absent now I write to them which heretofore have sinned, and to all other, that, if I come again, I will not spare:

3 Since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me, which to you-ward is not weak, but is mighty in you.

4 For though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God toward you.

Again (12:14), ["this is the third time"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 1) Paul comes to Corinth, and he equates this third [visit ]{.class_s36M}with the third [witness ]{.class_s36M}that what he is saying is so. ["In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established."]{.class_s36N} That is a quote from Deuteronomy 19:15. It is a reference to a Jewish, Old Testament court of law. A man could not be condemned of a crime unless two or three witnesses agreed to what was done. So each visit of Paul confirms what he has said to the Corinthians.

In verse 2, Paul gives them fair warning, just as he did ["the second time"]{.class_s36N} (which is a reference to his visit in Acts 20:2--3), that if they don't repent of their sins for which he has rebuked them in this letter, that he ["will not spare"]{.class_s36N} them. In other words, he is going to forcefully exercise [church discipline ]{.class_s36M}on the ones who will not turn from their sins.

The Corinthians are like little children---just daring Paul to say or do something about their sin (["since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me,"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 3). He tells them that if things aren't right when he arrives that they would find out pretty quick the kind of authority which he exercised as an apostle. He was going to lay right into them.

The idea behind verse 4 is that Paul was weak; he was infirmed. But so was Christ on the cross:[ "he was crucified in weakness."]{.class_s36N} But the "weak" Christ, that was put to death on the cross like a sinner, rose again in the power of God. Even so, the weak, frail Christian still has the power of God working through him toward others.

13:5 Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves. Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates?

6 But I trust that ye shall know that we are not reprobates.

["Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 5). ["The faith"]{.class_s36N} is more than just [salvation]{.class_s36M}. Usually this part of verse 5 is used to urge a person to make sure he is saved. But it is more than that. ["The faith"]{.class_s36N} [is the full body of Christian doctrine.]{.class_s36M} It is used the same way in Jude 3: ["the faith which was once delivered unto the saints."]{.class_s36N} To ["be in the faith"]{.class_s36N} is to be established in what you are [supposed to believe]{.class_s36M} as a Christian.

["How that Jesus Christ is in you."]{.class_s36N} Once more Paul reminds the Corinthians that, if they are really saved (["except ye be reprobates"]{.class_s36N}), Jesus Christ, in the person of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor. 3:16),[ lives in them]{.class_s36M}. This has come up several times between the two epistles: 1 Corinthians 6:19--20; 2 Corinthians 4:6--7, 6:16.

In verse 6, Paul has shown them evidence that he is saved. In the next verse, though, the last phrase indicates that the false teachers had succeeded in convincing, at least some of the Corinthians, that Paul was [not saved.]{.class_s36M} Undoubtedly this had to do with his speech (2 Cor. 10:10, 11:6).

I get that all of the time. One preacher said, "Anyone who writes the way Ruckman writes ought to spend some time in Hell." The idea is if you expose apostasy in [Fundamental Christianity]{.class_s36M}, then you cannot possibly be saved. R. L. Hymers phoned me up one time and said, "If you come over here and let [me]{.class_s36M} lead you to Christ and cast out the demons, then I'll let you preach in my church. I think you could really do something for God." I laughed at him.

Brethren, when I got saved, I saw God take away my cigars, my liquor, my dirty women, my dirty music, and my dirty talk. [He gave me a love for the Book,]{.class_s36M} a love for the brethren, and a love for clean things. If that weren't enough, [He called me into the ministry]{.class_s36M} and has given me souls saved every year, young men called to preach, and young men and women to train. All of my kids and grandkids are saved. All of my bills are paid. I am still in good enough health to play hockey at the age of [eighty]{.class_s36M}. What do I care [what]{.class_s36M} my enemies think?! I consider myself one of God's spoiled children. If the "brethren" want to talk, let them talk!

13:7 Now I pray to God that ye do no evil; not that we should appear approved, but that ye should do that which is honest, though we be as reprobates.

8 For we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth.

9 For we are glad, when we are weak, and ye are strong: and this also we wish, even your perfection.

10 Therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness, according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction.

["Now I pray to God that ye do no evil; not that we should appear approved"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 7). The truth of the matter is that most of your Fundamental Christian schools have such strict regulations, [not ]{.class_s36M}because they care about the students, but because the schools care about[ their own testimonies]{.class_s36M}. They keep the kids in a jail (with tall fences and barbed wire in some cases!) so that nobody can make a false move and hurt the [reputation of the school]{.class_s36M}. In that way they can take pictures of the red-cheeked young men and the pretty, clean young girls and say, "Look at this. [We ]{.class_s36M}make sure that [our ]{.class_s36M}students are clean and safe." That way everyone pats them on their backs and tells them what a good job they are doing and sends their kids to those schools for the [clean environment ]{.class_s36M}(\$). It's all about bringing in the students who pay the bills and build the [reputation of the school]{.class_s36M} to bring in students who pay the bills and build the [reputation of the school,]{.class_s36M} etc., It is of the flesh; [it is carnal.]{.class_s36M}

Paul is careful about that. He is not concerned whether he gets credit for the Corinthians living right as long as they live right. They can even think that he is lost, as long as they live right.

In verse 8, Paul is saying that God will not allow him to do anything [against ]{.class_s36M}the truth. That is what his enemies accused him of. But with Paul, God laid a strict course for him. It is like what he said in 1 Corinthians 9:16, ["For though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of: for necessity is laid upon me; yea, woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel!"]{.class_s36N} Paul had an [obligation]{.class_s36M}, given to him by God, to the truth. The only way he could violate that obligation was to his own hurt and detriment.

Verse 9, ["This also we wish, even your perfection."]{.class_s36N} Again, this is one of the purposes of the apostles (Eph. 4:12). Paul wants the Corinthians to grow up in Christ and be fully mature in the faith. A Christian is made perfect by the Scriptures (2 Tim. 3:17) and through suffering (1 Pet. 5:10; 2 Cor. 12:9).

Now, Paul gives [one last warning]{.class_s36M} to the Corinthians in verse 10. Paul is hoping that the Corinthians will read what he has written and repent and stop their sin [before]{.class_s36M} he arrives. But he is prepared to ["use sharpness, according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction.]{.class_s36N} This is what he spoke of in verses 3 and 4.

13:11 Finally, brethren, farewell. Be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace; and the God of love and peace shall be with you.

12 Greet one another with an holy kiss.

13 All the saints salute you.

14 The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all. Amen.

Paul begins his good-byes in verse 11. I have already made comments on ["be perfect"]{.class_s36N} in verse 9. ["Be of good comfort"]{.class_s36N} is the way Paul started out the epistle in 1:3--4. And it goes without saying that it is ["GOOD comfort"]{.class_s36N} Paul wishes for them. He wants them to be comforted when they get right and are doing right, not when they are sinning and following false teachers.

["Be of one mind"]{.class_s36N} would be "the mind of Christ" mentioned in Philippians 2:5 (practical application) and 1 Corinthians 2:16 (doctrinal standing). He tells two women in Philippi, ["Be of the same mind IN THE LORD"]{.class_s36N} (Phil. 4:2).

["Live in peace."]{.class_s36N} Don't be fussing and fighting all the time. He tells the Christians at Rome, ["If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men"]{.class_s36N} (Rom. 12:18). A bishop is not to be ["a striker"]{.class_s36N} or ["a brawler"]{.class_s36N} (1 Tim. 3:3).

Modern Christians have a knack for picking the wrong fights. As Christians we should be ["valiant for the truth" ]{.class_s36N}(Jer. 9:3). We should ["fight the good fight"]{.class_s36N} (2 Tim. 4:7) for Jesus Christ (John 14:6) and the word of God (John 17:17), using the spiritual weapons God has given us (2 Cor. 10:4--5).

I have already given a serious commentary on the ["holy kiss"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 12) under 1 Corinthians 16:20. But just for laughs, let's look at verse 12 in the "Phillip's Modern English" paraphrase: "Give each other a hearty handshake" or "A hearty handshake all around, please" (depending on which edition you get!). I'll give you a better one: ["Give 'em da raspberry!"]{.class_s36M}

Verse 13 is a standard greeting at the close of a letter.

Verse 14 is one of those great Trinitarian formulas in the New Testament. For another, look at Matthew 28:19 and see comments in chapter 3 of [Theological Studies,]{.class_s36M} volume I. Compare the benediction of verse 14 with that of Numbers 6:24--26.

God the Father: ["The LORD bless thee, and keep thee"]{.class_s36N} (Num. 6:24); ["the love of God"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14).

God the Son: ["The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee"]{.class_s36N} (Num. 6:25; ["The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14).

God the Holy Spirit: ["The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace"]{.class_s36N} (Num. 6:26); ["the communion of the Holy Ghost"]{.class_s36N} (vs. 14).

There's the Trinity.

"The second epistle to the Corinthians was written from Philippi, a city of Macedonia, by Titus and Lucas."
